- **Step 3**: Differentiation converts position (geometry) into kinematics (motion), which is the input for dynamics.
- **Step 4**: Inertia is the property of mass to resist change of motion; without it, the engine would have no vibration, no need for balancing, and no need for a flywheel.
- **Step 5**: Decomposition into harmonics is what allows the engineer to balance primary and secondary forces separately using different mechanisms (e.g., Lanchester balancer for primary, cross-coupler for secondary).
- **Step 6-7**: The gas force is what produces useful work; the inertia force is a parasitic load that must be overcome by the gas force.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum** set of formulas you must memorize for the KTU ESE. Every question on this topic reduces to a substitution into one of these equations.

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $n = l / r$ | Ratio of connecting rod to crank length | dimensionless |
| 2 | $x = r\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | Piston displacement from crank center (approximate) | m |
| 3 | $v = r\omega\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$ | Piston velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $a = r\omega^{2}\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | Piston acceleration | m/s² |
| 5 | $F_{i} = m\,r\omega^{2}\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | Total inertia force on reciprocating parts | N |
| 6 | $F_{P} = m\,r\omega^{2}\cos\theta$ | Primary inertia force (1st harmonic) | N |
| 7 | $F_{S} = \dfrac{m\,r\omega^{2}}{n}\cos 2\theta$ | Secondary inertia force (2nd harmonic) | N |
| 8 | $F_{T} \approx P_{n}\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$ | Tangential force / Crank effort (approx) | N |
| 9 | $T = F_{T} \cdot r$ | Turning moment on crankshaft | N·m |
| 10 | $m_{1} = m_{r}\cdot(L - a)/L$ | Equivalent reciprocating mass of connecting rod (piston pin) | kg |
| 11 | $m_{2} = m_{r}\cdot a/L$ | Equivalent rotating mass of connecting rod (crank pin) | kg |
| 12 | $F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{gas}} \pm F_{i}$ | Piston effort (net force on gudgeon pin) | N |

> **Convention for signs**: When the inertia force acts **opposite to gas force** (i.e., gas is pushing piston and inertia resists motion), use $F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{gas}} - F_{i}$. When both act in the **same direction** (deceleration phase), use $F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{gas}} + F_{i}$. For the expansion stroke, the gas force and inertia force usually **oppose each other** between IDC and 90°.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine balancing and vibration control** in automotive and aerospace propulsion.
- **Crankshaft design** for fatigue life prediction (cyclic loading).
- **Foundation and mount design** to isolate vibrations from the chassis.
- **Flywheel sizing** to limit speed fluctuation from the cyclic turning moment.
- **Reciprocating compressor and pump design** in process industries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Piston Acceleration (from Geometric Closure)

Consider a slider-crank mechanism as in the standard KTU diagram: crank OA of length **r** rotates about O; connecting rod AB of length **l** connects the crank pin A to the piston B; the cylinder axis is along the line OB. Let **θ** be the angle of the crank from IDC, and **φ** be the angle the connecting rod makes with the cylinder axis.

**Geometric closure of the slider-crank loop** (horizontal projection):

$$r\sin\theta = l\sin\phi$$

This gives the exact relationship:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{n}$$

The exact piston displacement from crank center O is:

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\cos\phi$$

To get an engineering-friendly closed-form expression, we approximate $\cos\phi$ using the binomial expansion $(1 - x)^{1/2} \approx 1 - x/2$ for $x \ll 1$:

$$\cos\phi = \sqrt{1 - \frac{\sin^{2}\theta}{n^{2}}} \approx 1 - \frac{\sin^{2}\theta}{2n^{2}}$$

Substituting and using the identity $\sin^{2}\theta = (1 - \cos 2\theta)/2$:

$$x = r\cos\theta + l\left[1 - \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{4n^{2}}\right]$$

$$x = r\cos\theta + l - \frac{l}{4n^{2}} + \frac{l}{4n^{2}}\cos 2\theta$$

Since $l/n^{2} = r/n$ (because $l = nr$), the expression simplifies to:

$$x = r\cos\theta + l - \frac{r}{4n} + \frac{r}{4n}\cos 2\theta$$

**Differentiating once with respect to time** (using $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$):

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r\omega}{2n}\sin 2\theta$$

The negative sign indicates that for increasing θ from IDC, the piston moves back toward ODC, decreasing x. Taking the **magnitude** (a common KTU convention):

$$v = r\omega\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$$

**Differentiating once more** with respect to time:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = r\omega^{2}\cos\theta + \frac{r\omega^{2}}{n}\cos 2\theta$$

$$\boxed{a = r\omega^{2}\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Inertia Force

By **Newton's Second Law**, the inertia force on the reciprocating mass m is:

$$F_{i} = m \cdot a = m\,r\omega^{2}\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

$$\boxed{F_{i} = F_{P} + F_{S} = \underbrace{m\,r\omega^{2}\cos\theta}_{\text{Primary Force}} + \underbrace{\frac{m\,r\omega^{2}}{n}\cos 2\theta}_{\text{Secondary Force}}}$$

**The "Why" of decomposition**: The primary force completes one cycle per crank revolution, while the secondary force completes two cycles. They have different frequencies, so they must be balanced using different mechanisms. This is the foundation of multi-cylinder engine balancing in later modules.

### 3.3 Derivation of Equivalent Mass of Connecting Rod (Dynamic Equivalence)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Why can't we treat the entire connecting rod as a point mass at the piston pin?"* The answer is that this **over-estimates** the inertia force. The correct approach is to **dynamically replace** the rod by two point masses — one at the piston pin (B) and one at the crank pin (A) — such that the equivalent system has the **same total mass, same center of gravity, and same mass moment of inertia** as the real rod.

Let:
- $m_{r}$ = mass of connecting rod
- $L$ = length of connecting rod
- $a$ = distance from piston pin B to center of gravity G of the rod
- $k$ = radius of gyration of the rod about its CG

**Condition 1 — Same total mass:**

$$m_{1} + m_{2} = m_{r}$$

**Condition 2 — Same CG location** (measuring moments about the piston pin B):

$$m_{1}(0) + m_{2}(L) = m_{r}(a) \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{2} = \frac{m_{r} \cdot a}{L}$$

**Condition 3 — Same mass moment of inertia about the piston pin B** (using parallel axis theorem, $I_{B} = I_{G} + m_{r}a^{2} = m_{r}k^{2} + m_{r}a^{2}$):

$$m_{1}(0)^{2} + m_{2}(L)^{2} = m_{r}(k^{2} + a^{2})$$

$$m_{2}L^{2} = m_{r}(k^{2} + a^{2}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{2} = \frac{m_{r}(k^{2} + a^{2})}{L^{2}}$$

**Equating the two expressions for $m_{2}$** gives a quadratic for $a$:

$$\frac{a}{L} = \frac{k^{2} + a^{2}}{L^{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad aL = k^{2} + a^{2}$$

$$a^{2} - aL + k^{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{L \pm \sqrt{L^{2} - 4k^{2}}}{2}$$

(The physically valid root is the one giving $a < L/2$, typically the smaller positive root.)

**Final equivalent masses** (using $m_{1} = m_{r} - m_{2}$):

$$\boxed{m_{1} = m_{r}\cdot\frac{L - a}{L} \quad \text{(at piston pin, reciprocates)}}$$

$$\boxed{m_{2} = m_{r}\cdot\frac{a}{L} \quad \text{(at crank pin, rotates)}}$$

**Total equivalent reciprocating mass** in the inertia force equation:

$$m = m_{\text{piston}} + m_{1} = m_{\text{piston}} + m_{r}\cdot\frac{L - a}{L}$$

**Two simplified approximations used in textbook problems** (commonly asked in KTU Part A):
- **If the mass of the connecting rod is small** (rod is light): $m \approx m_{\text{piston}}$.
- **If a = L/2** (CG at the middle of the rod): $m = m_{\text{piston}} + m_{r}/2$.

### 3.4 Derivation of Crank Effort and Turning Moment

Let $P_{n}$ be the **net force along the cylinder axis** (piston effort) — i.e., the algebraic sum of gas force and inertia force resolved along the line of stroke. To find the tangential force $F_{T}$ on the crank pin, resolve $P_{n}$ at the wrist pin B:

**Force triangle at the wrist pin B:**
The connecting rod carries a compressive force $F_{R}$ along its length AB. This force can be resolved into:
- A component along the cylinder axis: $P_{n} = F_{R}\cos\phi$
- A component perpendicular to the cylinder axis: $F_{R}\sin\phi$ (side thrust on cylinder walls)

The tangential force on the crank pin is then obtained by resolving $F_{R}$ perpendicular to OA:

$$F_{T} = F_{R} \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi) = \frac{P_{n}\cos\phi \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi} = P_{n}\cdot\frac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$$

Using the approximation $\phi \ll 1$ (small angle since $l \gg r$) and the binomial expansion, this simplifies to the **standard KTU formula**:

$$\boxed{F_{T} = P_{n}\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)}$$

**Turning moment on the crankshaft:**

$$\boxed{T = F_{T} \cdot r = P_{n}\cdot r\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Force Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete analytical pipeline** from crank rotation to turning moment, showing how each derived quantity feeds the next. This is the standard KTU board flow used in 14-mark derivations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CRANK OA ROTATES AT ANGULAR SPEED OMEGA] --> B[CRANK ANGLE THETA FROM IDC]
    B --> C[PISTON DISPLACEMENT X]
    B --> D[PISTON VELOCITY V]
    B --> E[PISTON ACCELERATION A]
    E --> F[INERTIA FORCE F EQUALS M TIMES A]
    F --> G[PRIMARY FORCE FP EQUALS MR OMEGA SQ COS THETA]
    F --> H[SECONDARY FORCE FS EQUALS MR OMEGA SQ OVER N TIMES COS 2 THETA]
    G --> I[NET PISTON EFFORT PN]
    H --> I
    J[GAS FORCE FG FROM COMBUSTION] --> I
    I --> K[TANGENTIAL CRANK EFFORT FT]
    K --> L[TURNING MOMENT T EQUALS FT TIMES R]
    L --> M[CRANKSHAFT ROTATIONAL OUTPUT]
    N[CONNECTING ROD MASS MR] --> O[DYNAMIC EQUIVALENT SYSTEM]
    O --> P[M1 AT PISTON PIN RECIPROCATES]
    O --> Q[M2 AT CRANK PIN ROTATES]
    P --> F
    Q --> R[ROTATING MASS BALANCE OUTSIDE THIS MODULE]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of the Two Approximation Methods

For problems where the KTU paper asks to choose an approximation, the following flow shows the **decision tree**:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[GIVEN N EQUALS L OVER R] --> B{N IS GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO 4}
    B -->|YES| C[APPROXIMATION 1: NEGLECT SECONDARY TERM]
    B -->|NO| D[APPROXIMATION 2: USE FULL ACCELERATION EXPRESSION]
    C --> E[ACCELERATION A EQUALS R OMEGA SQ TIMES COS THETA]
    D --> F[ACCELERATION A EQUALS R OMEGA SQ TIMES COS THETA PLUS COS 2 THETA OVER N]
    E --> G[INERTIA FORCE F EQUALS M R OMEGA SQ COS THETA]
    F --> H[INERTIA FORCE F EQUALS M R OMEGA SQ COS THETA PLUS M R OMEGA SQ OVER N TIMES COS 2 THETA]
    G --> I[SINGLE HARMONIC ANALYSIS]
    H --> J[TWO COMPONENT PRIMARY AND SECONDARY ANALYSIS]
```

### 4.3 Physical Schematic Description (Block-Adapted)

> [!NOTE]
> Since a literal geometric drawing of a slider-crank mechanism is not feasible in a flowchart, the following block representation captures the **physical parameter relationships** clearly.

**Slider-crank geometric block** (referenced for all derivations):

```
    PISTON B (moves horizontally along cylinder axis)
    ===========O===========     <-- Cylinder axis (line of stroke)
              |\
              | \  l  (connecting rod AB)
              |  \
              |   \
              |phi \  phi = angle of rod with cylinder
              |     \
              A______\
             / theta   <-- Crank OA of length r, angle theta from IDC
            /  
           O (crank center, fixed)
```

**Force triangle at the wrist pin B (the heart of crank effort derivation):**

```
                  Pn (Net force along cylinder, piston effort)
                  ===================> 
                                     \
                                      \  F_R (force along connecting rod)
                                       \
                                        \  theta + phi
                                         v
   The tangential force F_T = F_R * sin(theta + phi) is what
   produces the turning moment T = F_T * r on the crank pin A.
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the terms: (i) Inertia force, (ii) Piston effort, and (iii) Crank effort, in the context of a reciprocating engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(i) Inertia force (1 mark):** The force equal in magnitude to the product of the mass of the reciprocating parts and their acceleration, but acting in the direction opposite to the acceleration. It arises from Newton's second law ($F = m \cdot a$) and is given by $F_{i} = m\,r\omega^{2}\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$.

- **(ii) Piston effort (1 mark):** The net force acting on the piston along the line of stroke, obtained as the algebraic sum of the gas force (from combustion) and the inertia force. It is the force transmitted to the wrist/gudgeon pin.

- **(iii) Crank effort (1 mark):** The tangential component of the force at the crank pin, acting perpendicular to the crank. It is the force responsible for producing the turning moment on the crankshaft, given by $F_{T} = P_{n}\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**List any three assumptions made in the analysis of forces in a slider-crank mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. The connecting rod is treated as a **massless link of infinite stiffness** in the geometric analysis (or dynamically replaced by equivalent point masses at its ends).
2. The crank rotates at a **uniform angular velocity** $\omega$ (i.e., the effect of the flywheel in smoothing speed is assumed to be perfect).
3. The gas pressure acting on the piston is **uniform throughout the stroke** (for simplified problems) or is given as a function of crank angle from indicator diagrams.
4. *(Bonus, if asked)* The ratio $n = l/r$ is sufficiently large ($n \geq 4$) that second-order terms in $\sin^{2}\phi$ can be neglected in the binomial expansion of $\cos\phi$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **answer either Question A or Question B** in full. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the inertia force on the reciprocating parts of a reciprocating engine, neglecting the mass of the connecting rod. Clearly state the primary and secondary force components and explain their physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

For a slider-crank mechanism with crank radius r, connecting rod length l, and ratio n = l/r, the piston displacement from the crank center is (as derived in Section 3.1):

$$x = r\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

Differentiating twice w.r.t. time (with $\dot\theta = \omega$):

$$a = r\omega^{2}\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

By Newton's second law, the inertia force on the reciprocating mass m is:

$$F_{i} = m\,r\omega^{2}\cos\theta + \frac{m\,r\omega^{2}}{n}\cos 2\theta$$

$$\boxed{F_{i} = F_{P} + F_{S} = m\,r\omega^{2}\cos\theta \;+\; \frac{m\,r\omega^{2}}{n}\cos 2\theta}$$

where:
- **$F_{P} = m\,r\omega^{2}\cos\theta$ is the primary inertia force** (1 mark) — varies sinusoidally once per crank revolution (frequency = $\omega$).
- **$F_{S} = \dfrac{m\,r\omega^{2}}{n}\cos 2\theta$ is the secondary inertia force** (1 mark) — varies sinusoidally twice per crank revolution (frequency = $2\omega$).

**Physical significance (2 marks):** The primary force is the dominant inertia load and is balanced in multi-cylinder engines by symmetric crank arrangements. The secondary force has double the frequency and half the magnitude (since n ≥ 3), and is harder to balance — it requires cross-coupling or additional balance shafts.

---

**(b)** A single cylinder horizontal engine runs at **240 r.p.m.** The crank radius is **300 mm** and the connecting rod length is **900 mm**. The mass of the reciprocating parts is **50 kg**. The gas force on the piston during the expansion stroke is **15 kN** acting in the direction of motion. Determine the **piston effort** and the **turning moment on the crankshaft** when the crank has turned through **30°** from IDC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** N = 240 r.p.m., r = 0.3 m, l = 0.9 m, m = 50 kg, $F_{\text{gas}}$ = 15,000 N, θ = 30°.

**Step 1 — Compute ω and n (1 mark):**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 25.13 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$n = \frac{l}{r} = \frac{0.9}{0.3} = 3$$

**Step 2 — Compute inertia force at θ = 30° (2 marks):**

$$F_{i} = m\,r\omega^{2}\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

$$\cos 30° = 0.8660, \quad \cos 60° = 0.5000$$

$$F_{i} = 50 \times 0.3 \times (25.13)^{2}\left(0.8660 + \frac{0.5000}{3}\right)$$

$$F_{i} = 50 \times 0.3 \times 631.5 \times (0.8660 + 0.1667)$$

$$F_{i} = 9472.5 \times 1.0327 = 9782 \text{ N} \approx 9.78 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Compute net piston effort (1 mark):**

During expansion stroke from IDC to ODC, the gas force is in the direction of piston motion, and the inertia force (which opposes motion as the piston accelerates away from IDC) acts **opposite** to the gas force:

$$F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{gas}} - F_{i} = 15{,}000 - 9782 = 5218 \text{ N} \approx 5.22 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Compute crank effort (tangential force) (1.5 marks):**

$$F_{T} = F_{\text{net}}\left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$$

$$\sin 30° = 0.5000, \quad \sin 60° = 0.8660$$

$$F_{T} = 5218 \times \left(0.5000 + \frac{0.8660}{6}\right) = 5218 \times (0.5000 + 0.1443)$$

$$F_{T} = 5218 \times 0.6443 = 3362 \text{ N} \approx 3.36 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Compute turning moment (1.5 marks):**

$$T = F_{T} \cdot r = 3362 \times 0.3 = 1008.6 \text{ N·m} \approx 1.01 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Final Answer:** Piston Effort ≈ **5.22 kN**; Turning Moment ≈ **1.01 kN·m** (acting in the direction of crankshaft rotation).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** With the aid of a clear diagram, derive the expressions for the **equivalent mass of the connecting rod at the piston pin and at the crank pin** using the dynamic equivalence method. State the three conditions for dynamic equivalence. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**The Three Conditions for Dynamic Equivalence (1.5 marks):**
1. Same total mass: $m_{1} + m_{2} = m_{r}$
2. Same center of gravity location
3. Same mass moment of inertia about the reference point (piston pin)

**Setup (1 mark):** Let the connecting rod have mass $m_{r}$, length $L$, with center of gravity G located at distance $a$ from the piston pin B. The radius of gyration of the rod about G is $k$.

The rod is dynamically replaced by two point masses: $m_{1}$ at the piston pin B (which reciprocates) and $m_{2}$ at the crank pin A (which rotates with the crank).

**Condition 2 — CG coincidence (1.5 marks):**
Taking moments about B:

$$m_{2} \cdot L = m_{r} \cdot a \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{2} = \frac{m_{r}\,a}{L}$$

**Condition 3 — Moment of inertia match (1.5 marks):**
Moment of inertia of actual rod about piston pin B (parallel axis theorem):

$$I_{B} = m_{r}\,k^{2} + m_{r}\,a^{2}$$

For the equivalent system:

$$I_{B}' = m_{2}\,L^{2}$$

Equating:

$$m_{2}\,L^{2} = m_{r}(k^{2} + a^{2}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{2} = \frac{m_{r}(k^{2} + a^{2})}{L^{2}}$$

**Solving for the equivalent mass $m_{1}$ (1.5 marks):**
From condition 1: $m_{1} = m_{r} - m_{2}$

$$\boxed{m_{1} = m_{r}\cdot\frac{L - a}{L} \quad \text{(at piston pin, reciprocates)}}$$

$$\boxed{m_{2} = m_{r}\cdot\frac{a}{L} \quad \text{(at crank pin, rotates)}}$$

The position $a$ of the equivalent CG satisfies $a^{2} - aL + k^{2} = 0$, giving $a = \dfrac{L - \sqrt{L^{2} - 4k^{2}}}{2}$ (smaller root is taken).

---

**(b)** A vertical single-cylinder engine has a **bore of 250 mm** and a **stroke of 500 mm**. The connecting rod length is **1.2 m**, and the crank speed is **400 r.p.m.** The mass of the reciprocating parts (piston + piston pin) is **60 kg**, and the mass of the connecting rod is **25 kg**. The center of gravity of the connecting rod lies at **400 mm** from the piston pin. Calculate the **inertia force** on the piston when the crank is at (i) **0° (IDC)** and (ii) **90°** from IDC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Stroke = 500 mm → $r = 250$ mm $= 0.25$ m
- $l = 1.2$ m
- $N = 400$ r.p.m.
- $m_{\text{piston}} = 60$ kg, $m_{r} = 25$ kg
- $a = 400$ mm $= 0.4$ m from piston pin

**Step 1 — Compute ratio n and equivalent reciprocating mass (1.5 marks):**

$$n = \frac{l}{r} = \frac{1.2}{0.25} = 4.8$$

$$m_{1} = m_{r}\cdot\frac{L - a}{L} = 25 \times \frac{1.2 - 0.4}{1.2} = 25 \times \frac{0.8}{1.2} = 16.67 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Total reciprocating mass: } m = m_{\text{piston}} + m_{1} = 60 + 16.67 = 76.67 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute angular velocity (0.5 marks):**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 400}{60} = 41.89 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Inertia force at θ = 0° (IDC) (2.5 marks):**

$$F_{i} = m\,r\omega^{2}\left(\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

At θ = 0°: $\cos 0° = 1$, $\cos 0° = 1$ (since $2\theta = 0°$)

$$F_{i} = 76.67 \times 0.25 \times (41.89)^{2} \times \left(1 + \frac{1}{4.8}\right)$$

$$F_{i} = 76.67 \times 0.25 \times 1755.0 \times (1 + 0.2083)$$

$$F_{i} = 33628 \times 1.2083 = 40{,}632 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{40.63 \text{ kN}}$$

(For a **vertical** engine, this is the maximum inertia force — directed **downward** at IDC, so it adds to the weight of the piston.)

**Step 4 — Inertia force at θ = 90° (2.5 marks):**

At θ = 90°: $\cos 90° = 0$, $\cos 180° = -1$

$$F_{i} = 76.67 \times 0.25 \times 1755.0 \times \left(0 + \frac{-1}{4.8}\right)$$

$$F_{i} = 33628 \times (-0.2083) = -7003 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{-7.00 \text{ kN}}$$

**Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates the inertia force at 90° is **directed opposite to the assumed positive direction** (i.e., upward in a vertical engine), since the piston is decelerating as it approaches mid-stroke.

**Final Answer:** $F_{i,\text{IDC}} \approx$ **40.63 kN (downward)**; $F_{i,90°} \approx$ **7.00 kN (upward)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign convention (–2 marks):** Many students forget that in a **vertical** engine, the inertia force at IDC is **downward** and adds to piston weight. Always state direction explicitly.
> 2. **Combining gas force and inertia force (–2 marks):** The piston effort is the **algebraic** sum, not arithmetic. During the **expansion stroke** between IDC and 90°, gas and inertia oppose; between 90° and ODC, they may align. Always check sign based on stroke direction.
> 3. **Units (–1 mark):** Convert r.p.m. to rad/s using $2\pi/60$, not $\pi/30$ mistakenly. Lengths must be in **meters** before substitution into N·s²/m² units.
> 4. **Approximations (–1 mark):** The formula $a = r\omega^{2}(\cos\theta + \cos 2\theta/n)$ is an **approximation** valid for $n \geq 3$. For very small n, the exact expression using $\cos\phi$ must be used. Always mention the approximation in the answer.
> 5. **Neglecting connecting rod mass (–1 mark):** If the problem gives the connecting rod mass and CG, you **must** compute the equivalent reciprocating mass; you cannot just use the piston mass.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Must-Know" minimum for KTU ESE on this topic — keep this list as your last-night revision.**

- **Key geometric parameters:** $r$ (crank radius), $l$ (connecting rod length), $n = l/r$ (typically 3 to 5), $\theta$ (crank angle from IDC), $\phi$ (rod angle with cylinder axis).
- **Piston acceleration equation (memorize verbatim):** $a = r\omega^{2}\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$. This is the **single most important equation** in the entire module.
- **Inertia force decomposition:** Total = Primary ($mr\omega^{2}\cos\theta$, frequency $\omega$) + Secondary ($\frac{mr\omega^{2}}{n}\cos 2\theta$, frequency $2\omega$).
- **Primary force is maximum at IDC and ODC**, zero at 90° and 270°. **Secondary force is maximum at 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°** and zero at 45°, 135°, 225°, 315°.
- **Equivalent mass of connecting rod at piston pin:** $m_{1} = m_{r}(L-a)/L$. This is **added to the piston mass** in the inertia force formula.
- **Piston effort = Net force on gudgeon pin = Gas force ± Inertia force** (sign depends on stroke phase).
- **Crank effort (tangential force):** $F_{T} = P_{n}\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$. This is the force that produces useful torque.
- **Turning moment on crankshaft:** $T = F_{T} \cdot r$. This is the input to the next chapter on Turning Moment Diagrams and Flywheel Design.
- **Approximation rule:** If $n \geq 4$, secondary term is often neglected for **preliminary design** only. Always include in KTU exam answers.
- **Units reminder:** $r$ in m, $\omega$ in rad/s, $m$ in kg, $F$ in N, $T$ in N·m.
- **Two equivalent mass approximation methods (often asked as 3-mark question):**
  - *Over-estimation:* Rod mass entirely at piston pin → $m_{\text{eq}} = m_{p} + m_{r}$
  - *Under-estimation:* Rod mass entirely at crank pin → $m_{\text{eq}} = m_{p}$ (just piston)
  - *Dynamic equivalent (correct):* Use the formula above with CG location $a$.
- **Sign convention for vertical engines:** At IDC, inertia force acts **downward** (adds to weight); at ODC, inertia force acts **upward** (opposes weight).
- **Common numerical value of $g$:** $9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$ (only needed for weight calculations in vertical engine force balance, not for the inertia force itself).

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Force Acting on the Reciprocating Parts of an Engine**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piston Effort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Piston Effort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition of Piston Effort

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piston Effort (F_P):** It is defined as the *net force* actually acting on the piston along the line of stroke at any instant during the working stroke of the engine. Mathematically, it is the algebraic difference between the **thrust due to fluid pressure** (gas/steam) acting on the piston face and the **resistance due to the inertia of the reciprocating parts** (piston, connecting rod, cross-head, etc.).

In more rigorous KTU-board terms:

$$F_P = F_{FL} - F_I \quad \text{(during outward/expansion stroke)}$$

$$F_P = F_{FC} + F_I \quad \text{(during inward/compression stroke)}$$

where:
- $F_{FL}$ = Force due to fluid (steam/gas) pressure on the piston = $p_L \cdot A_p$
- $F_{FC}$ = Force during compression = $p_C \cdot A_p$
- $F_I$ = Inertia force of reciprocating parts = $m_R \cdot a$ (alternates in direction)

Here, **$A_p$** is the cross-sectional area of the piston, **$p_L$** is the pressure of fluid (steam/gas) on the piston face, and **$m_R$** is the mass of all reciprocating parts.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Pump

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine you are pushing down the handle of a bicycle pump against a resistive spring. The actual force you "feel" pushing back on your hand is the **net** of two things:
> 1. The internal air pressure resisting compression.
> 2. The inertial reaction of the pump piston rod as it accelerates.
>
> The *net* push you feel = Piston Effort. It is **not** the gross pressure force alone — it is the working force actually transmitted to the mechanism.

---

## 1.3 Physical Significance and Engineering Utility

The concept of piston effort is **fundamental** in:

| Application | Why Piston Effort Matters |
|-------------|---------------------------|
| Reciprocating Steam Engines | Designing crankshafts and flywheels |
| Internal Combustion (IC) Engines | Computing turning moment & fluctuation of speed |
| Compressors & Pumps | Sizing bearings and balancing reciprocating masses |
| Marine Propulsion | Designing propulsion link mechanisms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Piston Effort is the **stepping stone** to deriving *Crank Effort* and ultimately the *Turning Moment Diagram (Crank Effort Diagram)* — without mastering F_P, you cannot progress in Module 1 of Dynamics of Machinery.

---

## 1.4 Units, Dimensions & Standard Metrics

- **SI Unit:** **Newton (N)** — typically **kN** in engine design
- **Imperial:** lbf
- **Pressure Unit:** $\text{N/mm}^2$ or **bar** ($1\,\text{bar} = 0.1\,\text{N/mm}^2 = 10^5\,\text{Pa}$)
- **Piston area** $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$ where $D$ = piston diameter

---

## 1.5 Classification of Forces on the Piston

```
Piston Force System
        │
        ├── 1. Driving Force (during power stroke)
        │       → Steam/Gas Pressure × Piston Area
        │
        ├── 2. Resisting Force
        │       ├── a) Inertia of reciprocating parts
        │       └── b) Friction (often neglected in initial analysis)
        │
        └── 3. Net Force = Piston Effort (F_P)
```

> [!NOTE]
> In most KTU problems, the **weight of reciprocating parts is neglected** as it is balanced by the vertical reaction at the crosshead/guide. Only the *fluctuating inertia force* (due to acceleration) is considered.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### Step 1: Identify Forces on the Piston

The piston of a reciprocating engine is subjected to:
1. **Fluid Pressure Force** ($F_{FL}$ or $F_{FC}$) — along the cylinder axis.
2. **Inertia Force** ($F_I$) — alternating along the cylinder axis due to acceleration.
3. **Side thrust** from the connecting rod — balanced by the cylinder walls (not relevant to F_P).
4. **Friction force** — usually small, neglected unless specified.

### Step 2: Compute the Inertia Force

The instantaneous acceleration of the piston in a slider-crank mechanism (offset crank neglected) is:

$$a = r\omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right)$$

where:
- $r$ = crank radius
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $\theta$ = crank angle from inner dead centre (IDC)
- $n = L/r$ = ratio of connecting rod length to crank radius

Therefore the inertia force on reciprocating parts is:

$$F_I = m_R \cdot a = m_R \, r\omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right)$$

### Step 3: Apply the Sign Convention

| Direction of Piston Motion | Inertia Force Direction | Net Piston Effort |
|----------------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| Towards Crank (IDC) | Opposes motion (backward) | $F_P = F_{FL} - F_I$ |
| Away from Crank (ODC) | Opposes motion (forward) | $F_P = F_{FL} + F_I$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Take motion of piston away from the crank as **positive**. Then $F_I$ acts backward (negative) when piston accelerates forward, reducing the net effort.

### Step 4: Construct the Piston Effort Diagram

1. Plot **fluid pressure (or net pressure)** along the **Y-axis**.
2. Plot **crank angle $\theta$** along the **X-axis** (0° to 360°).
3. Mark pressures at:
   - **Cut-off (or release)** — steam engines
   - **Admission, compression, expansion, exhaust** — IC engines
4. Subtract the inertia pressure ordinates (above/at/below) to obtain the **net pressure diagram = Piston Effort Diagram**.

### Step 5: The Piston Effort Diagram vs. Crank Effort Diagram

| Piston Effort Diagram | Crank Effort Diagram |
|----------------------|---------------------|
| Plot of $F_P$ vs $\theta$ | Plot of $F_T$ vs $\theta$ |
| Area represents work done on piston per radian | Area represents turning work on crank per radian |
| — | Obtained by multiplying $F_P$ ordinate by $\dfrac{\sin(\theta+\phi)}{\cos\phi}$ |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Piston area | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$ | mm² |
| 2 | Net force on piston | $F_P = p_{\text{net}} \cdot A_p$ | N |
| 3 | Piston acceleration | $a = r\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | m/s² |
| 4 | Inertia force | $F_I = m_R \cdot r\omega^2\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | N |
| 5 | Inertia pressure | $p_I = \dfrac{F_I}{A_p}$ | N/m² |
| 6 | Net pressure | $p_{\text{net}} = p_{\text{fluid}} \pm p_I$ | N/m² |
| 7 | Crank angle $\phi$ in ICR | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{L + r\cos\theta}$ | rad |
| 8 | Crank effort (exact) | $F_T = F_P \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$ | N |
| 9 | Crank effort (approx.) | $F_T = F_P \!\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | N |
| 10 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{\text{Tangential force}}{\text{Piston effort}} = \dfrac{F_T}{F_P}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Mean piston effort | $F_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{\text{Area of } P\text{-effort diagram}}{\text{Length of base}}$ | N |
| 12 | Work done per cycle | $W = \int_0^{2\pi} F_T \cdot r \, d\theta$ | J |

> [!NOTE]
> The **approximate formula (9)** is used extensively in KTU 14-mark problems. The exact formula (8) is reserved for situations where high accuracy or oblique connecting rod analysis is demanded.

---

## 2.3 Why and How This Concept Works in Engineering

The piston effort, when multiplied by the crank radius, gives the **instantaneous torque on the crankshaft**. By plotting torque vs. crank angle, the engineer can:

- Determine the **size of the flywheel** required to limit speed fluctuation.
- Compute **fluctuation of energy** ($\Delta E$).
- Calculate the **coefficient of fluctuation of speed** ($C_S$).
- Diagnose **vibration and torsional irregularities** in multi-cylinder engines.

In real practice, **dynamometers** measure the crank-effort diagram directly; but design requires predicting it analytically using the piston effort approach.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Piston Acceleration

Consider a slider-crank mechanism with:
- Crank radius $r$
- Connecting rod length $L$
- Crank angle $\theta$ measured from inner dead centre (IDC)

The displacement of the piston from the crank centre is:

$$x = r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta}$$

Using the **Taylor series expansion** (since $r \ll L$):

$$\sqrt{L^2 - r^2\sin^2\theta} = L\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{L^2}} \approx L\left(1 - \dfrac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{2L^2}\right)$$

Substituting back:

$$x = r\cos\theta + L - \dfrac{r^2\sin^2\theta}{2L}$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$x = L + r\cos\theta - \dfrac{r^2}{2L}\cdot\dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2} = L + r\cos\theta - \dfrac{r^2}{4L} + \dfrac{r^2\cos 2\theta}{4L}$$

Since the constant terms $L - \dfrac{r^2}{4L}$ do not affect velocity or acceleration, we drop them and take the effective displacement from IDC as:

$$x_{\text{eff}} = r\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r\cos 2\theta}{4L}\right) = r\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{2n}\right)$$

where $n = L/r$ is the **ratio of connecting rod length to crank radius**.

Differentiate **twice** with respect to time (using $\dot\theta = \omega =$ constant):

$$v = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = r\omega\left(-\sin\theta - \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

$$a = \dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

The negative sign simply indicates that the acceleration vector points **toward the crank centre** (inward). Therefore the **magnitude** of piston acceleration is:

$$\boxed{a = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Inertia Force

By Newton's Second Law, the inertia force on the reciprocating parts is:

$$F_I = m_R \cdot a$$

Substituting the expression for $a$:

$$\boxed{F_I = m_R \, r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)}$$

The corresponding **inertia pressure** (used for diagrams) is:

$$p_I = \dfrac{F_I}{A_p} = \dfrac{m_R \, r\omega^2}{A_p}\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Crank Effort from Piston Effort

### Case A: Exact Relation (Using Oblique Connecting Rod Geometry)

From the **Iberal Triangle** (Inertia Crank Reciprocating Linkage):

$$\tan\phi = \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{L + r\cos\theta}$$

The piston effort $F_P$ acts along the line of stroke. The component of this force **tangential to the crank circle** is the **crank effort** $F_T$:

$$F_T = F_P \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$$

**Proof Sketch:**

Resolving the connecting rod force:
- Force in connecting rod = $F_P / \cos\phi$
- The tangential component on the crank pin = $F_P / \cos\phi \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)$

$$\therefore F_T = \dfrac{F_P \, \sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$$

### Case B: Approximate Relation (Used in Most KTU Problems)

Expand $\sin(\theta + \phi) = \sin\theta\cos\phi + \cos\theta\sin\phi$ and $\tan\phi = \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{L + r\cos\theta}$:

For small $\phi$ (which occurs when $L \gg r$), $\cos\phi \approx 1$, $\sin\phi \approx \phi \approx \tan\phi$:

$$F_T \approx F_P \left[ \sin\theta + \cos\theta\cdot\dfrac{r\sin\theta}{L + r\cos\theta} \right]$$

Using $\dfrac{r}{L} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ and $\dfrac{r\cos\theta}{L} \ll 1$:

$$F_T \approx F_P \left[ \sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin\theta\cos\theta}{n} \right]$$

Applying $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$:

$$\boxed{F_T \approx F_P \left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common textbook variant writes $\dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}$ instead of $\dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}$. Both forms appear in different texts. KTU board answers typically use $F_T = F_P\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right)$ — **always use the convention given in your course text**.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A steam engine has crank radius $r = 200\,\text{mm}$, connecting rod length $L = 600\,\text{mm}$, and reciprocating mass $m_R = 80\,\text{kg}$. The engine runs at $N = 300\,\text{rpm}$. If the net fluid force on the piston is $F_{FL} = 25\,\text{kN}$ at $\theta = 60°$ from IDC (outward stroke), determine:
1. Piston acceleration
2. Inertia force
3. Net piston effort
4. Approximate crank effort

### Solution

**Given:**
- $r = 0.2\,\text{m}$, $L = 0.6\,\text{m}$ ⟹ $n = L/r = 3$
- $m_R = 80\,\text{kg}$
- $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi(300)}{60} = 10\pi \approx 31.416\,\text{rad/s}$
- $\theta = 60°$, $F_{FL} = 25{,}000\,\text{N}$ (outward stroke)

**Step 1: Piston acceleration**

$$a = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

$$\cos 60° = 0.5,\quad \cos 120° = -0.5,\quad \omega^2 = (31.416)^2 = 986.96\,\text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$a = 0.2 \times 986.96 \times \left(0.5 + \dfrac{-0.5}{3}\right)$$

$$a = 197.39 \times (0.5 - 0.1667)$$

$$a = 197.39 \times 0.3333 = 65.80\,\text{m/s}^2 \quad (\text{towards crank})$$

**Step 2: Inertia force**

$$F_I = m_R \cdot a = 80 \times 65.80 = 5264\,\text{N} \approx 5.26\,\text{kN}$$

(opposing motion, so it acts inward when piston moves outward)

**Step 3: Net piston effort** (outward stroke, hence subtract inertia):

$$F_P = F_{FL} - F_I = 25{,}000 - 5264 = 19{,}736\,\text{N} \approx 19.74\,\text{kN}$$

**Step 4: Approximate crank effort**

$$F_T = F_P\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

$$\sin 60° = 0.8660,\quad \sin 120° = 0.8660$$

$$F_T = 19{,}736 \times \left(0.8660 + \dfrac{0.8660}{3}\right)$$

$$F_T = 19{,}736 \times (0.8660 + 0.2887)$$

$$F_T = 19{,}736 \times 1.1547 = 22{,}789\,\text{N} \approx 22.79\,\text{kN}$$

**Interpretation:** The crank effort is greater than the piston effort at $\theta = 60°$ because of the kinematic amplification by the slider-crank linkage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Piston Effort Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Crank Angle theta] --> B[Compute Piston Acceleration a]
    B --> B1[Use a = r*omega^2*(cos theta + cos 2 theta / n)]
    B1 --> C[Compute Inertia Force F_I = m_R * a]
    C --> D[Read Fluid Pressure p_L from Indicator Diagram]
    D --> E[Compute Fluid Force F_FL = p_L * A_p]
    E --> F{Stroke Direction?}
    F -->|Outward| G[F_P = F_FL - F_I]
    F -->|Inward| H[F_P = F_FL + F_I]
    G --> I[Net Piston Effort F_P]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute Obliquity Angle phi]
    J --> J1[Use tan phi = r*sin theta / L + r*cos theta]
    J1 --> K[Compute Crank Effort F_T]
    K --> K1[Use F_T = F_P * sin theta + phi / cos phi]
    K1 --> L[Plot Piston Effort Diagram F_P vs theta]
    K1 --> M[Plot Crank Effort Diagram F_T vs theta]
    L --> N[Compute Mean Piston Effort]
    M --> O[Compute Turning Moment T = F_T * r]
    N --> P[END: Use for Flywheel Sizing]
    O --> P
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Resolution Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cylinder Axis / Line of Stroke] --> B[F_P = Net Piston Effort]
    B --> C[Connecting Rod Force F_CR]
    C --> C1[F_CR = F_P / cos phi]
    C1 --> D[Crank Web Direction]
    D --> E[Tangential Component F_T]
    D --> F[Radial Component F_R]
    E --> G[Produces Torque T = F_T * r]
    F --> H[Balanced by Crank Pin Bearing]
    G --> I[Output Turning Moment]
    H --> I
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Piston Effort Diagram Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Piston Effort Diagram Zones] --> B[Expansion Stroke: F_P Positive]
    A --> C[Compression Stroke: F_P Negative]
    A --> D[Zero Effort Line]
    B --> B1[Steam Engines: after cut-off]
    B --> B2[IC Engines: after ignition]
    C --> C1[Steam Engines: during compression]
    C --> C2[IC Engines: during compression stroke]
    D --> D1[Represents change in sign of F_P]
    D1 --> E[Above Line: Driving]
    D1 --> F[Below Line: Resisting]
    E --> G[Positive Work Done]
    F --> H[Negative Work Done]
```

---

## 4.4 Engineering Schematic (Block Architecture) — Piston Effort to Flywheel Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Inputs: r, L, N, m_R, p_L theta] --> P2[Step 1: Compute F_FL theta]
    P2 --> P3[Step 2: Compute F_I theta]
    P3 --> P4[Step 3: Compute F_P theta = F_FL - F_I]
    P4 --> P5[Step 4: Compute F_T theta = F_P * sin theta + sin 2 theta / n]
    P5 --> P6[Step 5: Compute T theta = F_T * r]
    P6 --> P7[Step 6: Compute Total Work W per cycle]
    P7 --> P8[Step 7: Compute Mean Torque T_mean = W / 4 pi]
    P8 --> P9[Step 8: Find Max and Min Energy Fluctuations]
    P9 --> P10[Step 9: Compute Moment of Inertia of Flywheel]
    P10 --> P11[Step 10: Output: Flywheel Weight and Diameter]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Piston Effort" as applied to a reciprocating engine. How does it differ from the fluid force acting on the piston?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Piston effort is defined as the **net force actually acting on the piston** along the line of stroke at any instant, which is the algebraic difference between the fluid (steam/gas) pressure force on the piston face and the inertia force of the reciprocating parts.

It differs from the fluid force because the piston effort also **accounts for the inertia reaction** of reciprocating masses. During the outward stroke, the inertia force opposes the fluid force, reducing the net useful effort. Conversely, during the inward stroke, both forces act in the same direction, augmenting the effort.

*(Mark Split: Definition = 2 marks, Difference = 1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the significance of the obliquity angle ($\phi$) of the connecting rod in crank-effort computation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The obliquity angle $\phi$ is the small angle that the connecting rod makes with the line of stroke due to the geometry of the slider-crank mechanism.

$$\tan\phi = \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{L + r\cos\theta}$$

It accounts for the fact that the piston effort $F_P$ does **not** directly act tangentially on the crank pin. The component of $F_P$ acting tangentially (the crank effort $F_T$) is given by:

$$F_T = F_P \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$$

If $\phi$ is neglected, the computed crank effort would be erroneous, particularly near the dead centres, leading to incorrect flywheel sizing and crank-shaft design.

*(Mark Split: Definition & formula = 1 mark, Significance in $F_T$ = 2 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided within each module.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** A horizontal reciprocating engine has a cylinder bore of $200\,\text{mm}$ and a stroke of $400\,\text{mm}$. The crank and connecting rod lengths are $200\,\text{mm}$ and $600\,\text{mm}$ respectively. The mass of the reciprocating parts is $60\,\text{kg}$. When the crank has turned through an angle of $45°$ from the inner dead centre during the outward stroke, the net fluid force on the piston is $F_{FL} = 30\,\text{kN}$. If the engine runs at $300\,\text{rpm}$, calculate:
1. The acceleration of the piston.
2. The inertia force.
3. The net piston effort.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 200\,\text{mm} \Rightarrow A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2 = 0.03142\,\text{m}^2$
- Stroke = $400\,\text{mm} \Rightarrow r = 200\,\text{mm} = 0.2\,\text{m}$
- $L = 600\,\text{mm} = 0.6\,\text{m} \Rightarrow n = L/r = 3$
- $m_R = 60\,\text{kg}$
- $\theta = 45°$, $F_{FL} = 30\,\text{kN}$
- $N = 300\,\text{rpm} \Rightarrow \omega = \dfrac{2\pi(300)}{60} = 10\pi = 31.416\,\text{rad/s}$

**Step 1: Piston Acceleration**

$$\cos 45° = 0.7071,\quad \cos 90° = 0$$

$$a = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right) = 0.2 \times 986.96 \times \left(0.7071 + \dfrac{0}{3}\right)$$

$$a = 0.2 \times 986.96 \times 0.7071 = 139.56\,\text{m/s}^2 \quad (\text{towards crank centre})$$

**Step 2: Inertia Force**

$$F_I = m_R \cdot a = 60 \times 139.56 = 8374\,\text{N} \approx 8.37\,\text{kN}$$

(opposes outward motion)

**Step 3: Net Piston Effort**

$$F_P = F_{FL} - F_I = 30{,}000 - 8374 = 21{,}626\,\text{N} \approx 21.63\,\text{kN}$$

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Using the approximate formula, compute the crank effort at the same crank position and comment on the result.

**Solution:**

$$F_T = F_P\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

$$\sin 45° = 0.7071,\quad \sin 90° = 1$$

$$F_T = 21{,}626 \times \left(0.7071 + \dfrac{1}{3}\right)$$

$$F_T = 21{,}626 \times (0.7071 + 0.3333) = 21{,}626 \times 1.0404$$

$$F_T = 22{,}500\,\text{N} = 22.5\,\text{kN}$$

**Comment:** The crank effort (22.5 kN) is **greater** than the piston effort (21.63 kN). This is because at $\theta = 45°$, the connecting rod amplifies the force via the geometry of the linkage. This amplification varies with $\theta$ and equals unity only at the dead centres.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Define and distinguish between the **Piston Effort Diagram** and the **Crank Effort Diagram**. State the relationship between the two diagrams.

**Solution:**

| Aspect | Piston Effort Diagram | Crank Effort Diagram |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Plotted between | $F_P$ (Y-axis) vs $\theta$ (X-axis) | $F_T$ (Y-axis) vs $\theta$ (X-axis) |
| Represents | Net force on piston along stroke | Tangential force at crank pin |
| Nature | Easier to construct from indicator diagram | Obtained by transformation of P-effort diagram |
| Work relation | Area × crank radius ≠ work directly | Area × crank radius = work done on crank |

**Relationship:**

$$F_T = F_P \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi} \approx F_P\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right)$$

The crank effort diagram ordinates are obtained by multiplying each piston-effort ordinate by the factor $\dfrac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$, which varies from **zero at the dead centres** to a maximum near $\theta \approx 75°$–$90°$.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A single-cylinder, double-acting steam engine has the following data:
- Cylinder diameter = $250\,\text{mm}$, Stroke = $400\,\text{mm}$
- $L/r = 4$, $N = 250\,\text{rpm}$, $m_R = 100\,\text{kg}$
- Mean effective pressure = $0.5\,\text{N/mm}^2$

Estimate the mean torque on the crankshaft.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.25)^2 = 0.04909\,\text{m}^2$
- $p_m = 0.5\,\text{N/mm}^2 = 0.5 \times 10^6\,\text{N/m}^2 = 500\,\text{kPa}$
- $r = 0.2\,\text{m}$
- $L/r = 4 \Rightarrow L = 0.8\,\text{m}$

**Step 1: Work Done per Cycle**

For a double-acting engine, work is done on both sides:

$$W = p_m \cdot A_p \cdot L_s \cdot 2$$

where $L_s = 2r$ is the stroke length.

$$W = (0.5 \times 10^6) \times 0.04909 \times 0.4 \times 2$$

$$W = 500{,}000 \times 0.04909 \times 0.8 = 19{,}635\,\text{J}$$

**Step 2: Mean Torque**

For two revolutions per cycle in a double-acting steam engine:

$$T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot 2\pi} = \dfrac{19{,}635}{4\pi} = 1562\,\text{N·m}$$

**Verification using $T_{\text{mean}} = F_{T,\text{mean}} \cdot r$:**

Mean tangential force ≈ Mean piston effort (since both diagrams have equal areas):

$$F_{\text{mean}} = p_m \cdot A_p = 0.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.04909 = 24{,}544\,\text{N}$$

$$T_{\text{mean}} = F_{\text{mean}} \cdot r \cdot 2 = 24{,}544 \times 0.2 \times 2 = 9818\,\text{N·m}$$

The discrepancy arises because of the factor $\dfrac{\sin(\theta+\phi)}{\cos\phi}$. Using the approximate correction factor over the full cycle (≈ 1.0 / π for a perfect sinusoidal), the refined mean torque becomes $\approx 9818 \cdot (2/\pi) \cdot 2 \approx 12{,}500$ to $1562$ N·m range depending on assumption. *(In KTU-board standard practice, $T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{W}{4\pi}$ for double-acting engines is the accepted answer.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Confusion (–2 to –3 marks):** Students frequently forget that inertia force **opposes motion**. On the outward stroke, you SUBTRACT $F_I$; on the inward stroke, you ADD $F_I$. Mixing this up invalidates the entire P-effort diagram.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $\sin 2\theta$ term (–2 marks):** The correct approximation is $F_T = F_P\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right)$, **not** $\dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}$ in some texts. Always check which form your KTU module prescribes.
> 3. **Unit Conversion Errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Pressure must be in N/m² and area in m² to get N. Failing to convert bar → Pa or mm² → m² is the #1 cause of numerical mismatch.
> 4. **Neglecting crank obliquity (–2 marks):** When $L/r$ is small (< 4), the approximate formula is inaccurate. Always state the assumption when using it.
> 5. **Wrong angle reference (–2 marks):** Always measure $\theta$ from **IDC (Inner Dead Centre)** unless the question explicitly states otherwise. Mistakes of using ODC origin will produce inverted diagrams.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this checklist the night before your KTU Dynamics of Machinery exam.

- ✅ **Piston Effort ($F_P$)** = (Fluid pressure force) $\mp$ (Inertia force), depending on stroke direction.
- ✅ Fluid pressure force = $p_{\text{net}} \cdot A_p$.
- ✅ Inertia force $F_I = m_R \, r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$.
- ✅ Always measure $\theta$ from **Inner Dead Centre (IDC)**.
- ✅ $n = L/r$ — the **ratio of connecting rod length to crank radius** (typically 3 to 4 in engines).
- ✅ The P-effort diagram has a **zero line**; above the line is driving, below is resisting.
- ✅ Crank effort $F_T$ is obtained by multiplying $F_P$ by the obliquity factor $\dfrac{\sin(\theta+\phi)}{\cos\phi}$.
- ✅ Approximate: $F_T = F_P\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right)$ — used in 90% of KTU problems.
- ✅ Crank effort = $0$ at $\theta = 0°$ and $\theta = 180°$ (dead centres); this is **why flywheels are essential**.
- ✅ Work done per cycle = Area under crank-effort diagram × crank radius.
- ✅ Mean piston effort $F_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{\text{Area of P-effort diagram}}{\text{Base length (4 radian)}}$.
- ✅ Mean torque $T_{\text{mean}} = F_{T,\text{mean}} \cdot r$ for single-acting; $T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{W}{4\pi}$ for double-acting.
- ✅ P-effort diagram is constructed from the **indicator diagram** (for IC engines) or the **steam pressure diagram** (for steam engines), then corrected for inertia.
- ✅ The crank-effort diagram leads directly to the **Turning Moment Diagram**, the foundation for flywheel design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force Acting Along the Connecting Rod
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Force Acting Along the Connecting Rod

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

In a **slider-crank mechanism**, the **force acting along the connecting rod** is the **axial thrust** transmitted through the rod from the piston (slider) to the crank-pin (or vice-versa). It is the only force component that produces useful work; the perpendicular component merely wastes energy as side thrust on the cylinder walls.

Let:
- **F** = Force in the connecting rod (along the rod axis)
- **P_net** = Net piston effort (along the line of stroke)
- **φ** = Angle of inclination of connecting rod with the line of stroke
- **N** = Side thrust on piston (perpendicular to stroke)

The fundamental force-balance at the **piston pin** gives the defining relation:

$$F \cos\phi \;=\; P_{net}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The force in the connecting rod is *always greater* than the net piston effort because the rod is inclined (except at the dead centres where φ = 0 and F = P).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pushing a lawn-mower up a slanted handle. The force you apply *along the handle* (F) is larger than the *effective forward push* on the wheels (P_net). The extra component pushes sideways on your palms (side thrust N) and does no useful work.

Similarly, in an IC engine:
- The piston pushes the rod at a slant during most of the stroke.
- Only the axial projection **F cos φ** advances the piston; the rest **F sin φ** jams against the cylinder wall.

**Plain-English Summary:**

> "The connecting rod is a *slanted strut*. Pushing the piston straight along the bore requires a bigger push along the rod because part of that push is wasted sideways."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Engine) |
|---|---|---|
| Crank radius | r | **50 mm – 300 mm** |
| Connecting rod length | l | **150 mm – 900 mm** |
| Ratio n = l/r | n | **3 – 4** (typical) |
| Angle of crank from IDC | θ | **0° – 180°** |
| Angle of inclination | φ | **0° – sin⁻¹(1/n)** |
| Engine speed | N | **1000 – 6000 rpm** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Foundation — Why φ Exists

Because the connecting rod is *rigid and finite in length* (l), it cannot remain parallel to the line of stroke as the crank rotates. It swings through a small angle **φ**, maximum when the crank is at **90° from both dead centres**.

The geometric constraint that couples **θ** (crank angle) and **φ** (rod angle) is:

$$\boxed{\;\sin\phi \;=\; \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sin\theta\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this relation?** The vertical height of the crank-pin above the crank centre must equal the vertical height of the piston-pin above the crank centre. This is a pure trigonometry condition from triangle OAB where O = crank centre, A = crank pin, B = piston pin.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Reasoning |
|---|---|
| 1. Piston force P_net acts **along the stroke** (horizontal). | Gas pressure, inertia and friction are all collinear with piston motion. |
| 2. Connecting rod force F acts **along the rod** (slanted by φ). | A two-force rigid member can only carry force along its axis. |
| 3. At the piston pin, **equilibrium** must hold. | Newton's 3rd law: piston pushes rod with P_net, rod pushes piston with components of F. |
| 4. Resolve F into axial (cos φ) and lateral (sin φ) parts. | Standard 2-D resolution. |
| 5. Equate the axial component to P_net. | No net horizontal acceleration of the pin (in quasi-static analysis). |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table verbatim — these are the 6 equations tested every semester.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{l}\sin\theta = \dfrac{1}{n}\sin\theta$ | Angularity relation | — |
| 2 | $\cos\phi = \sqrt{1-\dfrac{1}{n^{2}}\sin^{2}\theta}$ | Cosine of rod angle | — |
| 3 | $F \;=\; \dfrac{P_{net}}{\cos\phi}$ | **Force in connecting rod** | N |
| 4 | $N \;=\; P_{net}\tan\phi \;=\; F\sin\phi$ | Side thrust on piston | N |
| 5 | $F \;\approx\; P_{net}\!\left[1+\dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{l}\right)^{\!2}\!\sin^{2}\theta\right]$ | Binomial expansion (l >> r) | N |
| 6 | $P_{net} \;=\; (P_{g}-P_{b})\cdot A \;-\; m\cdot a_{B} \;-\; F_{fric}$ | Net piston effort | N |

Where **a_B** = acceleration of the reciprocating parts, **A** = piston cross-sectional area.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of F |
|---|---|
| **IC Engine Design** | Sizing the connecting rod's cross-section for buckling & tensile load. |
| **Crank-shaft Bearings** | Side thrust N dictates cylinder-liner wear rate. |
| **Pumps & Compressors** | Rod force decides big-end bearing life. |
| **Robotics / Linkages** | Whitworth quick-return and crank-shaper mechanisms use the same force resolution. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation A — Force in the Connecting Rod

### Statement
**Prove that the force in the connecting rod of a slider-crank mechanism is equal to the net piston effort divided by the cosine of the angle of inclination.**

### Given / Sketch

A slider-crank mechanism with:
- Crank OA of length **r** at angle **θ** from the line of stroke.
- Connecting rod AB of length **l** at angle **φ** from the line of stroke.
- Piston B moves along the horizontal line passing through the crank centre O.
- Net piston effort = **P_net** acting horizontally on B (towards O).
- Force in rod = **F** acting along AB.

```
      F (along rod)
       ↘
        A ●
         \  φ
          \_______
        θ   l
   O ●—————●————→ Piston motion
             B  ← P_net
```

### Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the free body at the piston pin B.**
At B, two forces act:
- The piston effort **P_net** along the line of stroke.
- The reaction **F** from the connecting rod, acting along BA.

**Step 2 — Resolve F into horizontal and vertical components.**
The rod makes angle φ with the horizontal (line of stroke). Resolving:

$$F_{x} \;=\; F\cos\phi \quad \text{(horizontal, along stroke)}$$
$$F_{y} \;=\; F\sin\phi \quad \text{(vertical, perpendicular to stroke)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply horizontal equilibrium at pin B.**
The piston has no acceleration perpendicular to the cylinder walls (constrained), and for force balance along the stroke:

$$\sum F_{x} \;=\; 0$$

$$F\cos\phi \;-\; P_{net} \;=\; 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for F.**

$$\boxed{\;F \;=\; \frac{P_{net}}{\cos\phi}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**
- When φ → 0 (crank at dead centres), **cos φ → 1**, so **F = P_net**.
- When φ → φ_max (crank at 90°), **cos φ < 1**, so **F > P_net**.
- Therefore, the connecting rod force **peaks somewhere between the dead centres**, never exactly at them.

### Step 6 — Approximation using Binomial Expansion (for small φ, i.e., large n).

For l >> r, the term (r/l)·sin θ is small. Let:

$$\cos\phi \;=\; \sqrt{1-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\sin^{2}\theta}$$

Using $(1-x)^{-1/2} \approx 1 + \tfrac{x}{2} + \tfrac{3x^{2}}{8} + \dots$ for small x:

$$\frac{1}{\cos\phi} \;\approx\; 1 \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sin\theta\right)^{\!2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;F \;\approx\; P_{net}\!\left[\,1 + \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{r}{l}\right)^{\!2}\sin^{2}\theta\right]\;}$$

This is the **approximate expression** used in many KTU numericals when n ≥ 4.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Side Thrust N on the Piston

From vertical equilibrium at pin B (the piston is constrained against vertical motion by the cylinder wall, which supplies reaction **N**):

$$\sum F_{y} \;=\; 0$$

$$N \;-\; F\sin\phi \;=\; 0$$

Substituting F from the previous result:

$$N \;=\; F\sin\phi \;=\; \frac{P_{net}}{\cos\phi}\cdot\sin\phi \;=\; P_{net}\tan\phi$$

$$\boxed{\;N \;=\; P_{net}\,\tan\phi \;=\; P_{net}\cdot\frac{\sin\theta}{\sqrt{n^{2}-\sin^{2}\theta}}\;}$$

**Engineering insight:** The side thrust is **zero** at both dead centres (φ = 0) and **maximum** near 90° crank angle. This is why cylinder liners wear unevenly near mid-stroke.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When is F Maximum?

We want θ such that **dF/dθ = 0**. Using F = P_net / cos φ with sin φ = (1/n) sin θ:

$$\cos^{2}\phi \;=\; 1 - \frac{\sin^{2}\theta}{n^{2}}$$

Differentiating with respect to θ:

$$\frac{d}{d\theta}(\cos^{2}\phi) \;=\; -\frac{2\sin\theta\cos\theta}{n^{2}}$$

Setting derivative of F (equivalently 1/cos φ) to zero yields the condition:

$$\boxed{\;\sin\theta\cos\theta\!\left(\frac{1}{\cos^{2}\phi}\right) \;=\; 0\;}$$

Solving gives sin 2θ = 0 → θ = 0° or 180°, but combined with the trigonometric identity it yields **max F near θ = 90°** (with slight shift due to the (1/n)² term). At θ = 90°:

$$F_{max} \;=\; \frac{P_{net}}{\sqrt{1-1/n^{2}}} \;=\; \frac{P_{net}\cdot n}{\sqrt{n^{2}-1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For n = 4: F_max ≈ 1.032 P_net — only ~3 % higher than P_net. This is why designers keep n ≥ 4.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagram & Schematic

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow — Force Resolution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Crank OA rotates at angular velocity omega"] --> B["Crank pin A traces circle of radius r"]
    B --> C["Connecting rod AB constrained length l"]
    C --> D["Geometric constraint: sin phi equals r over l times sin theta"]
    D --> E["Piston B constrained to move along stroke"]
    E --> F["Gas pressure acts on piston crown"]
    F --> G["Inertia force m times aB acts on piston"]
    G --> H["Friction force opposes motion"]
    H --> I["Compute net piston effort Pnet"]
    I --> J["Resolve rod force F into components"]
    J --> K["Horizontal component F cos phi balances Pnet"]
    J --> L["Vertical component F sin phi equals side thrust N"]
    K --> M["Final F equals Pnet divided by cos phi"]
    L --> N["Final N equals Pnet times tan phi"]
    M --> O["Output: Connecting rod thrust and side reaction"]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Free-Body Architecture of the Piston Pin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PistonPin["Free Body at Piston Pin B"]
        P1["Pnet — horizontal toward crank centre"] -->|Compression| NodeX
        NodeX["Equilibrium node B"] -->|Equal and opposite| Fx["F cos phi"]
        NodeX -->|Side reaction from liner| Fy["F sin phi upward"]
        Fy --> N["Side thrust N on cylinder wall"]
    end
    Fx --> F1["Force F along connecting rod AB toward crank pin A"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Label Table

| Symbol | Meaning | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| O | Crank centre / Cylinder axis reference | Fixed pivot |
| A | Crank pin | Rotates on circle radius **r** |
| B | Piston pin (gudgeon) | Translates along stroke |
| θ | Crank angle from IDC | Measured at O |
| φ | Rod inclination from stroke | Measured at B |
| F | Axial rod force | Along AB |
| P_net | Net piston effort | Along line of stroke |
| N | Side thrust | Perpendicular to stroke |

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram is intentionally abstracted to a **functional block flow** because the precise geometric drawing of a 3-D slider-crank with inclined force vectors cannot be faithfully rendered in Mermaid. KTU examiners accept such block-level schematics provided they are correctly labelled.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 marks conceptual + 3 marks short)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** *(3 Marks)*

**"Define the term 'force along the connecting rod' in a slider-crank mechanism. Why is it always greater than or equal to the piston effort?"**

**Model Answer:**

The force along the connecting rod **F** is the axial compressive (or tensile) load transmitted through the connecting rod from the piston pin to the crank pin. It acts along the axis of the rod.

From force balance at the piston pin:

$$F\cos\phi \;=\; P_{net} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; F \;=\; \frac{P_{net}}{\cos\phi}$$

Since the inclination angle φ ranges from **0° to φ_max** (where 0 < cos φ ≤ 1), the denominator cos φ is **always ≤ 1**. Hence:

$$F \;\geq\; P_{net}$$

Equality holds only at the dead centres (φ = 0), where the rod aligns perfectly with the stroke.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *(3 Marks)*

**"What is meant by side thrust on the piston? How is it related to the connecting rod force and the piston effort?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Side thrust** is the lateral reaction force exerted by the connecting rod on the cylinder walls (transmitted through the piston). It acts **perpendicular to the line of stroke** and is the cause of cylinder-liner wear.

Resolving the rod force F at the piston pin:

- Component perpendicular to stroke = **F sin φ**

By vertical equilibrium at the piston pin:

$$N \;=\; F\sin\phi \;=\; \frac{P_{net}}{\cos\phi}\sin\phi \;=\; P_{net}\tan\phi$$

Thus, the side thrust N depends on both the rod force F and the angularity φ.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice**. Either Q(A) or Q(B) is to be attempted. Each typically has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### ✦ Question A — Derivation + Numerical

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified Past Paper]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the force in the connecting rod of a slider-crank mechanism in terms of the net piston effort and the angle of inclination of the rod with the line of stroke. State clearly the assumptions made. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** In a horizontal slider-crank mechanism, the crank radius is **200 mm** and the connecting rod length is **800 mm**. The crank rotates at **300 rpm**. When the crank has turned through **40° from IDC**, the net force on the piston is **30 kN**. Determine:
  (i) Velocity of the piston
  (ii) Acceleration of the piston
  (iii) Force along the connecting rod
  (iv) Side thrust on the cylinder walls **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-A(a) *Derivation*

*(Full derivation already given in SECTION 3.1 of these notes. Reproducing the key result:)*

**Assumption:** The connecting rod is a two-force member; the piston is in quasi-static equilibrium along the stroke.

**Free-body at piston pin B:**
- Piston effort P_net acts horizontally (along stroke).
- Rod reaction F acts along AB, inclined at angle φ.

**Resolving F:**
- Horizontal component: **F cos φ**
- Vertical component: **F sin φ**

**Horizontal equilibrium:**

$$F\cos\phi - P_{net} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; F = \frac{P_{net}}{\cos\phi}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\;F \;=\; \frac{P_{net}}{\cos\phi}\;}$$

where φ is found from the geometric relation:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r}{l}\sin\theta = \frac{1}{n}\sin\theta$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-A(b) *Numerical*

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Crank radius, r | 200 mm = **0.2 m** |
| Connecting rod length, l | 800 mm = **0.4 m** … *(correction: 0.8 m)* |
| Speed, N | 300 rpm |
| Crank angle, θ | 40° from IDC |
| Net piston effort, P_net | 30 kN = **30 000 N** |

**Step 1 — Compute the angularity ratio and φ.**

$$n \;=\; \frac{l}{r} \;=\; \frac{800}{200} \;=\; 4$$

$$\sin\phi \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sin\theta \;=\; \frac{1}{4}\sin 40^{\circ} \;=\; \frac{0.6428}{4} \;=\; 0.1607$$

$$\phi \;=\; \sin^{-1}(0.1607) \;=\; 9.24^{\circ}$$

$$\cos\phi \;=\; 0.9870$$

**Step 2 — Compute angular velocity ω.**

$$\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} \;=\; 31.416\;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — (i) Velocity of piston.**

The standard piston-velocity formula (derivable from differentiating x = r cos θ + l cos φ with the constraint):

$$v \;=\; r\omega\!\left[\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right]$$

Substituting:

$$v \;=\; 0.2 \times 31.416 \times \left[\sin 40^{\circ} + \frac{\sin 80^{\circ}}{8}\right]$$

$$v \;=\; 6.283 \times \left[0.6428 + \frac{0.9848}{8}\right]$$

$$v \;=\; 6.283 \times \left[0.6428 + 0.1231\right]$$

$$v \;=\; 6.283 \times 0.7659$$

$$\boxed{\;v \;\approx\; 4.81\;\text{m/s}\;}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Acceleration of piston.**

$$a_{B} \;=\; r\omega^{2}\!\left[\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right]$$

Substituting (note: 2θ = 80°, cos 80° = 0.1736):

$$a_{B} \;=\; 0.2 \times (31.416)^{2} \times \left[\cos 40^{\circ} + \frac{\cos 80^{\circ}}{4}\right]$$

$$a_{B} \;=\; 0.2 \times 986.96 \times \left[0.7660 + \frac{0.1736}{4}\right]$$

$$a_{B} \;=\; 197.39 \times \left[0.7660 + 0.0434\right]$$

$$a_{B} \;=\; 197.39 \times 0.8094$$

$$\boxed{\;a_{B} \;\approx\; 159.76\;\text{m/s}^{2}\;}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Force in the connecting rod.**

$$F \;=\; \frac{P_{net}}{\cos\phi} \;=\; \frac{30\,000}{0.9870}$$

$$\boxed{\;F \;\approx\; 30.395\;\text{kN}\;}$$

**Step 6 — (iv) Side thrust on cylinder walls.**

$$N \;=\; P_{net}\,\tan\phi \;=\; 30\,000 \times \tan 9.24^{\circ}$$

$$N \;=\; 30\,000 \times 0.1627$$

$$\boxed{\;N \;\approx\; 4.88\;\text{kN}\;}$$

---

### ✦ Question B — Sketch + Numerical (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the force analysis at the piston pin of a slider-crank mechanism. Show all components of force along and perpendicular to the connecting rod, and derive the relationship between the connecting rod force, piston effort and side thrust. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A petrol engine has a crank radius of **150 mm**, connecting rod length of **450 mm**, and cylinder bore of **100 mm**. The crank rotates at **1800 rpm**. At the instant when the crank is **30° from TDC** during the expansion stroke, the gas pressure is **0.6 MPa**. The mass of reciprocating parts is **5 kg**. Find:
  (i) Net force on the piston (gas force − inertia force)
  (ii) Force along the connecting rod
  (iii) Side thrust on the piston walls

**\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-B(a) *Sketch + Explanation*

*(Full derivation given in SECTION 3.1 and SECTION 3.2. Key sketch and relations:)*

```
        F
         ↘
          A ●——— rod direction
           \  φ
            \
   O ●———————● B  ←  Piston (slider)
                  ↑
                  N (side thrust on liner)
   ←—————— P_net ————
```

**At piston pin B, three forces meet:**
1. **P_net** along the line of stroke (towards crank centre).
2. **F** along the connecting rod (resistance).
3. **N** perpendicular to the stroke (side reaction from liner).

**Resolution of F:**
- Horizontal (along stroke): F cos φ = P_net
- Vertical (perpendicular): F sin φ = N

**Results:**

$$F \;=\; \frac{P_{net}}{\cos\phi}, \qquad N \;=\; P_{net}\tan\phi$$

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q-B(b) *Numerical*

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| r | 150 mm = **0.15 m** |
| l | 450 mm = **0.45 m** |
| D | 100 mm → A = π/4·(100)² = **7854 mm² = 7.854 × 10⁻³ m²** |
| N (speed) | 1800 rpm |
| θ | 30° from TDC on expansion stroke |
| P_g | 0.6 MPa = **0.6 N/mm² = 6 × 10⁵ N/m²** |
| m | 5 kg |

**Step 1 — Geometry.**

$$n \;=\; \frac{l}{r} \;=\; \frac{450}{150} \;=\; 3$$

$$\sin\phi \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sin\theta \;=\; \frac{1}{3}\sin 30^{\circ} \;=\; \frac{0.5}{3} \;=\; 0.1667$$

$$\phi \;=\; \sin^{-1}(0.1667) \;=\; 9.594^{\circ}, \qquad \cos\phi \;=\; 0.9860$$

**Step 2 — Angular velocity.**

$$\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1800}{60} \;=\; 188.496\;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration of piston (with θ measured from TDC).**

When θ is measured from TDC, the standard formula becomes (with sign convention):

$$a_{B} \;=\; r\omega^{2}\!\left[\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right]$$

At θ = 30°: cos θ = 0.866, cos 2θ = cos 60° = 0.5

$$a_{B} \;=\; 0.15 \times (188.496)^{2} \times \left[0.866 + \frac{0.5}{3}\right]$$

$$a_{B} \;=\; 0.15 \times 35530.6 \times \left[0.866 + 0.1667\right]$$

$$a_{B} \;=\; 5329.6 \times 1.0327 \;=\; 5503.6\;\text{m/s}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Inertia force.**

$$F_{i} \;=\; m\,a_{B} \;=\; 5 \times 5503.6 \;=\; 27\,518\;\text{N} \;\approx\; 27.52\;\text{kN}$$

**Step 5 — Gas force on piston.**

$$F_{g} \;=\; P_{g}\cdot A \;=\; 0.6 \times 7854 \;=\; 4712.4\;\text{N} \;\approx\; 4.712\;\text{kN}$$

**Step 6 — Net force on piston (during expansion, gas and inertia oppose each other when piston is decelerating toward TDC).**

$$P_{net} \;=\; F_{g} - F_{i} \;=\; 4.712 - 27.52$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{net} \;\approx\; -22.81\;\text{kN}\;}$$

(Negative sign indicates the net force acts **toward TDC**, i.e., the piston is being pulled back; magnitude 22.81 kN.)

**Step 7 — Force along the connecting rod (using magnitude).**

$$F \;=\; \frac{\vert P_{net}\vert}{\cos\phi} \;=\; \frac{22\,810}{0.9860}$$

$$\boxed{\;F \;\approx\; 23.13\;\text{kN}\;}$$

**Step 8 — Side thrust.**

$$N \;=\; \vert P_{net}\vert\,\tan\phi \;=\; 22\,810 \times \tan 9.594^{\circ}$$

$$N \;=\; 22\,810 \times 0.1692$$

$$\boxed{\;N \;\approx\; 3.86\;\text{kN}\;}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — common mistakes flagged by KTU evaluators:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the angle φ exists.** Many write F = P_net blindly, ignoring rod angularity. KTU deducts up to **3 marks** for this.
> 2. **Mixing θ and φ.** θ is the **crank angle**, φ is the **rod angle**. Always use θ to compute φ first via sin φ = (r/l) sin θ.
> 3. **Sign convention for net piston effort.** During expansion stroke near TDC, gas pushes piston outward while inertia force points inward. Subtract them correctly; do not add them blindly.
> 4. **Forgetting n = l/r.** When asked for *approximate* force, use F ≈ P_net[1 + ½(r/l)² sin²θ]. Examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Units inconsistency.** Always convert crank radius to **metres** before computing acceleration in m/s². Mixing mm and m is a top mark-loser.
> 6. **Side thrust direction.** Draw it explicitly on the diagram. A missing arrow costs a full mark in part (a).

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] The connecting rod is a **two-force member**, so its force is purely axial along AB.
- [x] At the piston pin, resolve the rod force **F** into:
  - **F cos φ** along the stroke (balances P_net)
  - **F sin φ** perpendicular to stroke (becomes side thrust N).
- [x] **Core formula:** $\;F = \dfrac{P_{net}}{\cos\phi}\;$ — never forget this.
- [x] **Side thrust formula:** $\;N = P_{net}\tan\phi = F\sin\phi\;$.
- [x] **Angularity relation:** $\;\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{l}\sin\theta = \dfrac{1}{n}\sin\theta\;$.
- [x] **Approximation (large n):** $\;F \approx P_{net}\!\left[1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\tfrac{r}{l}\right)^{2}\!\sin^{2}\theta\right]\;$.
- [x] F is **minimum at dead centres** (φ = 0) and **maximum near mid-stroke** (θ ≈ 90°).
- [x] Maximum side thrust occurs at θ where dN/dθ = 0 — approximately **θ = sin⁻¹(√(n²/(n² + 3)))**, but KTU rarely tests this.
- [x] Piston effort = (P_g − P_b)A − m·a_B − F_fric — net force available along the stroke.
- [x] Practical design rule: keep **n ≥ 4** to limit F to within ~3 % of P_net.
- [x] Drawing a clear **FBD of the piston pin** with φ labelled is mandatory for full marks in derivation questions.

**Final one-liner:** *"Push slanted, deliver straight — the rod's lean costs you a little extra push but a lot of side-wear."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thrust on the Sides of Cylinder Walls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Thrust on the Sides of Cylinder Walls ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In a single-cylinder reciprocating engine, the **thrust on the sides of the cylinder walls** (commonly called **side thrust** or **lateral thrust**) is the *net horizontal force* (perpendicular to the cylinder axis) exerted by the piston-skirt assembly against the inner cylindrical liner of the engine block. It arises because the connecting rod is *never perfectly aligned* with the cylinder axis except at the dead centres; at every other crank position, the gas pressure and inertia forces resolve into two components: 1. An **axial component** along the cylinder axis (transmitted to the crank-pin). 2. A **lateral (side) component** perpendicular to the cylinder axis — this is the thrust that bears against the cylinder wall. Mathematically, the side thrust at any crank angle **θ** is given by: $$ F_{N} \;=\; (F_{L} \pm F_{I})\,\tan\phi $$ where $F_L = p \cdot A$ is the load due to gas pressure, $F_I$ is the inertia force of the reciprocating parts, and **φ** is the obliquity angle of the connecting rod with respect to the cylinder axis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Emphasis (2024 Scheme):** Examiners expect students to distinguish clearly between *thrust due to gas pressure*, *thrust due to inertia*, and the *combined net thrust*. Confusing the direction of the inertia force (± sign) is the single largest source of marks lost on this topic. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are pushing a long stick (the connecting rod) against a wall (the piston) that can slide only up and down. If you push straight down along the wall, the wall feels no sideways force. But the moment you tilt your stick — say 20° off-vertical — part of your push now drags sideways against the wall. The wall has to push back laterally to keep the piston from moving sideways. In a reciprocating engine: - **You** = combustion gas pressure + inertia of the piston - **The stick** = connecting rod, tilting back and forth as the crank rotates - **The wall** = cylinder liner The lateral push the wall must absorb is the **side thrust**.
