It is greatest when the crank is around **90° past TDC** (where φ is maximum) and almost zero at top-dead-centre (TDC) and bottom-dead-centre (BDC). > [!NOTE] > **Why does it matter in engineering?** Excessive side thrust causes **cylinder-liner wear**, **piston-skirt scuffing**, **oil-film breakdown**, **noise (engine "knock"**) and **loss of mechanical efficiency**. Every engine designer must predict and minimise it. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in **bold**) - **n = l/r** — ratio of connecting-rod length to crank radius (typically **3.0 – 4.5** for passenger-car engines, **≈ 3.5** for medium-speed engines). - **φ (phi)** — obliquity angle of the connecting rod, generally **< 15°**. - **tan φ** — practically approximated by **sin φ** = (r/l)·sin θ when n > 4 (error < 1 %). - Maximum side thrust occurs around **θ = 90°** (mid-stroke for the working stroke). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Origin of Side Thrust — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Combustion gases act on the piston crown** with pressure *p* (N/m²). Total axial force = *p·A*. 2. **The connecting rod is inclined** at angle *φ* to the cylinder axis because the crank is away from a dead-centre. 3. **Force resolution at the gudgeon (wrist) pin**: - Force along the connecting rod (towards crank pin) → $F_Q = (F_L \pm F_I)/\cos\phi$ - Force perpendicular to cylinder axis → $F_N = (F_L \pm F_I)\tan\phi$ 4. **The piston cannot move sideways** (constrained by the cylinder wall). Hence the wall supplies an equal and opposite normal reaction. 5. **Inertia force of reciprocating parts** ($F_I$) is superimposed algebraically: - **Negative (downward)** during the **working (expansion) stroke** for $0° < θ < 90°$ and $270° < θ < 360°$ → **adds** to gas force. - **Positive (upward)** during **compression, suction, exhaust strokes** → **opposes** the (often near-zero) gas force. 6. The **net side thrust** is the instantaneous sum of these two contributions. ### 2.2 Why Does tan φ Govern the Side Thrust? From the geometry of the slider-crank mechanism: $$ \sin\phi \;=\; \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{l} \;=\; \dfrac{\sin\theta}{n} $$ For typical n > 3, φ is small (tan φ ≈ sin φ ≈ φ in radians), so: $$ \tan\phi \;\approx\; \dfrac{r}{l}\sin\theta \;=\; \dfrac{\sin\theta}{n} $$ This tells the student that **side thrust vanishes at θ = 0° and 180°** (dead centres) and is **maximum at θ = 90° and 270°** — purely a function of crank geometry. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Symbol | Governing Equation | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|--------|---------------------|-------|---------| | 1 | Connecting-rod length ratio | n | $n = \dfrac{l}{r}$ | — | Typically 3 – 4.5 | | 2 | Obliquity angle | φ | $\sin\phi = \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{l}$ | rad | 0 at TDC/BDC | | 3 | Tangent of obliquity | tan φ | $\tan\phi \approx \dfrac{\sin\theta}{n}$ | — | Small-angle approx. valid for n > 3 | | 4 | Gas force on piston | $F_L$ | $F_L = p \cdot A = p \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | N | p = indicated gas pressure at θ | | 5 | Inertia force of reciprocating parts | $F_I$ | $F_I = m\,\omega^{2}\,r\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)$ | N | Acts along cylinder axis | | 6 | Force along connecting rod | $F_Q$ | $F_Q = \dfrac{F_L \pm F_I}{\cos\phi}$ | N | Transmitted to crank pin | | 7 | **Side thrust (gas pressure component)** | $F_{N,L}$ | $F_{N,L} = F_L \cdot \tan\phi$ | N | Always pushes wall | | 8 | **Side thrust (inertia component)** | $F_{N,I}$ | $F_{N,I} = F_I \cdot \tan\phi$ | N | Direction flips sign of F_I | | 9 | **Net side thrust** | $F_N$ | $F_N = (F_L \pm F_I)\,\tan\phi$ | N | + when $F_I$ opposes $F_L$ | | 10 | Crank-pin effort (vertical) | $F_T$ | $F_T = (F_L \pm F_I)\!\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right)$ | N | Used for turning-moment diagrams | | 11 | Side thrust — approximate max | $F_{N,\max}$ | $\approx \dfrac{p_{max}\,A}{n}$ | N | At θ ≈ 90° for working stroke | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** In Formula (9), the *sign* of $F_I$ in the bracket is determined by the *direction* of the inertia force relative to the *direction* of the gas force. If both point the same way, use **(+ F_I)** in the bracket: $F_N = (F_L + F_I)\tan\phi$. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Engine NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Side-thrust variation is the root cause of engine "rumble" at low RPM. - **Piston-skin design:** Modern pistons use an *oval barrel* shape and *anti-skirt* profiles to counter asymmetric side-thrust loading. - **Cylinder-liner material selection:** Cast-iron liners with honed cross-hatch patterns retain oil film under cyclic side-thrust pulses. - **Connecting-rod alignment:** Offsets in the big-end bearing exaggerate side thrust → designers keep the rod as long as packaging allows (high n). - **F1 and racing engines** use *very short* rods (n ≈ 2) deliberately — they accept high side thrust in exchange for more uniform torque. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Geometric Relation Between φ and θ **Given:** Slider-crank mechanism with crank radius *r*, connecting rod length *l*, crank angle *θ* measured from TDC. **Step 1.** Drop a perpendicular from the crank-pin to the cylinder axis. From the triangle formed: $$ \sin\phi \;=\; \dfrac{\text{perpendicular offset of crank pin}}{\text{connecting-rod length}} \;=\; \dfrac{r\sin\theta}{l} $$ **Step 2.** Divide numerator and denominator by *l*: $$ \sin\phi \;=\; \dfrac{(r/l)\sin\theta}{1} \;=\; \dfrac{\sin\theta}{n} $$ **Step 3.** Hence the obliquity angle as a function of crank angle: $$ \boxed{\;\phi(\theta) \;=\; \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sin\theta}{n}\right)\;} $$ **Step 4.** Differentiate to find where φ is maximum (useful for locating peak side thrust): $$ \cos\phi\,\dfrac{d\phi}{d\theta} \;=\; \dfrac{\cos\theta}{n} $$ Setting $\frac{d\phi}{d\theta} = 0$ requires $\cos\theta = 0$, i.e. **θ = 90° or 270°**. This is the location of the *geometric maximum* of φ — independent of the load. However, the *true maximum of side thrust* depends on the combined variation of (load × tan φ) and may shift slightly. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force Resolution at the Gudgeon Pin **Free-body diagram of the piston:** Three forces act on it. - Gas pressure $F_L = pA$ (downwards along cylinder axis). - Inertia force $F_I$ (up or down depending on crank position). - Reaction from connecting rod $F_Q$ (along the rod, towards the crank pin). - Normal reaction from cylinder wall $F_N$ (perpendicular to cylinder axis). **Equilibrium along cylinder axis (vertical, *x*):** $$ F_Q \cos\phi \;=\; F_L \pm F_I $$ **Equilibrium perpendicular to cylinder axis (lateral, *y*):** $$ F_Q \sin\phi \;=\; F_N $$ **Step 1.** From the *x*-equation: $$ F_Q \;=\; \dfrac{F_L \pm F_I}{\cos\phi} $$ **Step 2.** Substitute into the *y*-equation: $$ F_N \;=\; F_Q\sin\phi \;=\; \dfrac{F_L \pm F_I}{\cos\phi}\,\sin\phi $$ **Step 3.** Recognise that $\dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos\phi} = \tan\phi$: $$ \boxed{\;F_N \;=\; (F_L \pm F_I)\,\tan\phi\;} $$ **Step 4.** Substitution of $\tan\phi \approx \dfrac{\sin\theta}{n}$ yields the engineering approximation: $$ F_N \;\approx\; \dfrac{(F_L \pm F_I)\,\sin\theta}{n} $$ This is the form used for **tabulating side thrust at successive crank angles** during board numerical problems. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inertia Force of the Reciprocating Parts Let *m* be the mass of all parts reciprocating with the piston (piston, gudgeon pin, portion of connecting rod, etc.). **Step 1.** Displacement of the piston from TDC: $$ x \;=\; r\!\left[(1-\cos\theta) + \dfrac{\sin^{2}\theta}{2n}\right] $$ **Step 2.** Velocity (differentiate w.r.t. time, using $\dot\theta = \omega$): $$ v \;=\; r\omega\!\left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right] $$ **Step 3.** Acceleration (differentiate again): $$ a \;=\; r\omega^{2}\!\left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right] $$ **Step 4.** By Newton's second law, inertia force (taken positive *upward* by convention): $$ \boxed{\;F_I \;=\; m\,r\omega^{2}\!\left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right]\;} $$ > When this force is **negative** (acts downward, aiding gas pressure), the side thrust increases; when **positive** (upward, opposing gas pressure), the side thrust decreases. --- ### 3.4 Derivative — Locating the True Maximum of Net Side Thrust For board problems the textbook approximation is acceptable: peak occurs at **θ ≈ 90°** for the working stroke. For a rigorous derivation, set $\dfrac{dF_N}{d\theta}=0$ where: $$ F_N(\theta) \;=\; \left[p(\theta)\,A \;+\; m\,r\omega^{2}\!\left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right)\right]\,\dfrac{\sin\theta}{n} $$ Differentiating and equating to zero gives a transcendental equation. Solving numerically with typical values (p ≈ 25 bar, n = 3.5, λ = 0.28) yields θ_max ≈ **85° – 95°** — justifying the textbook shortcut of taking θ = 90°. --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Pattern (KTU Board Standard) **Problem Pattern:** *"A single-cylinder, single-acting engine has the following data: D = 150 mm, L = 200 mm, r = 75 mm, n = 3.5, m = 12 kg, N = 1500 rpm. Compute the side thrust at θ = 30°, 60°, and 90° during the expansion stroke. Take gas pressure at these angles as 25 bar, 18 bar and 12 bar respectively."* **Solution Framework (template for the student):** $$ A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi (0.15)^{2}}{4} \;=\; 1.767 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^{2} $$ $$ \omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi(1500)}{60} \;=\; 157.08\,\text{rad/s} $$ $$ F_I(\theta) \;=\; (12)(0.075)(157.08)^{2}\!\left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{3.5}\right] $$ Pre-compute the bracket at each θ, multiply by $F_L = pA$, then compute $F_N = (F_L - F_I)\tan\phi$ with $\tan\phi = \sin\theta/n$. | θ | p (bar) | F_L = pA (N) | Bracket term | F_I (N) | sin θ / n = tan φ | Net F_N (N) | |---|---------|--------------|--------------|---------|-------------------|-------------| | 30° | 25 | 44 178 | 0.866 + 0.220 = 1.086 | 24 072 | 0.143 | **2 870** | | 60° | 18 | 31 808 | 0.500 − 0.102 = 0.398 | 8 822 | 0.245 | **5 636** | | 90° | 12 | 21 206 | 0 − 0.286 = −0.286 | −6 339 | 0.286 | **7 884** | > Note: At θ = 90° the inertia force is *negative* (downward), adding to the gas force. Hence net thrust is largest — exactly as predicted. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Direction & Sign Summary | Crank Position | Stroke | Direction of Gas Force | Direction of F_I | Sign in $F_N = (F_L \pm F_I)\tan\phi$ | |----------------|--------|----------------------|------------------|----------------------------------------| | 0° → 90° | Expansion | ↓ | ↑ (decelerating piston moving down) | $F_L - F_I$ → smaller side thrust | | 90° → 180° | Expansion | ↓ | ↓ (accelerating piston towards BDC) | $F_L + F_I$ → larger side thrust | | 180° → 270° | Expansion | ↓ | ↑ (decelerating piston at BDC) | $F_L - F_I$ | | 270° → 360° | Expansion | ↓ | ↓ (accelerating piston up) | $F_L + F_I$ | | All other strokes | Suction/Compression/Exhaust | ≈ 0 | ↑ or ↓ | $F_N \approx F_I \tan\phi$ (small) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Side-Thrust Generation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Combustion Chamber
High-Pressure Gas] -->|p, N per square metre| B[Piston Crown] B -->|Axial force pA| C[Gudgeon Pin Joint] C -->|Resolve force| D{Connecting Rod Inclined at phi} D -->|Along rod F sub Q| E[Crank Pin and Bearing] D -->|Perpendicular to axis| F[Normal Reaction from Cylinder Wall] G[Reciprocating Mass m] -->|Inertia force F sub I| C H[Crank Angle theta Sensor] -->|Provides sin theta| I[Geometric Module] I -->|Computes tan phi| F C --> J[Net Side Thrust F sub N equals bracket F sub L plus or minus F sub I bracket times tan phi] J --> K[Cylinder Liner Wear] J --> L[Piston Skirt Friction Loss] J --> M[NVH Engine Rumble] ``` ### 4.2 Free-Body Force Resolution Diagram (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PistonFBD [Free Body of Piston] V1[Gas Force F sub L = pA
Downward] --> P1[Piston Crown] P1 --> P2[Gudgeon Pin] V2[Inertia Force F sub I
Up or Down] --> P2 P2 --> P3[Connecting Rod Force F sub Q
Along Rod at angle phi] P2 --> P4[Wall Reaction F sub N
Perpendicular to Cylinder Axis] end P3 -->|tangential component| TQ[Crank Pin Tangential Force F sub T] P3 -->|radial component| RQ[Crank Pin Radial Force F sub R] ``` ### 4.3 Variation of Side Thrust with Crank Angle (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Start at theta equals 0 degrees TDC] --> S1[Compute sin phi equals r sin theta over l] S1 --> S2[Read gas pressure p from indicator diagram at theta] S2 --> S3[Compute F sub L equals p times A] S3 --> S4[Compute F sub I equals m omega squared r times bracket] S4 --> S5[Choose sign based on stroke phase] S5 --> S6[Compute F sub N equals F sub L plus or minus F sub I times tan phi] S6 --> S7[Tabulate value] S7 --> S8{theta equals 360 degrees?} S8 -->|No| S9[Increment theta by 30 or 45 degrees] S9 --> S1 S8 -->|Yes| S10[Plot F sub N versus theta] S10 --> S11[Identify Maximum Side Thrust Angle] ``` ### 4.4 Indicative Polar Plot — Where Side Thrust Peaks ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[Crank Position theta equals 0 degrees
Side Thrust approximately 0] --> B0[theta equals 45 degrees
Side Thrust rising] B0 --> C0[theta equals 90 degrees
Side Thrust PEAK for working stroke] C0 --> D0[theta equals 135 degrees
Side Thrust decreasing] D0 --> E0[theta equals 180 degrees BDC
Side Thrust approximately 0] E0 --> F0[theta equals 270 degrees
Side Thrust secondary peak during return] F0 --> A360[theta equals 360 degrees TDC
Cycle Repeats] ``` > [!NOTE] > The true polar diagram (indicator-diagram-derived) is non-sinusoidal because gas pressure *p(θ)* varies sharply during combustion. Mermaid cannot draw axes cleanly, so the block schematic above conveys the same information in a KTU-exam-friendly manner. Students are advised to sketch a Cartesian plot on graph paper during the exam. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *side thrust* in a reciprocating engine. Why does it vanish at the dead centres? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Side thrust is the force exerted by the piston on the cylinder walls in a direction perpendicular to the cylinder axis. It arises because the connecting rod is inclined at an angle φ to the cylinder axis during the stroke. Side thrust = $(F_L \pm F_I)\tan\phi$. At TDC and BDC, the crank and connecting rod are collinear with the cylinder axis (φ = 0°, tan φ = 0), hence side thrust = 0. **(3 marks)** — Award 1 mark each for definition, expression, and explanation at dead centres. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the significance of the *n = l/r* ratio in determining side thrust? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The ratio *n* governs the obliquity angle φ through $\sin\phi = (\sin\theta)/n$. A larger *n* (longer connecting rod) gives smaller φ for the same θ, hence lower side thrust. Conversely, a smaller *n* (shorter rod) magnifies side thrust. Passenger engines use n ≈ 3.5 to keep side thrust within acceptable cylinder-wear limits. **(3 marks)** — 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for inverse relationship, 1 mark for typical value. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part-B sub-question carries 7 marks. Internal choice means the student answers EITHER (a) OR (b) of the same block. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]* **(a)** Derive an expression for the **side thrust on the cylinder walls** of a single-cylinder reciprocating engine, clearly indicating the contribution of (i) gas pressure and (ii) inertia of reciprocating parts. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. Draw the free-body diagram of the piston (1 mark). 2. Resolve gas force $F_L = pA$ and inertia force $F_I$ along and perpendicular to the connecting rod (2 marks). 3. Equate forces along rod: $F_Q\cos\phi = F_L \pm F_I$ → $F_Q = (F_L \pm F_I)/\cos\phi$. (1 mark) 4. Side thrust (perpendicular to cylinder axis): $F_N = F_Q\sin\phi = (F_L \pm F_I)\tan\phi$. (1 mark) 5. Substitute $\tan\phi = (\sin\theta)/n$ to obtain $F_N = (F_L \pm F_I)(\sin\theta)/n$. (1 mark) 6. State conditions where the **+** or **−** sign applies (working stroke vs. idle stroke). (1 mark) --- **(b)** A single-cylinder, double-acting steam engine has the following data: - Cylinder bore, D = 250 mm; Stroke length, L = 400 mm - Crank radius, r = 200 mm; n = l/r = 4 - Mass of reciprocating parts, m = 60 kg - Speed, N = 240 rpm - Steam pressure during the stroke: p = 1.4 MPa (assumed constant) Compute the **side thrust** at crank angles θ = 30°, 60°, 90° and 120°. Also identify the crank angle of **maximum side thrust**. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Geometry (1 mark):** $$ A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi(0.25)^{2}}{4} \;=\; 4.909 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Angular speed (0.5 mark):** $$ \omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi(240)}{60} \;=\; 25.13\,\text{rad/s} $$ **Step 3 — Gas force (0.5 mark):** $$ F_L \;=\; pA \;=\; (1.4 \times 10^{6})(4.909 \times 10^{-2}) \;=\; 68\,726\,\text{N} $$ **Step 4 — Inertia force at each θ (1 mark):** $$ F_I(\theta) \;=\; (60)(0.20)(25.13)^{2}\!\left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{4}\right] $$ $$ \text{Pre-factor} \;=\; 60 \times 0.20 \times 631.6 \;=\; 7\,579\,\text{N} $$ | θ | cos θ | cos 2θ | cos θ + cos 2θ/4 | F_I (N) | |---|-------|--------|--------------------|---------| | 30° | 0.866 | 0.500 | 0.866 + 0.125 = 0.991 | 7 510 | | 60° | 0.500 | −0.500 | 0.500 − 0.125 = 0.375 | 2 842 | | 90° | 0.000 | −1.000 | 0 − 0.250 = −0.250 | −1 895 | | 120° | −0.500 | −0.500 | −0.500 − 0.125 = −0.625 | −4 737 | **Step 5 — Side thrust (2 marks):** Using $F_N = (F_L + F_I)\tan\phi$ with $\tan\phi = \sin\theta / n = \sin\theta/4$: | θ | F_L + F_I (N) | sin θ / 4 | F_N (N) | |---|----------------|-----------|---------| | 30° | 76 236 | 0.125 | **9 530** | | 60° | 71 568 | 0.217 | **15 530** | | 90° | 66 831 | 0.250 | **16 708** ← max | | 120° | 63 989 | 0.217 | **13 886** | **Step 6 — Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Maximum side thrust ≈ **16 708 N at θ = 90°**, as anticipated theoretically. > *(Mark distribution: 1 + 0.5 + 0.5 + 1 + 2 + 0.5 = 7.5 rounded to 7 marks as per KTU valuation; examiners usually give 7 to well-presented solutions.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]* **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **two sources of side thrust** on the cylinder walls of a reciprocating engine. How does the angle of inclination of the connecting rod affect the side thrust? **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. **Sketch** of slider-crank showing gas pressure, inertia force, connecting rod, and wall reaction. (2 marks) 2. **Source 1 — Gas pressure:** $F_L = pA$ acts axially; its perpendicular component $F_L\tan\phi$ presses the piston against the wall. (2 marks) 3. **Source 2 — Inertia force:** $F_I = m\omega^{2}r(\cos\theta + \cos 2\theta/n)$ also resolves into perpendicular component $F_I\tan\phi$. (2 marks) 4. **Effect of inclination angle φ:** Side thrust ∝ tan φ. With $\sin\phi = (\sin\theta)/n$, increasing *n* (longer rod) decreases φ for any given θ, reducing side thrust. Designers choose n ≥ 3 to keep φ < 15° and side thrust within allowable limits. (1 mark) --- **(b)** A single-cylinder four-stroke petrol engine running at 2000 rpm has the following specifications: D = 100 mm, L = 120 mm, m = 1.2 kg, gas pressures (assumed uniform during expansion) = 20 bar. Compute the **net thrust on the cylinder walls** at θ = 45°. Take n = 3.2. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Areas and angular velocity (1 mark):** $$ A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi(0.1)^{2}}{4} \;=\; 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{2} $$ $$ \omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi(2000)}{60} \;=\; 209.44\,\text{rad/s} $$ **Step 2 — Gas force (1 mark):** $$ F_L \;=\; pA \;=\; (20 \times 10^{5})(7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \;=\; 15\,708\,\text{N} $$ **Step 3 — Inertia force at θ = 45° (2 marks):** $$ F_I \;=\; (1.2)(0.06)(209.44)^{2}\!\left[\cos 45° + \dfrac{\cos 90°}{3.2}\right] $$ $$ F_I \;=\; (1.2)(0.06)(43\,865)\,(0.7071 + 0) \;=\; 2\,232\,\text{N} \quad (\text{upward}) $$ **Step 4 — Side thrust geometry (1 mark):** $$ \tan\phi \;=\; \dfrac{\sin 45°}{3.2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.7071}{3.2} \;=\; 0.2210 $$ **Step 5 — Net side thrust (2 marks):** Since $F_I$ is upward (opposing $F_L$), it is **subtracted** in the bracket: $$ F_N \;=\; (F_L - F_I)\tan\phi \;=\; (15\,708 - 2\,232)(0.2210) $$ $$ F_N \;=\; (13\,476)(0.2210) \;=\; \boxed{2\,978\,\text{N}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign of F_I** — Forgetting that $F_I$ acts *upward* during 0°–90° of the working stroke and including it *additively* in the bracket. This single error can invert your answer. > 2. **Using φ directly in degrees** in $\tan\phi$ without converting — minor but fatal if the calculator is in radian mode. > 3. **Forgetting the factor n in $\sin\theta/n$** — using $\sin\theta$ alone inflates the side thrust by a factor of *n* (≈ 3× error). > 4. **Ignoring the inertia component** — some texts quote only the gas-pressure side thrust. KTU examiners explicitly test the *combined* thrust. > 5. **Units inconsistency** — pressure must be in N/m² (Pa), not bar. Multiply bar by $10^{5}$ to convert. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Side thrust** = lateral force on cylinder walls perpendicular to cylinder axis. - ✅ Governing equation: **F_N = (F_L ± F_I) tan φ**. - ✅ Geometric link: **sin φ = (r sin θ)/l = (sin θ)/n**. - ✅ **tan φ ≈ sin θ / n** for typical engines with n > 3. - ✅ Two sources: **gas-pressure thrust** + **inertia-force thrust**. - ✅ Side thrust is **zero at dead centres** (TDC & BDC) and **maximum near 90°** during the working stroke. - ✅ The **± sign** of F_I in the bracket depends on the *relative direction* of inertia and gas forces — not on θ alone. - ✅ Maximum side thrust (approximate): **F_N,max ≈ p_max · A / n** at θ ≈ 90°. - ✅ Longer connecting rod (higher n) → smaller side thrust → better liner life. - ✅ For four-stroke engines, the side thrust during **non-working strokes** is dominated by inertia alone, since gas pressure ≈ 0. - ✅ Always draw a **free-body diagram** of the piston before writing equations — examiners reward a clean diagram with **at least 1 mark**. - ✅ Final numerical answer must carry **correct SI units (N)** and **2–3 significant figures**, matching the precision of the input data.

topics.title: Crank Pin Effort and Thrust on the Crankshaft Bearings - Numerical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Crank Pin Effort and Thrust on the Crankshaft Bearings - Numerical Examples # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition In the kinematic analysis of a slider-crank mechanism (the heart of every internal combustion engine, reciprocating compressor, and steam engine), the **inertia force generated by the reciprocating piston** must be transmitted through the connecting rod to the crank pin and finally to the crankshaft. This transmission produces three critical dynamic quantities that engineers must evaluate for the design of crankshafts, bearings, and bed plates: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Piston Effort (F_P)** — The net axial force acting on the piston (along the line of stroke) due to the inertia of the reciprocating parts. It is the unbalanced dynamic load that drives the crank. > > **Crank Pin Effort (F_Q)** — The total force transmitted along the connecting rod from the piston to the crank pin. Geometrically, it is the resultant of the piston effort resolved along the connecting rod axis. > > **Thrust on the Crankshaft Bearing (F_R)** — The radial component of the crank pin effort, which acts perpendicular to the crank and pushes outward on the main journal/bearing of the crankshaft. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Pedal System > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a bicycle on a stationary trainer.** Your foot pushes the pedal *horizontally* (this is the **piston effort**), but the pedal itself is attached to a crank arm that is tilted. The force you transmit *along the pedal-arm* (the connecting rod equivalent) is the **crank pin effort**. Now, the pedal-arm pushes sideways on the main spindle (crank bearing) — this sideways push is the **thrust on the crankshaft bearing**. Because the pedal is tilted, only the perpendicular component of your push actually loads the spindle sideways; the rest creates rotational torque. The same physics applies inside a real engine: the piston "pushes" horizontally, the connecting rod tilts by an angle φ, and the crankshaft bearing experiences only the *radial component* of the rod force. ## 1.3 Geometric Setup - **r** = Crank radius (m) - **l** = Length of connecting rod (m) - **n = l/r** = Ratio of connecting rod length to crank radius (dimensionless, typically **4 to 5** for IC engines) - **θ** = Angle of the crank measured from the **Inner Dead Centre (IDC)** - **φ** = Angle of obliquity of the connecting rod (tilting from the cylinder axis) - **m_r** = Mass of reciprocating parts (kg) - **m_c** = Mass of rotating parts concentrated at the crank pin (kg) - **N** = Crank speed in rpm; **ω = 2πN/60** rad/s ![Figure 1 — Slider-Crank Mechanism showing θ, φ, and force triangle] --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown ### Step 1 — Acceleration of the Reciprocating Parts The exact acceleration of the piston (when θ is measured from IDC) is: $$a = \omega^{2} \, r \left( \cos \theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right)$$ - The first term $\omega^{2} r \cos \theta$ comes from the **primary inertia** (assumed SHM of the crank-pin projection). - The second term $\omega^{2} r \cos 2\theta \, / \, n$ is a **correction** added because the piston is connected through a finite-length rod (not infinitely long). ### Step 2 — Net Inertia Force on the Piston (Piston Effort) By Newton's second law, the piston effort is the product of reciprocating mass and acceleration: $$F_{P} = m_{r} \cdot a = m_{r}\, \omega^{2}\, r \left( \cos \theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right)$$ This force acts **horizontally along the line of stroke** toward the crank when the piston is decelerating (acceleration directed toward crank), and away from the crank when accelerating. ### Step 3 — Angle of Obliquity of the Connecting Rod From the geometry of the slider-crank linkage, when the crank has rotated by θ from IDC: $$\sin \varphi = \dfrac{r}{l} \sin \theta = \dfrac{\sin \theta}{n}$$ For typical engines, φ is small (≈ 10° to 15°), so many simplified formulas use $\varphi \approx \sin\varphi \approx \tan\varphi$. ### Step 4 — Force in the Connecting Rod (Crank Pin Effort) The piston pushes along the line of stroke, but the connecting rod is tilted by φ. Resolving F_P along the rod direction: $$F_{Q} = \dfrac{F_{P}}{\cos \varphi}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This force **F_Q** is what acts at the crank pin. It is the "crank-pin effort" the KTU examiner tests. ### Step 5 — Resolving F_Q at the Crank Pin At the crank pin, F_Q can be split into two perpendicular components relative to the crank arm: - **Tangential component (F_T):** Perpendicular to the crank arm → produces the **turning moment** of the crank. - **Radial component (F_R):** Along the crank arm → produces the **side thrust on the main bearing**. The angle between F_Q (rod direction) and the crank arm is **(θ + φ)**. $$\boxed{\,F_{T} = F_{Q} \sin(\theta + \varphi) = \dfrac{F_{P} \sin(\theta + \varphi)}{\cos \varphi}\,}$$ $$\boxed{\,F_{R} = F_{Q} \cos(\theta + \varphi) = \dfrac{F_{P} \cos(\theta + \varphi)}{\cos \varphi}\,}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often write F_T = F_P × tan(θ + φ) or F_T = F_P × sin θ. **Both are wrong** unless the problem explicitly specifies an approximate solution. The correct expression is **F_T = F_P × sin(θ + φ) / cos φ**. ### Step 6 — Effect of Rotating Masses (Centrifugal Force) If the question involves the centrifugal force of the rotating masses (m_c concentrated at the crank pin), this force acts **radially outward along the crank arm**: $$F_{C} = m_{c}\, \omega^{2}\, r$$ This adds directly to F_R (it is purely radial), so: $$F_{R,\text{total}} = F_{R} + F_{C}$$ The tangential component receives no contribution from pure radial centrifugal force. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Typical Units | |---|----------|--------|---------|----------------| | 1 | Angular velocity | $\omega$ | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | | 2 | Crank ratio | $n$ | $n = \dfrac{l}{r}$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Obliquity angle | $\varphi$ | $\sin \varphi = \dfrac{\sin \theta}{n}$ | degrees | | 4 | Piston acceleration | $a$ | $a = \omega^{2} r \left( \cos \theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right)$ | m/s² | | 5 | Piston effort | $F_{P}$ | $F_{P} = m_{r}\, \omega^{2}\, r \left( \cos \theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right)$ | N | | 6 | Crank pin effort | $F_{Q}$ | $F_{Q} = \dfrac{F_{P}}{\cos \varphi}$ | N | | 7 | Tangential force | $F_{T}$ | $F_{T} = \dfrac{F_{P} \sin(\theta + \varphi)}{\cos \varphi}$ | N | | 8 | Radial force / Thrust on bearing | $F_{R}$ | $F_{R} = \dfrac{F_{P} \cos(\theta + \varphi)}{\cos \varphi}$ | N | | 9 | Centrifugal force (rotating mass) | $F_{C}$ | $F_{C} = m_{c}\, \omega^{2}\, r$ | N | | 10 | Total bearing thrust | $F_{R,\text{total}}$ | $F_{R,\text{total}} = F_{R} + F_{C}$ | N | | 11 | Torque on crankshaft | $T$ | $T = F_{T} \times r$ | N·m | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why does KTU test this so heavily?** Because every crank-shaft design, every main bearing selection, and every foundation bolt sizing in a reciprocating engine depends on knowing F_T (for torque and flywheel sizing) and F_R (for radial bearing load). A miscalculation here leads to bearing failure, crankshaft fatigue, and structural vibration in industrial machinery. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples ## NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 1 — Single-Angle Evaluation (Standard 14-mark Pattern) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern | 2024 Scheme | 14 Marks]** > > The crank and connecting rod of a steam engine are 300 mm and 1.2 m long respectively. The crank rotates at 200 rpm. The mass of the reciprocating parts is 250 kg. Determine: > (a) The net force on the piston when the crank has rotated through 60° from IDC. > (b) The force in the connecting rod (crank-pin effort). > (c) The tangential and radial components of the thrust on the crankshaft bearing. > Neglect the mass of the connecting rod and rotating parts. ### Given Data (Tabulisation) | Symbol | Value | Symbol | Value | |--------|-------|--------|-------| | r | 300 mm = **0.30 m** | m_r | **250 kg** | | l | 1.2 m | θ | **60°** from IDC | | N | 200 rpm | m_c | Neglected | ### Step 1 — Compute Angular Velocity ω $$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 200}{60}$$ $$\omega = \dfrac{1256.637}{60} = 20.944 \, \text{rad/s}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Crank Ratio n $$n = \dfrac{l}{r} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.30} = 4$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Angle of Obliquity φ $$\sin \varphi = \dfrac{\sin \theta}{n} = \dfrac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{4}$$ $$\sin \varphi = \dfrac{0.8660}{4} = 0.2165$$ $$\varphi = \sin^{-1}(0.2165) = 12.50^{\circ}$$ So we record: **θ + φ = 60° + 12.50° = 72.50°** ### Step 4 — Compute Piston Acceleration a $$a = \omega^{2} \, r \left( \cos \theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right)$$ Substituting values: $$a = (20.944)^{2} \times 0.30 \times \left( \cos 60^{\circ} + \dfrac{\cos 120^{\circ}}{4} \right)$$ $$a = 438.65 \times 0.30 \times \left( 0.5000 + \dfrac{-0.5000}{4} \right)$$ $$a = 131.60 \times \left( 0.5000 - 0.1250 \right)$$ $$a = 131.60 \times 0.3750 = 49.35 \, \text{m/s}^{2}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Piston Effort F_P $$F_{P} = m_{r} \times a = 250 \times 49.35$$ $$\boxed{F_{P} = 12337.5 \, \text{N} \approx 12.34 \, \text{kN}}$$ ### Step 6 — Compute Crank Pin Effort F_Q $$F_{Q} = \dfrac{F_{P}}{\cos \varphi} = \dfrac{12337.5}{\cos 12.50^{\circ}}$$ $$F_{Q} = \dfrac{12337.5}{0.9763}$$ $$\boxed{F_{Q} = 12637.3 \, \text{N} \approx 12.64 \, \text{kN}}$$ ### Step 7 — Compute Tangential Force F_T $$F_{T} = \dfrac{F_{P} \sin(\theta + \varphi)}{\cos \varphi} = F_{Q} \times \sin(72.50^{\circ})$$ $$F_{T} = 12637.3 \times 0.9537$$ $$\boxed{F_{T} = 12053.8 \, \text{N} \approx 12.05 \, \text{kN}}$$ ### Step 8 — Compute Radial Thrust on Bearing F_R $$F_{R} = \dfrac{F_{P} \cos(\theta + \varphi)}{\cos \varphi} = F_{Q} \times \cos(72.50^{\circ})$$ $$F_{R} = 12637.3 \times 0.3007$$ $$\boxed{F_{R} = 3800.2 \, \text{N} \approx 3.80 \, \text{kN}}$$ ### Step 9 — Verification Check (Sanity) The component magnitudes must satisfy: $$F_{Q}^{2} \stackrel{?}{=} F_{T}^{2} + F_{R}^{2}$$ $$(12637.3)^{2} = (12053.8)^{2} + (3800.2)^{2}$$ $$1.597 \times 10^{8} \approx 1.453 \times 10^{8} + 1.444 \times 10^{7} = 1.597 \times 10^{8} \;\; \checkmark$$ ### Final Result Table | Quantity | Symbol | Value | |----------|--------|-------| | Piston effort | F_P | **12.34 kN** | | Crank pin effort | F_Q | **12.64 kN** | | Tangential force | F_T | **12.05 kN** | | Radial thrust on bearing | F_R | **3.80 kN** | --- ## NUMERICAL EXAMPLE 2 — Tabulation over a Full Cycle (Standard KTU Pattern with Diagram) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern | 2024 Scheme | 14 Marks]** > > A horizontal engine runs at 240 rpm. The crank length is 200 mm and connecting rod length 800 mm. The mass of the reciprocating parts is 100 kg. If the mass of the rotating parts is 140 kg, determine, for crank positions θ = 0°, 30°, 60°, 90°, 120°, 150°, 180°: > (a) Piston effort, (b) Crank pin effort, (c) Tangential and radial components of crank pin effort, (d) Thrust on crankshaft bearings (including centrifugal effect). > Plot **only the piston effort vs crank angle** graph (describe in text/table). ### Given Data | Symbol | Value | Symbol | Value | |--------|-------|--------|-------| | r | 0.20 m | m_r | 100 kg | | l | 0.80 m | m_c | 140 kg | | N | 240 rpm | n = l/r | **4** | ### Preliminary Constants $$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 240}{60} = 25.133 \, \text{rad/s}$$ $$\omega^{2} = 631.65 \, \text{rad}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$ $$\omega^{2} r = 631.65 \times 0.20 = 126.33$$ $$\dfrac{\omega^{2} r}{n} = \dfrac{126.33}{4} = 31.58$$ $$F_{C} = m_{c} \, \omega^{2} r = 140 \times 126.33 = 17686.2 \, \text{N} \;\; (\text{constant for all } \theta)$$ **Acceleration formula** (multiplied by m_r = 100 kg): $$F_{P} = 100 \times 126.33 \left( \cos \theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{4} \right)$$ $$F_{P} = 12633 \left( \cos \theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{4} \right) \;\; \text{N}$$ ### Step-by-Step Column Evaluation **At θ = 0°:** - $\cos 0° = 1.000$, $\cos 0° = 1.000$ - $F_{P} = 12633 \times (1.000 + 0.2500) = 12633 \times 1.250 = 15791 \, \text{N}$ - $\sin 0° = 0 \Rightarrow \varphi = 0°$ - $F_{Q} = F_{P} / 1 = 15791 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{T} = F_{Q} \sin 0° = 0$ - $F_{R} = F_{Q} \cos 0° = 15791 \, \text{N}$ - Total bearing thrust = 15791 + 17686 = **33477 N** **At θ = 30°:** - $\cos 30° = 0.8660$, $\cos 60° = 0.5000$ - $F_{P} = 12633 \times (0.8660 + 0.1250) = 12633 \times 0.9910 = 12520 \, \text{N}$ - $\sin 30° = 0.5000 \Rightarrow \sin \varphi = 0.5000/4 = 0.1250 \Rightarrow \varphi = 7.18°$ - $\theta + \varphi = 37.18°$ - $F_{Q} = 12520 / \cos 7.18° = 12520 / 0.9922 = 12619 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{T} = 12619 \times \sin 37.18° = 12619 \times 0.6045 = 7628 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{R} = 12619 \times \cos 37.18° = 12619 \times 0.7966 = 10051 \, \text{N}$ - Total bearing thrust = 10051 + 17686 = **27737 N** **At θ = 60°:** - $\cos 60° = 0.5000$, $\cos 120° = -0.5000$ - $F_{P} = 12633 \times (0.5000 - 0.1250) = 12633 \times 0.3750 = 4737 \, \text{N}$ - $\sin 60° = 0.8660 \Rightarrow \sin \varphi = 0.2165 \Rightarrow \varphi = 12.50°$ - $\theta + \varphi = 72.50°$ - $F_{Q} = 4737 / \cos 12.50° = 4737 / 0.9763 = 4852 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{T} = 4852 \times \sin 72.50° = 4852 \times 0.9537 = 4627 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{R} = 4852 \times \cos 72.50° = 4852 \times 0.3007 = 1459 \, \text{N}$ - Total bearing thrust = 1459 + 17686 = **19145 N** **At θ = 90°:** - $\cos 90° = 0$, $\cos 180° = -1.000$ - $F_{P} = 12633 \times (0 - 0.2500) = -3158 \, \text{N}$ (negative sign ⇒ piston is accelerating away from crank, no resistance) - $\sin 90° = 1 \Rightarrow \sin \varphi = 1/4 = 0.2500 \Rightarrow \varphi = 14.48°$ - $\theta + \varphi = 104.48°$ - $F_{Q} = 3158 / \cos 14.48° = 3158 / 0.9681 = 3262 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{T} = 3262 \times \sin 104.48° = 3262 \times 0.9686 = 3159 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{R} = 3262 \times \cos 104.48° = 3262 \times (-0.2485) = -811 \, \text{N}$ (sign indicates direction reversal) - Total bearing thrust magnitude = 811 + 17686 = **18497 N** **At θ = 120°:** - $\cos 120° = -0.5000$, $\cos 240° = -0.5000$ - $F_{P} = 12633 \times (-0.5000 - 0.1250) = 12633 \times (-0.6250) = -7896 \, \text{N}$ - $\sin 120° = 0.8660 \Rightarrow \varphi = 12.50°$ - $\theta + \varphi = 132.50°$ - $F_{Q} = 7896 / \cos 12.50° = 7896 / 0.9763 = 8088 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{T} = 8088 \times \sin 132.50° = 8088 \times 0.7341 = 5937 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{R} = 8088 \times \cos 132.50° = 8088 \times (-0.6790) = -5492 \, \text{N}$ - Total bearing thrust magnitude = 5492 + 17686 = **23178 N** **At θ = 150°:** - $\cos 150° = -0.8660$, $\cos 300° = 0.5000$ - $F_{P} = 12633 \times (-0.8660 + 0.1250) = 12633 \times (-0.7410) = -9361 \, \text{N}$ - $\sin 150° = 0.5000 \Rightarrow \varphi = 7.18°$ - $\theta + \varphi = 157.18°$ - $F_{Q} = 9361 / \cos 7.18° = 9361 / 0.9922 = 9435 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{T} = 9435 \times \sin 157.18° = 9435 \times 0.3908 = 3687 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{R} = 9435 \times \cos 157.18° = 9435 \times (-0.9205) = -8685 \, \text{N}$ - Total bearing thrust magnitude = 8685 + 17686 = **26371 N** **At θ = 180° (ODC):** - $\cos 180° = -1.000$, $\cos 360° = 1.000$ - $F_{P} = 12633 \times (-1.000 + 0.2500) = 12633 \times (-0.7500) = -9475 \, \text{N}$ - $\sin 180° = 0 \Rightarrow \varphi = 0°$ - $F_{Q} = 9475 \, \text{N}$ - $F_{T} = F_{Q} \sin 180° = 0$ - $F_{R} = F_{Q} \cos 180° = -9475 \, \text{N}$ - Total bearing thrust magnitude = 9475 + 17686 = **27161 N** ### Consolidated Result Table | θ (°) | φ (°) | F_P (N) | F_Q (N) | F_T (N) | F_R (N) | Total Thrust on Bearing (N) | |-------|-------|---------|---------|---------|---------|-----------------------------| | 0 | 0.00 | 15791 | 15791 | 0 | 15791 | **33477** | | 30 | 7.18 | 12520 | 12619 | 7628 | 10051 | **27737** | | 60 | 12.50 | 4737 | 4852 | 4627 | 1459 | **19145** | | 90 | 14.48 | -3158 | 3262 | 3159 | -811 | **18497** | | 120 | 12.50 | -7896 | 8088 | 5937 | -5492 | **23178** | | 150 | 7.18 | -9361 | 9435 | 3687 | -8685 | **26371** | | 180 | 0.00 | -9475 | 9475 | 0 | -9475 | **27161** | ### Piston Effort Variation (Graph Description) > [!NOTE] > **Plot characteristics of F_P vs θ:** > - At θ = 0° (IDC), F_P is **maximum positive** (+15791 N) — this is the position of greatest resistance to forward motion of the crank. > - F_P decreases, crosses zero, and becomes **negative** near θ ≈ 80°. > - F_P reaches its **maximum negative value** near θ = 180° (ODC), around -9475 N. > - The graph is **asymmetric** about 90° because of the $\cos 2\theta$ correction term. > - The positive area under the curve represents work done **on** the gas; the negative area represents work done **by** the flywheel. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Force-Resolution Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Reciprocating Mass m_r"] --> B["Piston Acceleration a"] B --> C["Piston Effort F_P
(Horizontal Force)"] C --> D["Connecting Rod
Resolution along Rod"] D --> E["Crank Pin Effort F_Q
= F_P / cos phi"] E --> F["Tangential Component F_T
= F_Q sin theta + phi"] E --> G["Radial Component F_R
= F_Q cos theta + phi"] G --> H["Crankshaft Main Bearing
Side Thrust"] F --> I["Torque on Crankshaft
T = F_T x r"] J["Rotating Mass m_c"] --> K["Centrifugal Force F_C
= m_c omega squared r"] K --> H L["Crank Angle theta"] --> M["Obliquity Angle phi
sin phi = sin theta / n"] M --> D M --> F M --> G ``` ## 4.2 Free-Body Concept Map of the Slider-Crank Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CYL["CYLINDER (Line of Stroke)"] P["Piston"] -->|"F_P (along stroke)"| G["Gudgeon Pin"] end subgraph ROD["CONNECTING ROD (length l)"] G -->|"F_Q along rod, tilted by phi"| CP["Crank Pin"] end subgraph CRANK["CRANK ARM (length r, angle theta)"] CP -->|"F_T tangential
creates torque T = F_T . r"| SHAFT["Crankshaft"] CP -->|"F_R radial
side thrust on bearing"| SHAFT CP -->|"F_C centrifugal from m_c"| SHAFT end ``` ## 4.3 Algorithm for Solving KTU Numerical Problems ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: Extract r, l, N, m_r, m_c, theta"] --> S2["Step 2: Compute omega and n = l/r"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Find phi from sin phi = sin theta / n"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Compute F_P
= m_r . omega squared . r . cos theta + cos 2 theta / n"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compute F_Q = F_P / cos phi"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Resolve F_Q
F_T = F_Q . sin theta + phi
F_R = F_Q . cos theta + phi"] S6 --> S7["Step 7: If m_c given, add F_C = m_c . omega squared . r to F_R"] S7 --> S8["Step 8: Tabulate results and (if asked) plot graph"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | 3 Marks]** **Define the terms: (i) Piston effort, (ii) Crank-pin effort, and (iii) Thrust on the crankshaft bearing.** **Model Answer:** - **(i) Piston effort (F_P):** The net unbalanced force acting on the piston along the line of stroke due to the inertia of the reciprocating parts. It equals $F_{P} = m_{r}\, \omega^{2}\, r \left( \cos \theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right)$. - **(ii) Crank-pin effort (F_Q):** The component of the piston effort resolved along the axis of the connecting rod. It is the actual force transmitted to the crank pin and is given by $F_{Q} = \dfrac{F_{P}}{\cos \varphi}$. - **(iii) Thrust on the crankshaft bearing (F_R):** The radial component of the crank-pin effort, which acts along the crank arm and pushes sideways on the main bearing. It equals $F_{R} = \dfrac{F_{P} \cos(\theta + \varphi)}{\cos \varphi}$, plus the centrifugal force $m_{c} \omega^{2} r$ if rotating masses are present. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]** **Why is the angle of obliquity φ important in the analysis of the slider-crank mechanism?** **Model Answer:** The angle of obliquity φ represents the inclination of the connecting rod from the cylinder axis when the crank is at any position other than the dead centres. It is important because: 1. It determines the **side thrust on the cylinder walls** (piston side-thrust), which causes wear of the cylinder liner. 2. It governs the **magnitude of the crank-pin effort** $F_{Q} = F_{P} / \cos \varphi$ — a larger φ produces a larger rod force. 3. It influences the **component split** between tangential (useful torque-producing) and radial (bearing-thrust) forces at the crank pin. --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern | 14 Marks]** A horizontal engine has a bore of 250 mm and a stroke of 400 mm. The length of the connecting rod is 1.5 m and the crank speed is 240 rpm. The mass of the reciprocating parts is 200 kg. If the crank is at 45° from IDC, determine: **(a)** The net force on the piston (piston effort). **(7 marks)** **(b)** The crank-pin effort and the magnitude of the tangential and radial components of the thrust on the main bearing. **(7 marks)** #### Given Data | Symbol | Value | |--------|-------| | Stroke | 400 mm ⇒ r = **0.20 m** | | l | **1.50 m** | | N | **240 rpm** | | m_r | **200 kg** | | θ | **45°** | #### Solution **Angular velocity:** $$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 25.133 \, \text{rad/s}$$ **Crank ratio:** $$n = \dfrac{1.50}{0.20} = 7.5$$ **Angle of obliquity:** $$\sin \varphi = \dfrac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{7.5} = \dfrac{0.7071}{7.5} = 0.0943 \Rightarrow \varphi = 5.41^{\circ}$$ **Part (a): Piston Effort** $$F_{P} = m_{r}\, \omega^{2}\, r \left( \cos 45^{\circ} + \dfrac{\cos 90^{\circ}}{7.5} \right)$$ $$F_{P} = 200 \times (25.133)^{2} \times 0.20 \times \left( 0.7071 + 0 \right)$$ $$F_{P} = 200 \times 631.65 \times 0.20 \times 0.7071$$ $$\boxed{F_{P} = 17\,863 \, \text{N} \approx 17.86 \, \text{kN}}$$ **Part (b): Crank Pin Effort and Components** $$F_{Q} = \dfrac{F_{P}}{\cos \varphi} = \dfrac{17863}{\cos 5.41^{\circ}} = \dfrac{17863}{0.9956} = 17\,941 \, \text{N}$$ $$\theta + \varphi = 45^{\circ} + 5.41^{\circ} = 50.41^{\circ}$$ $$F_{T} = F_{Q} \sin(50.41^{\circ}) = 17941 \times 0.7707 = 13\,828 \, \text{N}$$ $$\boxed{F_{T} \approx 13.83 \, \text{kN}}$$ $$F_{R} = F_{Q} \cos(50.41^{\circ}) = 17941 \times 0.6372 = 11\,431 \, \text{N}$$ $$\boxed{F_{R} \approx 11.43 \, \text{kN}}$$ **Verification:** $F_{T}^{2} + F_{R}^{2} = (13828)^{2} + (11431)^{2} = 1.912 \times 10^{8} + 1.307 \times 10^{8} = 3.219 \times 10^{8}$. And $F_{Q}^{2} = (17941)^{2} = 3.219 \times 10^{8}$. ✓ --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Pattern | 14 Marks]** A petrol engine has the following data: Crank radius r = 75 mm, connecting rod length l = 225 mm, crank speed = 3000 rpm, mass of reciprocating parts = 2.0 kg, mass of rotating parts = 2.5 kg. **(a)** Determine the crank-pin effort and the thrust on the main bearing when the crank is at 60° from IDC. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Also find the net turning moment on the crankshaft at this position. **(7 marks)** #### Given Data | Symbol | Value | |--------|-------| | r | **0.075 m** | | l | **0.225 m** | | N | **3000 rpm** | | m_r | **2.0 kg** | | m_c | **2.5 kg** | | θ | **60°** | #### Solution **Angular velocity:** $$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 314.16 \, \text{rad/s}$$ **Crank ratio:** $$n = \dfrac{0.225}{0.075} = 3$$ **Angle of obliquity:** $$\sin \varphi = \dfrac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{3} = \dfrac{0.866}{3} = 0.2887 \Rightarrow \varphi = 16.78^{\circ}$$ **Part (a): Crank Pin Effort and Thrust on Bearing** $$F_{P} = m_{r}\, \omega^{2}\, r \left( \cos 60^{\circ} + \dfrac{\cos 120^{\circ}}{3} \right)$$ $$F_{P} = 2.0 \times (314.16)^{2} \times 0.075 \times \left( 0.5 - 0.1667 \right)$$ $$F_{P} = 2.0 \times 98696 \times 0.075 \times 0.3333$$ $$F_{P} = 2.0 \times 7402.2 \times 0.3333 = 4934.8 \, \text{N}$$ **Crank-pin effort:** $$F_{Q} = \dfrac{F_{P}}{\cos \varphi} = \dfrac{4934.8}{\cos 16.78^{\circ}} = \dfrac{4934.8}{0.9574} = 5154.2 \, \text{N}$$ $$\boxed{F_{Q} \approx 5.15 \, \text{kN}}$$ **Radial thrust (plus centrifugal):** $$F_{R,\text{component}} = F_{Q} \cos(60^{\circ} + 16.78^{\circ}) = 5154.2 \times \cos(76.78^{\circ})$$ $$F_{R,\text{component}} = 5154.2 \times 0.2280 = 1175.2 \, \text{N}$$ $$F_{C} = m_{c}\, \omega^{2} r = 2.5 \times 98696 \times 0.075 = 18505.5 \, \text{N}$$ $$F_{R,\text{total}} = 1175.2 + 18505.5 = 19680.7 \, \text{N}$$ $$\boxed{F_{R,\text{total}} \approx 19.68 \, \text{kN}}$$ **Part (b): Turning Moment on Crankshaft** Tangential component: $$F_{T} = F_{Q} \sin(\theta + \varphi) = 5154.2 \times \sin(76.78^{\circ})$$ $$F_{T} = 5154.2 \times 0.9736 = 5018.5 \, \text{N}$$ Turning moment: $$T = F_{T} \times r = 5018.5 \times 0.075$$ $$\boxed{T = 376.4 \, \text{N·m}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose 2-3 Marks Silently):** > > 1. **Forgetting φ when θ is given.** Always check: is the crank angle measured from IDC or from TDC? Is φ asked but the student has only used sin θ (i.e., assumed φ ≈ 0)? This single omission loses 2 marks. > > 2. **Sign confusion.** Piston effort F_P is **positive** in the first half of the stroke (0° to ~90°) and **negative** in the second half. The crank pin effort magnitude is always positive; only F_R and F_T carry directional signs. > > 3. **Mixing up F_Q components.** A common mistake is to write $F_{T} = F_{P} \sin \theta$ instead of $F_{T} = F_{Q} \sin(\theta + \varphi) = \dfrac{F_{P} \sin(\theta + \varphi)}{\cos \varphi}$. > > 4. **Rotating masses vs reciprocating masses.** The centrifugal force $m_{c} \omega^{2} r$ acts along the crank and **adds only to F_R**, not to F_T. Many students erroneously add it to F_T. > > 5. **Units mismatch.** Always express r and l in **metres** before substituting into the formulas; otherwise, the answer comes out 1000 times too large. > > 6. **Forgetting the unit "kN" conversion.** If the problem statement says "in kN", divide your final N value by 1000 — easy 0.5 mark loss. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Piston effort** $F_{P} = m_{r} \omega^{2} r \left( \cos \theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n} \right)$ — this is the **starting point** for all subsequent calculations. - **Crank ratio** $n = l/r$. For IC engines, n ≈ 4 to 5; for slow-speed engines, n ≈ 5 to 6. - **Angle of obliquity** $\sin \varphi = \dfrac{\sin \theta}{n}$. Always compute φ before resolving F_Q. - **Crank-pin effort** $F_{Q} = \dfrac{F_{P}}{\cos \varphi}$ — the force *along the connecting rod*. - **Tangential force** $F_{T} = F_{Q} \sin(\theta + \varphi)$ — creates **torque** $T = F_{T} \times r$. - **Radial force / Bearing thrust** $F_{R} = F_{Q} \cos(\theta + \varphi)$ — pushes on the **main journal**. - **Rotating masses** add $F_{C} = m_{c} \omega^{2} r$ **only to F_R** (radially outward). - **Piston effort is maximum at IDC** (θ = 0°), reaches **zero** near θ ≈ 80°, and is **maximum negative at ODC**. - **F_T is zero at both dead centres** (θ = 0° and 180°) and maximum around θ ≈ 75° to 80°. - **Graphs** typically requested: F_P vs θ, F_T vs θ, F_R vs θ, and crank effort vs θ. The crank effort is plotted in the direction of the crank. - **Sanity check identity:** $F_{Q}^{2} = F_{T}^{2} + F_{R}^{2}$ should always hold (use as a self-correction during exam). - **S.I. units strictly:** r, l in **metres**; m in **kg**; ω in **rad/s**; final forces in **N** (or kN as required).

topics.title: Flywheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flywheel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flywheel (KTU Definition):**
> A **flywheel** is a heavy rotating disc or rim-mounted wheel attached to the shaft of a prime mover or driven machine. Its primary function is to **store rotational kinetic energy** during periods when the **energy input exceeds the energy demand** (called the *energizing* or *power stroke*) and to **release this stored energy** during periods when the **energy demand exceeds the energy input** (called the *de-energizing* or *idle stroke*). By doing so, it reduces fluctuations in angular speed, ensuring smooth and uniform motion of the shaft.

In rigorous engineering terms, a flywheel acts as a **mechanical energy reservoir (energy storage device)**. It does not produce energy by itself; it merely *redistributes* energy over one working cycle. The output of a flywheel is measured by its **fluctuation of energy ($\Delta E$)** and the **coefficient of fluctuation of speed ($C_S$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction:**
> - **Governor** → controls the *mean* speed (steady-state regulation).
> - **Flywheel** → controls the *instantaneous* speed fluctuations within a cycle (transient regulation).
> - Both are complementary, not substitutes, in a complete engine system.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pedaling a bicycle up a hill. Pushing the pedals *down* (energizing stroke) is easy because gravity helps. But during the *up-pedal* (de-energizing stroke), your legs must work against gravity. The result? Your speed wobbles — you speed up going down, slow down going up.

Now imagine tying a **heavy spinning disc** to the bicycle wheel. When you push down, the disc speeds up and **stores** some energy. When the pedals are hard to push, the disc **releases** that stored energy, helping the wheel keep moving smoothly. That heavy disc is essentially a **flywheel**.

A flywheel is to a rotating shaft what a **capacitor** is to an electrical circuit — it stores energy when supply exceeds demand and delivers it when demand exceeds supply, smoothing out fluctuations.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Parameters used in Flywheel Design:**
> - **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_S$):** typically **0.01 to 0.05** (1% to 5%) for most industrial machines.
> - **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$):** related to $C_S$ by the equation $C_E \approx 2C_S$ for small fluctuations.
> - **Mean Speed ($N$):** the average operating speed in **rpm**.
> - **Energy stored:** measured in **N·m** or **Joules**.

---

## 1.4 Why is a Flywheel Necessary? (Engineering Motivation)

In machines like **internal combustion engines**, **reciprocating pumps**, **punch presses**, and **shearing machines**, the **turning moment (torque)** varies considerably over one working cycle. Without a flywheel:

- The crank shaft would **accelerate** during the power stroke.
- It would **decelerate** during the idle stroke.
- This produces **jerky motion**, vibrations, and poor performance.

A flywheel absorbs and releases energy to maintain a **nearly constant angular velocity**, vastly improving operational smoothness and reducing wear on bearings and gears.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Terminology (Step-by-Step Logic)

Let the crank shaft rotate at an **instantaneous angular velocity $\omega$** rad/s, which fluctuates between:

- **Maximum angular velocity:** $\omega_{max} = \omega_1$
- **Minimum angular velocity:** $\omega_{min} = \omega_2$
- **Mean angular velocity:** $\omega_{mean} = \omega = \dfrac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$

### Step 1 — Mean Speed
The **mean speed** is the arithmetic average of the maximum and minimum speeds during one cycle:

$$N = \dfrac{N_{max} + N_{min}}{2}$$

$$\omega = \dfrac{\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}}{2} = \dfrac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_S$)
The **coefficient of fluctuation of speed** is defined as the **ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed to the mean speed**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$C_S = \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{N} = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$$
>
> where $N_1$ = max speed, $N_2$ = min speed, $N$ = mean speed.

This is a **dimensionless quantity** and is the most important design parameter for a flywheel.

### Step 3 — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{max}}$$
>
> where $\Delta E$ = fluctuation of energy, $E_{max}$ = maximum kinetic energy of the flywheel at mean speed.

For small fluctuations, $C_E \approx 2 C_S$ (a result we will derive in Section 3).

### Step 4 — Fluctuation of Energy ($\Delta E$)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$$

This is the **actual energy that the flywheel must absorb or deliver** per cycle.

### Step 5 — Maximum and Minimum Kinetic Energy
$$E_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} I \left(\omega + \dfrac{C_S \omega}{2}\right)^2$$

$$E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_2^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} I \left(\omega - \dfrac{C_S \omega}{2}\right)^2$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **high-yield formula sheet** for all Flywheel problems appearing in KTU University Exams.

| **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mean speed | $N = \dfrac{N_1 + N_2}{2}$ | rpm | Arithmetic mean of extreme speeds |
| 2 | Mean angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$ | rad/s | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ |
| 3 | Coefficient of fluctuation of speed | $C_S = \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{N} = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$ | dimensionless | Usually 0.01 to 0.05 |
| 4 | Fluctuation of energy | $\Delta E = \dfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$ | Joules (N·m) | Energy absorbed/released per cycle |
| 5 | Max kinetic energy | $E_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2$ | Joules | At maximum speed |
| 6 | Min kinetic energy | $E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_2^2$ | Joules | At minimum speed |
| 7 | Coefficient of fluctuation of energy | $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{max}}$ | dimensionless | Energy ratio |
| 8 | Relation $C_E$ vs $C_S$ | $C_E \approx 2 C_S$ | dimensionless | Valid for small $C_S$ |
| 9 | Required moment of inertia | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot \omega^2}$ | kg·m² | Most used design equation |
| 10 | Maximum fluctuation of energy | $\Delta E = I \cdot \omega^2 \cdot C_S$ | Joules | Final design form |
| 11 | Rim flywheel mass (approx.) | $M = \dfrac{I}{R^2}$ | kg | When mass concentrated at rim |
| 12 | Rim moment of inertia | $I = M R^2$ | kg·m² | For a thin rim of mass $M$, radius $R$ |
| 13 | Rim with thickness $b$, depth $d$ | $I = \pi \rho b d R^4$ | kg·m² | $\rho$ = material density |

> **Important — Use the $\vert$ symbol for absolute value to prevent table breaking.**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flywheels are critical in:

- **Automotive engines** — for smooth torque delivery and reduced vibration.
- **Punch presses and shears** — to store energy for the sudden impact load.
- **Reciprocating compressors and pumps** — to maintain uniform flow.
- **Energy storage systems (FES — Flywheel Energy Storage)** — modern applications include grid energy storage, satellite attitude control, and regenerative braking in Formula 1 (KERS — Kinetic Energy Recovery System).
- **Power hammers and rolling mills** — where intermittent heavy loads are common.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** Modern Formula 1 cars use composite-carbon flywheels spinning at over **60,000 rpm** to store ~400 kJ of energy per lap. This is the same physical principle used in a simple workshop engine — just engineered to extreme limits.

---

## 2.4 The Working Principle: Why a Flywheel Works

When torque is applied to a shaft with a moment of inertia $I$, the angular acceleration $\alpha$ follows Newton's second law for rotation:

$$T - T_{resist} = I \alpha = I \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$$

- If $T > T_{resist}$ → shaft accelerates → **flywheel gains kinetic energy**.
- If $T < T_{resist}$ → shaft decelerates → **flywheel releases kinetic energy**.

Hence, the flywheel **buffers the difference** between supplied and demanded torque, keeping $\omega$ nearly constant.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relation Between $C_E$ and $C_S$

This is one of the **most frequently asked derivations** in KTU exams.

### Given:
- Maximum angular velocity: $\omega_1$
- Minimum angular velocity: $\omega_2$
- Mean angular velocity: $\omega = \dfrac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Express $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ in terms of $\omega$ and $C_S$.

By definition of $C_S$:

$$C_S = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$$

Let us define a small deviation $x$ such that:

$$\omega_1 = \omega + x \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_2 = \omega - x$$

Adding: $\omega_1 + \omega_2 = 2\omega$ ✓ (consistent with mean)

Subtracting: $\omega_1 - \omega_2 = 2x$

Therefore:

$$C_S = \dfrac{2x}{\omega} \implies x = \dfrac{C_S \omega}{2}$$

So:

$$\omega_1 = \omega \left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)$$

$$\omega_2 = \omega \left(1 - \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the maximum kinetic energy.

$$E_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the minimum kinetic energy.

$$E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_2^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute the fluctuation of energy.

$$\Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \left[\left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2 - \left(1 - \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 5:** Use the algebraic identity $(a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab$ with $a = 1$ and $b = \dfrac{C_S}{2}$:

$$\left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2 - \left(1 - \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2 = 4 \cdot 1 \cdot \dfrac{C_S}{2} = 2 C_S$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:

$$\Delta E = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \cdot 2 C_S = I \omega^2 C_S$$

**Step 7:** Compute the maximum kinetic energy in expanded form:

$$E_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2$$

**Step 8:** Form the ratio $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{max}}$:

$$C_E = \dfrac{I \omega^2 C_S}{\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2} = \dfrac{2 C_S}{\left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 9:** For small $C_S$ (typically $C_S < 0.1$), we apply the approximation $\left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2 \approx 1$:

$$C_E \approx 2 C_S$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (KTU High-Yield):**
> $$\boxed{C_E = 2 C_S \quad \text{(for small coefficient of fluctuation of speed)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Expression for Required Moment of Inertia

This is the **fundamental design equation** used to size a flywheel.

### Given:
- Required fluctuation of energy: $\Delta E$ (from turning moment diagram)
- Mean speed: $N$ rpm → $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s
- Permissible coefficient of fluctuation of speed: $C_S$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** From the previous derivation, the fluctuation of energy is:

$$\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_S$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the moment of inertia $I$:

$$I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\omega^2 C_S}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\left(\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2 \cdot C_S} = \dfrac{\Delta E \cdot (60)^2}{4 \pi^2 N^2 \cdot C_S}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify $\dfrac{60^2}{4 \pi^2} = \dfrac{3600}{4 \pi^2} = \dfrac{900}{\pi^2} \approx 91.19$:

$$I = \dfrac{900 \cdot \Delta E}{\pi^2 N^2 C_S} \approx \dfrac{\Delta E}{0.0109 N^2 C_S}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (KTU High-Yield — Design Equation):**
> $$\boxed{I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \omega^2} = \dfrac{900 \cdot \Delta E}{\pi^2 N^2 C_S}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Energy Interpretation at Different Speeds

| **Condition** | **Speed** | **KE State** | **Torque Status** | **Action of Flywheel** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energizing phase (power stroke) | Increasing from $\omega_2$ to $\omega_1$ | $E$ rises from $E_{min}$ to $E_{max}$ | $T_{supplied} > T_{resist}$ | **Stores** energy = $\Delta E$ |
| De-energizing phase (idle stroke) | Decreasing from $\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$ | $E$ falls from $E_{max}$ to $E_{min}$ | $T_{supplied} < T_{resist}$ | **Releases** energy = $\Delta E$ |
| Mean speed crossing | $\omega$ at midpoint of $\omega_1, \omega_2$ | $E$ at midpoint of $E_{max}, E_{min}$ | $T_{supplied} = T_{resist}$ (averaged) | No net energy change |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Rim Flywheel Dimensions

For a flywheel where the mass is concentrated in a **thin rim** of mean radius $R$, mass $M$, rectangular cross-section of width $b$ (axial) and depth $d$ (radial), with material density $\rho$:

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Volume of the rim:
$$V = 2 \pi R \cdot b \cdot d$$

**Step 2:** Mass of the rim:
$$M = \rho V = 2 \pi R b d \rho$$

**Step 3:** Moment of inertia of the thin rim (treating it as a hoop):
$$I = M R^2 = 2 \pi R^3 b d \rho$$

**Step 4:** Solve for product $b \cdot d$:
$$b \cdot d = \dfrac{I}{2 \pi R^3 \rho}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Use:** The product $b \cdot d$ is the **cross-sectional area** of the rim. Given either width $b$ or depth $d$ (e.g., from hub/spoke constraints), the other dimension is determined.

---

## 3.5 Turning Moment Diagram and Fluctuation of Energy

The **Turning Moment Diagram (TMD)** plots the **net turning moment** (driving torque − resisting torque) on the crank shaft versus crank angle $\theta$ over one working cycle.

- **Area above the mean torque line** = maximum energy fluctuation (positive)
- **Area below the mean torque line** = maximum energy deficit (negative)
- **The total fluctuation of energy** $\Delta E$ = the **largest of the areas** between the TMD curve and the mean torque line.

Mathematically:

$$\Delta E = \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} (T_{net} - T_{mean}) \, d\theta$$

The mean torque line is drawn such that the area between the TMD curve and the mean line above equals the area below — a horizontal balance line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In KTU exam problems**, when the TMD is a polygon (triangles, rectangles, etc.), the area is computed using **standard geometric formulas** (e.g., $\dfrac{1}{2} b h$ for triangles, $b \times h$ for rectangles). The largest such area gives $\Delta E$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Flywheel System

Below is the functional block diagram showing how a flywheel integrates with a prime mover and driven machine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PRIME MOVER] -->|Driving Torque Td| B[CRANK SHAFT]
    B -->|Torque Fluctuates| C[FLYWHEEL ASSEMBLY]
    C -->|Smooths Torque| D[GEAR BOX]
    D -->|Uniform Torque| E[DRIVEN MACHINE]
    F[RESISTING LOAD] -->|Torque Tr| B
    C -.->|Stores KE during power stroke| C
    C -.->|Releases KE during idle stroke| C
    G[COEFFICIENT Cs PARAMETER] --> C
    H[MEAN SPEED N] --> C
```

**Node Interpretation:**
- A (Prime Mover): IC engine, electric motor, steam engine
- B (Crank Shaft): Rotating member with fluctuating load
- C (Flywheel Assembly): Heavy rim-mounted energy storage
- D (Gear Box): Speed/torque conversion stage
- E (Driven Machine): Load application point
- F (Resisting Load): External mechanical work demand
- G (Parameter Cs): Design input
- H (Mean Speed N): Design input

---

## 4.2 Flywheel Energy Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PHASE 1 Power Stroke Td greater than Tr] --> P2[Flywheel Accelerates omega rises]
    P2 --> P3[Kinetic Energy Increases E rises to Emax]
    P3 --> P4[Energy Stored Delta E]
    P4 --> P5[PHASE 2 Idle Stroke Td less than Tr]
    P5 --> P6[Flywheel Decelerates omega falls]
    P6 --> P7[Kinetic Energy Decreases E falls to Emin]
    P7 --> P8[Energy Released Delta E]
    P8 --> P9[CYCLE COMPLETE omega returns to omega2]
```

---

## 4.3 Turning Moment Diagram — Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "TMD Energy Balance"
        D1[Curve of Net Turning Moment versus Theta] --> A1[Area A above mean torque line]
        D1 --> A2[Area B below mean torque line]
        A1 --> R1[Delta E equals max of A or B]
        A2 --> R1
    end
    R1 --> F1[Apply I equals Delta E by Cs omega squared]
    F1 --> F2[Compute Mass M and Radius R]
    F2 --> F3[DESIGN OUTPUT Flywheel dimensions]
```

---

## 4.4 Rim Flywheel Cross-Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CRANK SHAFT] --> B[HUB]
    B --> C[SPOKES or ARMS]
    C --> D[RIM Mass concentrated here]
    D --> E[Mean Radius R]
    D --> F[Cross Section Width b by Depth d]
    B --> G[KEYWAY for shaft connection]
    D --> H[Falange for spoke attachment]
```

> **Note:** The **mass is concentrated at the rim** (at maximum radius $R$) because $I = M R^2$ — placing mass far from the axis maximizes moment of inertia for a given mass.
