<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define the following terms with respect to a flywheel:**
**(a)** Coefficient of fluctuation of speed,
**(b)** Coefficient of fluctuation of energy,
**(c)** Fluctuation of energy.

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_S$):**
> It is defined as the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed to the mean speed of the flywheel.
> $$C_S = \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{N} = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$$
> where $N_1, N_2$ are the maximum and minimum speeds and $N$ is the mean speed.

**(b)** **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$):**
> It is the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of energy to the maximum kinetic energy of the flywheel.
> $$C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{max}}$$

**(c)** **Fluctuation of Energy ($\Delta E$):**
> It is the difference between the maximum and minimum kinetic energies of the flywheel during one working cycle.
> $$\Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State the function of a flywheel. Why is it not considered as an energy-generating device?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Function:** A flywheel stores kinetic energy when the supply torque exceeds the resisting torque and releases it when the resisting torque exceeds the supply torque. This smooths out speed fluctuations during one working cycle.
>
> **Not an energy-generating device:** A flywheel does not create energy; it only stores energy temporarily and releases it later. Over one complete cycle, the net energy change is zero. Hence it is purely an **energy-regulating device**, not a generator.

---

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Answer any **one full question** from the module. Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** A punching machine makes 25 holes per minute in a 25 mm thick steel plate. The punching force is 80 kN and the coefficient of fluctuation of speed is 0.15. The flywheel is to be made of cast iron with a permissible stress of 7.5 MPa. The mean diameter of the flywheel is 1.2 m. Determine:
1. The mass of the flywheel, and
2. The dimensions of the cross-section of the rim assuming it to be a rectangle with depth twice the width.

**(b)** Derive the relation between the coefficient of fluctuation of speed and the coefficient of fluctuation of energy for a flywheel.

---

### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Step 1 — Punching energy per stroke:**
Work done per hole = Force × Distance punched
$$W = F \times t = 80 \times 10^3 \times 0.025 = 2000 \, \text{J}$$

(Here, $t$ = thickness of plate, since the punch travels through the plate thickness to make a hole.)

**Step 2 — Power required by the machine:**
25 holes per minute → 25 strokes/min

$$P = 25 \times 2000 = 50{,}000 \, \text{J/min} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{60} = 833.33 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Time per stroke:**
$$t_s = \dfrac{60}{25} = 2.4 \, \text{s/stroke}$$

**Step 4 — Fluctuation of energy:**
For a punching press, the entire work occurs during the stroke. The flywheel must deliver this energy in one cycle:
$$\Delta E = 2000 \, \text{J} = 2 \times 10^3 \, \text{J}$$

(Alternative: For a punching press, $\Delta E$ = work done per stroke when the energy is delivered entirely from the flywheel deceleration, multiplied by a factor.)

**Step 5 — Required moment of inertia:**
Assuming mean speed $N$ to be determined or from the stress condition. Let us use the standard formula with $C_S$ for small fluctuations. We need the mean speed; assume $N = 200$ rpm (typical for punching machines, often given in the problem). For complete problem, $N$ would be specified. Using:

$$I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot \omega^2}$$

With $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, $N$ to be given. Let us assume $N = 240$ rpm (since not specified, the typical assumption in such KTU problems is given in the problem statement, here we demonstrate the methodology).

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 25.13 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$I = \dfrac{2000}{0.15 \times (25.13)^2} = \dfrac{2000}{0.15 \times 631.65} = \dfrac{2000}{94.75} = 21.11 \, \text{kg·m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Mass of the flywheel (rim approximation):**
For a thin rim, $I = M R^2$. With mean diameter $D = 1.2$ m → $R = 0.6$ m:

$$M = \dfrac{I}{R^2} = \dfrac{21.11}{(0.6)^2} = \dfrac{21.11}{0.36} = 58.64 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 7 — Rim cross-section:**
Density of cast iron $\rho = 7200 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

$$M = 2 \pi R \cdot b \cdot d \cdot \rho$$

Given $d = 2b$:

$$58.64 = 2\pi (0.6)(b)(2b)(7200) = 2\pi (0.6)(2b^2)(7200)$$

$$58.64 = 54{,}285.6 \, b^2$$

$$b^2 = 1.08 \times 10^{-3} \implies b = 0.0329 \, \text{m} \approx 33 \, \text{mm}$$

$$d = 2b = 66 \, \text{mm}$$

> **Final Answer:**
> - Mass $M \approx 58.64$ kg
> - Width $b \approx 33$ mm, Depth $d \approx 66$ mm

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part A (a):**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s** — using $N$ instead of $\omega$ in the $I$ formula. This gives a 7-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing punching work** — using the wrong thickness direction. The punch travels the thickness of the plate.
> 3. **Not using $\rho$ correctly** — density of cast iron is 7200 kg/m³, not 7800 (which is steel).
> 4. **Mixing up width and depth** — always state which is which clearly.

---

### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Derivation of $C_E$ in terms of $C_S$:**

Let $\omega_1$ = max angular velocity, $\omega_2$ = min angular velocity, $\omega$ = mean angular velocity.

By definition:
$$\omega = \dfrac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$$

$$C_S = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$$

Therefore:
$$\omega_1 = \omega\left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right) \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_2 = \omega\left(1 - \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)$$

Maximum kinetic energy:
$$E_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2$$

Minimum kinetic energy:
$$E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_2^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2$$

Fluctuation of energy:
$$\Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \left[\left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2 - \left(1 - \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2\right]$$

Using $(a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab$:

$$\Delta E = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \cdot 4 \cdot \left(\dfrac{C_S}{2}\right) \cdot 1 = I \omega^2 C_S$$

Coefficient of fluctuation of energy:
$$C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{max}} = \dfrac{I \omega^2 C_S}{\dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2} = \dfrac{2 C_S}{\left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2}$$

For small $C_S$ (typically $< 0.1$):
$$\left(1 + \dfrac{C_S}{2}\right)^2 \approx 1$$

$$\boxed{\therefore C_E \approx 2 C_S}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part A (b):**
> - **MUST show every algebraic step.** KTU examiners award 7 marks distributed across the steps. Skipping the identity $(a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab$ loses 2 marks.
> - **State the assumption** "$C_S$ is small" explicitly when using the approximation. Otherwise the answer is incomplete.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a)** A four-stroke IC engine develops 25 kW of brake power at 320 rpm. The turning moment diagram gives a fluctuation of energy of 12% of the work done per cycle. The mean diameter of the flywheel is 1.5 m. The coefficient of fluctuation of speed is 0.04. Determine:
1. The moment of inertia of the flywheel.
2. The mass of the flywheel.

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a flywheel. State the difference between a flywheel and a governor.

---

### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Step 1 — Work done per cycle:**
Number of working cycles per second for a **four-stroke engine** = $N/2$ per second.
$$n_{cycle} = \dfrac{N}{2 \times 60} = \dfrac{320}{120} = 2.667 \, \text{cycles/s}$$

**Step 2 — Work done per cycle:**
$$W_{cycle} = \dfrac{P}{n_{cycle}} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{2.667} = 9375 \, \text{J}$$

**Step 3 — Fluctuation of energy (12% of work per cycle):**
$$\Delta E = 0.12 \times 9375 = 1125 \, \text{J}$$

**Step 4 — Mean angular velocity:**
$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 320}{60} = 33.51 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Required moment of inertia:**
$$I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \omega^2} = \dfrac{1125}{0.04 \times (33.51)^2} = \dfrac{1125}{0.04 \times 1122.96} = \dfrac{1125}{44.92} = 25.04 \, \text{kg·m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Mass of the flywheel:**
$R = 1.5/2 = 0.75$ m, treating as thin rim:
$$M = \dfrac{I}{R^2} = \dfrac{25.04}{(0.75)^2} = \dfrac{25.04}{0.5625} = 44.52 \, \text{kg}$$

> **Final Answer:**
> - $I \approx 25.04$ kg·m²
> - $M \approx 44.52$ kg

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Question B (a):**
> - **CRITICAL: Four-stroke vs Two-stroke.** A four-stroke engine completes **1 working cycle per 2 revolutions**, not 1 cycle per revolution. Many students divide by $N/60$ instead of $N/120$, losing 3 marks.
> - The 12% applies to work per **cycle**, not per revolution.

---

### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Construction:**
A flywheel consists of:
- **Hub:** Central portion that mounts on the crank shaft via key and keyway.
- **Spokes (or arms):** Connect the hub to the rim; provide radial support and balance.
- **Rim:** The outer heavy portion where most of the mass is concentrated. Cross-section is typically rectangular or I-shaped for strength.
- **Falanges:** Web sections connecting the spokes to the rim (in disc-type flywheels, the disc replaces spokes).

**Working:**
- During the **power stroke** (driving torque > resisting torque), the flywheel accelerates and stores kinetic energy.
- During the **idle stroke** (driving torque < resisting torque), the flywheel decelerates and releases the stored kinetic energy.
- This energy exchange smooths out speed fluctuations.

**Flywheel vs Governor:**

| **S.No.** | **Flywheel** | **Governor** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Reduces *fluctuation* of speed within a cycle | Regulates *mean* speed over a period |
| 2 | Acts on the *instantaneous* speed variations | Acts on the *average* speed changes due to load variation |
| 3 | Operates continuously within a cycle | Operates intermittently as load changes |
| 4 | A *mechanical energy storage* device | A *control* device (senses + corrects speed) |
| 5 | Does not consume or produce energy | Adjusts fuel/admission to maintain speed |
| 6 | Designed using fluctuation of energy $\Delta E$ | Designed using sensitivity, stability, etc. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Question B (b):**
> - **Always draw a labelled sketch** (or block diagram) — 2 marks are reserved for the figure.
> - The comparison table format **earns full marks** for clarity.
> - Mentioning the **governor controls the *mean* speed** is a high-yield keyword examiners look for.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Flywheel**

- **Primary function:** Store and release rotational KE to smooth out speed fluctuations.
- **Energy stored** (max): $E_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2$
- **Energy released** (min): $E_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_2^2$
- **Fluctuation of energy:** $\Delta E = \dfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2)$
- **Coefficient of fluctuation of speed:** $C_S = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$ (dimensionless, range 0.01 to 0.05).
- **Coefficient of fluctuation of energy:** $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{max}}$.
- **Golden relation:** $C_E \approx 2C_S$ (valid for small $C_S$).
- **Design equation (most used):** $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \, \omega^2}$.
- **Rim flywheel mass:** $M = \dfrac{I}{R^2}$ (thin-rim approximation).
- **Rim cross-section area:** $b \cdot d = \dfrac{I}{2\pi R^3 \rho}$.
- **Four-stroke engine:** 1 cycle = 2 revolutions; divide power by $N/(2 \times 60)$.
- **Two-stroke engine:** 1 cycle = 1 revolution; divide power by $N/60$.
- **Punching press work:** $W = F \times t$ where $t$ = plate thickness.
- **TMD mean line:** Horizontal line such that area above = area below (in magnitude); the largest area = $\Delta E$.
- **Flywheel ≠ Governor:** Flywheel → transient; Governor → steady-state mean speed.
- **Units check:** $I$ in kg·m², $\omega$ in rad/s, $\Delta E$ in Joules.
- **Industrial $C_S$ values:** 0.01 (precision machines) to 0.05 (heavy machinery); punching presses may have higher $C_S$ (up to 0.2).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turning Moment Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Turning Moment Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Turning Moment Diagram (TMD)** is a graphical plot that depicts the **variation of the turning moment (or crank effort) acting on the crankshaft** of an engine (or prime mover) as a function of the **crank angle (θ)** during one complete working cycle.

Mathematically, the turning moment at any instant is given by the **difference between the torque developed by the working fluid and the resisting torque**:

$$T = T_{\text{fluid}} - T_{\text{resist}}$$

- When $T > 0$ → crankshaft **accelerates** (driving the load).
- When $T < 0$ → crankshaft **decelerates** (load assists/drives the crank).
- When $T = 0$ → crankshaft is momentarily at uniform speed.

The **area under the TMD** for one complete cycle represents the **net work done per cycle**, which for a 4-stroke engine spans **two revolutions ($4\pi$ radians)**, and for a 2-stroke engine spans **one revolution ($2\pi$ radians)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Pedaling Cyclist on a Hilly Track** 🚴

Imagine you are pedaling a bicycle up a hilly road:
- On the **flat ground**, your leg pushes *consistently* — the crankshaft feels a steady turning moment.
- When going **uphill**, you need to push **harder** — large turning moment, speed builds up momentum.
- When going **downhill**, you barely push (or even brake) — turning moment becomes small or even negative.
- Your leg's effort is the **"driving torque"**, the road + slope is the **"resisting torque"**, and the resulting pedaling ease is the **net turning moment**.

The **Turning Moment Diagram** is essentially a **graphical record of this fluctuating pedaling effort** through one complete pedal cycle. A practical machine cannot have a constant turning moment — it fluctuates, and we need a **flywheel** (a heavy rotating mass) to absorb excess energy during surplus periods and release it during deficit periods, much like a flywheel on an old steam engine or a modern car's inertial ring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - Turning Moment $T$ → **N·m** (Newton-metres)
> - Crank angle $\theta$ → **radians** (or degrees in textbook plots)
> - Angular velocity $\omega$ → **rad/s**
> - Speed $N$ → **rpm (revolutions per minute)**
> - Energy fluctuation $\Delta E$ → **N·m or Joules (J)**
> - Mass moment of inertia $I = mk^2$ → **kg·m²**
> - Standard gravitational constant: **$g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need a Turning Moment Diagram?

The driving torque from the fuel combustion (or steam pressure) is **not constant** through a cycle. In a 4-stroke IC engine:
- **Power stroke:** large driving torque
- **Compression, suction, exhaust strokes:** the piston is being pushed, so crankshaft must supply torque

This **unevenness** causes the crankshaft speed to fluctuate. We need the TMD to:
1. **Design the flywheel** to limit speed fluctuation.
2. **Determine the fluctuation of energy** ($\Delta E$).
3. **Calculate mean torque** ($T_{\text{mean}}$) of the engine.

### 2.2 Key Engine Performance Metrics Derived from TMD

**Step 1 — Mean Turning Moment ($T_{\text{mean}}$):**
The constant torque that would do the same work per cycle as the actual fluctuating torque.

**Step 2 — Work Done Per Cycle ($W$ or $W_{\text{cycle}}$):**
The area enclosed by the TMD taken over the base line of the resisting torque (i.e., $T_{\text{mean}}$ line).

**Step 3 — Fluctuation of Energy ($\Delta E$):**
The difference between maximum and minimum energies stored in the flywheel. Geometrically, it is the **largest area between the TMD curve and the mean torque line**.

**Step 4 — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$):**
Ratio of fluctuation of energy to the work done per cycle.

**Step 5 — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_S$):**
Ratio of difference between maximum and minimum speeds to the mean speed.

**Step 6 — Flywheel Mass Moment of Inertia ($I$ or $mk^2$):**
Calculated using the speed fluctuation limit and energy storage requirement.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Mean turning moment | $T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{\text{Area of TMD}}{L} = \dfrac{W}{2\pi N}$ (2-stroke) | $L$ = base length of TMD (in radians) |
| 2 | Work done per cycle (4-stroke) | $W = T_{\text{mean}} \times 4\pi$ | $N$ in rps |
| 3 | Work done per cycle (2-stroke) | $W = T_{\text{mean}} \times 2\pi$ | Used for petrol/kerosene engines |
| 4 | Fluctuation of energy | $\Delta E = E_{\max} - E_{\min}$ | Joules or N·m |
| 5 | Geometric $\Delta E$ from TMD | $\Delta E = \text{Largest area between TMD and } T_{\text{mean}} \text{ line}$ | Read from diagram (units of N·m·rad = J) |
| 6 | Coefficient of fluctuation of energy | $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W}$ | Dimensionless, typically $0.05$ to $0.20$ |
| 7 | Coefficient of fluctuation of speed | $C_S = \dfrac{\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}}{\omega_{\text{mean}}}$ | Dimensionless, typically $0.01$ to $0.05$ |
| 8 | Mean angular velocity | $\omega_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{\omega_{\max} + \omega_{\min}}{2}$ | rad/s |
| 9 | Energy stored at speed $\omega$ | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} m k^2 \omega^2$ | Joules |
| 10 | Flywheel inertia formula | $I = m k^2 = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}^2}$ | kg·m² |
| 11 | Rim-type flywheel | $D^2 = \dfrac{\Delta E \cdot g}{C_S \cdot \pi^2 \cdot N^2 \cdot k^2 \cdot n}$ | $N$ in rpm, $n$ = number of arms |
| 12 | Torque-power-speed relation | $P = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | $P$ in watts, $N$ in rpm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Numerical Conversions to Memorise for KTU Exams:**
> - $\omega\ (\text{rad/s}) = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ where $N$ is in **rpm**
> - $1\ \text{kW} = 1000\ \text{W} = 1000\ \text{N·m/s}$
> - $1\ \text{hp} = 745.7\ \text{W} \approx 746\ \text{W}$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of TMD |
|-------------------|--------------------|
| **IC Engine Design** | Sizing the flywheel of petrol/diesel cars, trucks, gensets |
| **Steam Engine Plants** | Old textile mills used TMD + flywheel analysis to smoothen power delivery |
| **Presses & Punches** | Flywheels accumulate energy from a small motor and release it in a short punching stroke |
| **Rolling Mills** | Heavy flywheels store energy between passes of metal |
| **Reciprocating Pumps/Compressors** | TMD used to balance shaft rotation and reduce vibration |
| **Wind Turbines** | Rotational inertia (acting as flywheel) smoothens torque spikes from gusts |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean Turning Moment from TMD

**Statement:** Prove that the mean turning moment over a cycle equals the area of the TMD divided by its base length (crank angle covered).

**Derivation:**

Let the turning moment $T$ vary as a function of crank angle $\theta$ over one full working cycle. The **elementary work done** by the turning moment when the crank rotates through an infinitesimal angle $d\theta$ is:

$$dW = T \cdot d\theta$$

Integrating over the complete cycle of crank rotation (angle $L$ — which is $4\pi$ rad for 4-stroke and $2\pi$ rad for 2-stroke):

$$W = \int_{0}^{L} T \, d\theta$$

By definition, the area under the TMD curve equals this integral:

$$\text{Area of TMD} = \int_{0}^{L} T \, d\theta = W$$

If the mean turning moment is $T_{\text{mean}}$, the constant torque doing the same work $W$ over the same angle $L$ satisfies:

$$W = T_{\text{mean}} \times L$$

Equating both expressions:

$$T_{\text{mean}} \times L = \int_{0}^{L} T \, d\theta$$

Solving for the mean turning moment:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{\text{Area of TMD}}{L}}$$

This is the **fundamental relation** used in **every KTU problem** on TMD.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between $C_S$, $C_E$, and $\omega_{\text{mean}}$

**Statement:** Show that $\Delta E = I \cdot C_S \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}^2$.

**Derivation:**

Energy stored in the flywheel at any speed $\omega$ is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

At maximum speed $\omega_{\max}$:

$$E_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} I \, \omega_{\max}^2$$

At minimum speed $\omega_{\min}$:

$$E_{\min} = \frac{1}{2} I \, \omega_{\min}^2$$

Fluctuation of energy:

$$\Delta E = E_{\max} - E_{\min} = \frac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_{\max}^2 - \omega_{\min}^2 \right)$$

Apply the algebraic identity $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min} \right) \left( \omega_{\max} + \omega_{\min} \right)$$

Substitute the definition of mean speed $\omega_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{\omega_{\max} + \omega_{\min}}{2}$:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min} \right) \cdot 2 \, \omega_{\text{mean}}$$

$$\Delta E = I \left( \omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min} \right) \omega_{\text{mean}}$$

Now apply the definition of coefficient of fluctuation of speed:

$$C_S = \dfrac{\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}}{\omega_{\text{mean}}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min} = C_S \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}$$

Substitute back:

$$\Delta E = I \cdot (C_S \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}) \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E = I \cdot C_S \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}^2}$$

**Rearranging for flywheel inertia:**

$$\boxed{I = m k^2 = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}^2}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rim-Type Flywheel Diameter Formula

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the mean diameter of a rim-type flywheel.

**Derivation:**

A rim-type flywheel has most of its mass concentrated in the rim. The rim is approximated as a thin circular ring of:
- Mean diameter $D$
- Rectangular cross-section of width $b$ and thickness $t$
- $n$ arms of length $k$ (from shaft centre to rim centre)

The **kinetic energy** of the rim is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} I_{\text{rim}} \, \omega^2 = \frac{1}{2} \left( m_{\text{rim}} \cdot \frac{D^2}{4} \right) \omega^2$$

Since the area of the rim cross-section is $A = b \times t$, the rim mass is $m_{\text{rim}} = \rho \cdot A \cdot \pi D$ where $\rho$ is the density of the rim material.

Now, the fluctuation of energy from the mean speed is:

$$\Delta E = I \cdot C_S \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}^2 = \left( m k^2 \right) C_S \omega_{\text{mean}}^2$$

If we neglect the inertia of arms and hub (rim-only design), the **kinetic energy at any speed is entirely in the rim**. The change in kinetic energy between $\omega_{\max}$ and $\omega_{\min}$ is:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} m k^2 \left( \omega_{\max}^2 - \omega_{\min}^2 \right)$$

Substituting the mean speed relation and converting $\omega_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ (with $N$ in rpm):

$$\Delta E = m k^2 \cdot C_S \cdot \left( \frac{2\pi N}{60} \right)^2$$

Solving for $m k^2$:

$$m k^2 = \frac{\Delta E \cdot (60)^2}{C_S \cdot 4\pi^2 N^2}$$

Since $k = D/2$ for rim-type, $k^2 = D^2/4$. With $n$ arms of cross-section $A_a$ and arm length equal to $(D-b)/2 \approx D/2$ for thin rims, the mass of arms is much smaller and is added as a correction factor. The final design equation including arms correction is:

$$\boxed{D^2 = \dfrac{\Delta E \cdot g}{C_S \cdot \pi^2 \cdot N^2 \cdot k^2 \cdot n}}$$

where:
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ (gravity constant — used to convert weight to mass)
- $k^2$ is a constant depending on flywheel geometry (specific to design tables)
- $n$ is the number of arms

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Numerical Strategy (Analytical Matrix)

| Step | Action in Problem | Key Formula / Output |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Step 1** | Identify the engine type | 4-stroke ($L = 4\pi$ rad) or 2-stroke ($L = 2\pi$ rad) |
| **Step 2** | Calculate the area of the TMD | Use trapezoidal rule, geometry of shaded regions, or summation of triangular/trapezoidal areas |
| **Step 3** | Calculate mean turning moment | $T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{\text{Area}}{L}$ |
| **Step 4** | Calculate work done per cycle | $W = T_{\text{mean}} \times L$ |
| **Step 5** | Identify largest area between TMD and $T_{\text{mean}}$ line | This is the **fluctuation of energy $\Delta E$** |
| **Step 6** | Calculate $C_E$ (if asked) | $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W}$ |
| **Step 7** | Convert $N$ to $\omega_{\text{mean}}$ | $\omega_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ |
| **Step 8** | Calculate $mk^2$ (flywheel inertia) | $mk^2 = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}^2}$ |
| **Step 9** | (Optional) Calculate rim diameter | $D^2 = \dfrac{\Delta E \cdot g}{C_S \cdot \pi^2 N^2 k^2 n}$ |
| **Step 10** | Sanity check | Verify units are consistent (J, kg·m², m) |

### 3.5 Worked Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

**Problem [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]:**

> The turning moment diagram for a 4-stroke engine has been drawn to a scale of **$1\ \text{mm} = 600\ \text{N·m}$** vertically and **$1\ \text{mm} = 6°$** horizontally. The area of the diagram above and below the mean torque line are respectively **$562\ \text{mm}^2$** and **$48\ \text{mm}^2$**. The speed is **300 rpm**. Find:
> (a) Mean turning moment
> (b) Work done per cycle
> (c) Fluctuation of energy
> (d) Power developed

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mean Turning Moment:**

The TMD covers one full cycle = $4\pi$ rad for 4-stroke.

Total area of TMD = $562 + 48 = 610\ \text{mm}^2$

$$\text{Mean turning moment} = T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{\text{Area in N·m·rad}}{\text{Cycle length in rad}}$$

Convert area: $610\ \text{mm}^2 \times 600\ \text{N·m/mm (vertical)} \times 6^\circ/\text{mm (horizontal)}$

$$610\ \text{mm}^2 = 610 \times 600 \times 6\ \text{N·m·degree} = 2{,}196{,}000\ \text{N·m·degree}$$

Convert degrees to radians: $1\ \text{rad} = \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ degrees

$$W = \dfrac{2{,}196{,}000 \times \pi}{180}\ \text{N·m} = 38{,}330.6\ \text{N·m}$$

$$T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{W}{4\pi} = \dfrac{38{,}330.6}{4\pi} = \dfrac{38{,}330.6}{12.566} \approx 3050\ \text{N·m}$$

**Part (b) — Work Done Per Cycle:**

$$W = T_{\text{mean}} \times 4\pi = 3050 \times 4\pi \approx 38{,}330.6\ \text{J} \approx 38.33\ \text{kJ}$$

**Part (c) — Fluctuation of Energy:**

The largest area between TMD and mean line = **562 mm²** (largest shaded region).

Convert this area to N·m:

$$\Delta E = 562 \times 600 \times 6 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}\ \text{N·m}$$

$$\Delta E = 2{,}023{,}200 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} \approx 35{,}307\ \text{N·m} \approx 35.31\ \text{kJ}$$

**Part (d) — Power Developed:**

Convert speed to rad/s: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416\ \text{rad/s}$

$$P = T_{\text{mean}} \times \omega = 3050 \times 31.416 \approx 95{,}819\ \text{W} \approx 95.82\ \text{kW}$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Turning Moment Diagram Analysis

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **complete analytical pipeline** used to solve KTU TMD problems — from raw data input to final flywheel design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: TMD Areas and Scales"] --> B{"Identify Engine Type"}
    B -- "4-Stroke" --> C1["Cycle length L = 4π rad"]
    B -- "2-Stroke" --> C2["Cycle length L = 2π rad"]
    C1 --> D["Compute Total Area of TMD in N·m·rad"]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E["Mean Turning Moment T_mean = Area ÷ L"]
    E --> F["Work Done per Cycle W = T_mean × L"]
    F --> G{"Largest Area between TMD and T_mean line"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Fluctuation of Energy ΔE = Largest Area in N·m"]
    H --> I["Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy C_E = ΔE ÷ W"]
    H --> J["Mean Angular Velocity ω_mean = 2πN ÷ 60"]
    J --> K["Given or chosen C_S"]
    K --> L["Flywheel Inertia mk² = ΔE ÷ C_S ω_mean²"]
    L --> M{"Rim-type Flywheel Design"}
    M -- "Yes" --> N["Compute Mean Diameter D"]
    M -- "No" --> O["Final mk² value reported"]
    N --> P["Final Output: Flywheel Specs"]
    O --> P
    I --> P
```

### 4.2 Conceptual TMD Plot (Schematic via Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X["TMD Variation over one cycle"]
        A1["T rises sharply during power stroke"] --> A2["T peaks at maximum"]
        A2 --> A3["T falls to minimum during exhaust/compression"]
        A3 --> A4["T crosses T_mean line"]
        A4 --> A5["T rises to next cycle"]
    end
    subgraph Y["Reference Lines"]
        B1["Horizontal line of T_mean"]
        B2["Zero torque baseline"]
    end
    A1 -. "shaded surplus area +ΔE" .-> B1
    A3 -. "shaded deficit area -ΔE" .-> B1
```

### 4.3 Typical TMD — Triangle-Alternation Form (Most Common KTU Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0["Crank at 0°: T = 0"] --> T1["T rises linearly to peak T_max at 60°"]
    T1 --> T2["T drops to -T_min at 180°"]
    T2 --> T3["T rises back to 0 at 360°"]
    T3 --> T0

    subgraph MeanLine["Horizontal mean torque reference"]
        ML["T_mean drawn as dashed line through the plot"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagram Substitution:** A traditional TMD is a **2-D curve (T vs θ)**. Since Mermaid cannot render Cartesian plots directly, the **functional flow** above represents the **sequence of torque values** that a real TMD plot would display. In KTU exams, the actual TMD is given as a graph in the question paper; students only need to **read areas and apply formulas**.

### 4.4 Energy Fluctuation Visualization (Surplus vs Deficit Areas)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Surplus["Surplus Energy (Engine delivers more than load needs)"]
        S1["Area between TMD curve and T_mean line, above T_mean"]
    end
    subgraph Deficit["Deficit Energy (Engine delivers less than load needs)"]
        D1["Area between TMD curve and T_mean line, below T_mean"]
    end
    Surplus -- "ΔE_max (E stored in flywheel)" --> Deficit
    Deficit -- "Flywheel releases this energy" --> Surplus
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a Turning Moment Diagram. What is the significance of the area under the TMD?**

**Model Answer:**

A Turning Moment Diagram (TMD) is a plot of the **net turning moment acting on the crankshaft** as a function of the **crank angle** over one complete working cycle. It shows how the driving torque from combustion (or steam) and the resisting torque interact to produce a fluctuating net torque.

**Significance of area under TMD:**
1. The area under the TMD represents the **work done per cycle** by the engine (in N·m or Joules).
2. It is used to compute the **mean turning moment** by dividing the area by the crank angle covered ($4\pi$ for 4-stroke, $2\pi$ for 2-stroke).
3. The **largest area between the TMD and the mean torque line** gives the **fluctuation of energy ($\Delta E$)**, which is critical for **flywheel design**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What are the coefficients of fluctuation of energy ($C_E$) and fluctuation of speed ($C_S$)? State their typical ranges.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$):** It is the ratio of the **maximum fluctuation of energy** to the **work done per cycle**.
$$C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W}$$
- **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_S$):** It is the ratio of the **difference between maximum and minimum angular speeds** to the **mean angular speed**.
$$C_S = \dfrac{\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}}{\omega_{\text{mean}}}$$

**Typical Ranges (per KTU textbook values):**
- $C_E$ ranges from **0.05 to 0.20** depending on the engine.
- $C_S$ ranges from **1/50 to 1/100** (i.e., 0.01 to 0.05) for industrial engines.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q3. (a)** The turning moment diagram for a 4-stroke gas engine is represented by a trapezium with the following ordinates at equal intervals of crank angle:

| Crank angle (deg) | 0 | 30 | 60 | 90 | 120 | 150 | 180 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Turning moment (kN·m) | 0 | 8.0 | 14.0 | 16.0 | 14.0 | 8.0 | 0 |

The resisting torque is constant at **$4\ \text{kN·m}$**. The engine runs at **240 rpm**. The total mass of the flywheel is **300 kg** and the radius of gyration is **0.4 m**. Find:
1. Power developed
2. Total fluctuation of energy
3. Coefficient of fluctuation of energy
4. Coefficient of fluctuation of speed
5. Range of speed

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail with neat sketches the **construction of a typical turning moment diagram** for a **4-stroke IC engine**. Discuss how the area above and below the mean torque line is used to determine the **fluctuation of energy**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q3(a):**

**Given Data:**
- 4-stroke engine, $N = 240$ rpm
- Ordinate interval = 30° = $\pi/6$ rad
- Resisting torque $T_R = 4\ \text{kN·m}$ = constant
- Mass of flywheel $m = 300$ kg, $k = 0.4$ m
- Number of intervals $n = 6$

**Step 1 — Net Turning Moment (Driving - Resisting):**

| Crank angle (deg) | 0 | 30 | 60 | 90 | 120 | 150 | 180 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T$ (kN·m) | 0 | 8.0 | 14.0 | 16.0 | 14.0 | 8.0 | 0 |
| $T_R$ (kN·m) | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| **Net $T$ (kN·m)** | **-4** | **+4** | **+10** | **+12** | **+10** | **+4** | **-4** |

**Step 2 — Apply Simpson's Rule for Area of TMD:**

Simpson's 1/3 rule (works because $n = 6$ is even):

$$\text{Area} = \frac{h}{3} \left[ (y_0 + y_6) + 4(y_1 + y_3 + y_5) + 2(y_2 + y_4) \right]$$

Where $h = 30° = \pi/6$ rad = $0.5236$ rad.

$$\text{Area} = \frac{0.5236}{3} \left[ (-4 + (-4)) + 4(4 + 12 + 4) + 2(10 + 10) \right]$$

$$= 0.1745 \left[ -8 + 4(20) + 2(20) \right] = 0.1745 \left[ -8 + 80 + 40 \right] = 0.1745 \times 112$$

$$\text{Area of TMD} = 19.55\ \text{kN·m·rad}$$

**Step 3 — Mean Turning Moment:**

$$T_{\text{mean}} = \frac{\text{Area}}{L} = \frac{19.55}{4\pi} = \frac{19.55}{12.566} = 1.556\ \text{kN·m}$$

**Step 4 — Power Developed:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 25.133\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$P = T_{\text{mean}} \times \omega = 1556 \times 25.133 = 39{,}107\ \text{W} \approx 39.11\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Fluctuation of Energy:**

Energy above $T_{\text{mean}}$:
| Point | 30° | 60° | 90° | 120° | 150° |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Net $T$ (kN·m) | 4 | 10 | 12 | 10 | 4 |
| Above $T_{\text{mean}}$ (kN·m) | 2.444 | 8.444 | 10.444 | 8.444 | 2.444 |

Energy below $T_{\text{mean}}$:
| Point | 0° | 180° |
|---|---|---|
| Net $T$ (kN·m) | -4 | -4 |
| Below $T_{\text{mean}}$ (kN·m) | 5.556 | 5.556 |

Apply Simpson's rule to the **above mean** areas (from 30° to 150°, $n=4$ even):

$$A_1 = \frac{0.5236}{3} \left[ (2.444 + 2.444) + 4(8.444) + 2(10.444) \right]$$

$$= 0.1745 \left[ 4.888 + 33.776 + 20.888 \right] = 0.1745 \times 59.552 = 10.39\ \text{kN·m·rad}$$

Below mean: From 0° to 30° (trapezoid):

$$A_2 = \frac{0.5236}{2}(5.556 + 2.444) = 0.2618 \times 8.0 = 2.094\ \text{kN·m·rad}$$

Total above mean: $A_1 + A_2/2$ (using proper partitioning):

$$\Delta E = \text{above} \approx 10.39\ \text{kJ} \quad \text{(largest area, taken as max surplus)}$$

**Step 6 — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy:**

Work per cycle $W = T_{\text{mean}} \times 4\pi = 1.556 \times 12.566 = 19.55\ \text{kJ}$

$$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{W} = \frac{10.39}{19.55} = 0.5315$$

(Note: This high $C_E$ arises because the net torque is small and the fluctuation is large — the engine has low effective driving torque.)

**Step 7 — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed:**

$$mk^2 = 300 \times (0.4)^2 = 300 \times 0.16 = 48\ \text{kg·m}^2$$

$$C_S = \frac{\Delta E}{mk^2 \cdot \omega^2} = \frac{10{,}390}{48 \times (25.133)^2} = \frac{10{,}390}{48 \times 631.66} = \frac{10{,}390}{30{,}320} = 0.3427$$

**Step 8 — Range of Speed:**

$$C_S = \frac{\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}}{\omega_{\text{mean}}}$$

$$\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min} = 0.3427 \times 25.133 = 8.61\ \text{rad/s}$$

Using $\omega_{\text{mean}} = (\omega_{\max} + \omega_{\min})/2$:

$$\omega_{\max} = \omega_{\text{mean}} + 4.305 = 29.44\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{\min} = \omega_{\text{mean}} - 4.305 = 20.83\ \text{rad/s}$$

Convert to rpm: $\omega = 2\pi N/60 \Rightarrow N = 60\omega/(2\pi) = 9.55\omega$

$$N_{\max} = 9.55 \times 29.44 = 281.2\ \text{rpm}$$

$$N_{\min} = 9.55 \times 20.83 = 198.9\ \text{rpm}$$

**Range** = $N_{\max} - N_{\min} = 281.2 - 198.9 = 82.3\ \text{rpm}$

---

#### **Model Solution to Q3(b):**

A typical TMD for a 4-stroke IC engine is constructed as follows:

1. **X-axis:** Crank angle $\theta$ in degrees (0° to 720° for one full cycle of 4-stroke).
2. **Y-axis:** Turning moment $T$ in N·m.
3. **Curve shape:**
   - **Suction stroke (0°–180°):** $T$ is **negative** (piston is being pulled down by crank).
   - **Compression stroke (180°–360°):** $T$ is **negative** (piston is being pushed up).
   - **Power/Expansion stroke (360°–540°):** $T$ is **largely positive** with a peak.
   - **Exhaust stroke (540°–720°):** $T$ is **negative** (piston is being pushed up).

4. **Mean Torque Line:** A horizontal line at $T_{\text{mean}}$ such that the area above equals the area below (algebraic areas are balanced).

5. **Fluctuation of Energy:** The **largest area enclosed between the TMD curve and the mean torque line** — geometrically representing how much energy must be supplied by the flywheel (during deficit) or absorbed by the flywheel (during surplus).

The areas above $T_{\text{mean}}$ represent **surplus energy** (the flywheel accelerates and stores this energy), and the areas below $T_{\text{mean}}$ represent **deficit energy** (the flywheel decelerates and releases this energy to keep the load moving). The **largest area** of either type is the **maximum fluctuation of energy $\Delta E$**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**Q4. (a)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke oil engine has a flywheel of mass **250 kg** with a radius of gyration of **0.35 m**. The turning moment diagram has areas above and below the mean torque line as **$A_1 = 4.2 \times 10^4\ \text{mm}^2$** and **$A_2 = 0.6 \times 10^4\ \text{mm}^2$** respectively. The scales are **$1\ \text{mm} = 80\ \text{N·m}$** and **$1\ \text{mm} = 4°$**. The engine speed is **360 rpm**. Find:
1. Fluctuation of energy
2. Coefficient of fluctuation of energy
3. Coefficient of fluctuation of speed
4. Range of speed

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the role of a **flywheel** in an IC engine. How does the TMD govern the design parameters of the flywheel? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q4(a):**

**Given:**
- $m = 250$ kg, $k = 0.35$ m
- $A_1 = 4.2 \times 10^4\ \text{mm}^2$ (above mean — surplus)
- $A_2 = 0.6 \times 10^4\ \text{mm}^2$ (below mean — deficit)
- Scale: 1 mm vertical = 80 N·m, 1 mm horizontal = 4°
- $N = 360$ rpm (4-stroke engine)

**Step 1 — Convert Areas to N·m (with angle in degrees):**

$$\Delta E_1 = A_1 \times 80\ \text{N·m/mm} \times 4°/\text{mm} = 4.2 \times 10^4 \times 320\ \text{N·m·deg}$$

$$= 1.344 \times 10^7\ \text{N·m·deg}$$

Convert degrees to radians: $\times \pi/180$:

$$\Delta E_1 = 1.344 \times 10^7 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 234{,}575\ \text{N·m} = 234.575\ \text{kJ}$$

$$\Delta E_2 = 0.6 \times 10^4 \times 320 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 33{,}510\ \text{N·m} = 33.51\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Fluctuation of Energy:**

The **larger area** governs the design:

$$\boxed{\Delta E = 234.575\ \text{kJ} = 2.346 \times 10^5\ \text{J}}$$

**Step 3 — Total Area and Work Done:**

Total area $= (4.2 + 0.6) \times 10^4 = 4.8 \times 10^4\ \text{mm}^2$

$$W = 4.8 \times 10^4 \times 320 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 268{,}083\ \text{N·m} = 268.08\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy:**

$$C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W} = \dfrac{234.575}{268.083} = 0.875$$

**Step 5 — Mean Angular Velocity:**

$$\omega_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 360}{60} = 37.70\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 6 — Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed:**

$$mk^2 = 250 \times (0.35)^2 = 250 \times 0.1225 = 30.625\ \text{kg·m}^2$$

$$C_S = \dfrac{\Delta E}{mk^2 \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}^2} = \dfrac{2.346 \times 10^5}{30.625 \times (37.70)^2} = \dfrac{2.346 \times 10^5}{30.625 \times 1421.29} = \dfrac{2.346 \times 10^5}{43{,}524}$$

$$C_S = 5.39$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout #1:**
> A value of $C_S > 1$ is **physically unrealistic**. This indicates the flywheel is **massively undersized** for the engine. In real practice, such a high $C_S$ would mean the engine cannot run smoothly. The students should **report the value as computed**, but also note that the design specification is impractical. KTU examiners look for the **computation skill**, not the physical plausibility, but a thoughtful remark may earn bonus appreciation marks.

**Step 7 — Range of Speed:**

$$\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min} = C_S \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}} = 5.39 \times 37.70 = 203.2\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{\max} = \omega_{\text{mean}} + 101.6 = 139.3\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{\min} = \omega_{\text{mean}} - 101.6 = -63.9\ \text{rad/s} \quad \text{(unphysical — confirms undersized flywheel)}$$

Convert to rpm: $N = 9.55\omega$

$$N_{\max} = 9.55 \times 139.3 = 1330.3\ \text{rpm}$$

$$N_{\min} = 9.55 \times (-63.9) = \text{(negative — physically stops the engine)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2 — Conversion Mistake:**
> Many students make conversion errors between **degrees and radians** when computing the work done from TMD area. **Always remember:**
> - 1 revolution = $360°$ = $2\pi$ rad
> - 1° = $\pi/180$ rad ≈ 0.01745 rad
> - **For 4-stroke:** Cycle angle = $4\pi$ rad = $720°$
> - **For 2-stroke:** Cycle angle = $2\pi$ rad = $360°$
>
> A common mistake is to divide the total area (in N·m·deg) directly by $4\pi$ **without** first converting degrees to radians. This gives a wrong answer by a factor of $\pi/180 \approx 0.01745$. **Always convert to SI base units before applying formulas.**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q4(b) — Role of Flywheel and TMD-Flywheel Linkage:**

**Block Diagram of Flywheel Function:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Engine Crank Shaft"] --> B["Driving Torque T_d"]
    B --> C["Net Turning Moment Net T = T_d - T_R"]
    C --> D["Turning Moment Diagram TMD"]
    D --> E["Identify Surplus and Deficit Areas"]
    E --> F["Calculate Fluctuation of Energy Delta E"]
    F --> G["Compute Flywheel Inertia mk squared"]
    G --> H["Sizing of Flywheel Mass and Diameter"]
    H --> I["Smoother Crankshaft Rotation"]
    I --> A
```

**Role of the Flywheel:**

1. **Energy Storage Reservoir:** During surplus energy periods (TMD above mean line), the flywheel **stores kinetic energy** by accelerating.
2. **Energy Release:** During deficit periods (TMD below mean line), the flywheel **releases stored energy** by decelerating.
3. **Speed Regulation:** It limits the **coefficient of fluctuation of speed ($C_S$)** to acceptable limits (typically 0.01–0.05).
4. **Load Smoothing:** It smooths out the **pulsating torque** delivered to a connected machine (e.g., a press, generator, rolling mill).
5. **Reduced Mechanical Stress:** It reduces vibration, shock loading, and wear on bearings, gears, and shafts.

**How TMD Governs Flywheel Design:**

- The **largest shaded area** in the TMD between the curve and the $T_{\text{mean}}$ line **directly determines $\Delta E$**.
- $\Delta E$ is then used with the chosen $C_S$ and mean speed $\omega_{\text{mean}}$ to compute the required $mk^2$.
- For rim-type flywheels, the diameter $D$ is then calculated using $D^2 = \dfrac{\Delta E \cdot g}{C_S \cdot \pi^2 \cdot N^2 \cdot k^2 \cdot n}$.
- A **highly fluctuating TMD** (large areas above and below mean) requires a **heavier or larger-radius flywheel**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 TMD**

- **TMD Definition:** Plot of net turning moment $T = T_{\text{fluid}} - T_{\text{resist}}$ versus crank angle $\theta$.
- **Cycle angle:** 4-stroke = $4\pi$ rad, 2-stroke = $2\pi$ rad.
- **Mean turning moment:** $T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{\text{Area of TMD}}{\text{Cycle angle}}$.
- **Work per cycle:** $W = T_{\text{mean}} \times L$ where $L = 4\pi$ (4-stroke) or $2\pi$ (2-stroke).
- **Fluctuation of energy $\Delta E$:** Largest area between TMD and $T_{\text{mean}}$ line (in N·m or J).
- **Coefficient of fluctuation of energy:** $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W}$ (dimensionless).
- **Coefficient of fluctuation of speed:** $C_S = \dfrac{\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}}{\omega_{\text{mean}}}$ (dimensionless).
- **Mean speed:** $\omega_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{\omega_{\max} + \omega_{\min}}{2}$.
- **Key identity:** $\Delta E = I \cdot C_S \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}^2$ where $I = m k^2$.
- **Flywheel inertia formula:** $m k^2 = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_S \cdot \omega_{\text{mean}}^2}$.
- **Rim-type flywheel diameter:** $D^2 = \dfrac{\Delta E \cdot g}{C_S \cdot \pi^2 \cdot N^2 \cdot k^2 \cdot n}$.
- **Typical $C_S$ values:** 0.01–0.05 for industrial engines, 0.05–0.10 for punching/pressing machinery.
- **Typical $C_E$ values:** 0.05–0.20.
- **Units conversion:** $1\ \text{HP} = 746\ \text{W}$, $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ ($N$ in rpm), $P = T\omega$.
- **Degree ↔ Radian conversion in TMD area:** Always multiply by $\pi/180$ to convert deg·N·m to rad·N·m (= Joules).
- **Geometric areas in TMD:** Use **Simpson's 1/3 rule** if ordinates are at equal intervals, or **trapezoidal rule** for irregular spacing.
- **Mean torque line:** Positioned such that the algebraic sum of areas above equals the algebraic sum of areas below it.
- **Final sanity checks:** $mk^2$ should be positive and physically realistic; $\omega_{\max} > \omega_{\min}$; $C_S < 1$ for a working engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Frequently Asked KTU Concepts (Quick Recall):**
> - *Why 4-stroke cycle length is $4\pi$ rad?* → Crankshaft completes **two revolutions** per working cycle.
> - *Why are areas in TMD expressed in N·m·deg or N·m·rad?* → Area = moment (N·m) × angle (deg or rad). The product is **energy (Joules)**.
> - *Why is $g$ used in rim-diameter formula?* → $g$ converts **weight** (N) to **mass** (kg) when $W$ is given in N.
> - *What is the role of Simpson's rule here?* → Numerical integration of TMD area when no algebraic equation of $T(\theta)$ is given.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flywheel in Different Applications Like IC Engines
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flywheel in Different Applications Like IC Engines

# ⚙️ FLYWHEEL IN DIFFERENT APPLICATIONS LIKE IC ENGINES

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 Anchor Concept:** This topic bridges **kinematics of mechanisms** (Module 1) with **force analysis in engines & machines** (Module 2). The flywheel is a **mechanical energy reservoir** — it absorbs energy during surplus periods and releases it during deficit periods, keeping the speed of the prime mover within practical limits.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Flywheel (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *flywheel* is a heavy rotating disc or rim mounted on the crankshaft of an IC engine (or other prime mover) which stores **kinetic energy** in the form of rotational inertia and, by virtue of its moment of inertia, smooths out the fluctuations of speed that arise due to the intermittent nature of the driving torque in a 4-stroke/2-stroke IC engine.

Mathematically, it functions as a **mechanical capacitor** — the stored energy at mean angular velocity $\omega$ is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

where $I$ = mass moment of inertia of the flywheel about the axis of rotation (in **kg·m²**), and $\omega$ = angular speed in **rad/s**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Pedal-Powered Toy Car

Imagine a child on a **bicycle** pedalling slowly down a flat road. When the child pushes the pedal (down-stroke), the wheel spins fast; when the pedal is on the up-stroke (no torque), the wheel keeps moving smoothly without the child. The **big wheel of the bicycle acts exactly as a flywheel**:

- **During the power stroke** → wheel absorbs energy → it speeds up a little.
- **During the idle stroke** → wheel returns energy → it slows down a little.
- The **total speed change is tiny** because the wheel is heavy.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** A flywheel is to a prime mover what a **capacitor** is to an electronic circuit — it stores energy during peaks and supplies it during valleys, only that the stored form is **kinetic**, not electrical.

---

### 1.3 Why Is a Flywheel Needed in an IC Engine?

In a single-cylinder 4-stroke IC engine:
- Only **one power stroke per two revolutions** (720° of crank rotation).
- The remaining 540° has **no useful torque**.
- Without a flywheel, the engine would jerk violently.

The flywheel keeps the speed within a tolerable band, defined by the **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed** ($C_s$).

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical IC Engine Value |
|----------|--------|-------------------------|
| Coefficient of fluctuation of speed | $C_s$ | **1/100 to 1/40** (1% – 2.5%) |
| Coefficient of fluctuation of energy | $C_E$ | depends on load |
| Energy fluctuation | $\Delta E$ | in Joules (J) |
| Mean angular velocity | $\omega$ | rad/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** $C_s$ and $C_E$ are **NOT the same** in general. $C_E = \Delta E / E$, while $C_s = (\omega_{max} - \omega_{min})/\omega$. For small $C_s$, $C_E \approx C_s$, but the exact relationship is **$C_E = C_s$ only if we use the linearised energy equation** $\Delta E = I\omega^2 C_s$.

---

### 1.4 Real-World IC Engine Applications

| Application | Role of Flywheel |
|-------------|------------------|
| **Single-cylinder petrol engine** (motorcycles, lawn-mowers) | Stores energy between 3 idle strokes and 1 power stroke. |
| **Multi-cylinder 4-stroke engine** (cars, trucks) | Smooths torque ripples between cylinders. |
| **Diesel generator set** | Maintains constant frequency (50 Hz / 60 Hz) of output. |
| **Punching / Shearing press** | Provides peak energy during the short impact stroke. |
| **Line shaft of factory** | Compensates peak loads of individual machines. |
| **Trolley / Tram wheel** | Stores braking energy for reuse. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Concept of Fluctuation of Energy

In any IC engine, the **crank-effort (or turning-moment) diagram** shows that the actual torque supplied by the gas is **not constant** around one cycle. Let the work done by the crank during a complete cycle be plotted against the crank angle.

- **Surplus Energy (ΔE₊):** Energy supplied by the gas *above* the mean resistance line (rectangle area).
- **Deficit Energy (ΔE₋):** Energy required by the load *below* the mean resistance line.
- **Maximum Fluctuation of Energy (ΔE):** The greatest difference between the energy supplied and the energy required, *read from the turning-moment diagram*. This is the single most important number for flywheel design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** The fluctuation of energy $\Delta E$ is read directly from the **turning-moment / crank-effort diagram** as the area enclosed between the T-θ curve and the mean resistance line. It is the **maximum surplus (or maximum deficit) area** between successive intersections.

---

### 2.2 Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_s$)

The ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed to the mean speed:

$$C_s = \frac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\omega_{mean}} = \frac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N_{mean}}$$

Typical values:
- **Electric motors / generators:** 1/200 to 1/100
- **Punching / Shearing presses:** 1/10 to 1/20
- **IC Engines (continuous load):** 1/50 to 1/60
- **IC Engines (intermittent load):** 1/80 to 1/100
- **Line shafts:** 1/20 to 1/40

---

### 2.3 Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$)

The ratio of maximum fluctuation of energy to the **energy stored in the flywheel** (at mean speed):

$$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{stored}} = \frac{\Delta E}{\frac{1}{2} I \omega_{mean}^2}$$

---

### 2.4 Exact Relation Between $C_s$ and $C_E$

Starting from the definition:
$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2)$$

Let $\omega_{max} = \omega + \delta$, $\omega_{min} = \omega - \delta$, where $\omega$ is mean and $\delta$ is the half-fluctuation.

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \left[ (\omega + \delta)^2 - (\omega - \delta)^2 \right] = \frac{1}{2} I (4 \omega \delta) = 2 I \omega \delta$$

Since $C_s = \dfrac{2\delta}{\omega}$, we get $\delta = \dfrac{C_s \omega}{2}$:

$$\Delta E = 2 I \omega \cdot \frac{C_s \omega}{2} = I \omega^2 C_s$$

Comparing with $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$:

$$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2} = \frac{I \omega^2 C_s}{\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2} = 2 C_s$$

> [!NOTE]
> **EXACT KTU result:** $\boxed{C_E = 2 C_s}$. For small $C_s$ (i.e., $C_s \le 0.1$), this is the universally used formula. Some textbooks give $C_E = C_s$ when they treat $\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2) \approx I\omega \Delta\omega$ with a slightly different convention — **always use the convention specified in the problem**.

---

### 2.5 Master Formula for Flywheel Design

From $\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$:

$$\boxed{I = \frac{\Delta E}{C_s \omega^2}}$$

This gives the required **mass moment of inertia** in kg·m². The next step (Section 3.4) converts this into the **rim geometry** (diameter and cross-section).

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABLE — KTU High-Yield Formulae (all units S.I.)**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Energy stored in flywheel | $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | Joules (J) |
| 2 | Max fluctuation of energy | $\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2)$ | From T-θ diagram |
| 3 | Simplified fluctuation | $\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$ | Linearised; widely used |
| 4 | Coefficient of fluctuation of speed | $C_s = \dfrac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\omega}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Coefficient of fluctuation of energy | $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{stored}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Exact link | $C_E = 2 C_s$ | Standard KTU result |
| 7 | Required moment of inertia | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_s \omega^2}$ | kg·m² |
| 8 | Rim mass (with $k = R/r$) | $M = \pi \rho \, b \, t \, (R^2 + r^2)$ | where $b$=width, $t$=thickness |
| 9 | Moment of inertia of rim | $I = M \cdot \dfrac{R^2 + r^2}{2}$ | For hollow rim |
| 10 | Rim (mean radius) | $R = \dfrac{D_{mean}}{2}$ | meters |
| 11 | Peripheral velocity | $v = \pi D_{mean} \cdot N / 60$ | m/s, limited to **30 m/s for cast iron**, **60 m/s for steel** |
| 12 | Crank effort | $T = P \cdot \tan \alpha$ | Inertia-torque method |
| 13 | Mean torque | $T_{mean} = \dfrac{\text{Work per cycle}}{4\pi}$ | (4-stroke, 2 revolutions) |
| 14 | Turning moment | $M_T = T \cdot r$ | N·m (or kgf·m) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** $\Delta E$ comes from the **turning-moment diagram** and is in units of **work** (N·m or J). It is **NOT** torque itself. The units trap catches many KTU students.

---

### 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why These Numbers Matter

1. **Generator sets:** Keeping $C_s$ small ensures the output frequency stays within ±1% of nominal (50/60 Hz), critical for grid stability.
2. **Automobile engines:** A smaller $C_s$ means smoother drivability and less vibration.
3. **Press flywheels:** These operate at **high** $C_s$ because the load is intermittent and a large $\Delta E$ is absorbed each stroke.
4. **Energy efficiency:** Heavier flywheels lose more energy to windage and friction, so design always balances smoothness against losses.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in a Rotating Disc

A flywheel of mass $M$ rotating about its axis with angular velocity $\omega$ has every mass element $dm$ at radius $r$ moving with linear speed $v = r\omega$.

Kinetic energy of the element: $dE = \frac{1}{2}(dm)(r\omega)^2 = \frac{1}{2} dm \, r^2 \omega^2$.

Integrating over the entire body:

$$E = \frac{1}{2}\omega^2 \int r^2 \, dm = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

**This is the master energy formula** — a direct consequence of the definition $I = \int r^2 dm$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Exact Link Between $C_s$ and $C_E$

**Given:** $\omega_{max} = \omega_m + \Delta\omega / 2$ and $\omega_{min} = \omega_m - \Delta\omega / 2$, where $\omega_m$ is the mean angular velocity and $\Delta\omega$ is the peak-to-peak fluctuation.

**Step 1:** Maximum fluctuation of energy:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2 \right)$$

**Step 2:** Factor as a difference of squares:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_{max} + \omega_{min} \right) \left( \omega_{max} - \omega_{min} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\omega_{max} + \omega_{min} = 2\omega_m$ and $\omega_{max} - \omega_{min} = \Delta\omega$:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \cdot 2 \omega_m \cdot \Delta\omega = I \omega_m \cdot \Delta\omega$$

**Step 4:** By definition, $C_s = \dfrac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\omega_m} = \dfrac{\Delta\omega}{\omega_m}$, hence $\Delta\omega = C_s \, \omega_m$.

**Step 5:** Substituting:

$$\Delta E = I \omega_m \cdot C_s \, \omega_m = I \omega_m^2 C_s$$

**Step 6:** Energy stored at mean speed is $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_m^2$. Therefore:

$$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{E} = \frac{I \omega_m^2 C_s}{\frac{1}{2} I \omega_m^2} = 2 C_s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End Result:** $C_E = 2 C_s$ (exact). KTU board problems almost always use this form. Some textbooks (Khurmi, Shigley) use a slightly different convention; **for KTU 2024, follow the derivation above.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flywheel Required Moment of Inertia

The flywheel must store enough kinetic energy to absorb $\Delta E$ without letting the speed fluctuate beyond $C_s$.

From the working formula $\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$, solve for $I$:

$$I = \frac{\Delta E}{C_s \omega^2}$$

Expressing $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$:

$$I = \frac{\Delta E}{C_s \left( \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \right)^2} = \frac{900 \, \Delta E}{C_s \pi^2 N^2} \approx \frac{91.16 \, \Delta E}{C_s N^2}$$

where $N$ is in **rpm** and $\Delta E$ in **J**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Rim Dimensions (Hollow Section)

In practice the flywheel is built as a **thin rim** of inner radius $r$, outer radius $R$, width $b$, with mass density $\rho$ of the material.

**Step 1 — Mass of the rim:**
$$M = \pi (R^2 - r^2) \, b \, \rho$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia** (annular disc, mass concentrated near rim):
$$I = \frac{M (R^2 + r^2)}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Practical simplification:** Since the rim is thin, $r \approx R \approx R_{mean}$, and:
$$I \approx M R_{mean}^2$$

**Step 4 — Combine with $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_s \omega^2}$:**
$$M R_{mean}^2 = \frac{\Delta E}{C_s \omega^2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for rim diameter** if $M$ is known, or for $M$ if $D_{mean}$ is known:

$$D_{mean} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta E}{M C_s \omega^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Peripheral speed limit** (safety against rim bursting):

$$v = \pi D_{mean} N / 60 \le v_{safe}$$

where $v_{safe}$ = **25–30 m/s for cast iron**, **40–60 m/s for forged steel**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Energy Fluctuation from Crank-Effort Diagram

For a single-cylinder 4-stroke engine with turning-moment diagram $T$ vs $\theta$:

- Plot the mean torque line $T_{mean} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{4\pi}$ (since the cycle is $4\pi$ rad for 4-stroke).
- Compute the cumulative energy deviation:

$$E_{excess}(\theta) = \int_{0}^{\theta} (T - T_{mean}) \, d\theta$$

- The maximum positive value is **ΔE₊**, the maximum negative is **ΔE₋**.
- The **greater of |ΔE₊| and |ΔE₋|** is the design **ΔE**.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for KTU Problems

| Given | Find | Strategy |
|-------|------|----------|
| Turning-moment diagram, $N$, $C_s$ | $I$, $M$, $D$ | 1. Read $\Delta E$ from T-θ diagram. 2. $I = \Delta E/(C_s \omega^2)$. 3. $M = I/R^2$ for rim. |
| $N$, $I$, $C_s$ | $\Delta E$ | $\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$ |
| $I$, $C_s$ | $C_E$ | $C_E = 2 C_s$ |
| Press: $W$ (work), $n$ (strokes/min), $C_s$ | $I$ | $\Delta E = W$, $I = W/(C_s \omega^2)$ |
| Line shaft: $P$ (mean power), torque fluctuation | $I$ | $\Delta E = \text{area between T-θ and Tmean}$ |

---

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 pattern):** A single-cylinder 4-stroke IC engine has a turning-moment diagram. The maximum fluctuation of energy, as read from the diagram, is **3500 J**. The engine runs at **240 rpm** with a permissible fluctuation of speed of **1.5%**. Find (a) the moment of inertia of the flywheel rim, and (b) the mass of the rim if its mean radius is **0.6 m**.

**Solution:**

**Given:** $\Delta E = 3500$ J, $N = 240$ rpm, $C_s = 1.5/100 = 0.015$, $R = 0.6$ m.

**(a) Moment of inertia:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 8\pi = 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$I = \frac{\Delta E}{C_s \omega^2} = \frac{3500}{0.015 \times (25.133)^2} = \frac{3500}{0.015 \times 631.65} = \frac{3500}{9.475}$$

$$I \approx 369.4 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**(b) Mass of the rim:**

Using $I = M R^2$:

$$M = \frac{I}{R^2} = \frac{369.4}{(0.6)^2} = \frac{369.4}{0.36} \approx 1026 \text{ kg}$$

**Cross-check (peripheral speed):**

$$v = \pi D_{mean} N / 60 = \pi \times 1.2 \times 240 / 60 \approx 15.08 \text{ m/s}$$

This is well within the safe limit for cast iron (≤ 30 m/s). ✔

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU ESE answers**, always quote the value of $\omega$ explicitly, state the formula, and box the final numerical answer with units.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Functional Block Diagram of a Flywheel System in an IC Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Combustion in Cylinder] --> B[Crank Effort Diagram T vs theta]
    B --> C[Turning Moment M_T = T times r]
    C --> D{Torque Compare}
    D -->|T greater than Tmean| E[Surplus Energy Delta E plus]
    D -->|T less than Tmean| F[Deficit Energy Delta E minus]
    E --> G[Flywheel Stores Kinetic Energy]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Speed fluctuates omega max to omega min]
    H --> I{Check Cs less than limit}
    I -->|Yes| J[Smooth Output to Load]
    I -->|No| K[Re-Design Flywheel Increase I or D]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid — Energy Flow inside the Flywheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Surplus Power Stroke] -->|delta E plus| B[Flywheel]
    B -->|delta E minus| C[Deficit Stroke]
    B -->|E stored equals half I omega squared| D[Kinetic Reservoir]
    D -->|Absorbed during peak| A
    D -->|Released during valley| C
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid — Crank-Effort Diagram with Mean Line

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Start of cycle theta equals 0] --> B[T equals 0 at TDC]
    B --> C[T rises with combustion peak]
    C --> D[T crosses Tmean upward]
    D --> E[T peaks at maximum Tmax]
    E --> F[T crosses Tmean downward]
    F --> G[T drops below Tmean at idle strokes]
    G --> H[T returns to 0 at end of cycle]
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Schematic:** The crank-effort diagram is a closed loop around the **mean resistance line** $T_{mean} = W_{net}/(4\pi)$. The largest loop (max area) gives $\Delta E$ for design.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid — Flywheel Rim Cross-Section (Annular)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Outer Radius R] --> B[Rim Width b]
    B --> C[Inner Radius r]
    C --> D[Thickness t in axial direction]
    D --> E[Hub and Arms connect to Crank shaft]
```

**Geometric relationships for the rim:**

$$A_{rim} = \pi(R^2 - r^2) \quad \text{(cross-sectional area perpendicular to rotation)}$$

$$V_{rim} = A_{rim} \times b = \pi b t (R - r) \quad \text{(assuming thin web of thickness } t\text{)}$$

$$M_{rim} = \rho V_{rim} = \rho \pi b t (R - r)$$

---

### 4.5 Mermaid — Decision Tree for $C_s$ Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Cs] --> B{Application Type}
    B -->|Generator Set| C[Cs equals 1 by 200 to 1 by 100]
    B -->|IC Engine continuous| D[Cs equals 1 by 50]
    B -->|IC Engine intermittent| E[Cs equals 1 by 80 to 1 by 100]
    B -->|Punching Press| F[Cs equals 1 by 10 to 1 by 20]
    B -->|Line Shaft| G[Cs equals 1 by 20 to 1 by 40]
    C --> H[Use Cs to find I]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute M and Dmean]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "coefficient of fluctuation of speed" and "coefficient of fluctuation of energy" as applied to a flywheel.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **coefficient of fluctuation of speed** ($C_s$) is defined as the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed to the mean speed of the flywheel.
>
> $$C_s = \frac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\omega_{mean}} = \frac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N_{mean}}$$
>
> The **coefficient of fluctuation of energy** ($C_E$) is the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of energy to the energy stored in the flywheel at the mean speed.
>
> $$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{\frac{1}{2} I \omega_{mean}^2}$$
>
> For a flywheel, the exact relation is $C_E = 2 C_s$. $C_s$ is dimensionless and typically ranges from 1/200 (precise machinery) to 1/10 (punching presses).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is a flywheel essential in a single-cylinder 4-stroke IC engine, but less critical in a multi-cylinder engine?**

**Model Answer:**

> In a single-cylinder 4-stroke IC engine, only **one power stroke occurs in two revolutions (720°)**. The remaining 540° has no driving torque, which would cause severe speed fluctuations without a flywheel.
>
> In a multi-cylinder engine (e.g., 4-cylinder 4-stroke), the power strokes are **evenly spaced every 180°** of crank rotation. The torque produced is much more uniform because the firing order is staggered, hence the speed fluctuation is naturally small and a **smaller, lighter flywheel** suffices.
>
> Thus, a flywheel is **essential** in single-cylinder engines for smoothing, while in multi-cylinder engines it is mainly for **fine smoothing and storing cranking energy**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, modified for 2024 scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relation between the **coefficient of fluctuation of energy ($C_E$)** and the **coefficient of fluctuation of speed ($C_s$)** of a flywheel.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-stroke single-cylinder IC engine develops **8 kW** at **300 rpm**. The turning-moment diagram has a **maximum fluctuation of energy of 2800 J**. If the coefficient of fluctuation of speed is to be limited to **0.02**, determine:
   (i) the moment of inertia of the flywheel,
   (ii) the mass of the flywheel rim if its mean diameter is **1.0 m**,
   (iii) the cross-sectional area of the rim if its width-to-thickness ratio is **4:1**, and the rim is made of cast iron with density **7200 kg/m³**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]**

*Step 1:* Define the speeds. Let $\omega$ be the mean angular velocity. The maximum and minimum speeds are:

$$\omega_{max} = \omega + \frac{\Delta\omega}{2}, \qquad \omega_{min} = \omega - \frac{\Delta\omega}{2}$$

*Step 2:* Maximum fluctuation of energy is the change in kinetic energy between these speeds:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2 \right)$$

*Step 3:* Apply $a^2 - b^2 = (a+b)(a-b)$:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_{max} + \omega_{min} \right) \left( \omega_{max} - \omega_{min} \right)$$

*Step 4:* Substitute $\omega_{max} + \omega_{min} = 2\omega$ and $\omega_{max} - \omega_{min} = \Delta\omega$:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \cdot 2\omega \cdot \Delta\omega = I \omega \Delta\omega$$

*Step 5:* By definition of $C_s$:

$$C_s = \frac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\omega} = \frac{\Delta\omega}{\omega} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta\omega = C_s \omega$$

*Step 6:* Substituting:

$$\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$$

*Step 7:* By definition of $C_E$ and using $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$:

$$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{stored}} = \frac{I \omega^2 C_s}{\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2} = 2 C_s$$

$$\boxed{C_E = 2 C_s}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

*Given:* $P = 8$ kW, $N = 300$ rpm, $\Delta E = 2800$ J, $C_s = 0.02$, $D_{mean} = 1.0$ m, $b/t = 4$, $\rho = 7200$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Compute $\omega$:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi \approx 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Moment of inertia:**

$$I = \frac{\Delta E}{C_s \omega^2} = \frac{2800}{0.02 \times (31.416)^2} = \frac{2800}{0.02 \times 986.96} = \frac{2800}{19.739}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 141.86 \text{ kg·m}^2}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Mass of the rim** (using $I = M R^2$ for a thin rim, $R = 0.5$ m):

$$M = \frac{I}{R^2} = \frac{141.86}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{141.86}{0.25} \approx 567.4 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Cross-sectional area:**

Volume of rim: $V = M / \rho = 567.4 / 7200 \approx 0.0788$ m³.

With $b = 4t$, the rim is approximated as a thin ring of cross-section $b \times t$ and mean circumference $\pi D_{mean}$:

$$V = \pi D_{mean} \cdot b \cdot t = \pi \times 1.0 \times 4t \times t = 4\pi t^2$$

Setting equal to $0.0788$:

$$t^2 = \frac{0.0788}{4\pi} = \frac{0.0788}{12.566} = 0.00627 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = 0.0792 \text{ m}$$

$$b = 4t = 0.317 \text{ m}$$

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = b \cdot t = 0.317 \times 0.0792 \approx 0.0251 \text{ m}^2 = 251 \text{ cm}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Peripheral speed $= \pi D_{mean} N / 60 = \pi \times 1 \times 300/60 = 15.71$ m/s. Safe for cast iron. ✔

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the construction of a **flywheel rim** and derive the expression for the **mass moment of inertia of a thin hollow rim**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A punching press makes **25 working strokes per minute**. The total work done per stroke is **12,000 J**, of which **80% is supplied by the flywheel**. The press runs at a mean speed of **20 rpm** and the coefficient of fluctuation of speed is **0.10**. Find the moment of inertia and the mass of the flywheel rim if its mean diameter is **1.5 m**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Construction and Inertia Derivation [7 Marks]**

A flywheel rim is the **outer ring** of the flywheel, which carries the bulk of the rotating mass. The hub and arms (spokes) are designed only to connect the rim to the crankshaft and are **deliberately kept light** so that the moment of inertia is maximised for a given mass.

For a **thin hollow rim** of inner radius $r$, outer radius $R$, width $b$, and density $\rho$:

**Step 1:** Mass of the rim:

$$M = \pi(R^2 - r^2)\, b\, \rho$$

**Step 2:** A thin annular element at radius $x$, thickness $dx$, has mass $dm = 2\pi x \cdot b \cdot \rho \cdot dx$.

**Step 3:** Moment of inertia:

$$I = \int_{r}^{R} x^2 \, dm = \int_{r}^{R} x^2 \cdot (2\pi x b \rho) \, dx = 2\pi b \rho \int_{r}^{R} x^3 \, dx$$

$$I = 2\pi b \rho \left[ \frac{x^4}{4} \right]_{r}^{R} = \frac{\pi b \rho}{2} (R^4 - r^4)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $R^4 - r^4 = (R^2 - r^2)(R^2 + r^2)$ and $M = \pi b \rho (R^2 - r^2)$:

$$I = \frac{M (R^2 + r^2)}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Thin-rim limit:** If $r \to R \approx R_{mean}$:

$$\boxed{I \approx M R_{mean}^2}$$

This shows the design principle: **place the mass far from the axis** to maximise $I$ for a given $M$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

*Given:* $n = 25$ strokes/min, $W_{stroke} = 12000$ J, $W_{flywheel} = 0.8 \times 12000 = 9600$ J, $N = 20$ rpm, $C_s = 0.10$, $D_{mean} = 1.5$ m.

**Step 1 — Mean angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 20}{60} = \frac{2\pi}{3} \approx 2.094 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum fluctuation of energy** = work done by the flywheel per stroke:

$$\Delta E = W_{flywheel} = 9600 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Moment of inertia:**

$$I = \frac{\Delta E}{C_s \omega^2} = \frac{9600}{0.10 \times (2.094)^2} = \frac{9600}{0.10 \times 4.386} = \frac{9600}{0.4386}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 21{,}888 \text{ kg·m}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of the rim** (using $I = M R^2$, with $R = 0.75$ m):

$$M = \frac{I}{R^2} = \frac{21{,}888}{(0.75)^2} = \frac{21{,}888}{0.5625} \approx 38{,}912 \text{ kg}$$

This very large mass confirms that punching-press flywheels are **massive** compared with engine flywheels — typically several tonnes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Peripheral-speed check:** $v = \pi \times 1.5 \times 20 / 60 \approx 1.57$ m/s — extremely low. This is normal for a slow press; steel rim of $v_{safe} = 60$ m/s is over-engineered. ✔

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert $N$ from **rpm to rad/s** before computing $\omega$ — loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $\Delta E$ with $T$:** $\Delta E$ is **work/area**, not torque. The numerical value is read from a **diagram in N·m (per radian of crank angle)**, then converted to **Joules** by multiplying by the appropriate angle span.
> 3. **Using $C_E = C_s$ instead of $C_E = 2 C_s$:** This is a board-favourite trick. Always show the derivation and the final boxed relation.
> 4. **Forgetting to use $D_{mean}$, not $D_{outer}$** for rim calculations.
> 5. **Skipping the peripheral-speed check:** Examiners explicitly look for $v \le 30$ m/s (cast iron) or $60$ m/s (steel). Omitting this is a **deductable** in Part B.

---

### 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-density revision checklist — KTU Module 2, Flywheel in IC Engines:**

- ☐ **Flywheel** = energy reservoir that smooths torque fluctuations in prime movers.
- ☐ **Energy stored:** $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$.
- ☐ **Maximum fluctuation of energy ($\Delta E$):** Largest area between the turning-moment curve and the **mean torque line** in the T-θ diagram.
- ☐ **Coefficient of fluctuation of speed:** $C_s = (\omega_{max} - \omega_{min})/\omega$ — dimensionless.
- ☐ **Coefficient of fluctuation of energy:** $C_E = \Delta E / E_{stored}$ — dimensionless.
- ☐ **EXACT relation (board-standard):** $\boxed{C_E = 2 C_s}$.
- ☐ **Working formula:** $\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$.
- ☐ **Required moment of inertia:** $I = \Delta E / (C_s \omega^2)$.
- ☐ **Rim mass moment:** $I = M R^2$ (thin-rim approximation).
- ☐ **Rim inertia exact:** $I = M(R^2 + r^2)/2$ for a hollow annular rim.
- ☐ **Peripheral speed limit:** $v = \pi D_{mean} N / 60 \le 30$ m/s (cast iron) or $\le 60$ m/s (steel).
- ☐ **Typical $C_s$ values:** generators 1/200, IC engines 1/50–1/100, presses 1/10–1/20, line shafts 1/20–1/40.
- ☐ **Single-cylinder 4-stroke:** One power stroke per 720° → biggest need for flywheel.
- ☐ **Multi-cylinder engine:** Power strokes overlap → smaller flywheel suffices.
- ☐ **Punching press:** Flywheel is the **primary** energy source; engine only tops up losses.
- ☐ **Unit conversions:** 1 kgf·m = 9.81 N·m ; 1 kWh = $3.6 \times 10^6$ J; $\omega$ (rad/s) = $2\pi N/60$ (with N in rpm).
- ☐ **Mean torque for 4-stroke:** $T_{mean} = W_{cycle} / (4\pi)$.
- ☐ **Mean torque for 2-stroke:** $T_{mean} = W_{cycle} / (2\pi)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Punching Press - Numerical Examples - Gyroscopics: Gyroscopic Couple and Precessional Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Punching Press - Numerical Examples - Gyroscopics: Gyroscopic Couple and Precessional Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Gyroscopic Couple — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gyroscopic Couple (C)** is the *couple* acting on a rotating body (a gyroscope or a rotor) whose axis of spin is forced to change direction. Mathematically, it is the *time rate of change* of the angular momentum vector of the spinning mass about its instantaneous axis of rotation.

$$
\vec{C} \;=\; \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt} \;=\; I\,\vec{\omega}\,\times\,\vec{\omega_p}
$$

where
- $I$ = Mass moment of inertia of the rotor about its spin axis (kg·m²)
- $\vec{\omega}$ = Angular velocity of spin (rad/s)
- $\vec{\omega_p}$ = Angular velocity of precession (rad/s) — the rate at which the spin axis itself turns in space

## 1.2 Precessional Motion — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Precessional Motion** is the *steady rotational motion of the axis of spin* of a gyroscope about a second axis (the precession axis), produced when an external couple is applied about that second axis. Precession is *not* a wobble — it is a smooth, conical sweep of the spin axis in space.

## 1.3 The Punching Press — Application Context

A **punching press** is a machine tool used to shear holes in sheet metal. It uses a heavy **flywheel–crankshaft–connecting rod–ram (punch)** mechanism. The crankshaft rotates at a high steady speed; at every revolution (or every few revolutions), the descending punch meets the workpiece and is **suddenly arrested** by the metal's resistance.

This sudden resistance is the source of the *gyroscopic disturbance* studied in this topic. While the punch is being resisted, the **axis of the spinning crankshaft and flywheel is forced to "swing"** in space — a textbook example of precessional motion.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Bicycle Wheel Trick

> [!TIP]
> **Hold a spinning bicycle wheel by both ends of its axle.** Tilt the axle to the right or left — you will feel a strong, surprising *twisting force* trying to rotate the axle in a direction *perpendicular* to the tilt. That perpendicular twist is the **gyroscopic couple**. The way the axle "precesses" around as you move it is the **precessional motion**. A punching press behaves in the same way: the spinning flywheel resists any attempt to tip its axis, and that resistance manifests as the gyroscopic couple on the press frame.

## 1.5 Standard KTU Symbols & Units

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Mass moment of inertia | $I$ | kg·m² |
| Spin angular velocity | $\omega$ | rad/s |
| Precession angular velocity | $\omega_p$ | rad/s |
| Gyroscopic couple | $C$ | N·m |
| Speed in rpm | $N$ | rpm |
| Time of punching | $t$ | s |

The conversion constant to remember:

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vector Origin of the Gyroscopic Couple

A spinning rotor carries an **angular momentum vector** $\vec{H}$ that lies along the spin axis with magnitude

$$
H \;=\; I\,\omega
$$
