If an external influence forces this axis to turn in space at angular velocity $\vec{\omega_p}$, the *tip* of the $\vec{H}$ vector traces a circle. The *time rate of change* of $\vec{H}$ gives the gyroscopic couple:

$$
\vec{C} \;=\; \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt} \;=\; I\,\vec{\omega} \times \vec{\omega_p}
$$

Because the magnitudes are constants and the angle between $\vec{\omega}$ and $\vec{\omega_p}$ is **90°**, the magnitude simplifies to the KTU-favourite product formula:

$$
\boxed{\;C \;=\; I\,\omega\,\omega_p\;}
$$

## 2.2 Why the Punching Press Produces Precession

Step-by-step logic:

- The flywheel–crankshaft assembly spins steadily at $\omega$ about its **own shaft axis** (the spin axis).
- During the punching stroke (lasting $t$ seconds), the punch meets the workpiece and is suddenly slowed.
- The line of action of the **resistive force** from the workpiece passes through the punch, *perpendicular* to the spin axis.
- This force produces a **moment about an axis perpendicular to the spin axis** — i.e., it forces the spin axis to rotate (precess).
- The **magnitude** of the forced rotation rate of the spin axis is $\omega_p$, measured in rad/s.
- The **gyroscopic couple** $C = I\omega\omega_p$ therefore acts on the **frame** of the press, attempting to tilt it sideways.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in Punching Press |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Spin speed in rad/s | From rpm |
| 2 | $\omega_p = \dfrac{2\pi}{t}$ or $\dfrac{\theta}{t}$ | Precession speed in rad/s | $t$ = time for one crank turn during punching |
| 3 | $C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p$ | Gyroscopic couple (N·m) | Magnitude |
| 4 | $C_r = -C$ | Reactive couple | Equal & opposite to applied C |
| 5 | $T = C$ | Torque on frame = couple | Force on bearings |
| 6 | $F = \dfrac{C}{L}$ | Bearing reaction force | $L$ = distance between bearings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU board problems, the most common assumption is that **one complete revolution of the crank takes place during the punching time $t$**. This means the precession angle per punching stroke is $\theta = 2\pi$ rad, giving $\omega_p = 2\pi/t$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Press-frame design**: Knowing $C$ allows designers to size the frame, bolts, and bearings so the press does not "walk" or topple sideways during punching.
- **Vibration and noise reduction**: Reactive couples produce vibrations; balancing them improves operator safety and machine life.
- **Foundation design**: Civil engineers use $C$ values to size the machine foundation.
- **Aerospace & Naval analog**: The same $C = I\omega\omega_p$ governs ship-turning, aircraft-rolling, and gyroscope-instrument drift.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation — From First Principles to $C = I\omega\omega_p$

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**

Let the spin axis be **horizontal** (X-axis), with the rotor spinning at angular velocity $\vec{\omega} = \omega\,\hat{i}$. The applied couple from the punch resistance causes the spin axis to turn about a **vertical** axis (Z-axis) at angular velocity $\vec{\omega_p} = \omega_p\,\hat{k}$.

**Step 2 — Angular momentum vector.**

$$
\vec{H} \;=\; I\,\vec{\omega} \;=\; I\,\omega\,\hat{i}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the time derivative of $\vec{H}$ as the spin axis precesses.**

Because $\hat{i}$ rotates with the spin axis (which itself rotates at $\omega_p$ about Z), the unit vector $\hat{i}$ changes in time as

$$
\frac{d\hat{i}}{dt} \;=\; \vec{\omega_p} \times \hat{i} \;=\; \omega_p\,\hat{k} \times \hat{i} \;=\; \omega_p\,\hat{j}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the product rule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{C} \;=\; \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{d}{dt}(I\omega\,\hat{i})
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $I$ and $\omega$ are constant in magnitude, only $\hat{i}$ varies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{C} \;=\; I\,\omega \left(\frac{d\hat{i}}{dt}\right) \;=\; I\,\omega\left(\omega_p\,\hat{j}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude.**

$$
\boxed{\;C \;=\; I\,\omega\,\omega_p\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The direction $\hat{j}$ is perpendicular to **both** $\hat{i}$ (spin) and $\hat{k}$ (precession). By the right-hand rule, this is the direction the gyroscopic couple *acts on the spin axis*. The *reactive* couple on the frame is $-\hat{j}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Basic Gyroscopic Couple

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**Problem Statement:**
A horizontal crankshaft of a punching press runs at **240 rpm**. The rotating parts (flywheel + crankshaft) have a mass moment of inertia of **30 kg·m²** about the spin axis. The punching operation on a metal plate takes **0.5 seconds**, during which the crank turns through one complete revolution. Determine:
(a) The spin angular velocity $\omega$
(b) The precession angular velocity $\omega_p$
(c) The gyroscopic couple $C$
(d) The reactive couple $C_r$

### Solution

**Given:**
- $N = 240$ rpm
- $I = 30$ kg·m²
- $t = 0.5$ s (one revolution during punching)
- $\theta = 2\pi$ rad

**Part (a) — Spin angular velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega \;=\; 8\pi \;\approx\; 25.133 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Precession angular velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_p \;=\; \frac{\theta}{t} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{0.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_p \;=\; 4\pi \;\approx\; 12.566 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Gyroscopic couple:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \;=\; I\,\omega\,\omega_p \;=\; 30 \times 25.133 \times 12.566
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \;=\; 9475.5 \text{ N·m} \;\approx\; 9.48 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (d) — Reactive couple:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_r \;=\; -C \;=\; -9.48 \text{ kN·m (opposite direction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Validation Check:** Whenever you see "spin in rad/s × precession in rad/s × I", the unit must come out as **kg·m²·rad²/s² = kg·m²/s² = N·m**. This is a quick way to confirm you have used the correct formula.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Direction of Sway of the Press

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Problem Statement:**
An electric motor running at **1200 rpm** drives a vertical-shaft punching press. The moment of inertia of the rotating system about the spin axis is **5 kg·m²**. The punching time is **0.1 s** per hole and the crank completes one revolution during the punching stroke. Determine:
(a) The gyroscopic couple
(b) The direction in which the press will tend to sway

### Solution

**Given:**
- $N = 1200$ rpm
- $I = 5$ kg·m²
- $t = 0.1$ s

**Part (a) — Compute the couple:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} \;=\; 40\pi \;\approx\; 125.66 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_p \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{0.1} \;=\; 20\pi \;\approx\; 62.83 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \;=\; I\,\omega\,\omega_p \;=\; 5 \times 125.66 \times 62.83
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \;=\; 39{,}478 \text{ N·m} \;\approx\; 39.48 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Direction of sway (Right-Hand Rule Analysis):**

Set up coordinate frame at the motor:
- $\vec{\omega}$ points **upward** along the vertical shaft (take it as $+\hat{k}$, assuming spin is anticlockwise viewed from above).
- $\vec{\omega_p}$ is the rate of tilt of the spin axis due to the resistive force from the punch; let it be along $+\hat{i}$ (East, say).
- Gyroscopic couple vector on the rotor:

$$
\vec{C} \;=\; I\,\vec{\omega}\times\vec{\omega_p} \;=\; I\omega\omega_p\,(\hat{k}\times\hat{i}) \;=\; I\omega\omega_p\,\hat{j}
$$

$\hat{j}$ points **North**. Therefore the **reactive couple** $C_r$ on the **press frame** points **South**.

> The press frame will try to sway such that its top tilts toward the **South** (assuming the punch resistance comes from the East). In practice, the entire press "rocks" or "walks" in this direction unless bolted down.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Bearing Reaction Force on Frame

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**Problem Statement:**
A horizontal-shaft punching press has its flywheel–crankshaft rotating at **360 rpm**. The moment of inertia of the rotating mass about the spin axis is **20 kg·m²**. The punching time is **1/3 second** and the crank completes one revolution during the punch. The bearings supporting the crankshaft are spaced **1.2 m apart**. Determine:
(a) The gyroscopic couple
(b) The magnitude of the reaction force at each bearing due to this couple

### Solution

**Given:**
- $N = 360$ rpm
- $I = 20$ kg·m²
- $t = 1/3$ s
- $L = 1.2$ m (distance between bearings)

**Part (a) — Spin and precession velocities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 360}{60} \;=\; 12\pi \;\approx\; 37.70 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_p \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{1/3} \;=\; 6\pi \;\approx\; 18.85 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gyroscopic couple:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \;=\; I\,\omega\,\omega_p \;=\; 20 \times 37.70 \times 18.85
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \;=\; 14{,}210 \text{ N·m} \;\approx\; 14.21 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Bearing reaction force:**

The couple $C$ acts as a couple about the shaft centre. The two bearings must provide equal and opposite vertical reactions $F$ separated by distance $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \;=\; F \times L \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; F \;=\; \frac{C}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \;=\; \frac{14{,}210}{1.2} \;\approx\; 11{,}842 \text{ N} \;\approx\; 11.84 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each bearing experiences **≈ 11.84 kN** lateral load due to the gyroscopic effect alone — *on top of* the normal radial loads. This is why punching-press foundations must be massive.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Three Examples

| Quantity | Example 1 | Example 2 | Example 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ (rpm) | 240 | 1200 | 360 |
| $I$ (kg·m²) | 30 | 5 | 20 |
| $t$ (s) | 0.5 | 0.1 | 1/3 |
| $\omega$ (rad/s) | 25.13 | 125.66 | 37.70 |
| $\omega_p$ (rad/s) | 12.57 | 62.83 | 18.85 |
| $C$ (N·m) | 9475.5 | 39 478 | 14 210 |
| Extra output | Reactive direction | Direction of sway | Bearing force 11.84 kN |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Gyroscopic Effect in a Punching Press

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input AC Supply] --> B[Electric Motor]
    B --> C[Belt or Gear Drive]
    C --> D[Flywheel Coupled to Crankshaft]
    D --> E[Crank Pin Rotates in Circle]
    E --> F[Connecting Rod Translates to Ram]
    F --> G[Punch Descends Onto Workpiece]
    G --> H[Resistive Force From Metal Plate]
    H --> I[Force Acts Perpendicular to Spin Axis]
    I --> J[Spin Axis Forced to Precess]
    J --> K[Precession Angular Velocity omega_p]
    D --> L[Mass Moment of Inertia I]
    L --> M[Spin Angular Velocity omega]
    M --> N[Compute Gyroscopic Couple C equals I omega omega_p]
    K --> N
    N --> O[Reactive Couple Cr on Press Frame]
    O --> P[Frame Tends to Sway or Tilt]
    P --> Q[Reaction Force at Bearings F equals C over L]
    Q --> R[Foundation Design and Bolt Sizing]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Vector Geometry Diagram — Spin × Precession = Couple

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Spin Axis omega along X] --> B[Cross Product with omega_p along Z]
    B --> C[Resultant Couple Vector C along Y]
    D[Reactive Couple on Frame minus C along negative Y] --> E[Frame Tilts in negative Y direction]
    A --> F[Right Hand Rule: Fingers curl from omega to omega_p]
    F --> C
```

## 4.3 Textual Schematic — The Three Axes in 3-D

Picture a three-dimensional right-handed coordinate frame fixed at the centre of the spinning rotor:

| Axis | Physical Meaning in Punching Press |
|---|---|
| X-axis ($\hat{i}$) | Spin axis (crankshaft centreline) — direction of $\vec{\omega}$ |
| Y-axis ($\hat{j}$) | Direction of gyroscopic couple $\vec{C} = I\,\vec{\omega}\times\vec{\omega_p}$ |
| Z-axis ($\hat{k}$) | Direction of precession $\vec{\omega_p}$ (tilting of the spin axis) |

```
                 Z (Precession)
                  ↑
                  |
                  |
                  |
                  |
                  +--------→  Y (Couple C direction)
                 /
                /
               ↙
              X (Spin axis ω)
```

## 4.4 Physical Block Diagram — Forces on the Press Frame During a Punch

```
       Press Frame
       ┌─────────────────┐
       │   Flywheel ↻ω   │←←←←  Reactive Couple C_r (tilts frame)
       │   ┌─────────┐   │
       │   │ Crank+  │   │
       │   │ Pin      │   │────→ Spin axis (horizontal)
       │   └────┬────┘   │
       │        │        │
       │   Connecting Rod│
       │        │        │
       │   ┌────▼────┐   │
       │   │  Punch  │   │↓↓↓↓ Resistive force from metal plate
       │   └─────────┘   │
       └─────────────────┘
              ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓
          Foundation (must resist F = C/L)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Set B]**
> **"Define gyroscopic couple and precessional motion. State the formula relating them to the mass moment of inertia of the rotor."**

**Model Answer:**
Gyroscopic couple is the rate of change of angular momentum of a spinning rotor when the direction of its spin axis is forced to change. Precessional motion is the smooth conical sweep of the spin axis in space at angular velocity $\omega_p$. The relation is

$$
\boxed{C \;=\; I\,\omega\,\omega_p}
$$

where $I$ = mass moment of inertia (kg·m²), $\omega$ = spin speed (rad/s), $\omega_p$ = precession speed (rad/s).

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Set A]**
> **"Why does a punching press experience a gyroscopic couple during the punching stroke?"**

**Model Answer:**
During the punching stroke, the descending punch meets the metal plate and is suddenly resisted. This resistive force acts perpendicular to the spinning crankshaft–flywheel axis, forcing the spin axis to precess. Because the rotor carries angular momentum $H = I\omega$ along its spin axis, the change in direction of $\vec{H}$ manifests as a couple $C = I\omega\omega_p$ on the rotor (and an equal & opposite reactive couple on the press frame).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

A horizontal-shaft punching press operates at **300 rpm**. The rotating parts (flywheel + crankshaft) have a mass moment of inertia of **40 kg·m²** about the spin axis. During punching, the crank completes **one full revolution in 0.4 seconds**.

**(a)** Determine the spin angular velocity, precession angular velocity, and the **magnitude of the gyroscopic couple**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the two main bearings of the crankshaft are **1.5 m apart**, determine the **magnitude of the reaction force at each bearing** caused by the gyroscopic couple. Also describe, with the aid of the right-hand rule, the **direction in which the press frame tends to sway**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Magnitude of Gyroscopic Couple**

Given: $N = 300$ rpm, $I = 40$ kg·m², $t = 0.4$ s.

**Step 1: Spin angular velocity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} \;=\; 10\pi \;\approx\; 31.416 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Precession angular velocity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_p \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{t} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{0.4} \;=\; 5\pi \;\approx\; 15.708 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Gyroscopic couple.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \;=\; I\,\omega\,\omega_p \;=\; 40 \times 31.416 \times 15.708
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \;=\; 19{,}739 \text{ N·m} \;\approx\; 19.74 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Bearing Reaction & Sway Direction**

**Step 4: Bearing force.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \;=\; \frac{C}{L} \;=\; \frac{19{,}739}{1.5} \;\approx\; 13{,}159 \text{ N} \;\approx\; 13.16 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Direction of sway.**

Apply the right-hand rule: with $\vec{\omega}$ pointing along the spin axis and $\vec{\omega_p}$ along the perpendicular precession axis, the cross product $\vec{\omega}\times\vec{\omega_p}$ gives the gyroscopic couple direction on the rotor. The reactive couple acts in the opposite direction, causing the frame to tilt/sway accordingly. The student must explicitly state which direction this is.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

The vertical shaft of an electrically-driven punching press rotates at **900 rpm**. The moment of inertia of the rotating system about the spin axis is **6 kg·m²**. The punching operation takes **0.2 s** per hole, and the crank completes one revolution during this time.

**(a)** Determine the **spin angular velocity**, the **precession angular velocity**, and the **gyroscopic couple** experienced by the rotor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the magnitudes of gyroscopic couple if the same press were re-designed to operate at **1500 rpm** with a punching time of **0.1 s**, keeping $I$ unchanged. What practical conclusion can be drawn about motor speed selection for punching presses? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_1 \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 900}{60} \;=\; 30\pi \;\approx\; 94.25 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{p1} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{0.2} \;=\; 10\pi \;\approx\; 31.42 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 \;=\; I\,\omega_1\,\omega_{p1} \;=\; 6 \times 94.25 \times 31.42 \;\approx\; 17{,}765 \text{ N·m} \;\approx\; 17.77 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Comparison**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_2 \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} \;=\; 50\pi \;\approx\; 157.08 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{p2} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{0.1} \;=\; 20\pi \;\approx\; 62.83 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2 \;=\; 6 \times 157.08 \times 62.83 \;\approx\; 59{,}218 \text{ N·m} \;\approx\; 59.22 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C_2}{C_1} \;=\; \frac{59.22}{17.77} \;\approx\; 3.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical Conclusion:**
Increasing the motor speed by 1.67× and halving the punching time each *increase* the gyroscopic couple by ~3.33× — a **disproportionate jump**. Hence, very high motor speeds in punching presses are *undesirable* from a gyroscopic-stability viewpoint; designers prefer moderate motor speeds combined with a heavy flywheel (large $I$) for energy storage, accepting that this also raises $C$ linearly with $I$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Mixing up rpm and rad/s**: Always convert $N$ using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. A common error is to use $\omega = N$ or $\omega = \pi N/30$ incorrectly.
> 2. **Forgetting the reactive couple sign**: The couple *on the rotor* and the couple *on the frame* are equal in magnitude but **opposite in direction**. Marks are reserved for stating both.
> 3. **Direction of sway question is often skipped**: Even if the numerical answer is correct, failure to apply the right-hand rule to identify the sway direction costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Punching time vs. one-revolution assumption**: In KTU problems, "one complete revolution during punching" implies $\theta = 2\pi$ and $\omega_p = 2\pi/t$. Do **not** assume $\omega_p = \omega$ unless explicitly stated.
> 5. **Units mismatch**: Forgetting to write **N·m** after a numerical answer is a half-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Gyroscopic couple** $C = I\omega\omega_p$ is the cornerstone formula — memorise it.
- [x] Always convert **rpm → rad/s** using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ before plugging into $C$.
- [x] **Precession rate** $\omega_p$ in punching-press problems is *almost always* $\omega_p = 2\pi/t$, where $t$ is the punching-stroke time.
- [x] **Reactive couple** equals $-C$; it acts on the **frame**, not on the rotor.
- [x] **Bearing force** $F = C/L$ is needed for foundation/bolt design.
- [x] **Direction analysis** uses the right-hand rule: $\vec{C} = \vec{\omega}\times\vec{\omega_p}$.
- [x] **Units check**: $I$ in kg·m², $\omega$ and $\omega_p$ in rad/s → $C$ in **N·m**.
- [x] In a punching press, the gyroscopic effect is *harmful* (causes sway, vibration, foundation stress), unlike in gyroscopes/navigation where it is *useful*.
- [x] For a *given* $I$, **higher motor speed** and **shorter punching time** both **increase** $C$ — often disproportionately.
- [x] Real KTU board problems always include the phrase *"one complete revolution during punching"* — translate this immediately as $\omega_p = 2\pi/t$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on Aeroplanes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on Aeroplanes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gyroscopic Couple on an Aeroplane:** The gyroscopic couple is the *reactive torque* generated in a spinning rotor (propeller or turbojet turbine) when the axis of spin is tilted or reoriented in space. In an aeroplane, the propeller (or jet-engine turbine) acts as a **gyroscope**, and whenever the aircraft performs **pitching, turning, or rolling**, a gyroscopic couple is induced which produces an *unintended turning effect* on the aeroplane about its longitudinal or vertical axis.

Mathematically, the magnitude of the gyroscopic couple is given by:

$$T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p}\,\sin(\theta)$$

where:
- $I_{s}$ = Mass moment of inertia of the propeller/turbine about its **axis of spin** $(\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{2})$
- $\omega_{s}$ = Angular velocity of spin (rad/s)
- $\omega_{p}$ = Angular velocity of precession (rad/s)
- $\theta$ = Angle between the spin axis and the precession axis

When the two axes are mutually perpendicular (as in most aeroplane manoeuvres), $\theta = 90^{\circ}$ and:

$$T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p}$$

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy 🎯

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a spinning bicycle wheel held by a string at one end of its axle.** Spin the wheel fast, then try to tilt the free end up or down. You will immediately feel a strong resistance, and the wheel will try to *turn sideways* (perpendicular to the direction of tilt). This sideways turning tendency is exactly what a gyroscopic couple is.

Applied to an aeroplane:
- The **propeller** = spinning bicycle wheel.
- **Taking off (nose rises)** or **landing (nose drops)** = tilting the axle.
- The **unwanted sideways swing** of the nose = the gyroscopic turning effect the pilot must counteract using the rudder.

## 1.3 Three Manoeuvres Where Gyroscopic Couple Becomes Significant

| # | Manoeuvre | Motion of Aeroplane | Axis of Precession |
|---|-----------|---------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Taking-off (Pitching up)** | Nose moves upward | Lateral (pitch) axis |
| 2 | **Landing (Pitching down)** | Nose moves downward | Lateral (pitch) axis |
| 3 | **Turning / Banking** | Aeroplane yaws and rolls | Vertical / longitudinal axis |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard gravitational acceleration:** $\mathbf{g \;=\; 9.81 \, m/s^{2}}$
- **Standard sea-level air density:** $\mathbf{\rho \;=\; 1.225 \, kg/m^{3}}$
- **Conversion:** $\mathbf{1 \; \text{rpm} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi}{60} \, rad/s}$
- **Aircraft Reference Frame Axes (Body Axes):**
  - **X-axis** → Longitudinal (nose-to-tail) — *Spin axis of propeller*
  - **Y-axis** → Lateral (wingtip-to-wingtip) — *Pitch axis*
  - **Z-axis** → Vertical (top-to-bottom) — *Yaw axis*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamentals — Angular Momentum & Precession

The fundamental concept governing gyroscopic effects is the **conservation of angular momentum** for a rotating rigid body:

$$\vec{H} \;=\; I_{s}\,\vec{\omega}_{s}$$

When the orientation of the spin axis changes (i.e., when the body **precesses** with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}_{p}$), the angular momentum vector $\vec{H}$ also changes direction. The *rate of change* of angular momentum is mathematically equal to the external torque required, which in turn equals the **reactive gyroscopic couple**.

$$\vec{T}_{gyro} \;=\; \dfrac{d\vec{H}}{dt} \;=\; \vec{\omega}_{p} \times \vec{H} \;=\; \vec{\omega}_{p} \times (I_{s}\vec{\omega}_{s})$$

Taking magnitudes (with perpendicular axes):

$$\boxed{\,T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p}\,}$$

## 2.2 Three Operational Scenarios — Step-by-Step Logic

### Scenario A — Pitching (Taking-Off or Landing) ✈️⬆️⬇️

1. Engine/propeller rotates at angular velocity $\omega_{s}$ about the longitudinal (X) axis of the aeroplane.
2. During take-off, the aeroplane pitches upward with angular velocity $\omega_{p}$ about the lateral (Y) axis.
3. Spin axis (X) and precession axis (Y) are **mutually perpendicular**, hence $\sin(90^{\circ}) = 1$.
4. The **active gyroscopic couple** acts about the Y-axis; the **reactive gyroscopic couple** acts about the Z-axis (vertical), swinging the nose **left or right**.
5. The pilot must apply a corrective force on the rudder to neutralise this swing.

$$T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\left(\dfrac{v}{r}\right)$$

where $v$ = velocity of the aeroplane, $r$ = radius of curvature of the flight path during take-off.

### Scenario B — Turning / Banking 🔄

1. Aeroplane moves forward with velocity $v$ along a curved path of radius $R$.
2. Angular velocity of turning (precession): $\omega_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{v}{R}$.
3. The propeller is often tilted at a banking angle $\theta$ from horizontal.
4. Only the **vertical component of $\omega_{s}$** contributes to the angular momentum that gets precessed.

$$T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p}\,\sin(\theta)$$

### Scenario C — Rolling (Banking turn sustained) 🌀

1. The aeroplane rolls about its longitudinal (X) axis with angular velocity $\omega_{r}$.
2. Here the spin axis coincides with the precession axis — the gyroscopic effect is theoretically **zero** about the roll axis itself.
3. However, due to engine precession relative to the aircraft frame during banking, an **effective precession component** appears, and a small reactive couple is generated about the yaw axis.

## 2.3 Active vs. Reactive Gyroscopic Couple

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Active Couple:** The couple applied by the engine torque trying to precess the rotor. Acts about the precession axis.
> - **Reactive Couple:** The equal and opposite couple exerted by the rotor on the aircraft structure. Acts about an axis perpendicular to the precession axis (Newton's Third Law).
> - It is the **reactive couple** that swings the aeroplane's nose sideways.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Manoeuvre | Governing Equation | Units |
|---|-----------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | General gyroscopic couple | $T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p}\,\sin(\theta)$ | N·m |
| 2 | Couple when spin ⟂ precession | $T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p}$ | N·m |
| 3 | Take-off pitching | $T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\left(\dfrac{v}{r}\right)$ | N·m |
| 4 | Landing pitching | $T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\left(\dfrac{v}{r}\right)$ (same magnitude, opposite sense) | N·m |
| 5 | Turning (banked) | $T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\left(\dfrac{v}{R}\right)\sin(\theta)$ | N·m |
| 6 | Angular momentum | $H \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}$ | kg·m²/s |
| 7 | rpm → rad/s | $\omega_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s |
| 8 | Curvilinear precession | $\omega_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{v}{R}$ | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> All variables must be in **SI units** before substitution. Common student pitfall: using $N$ (rpm) directly without converting to $\omega_{s}$ (rad/s).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aircraft Design:** Designers compute gyroscopic couples to specify **rudder sizing** and **aileron trim compensators** so that the pilot can counter the unwanted yaw during take-off and landing.
- **Helicopter Rotor Design:** The main rotor of a helicopter is a gyroscope; its gyroscopic couple is responsible for **transverse flow effect** and **tail-rotor thrust requirements**.
- **Jet Engine Gyroscopic Effects:** Modern turbofan/turbojet engines have high-speed turbine rotors (15,000+ rpm) producing significant gyroscopic moments during aerobatic manoeuvres.
- **Stability Augmentation Systems (SAS):** Modern fly-by-wire aircraft use automated systems to apply counter-yaw during pitching to neutralise gyroscopic couples within milliseconds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gyroscopic Couple During Pitching (Take-Off)

### Given:
- Mass moment of inertia of propeller = $I_{s}$
- Angular velocity of propeller (spin) = $\omega_{s}$
- Velocity of aeroplane during take-off = $v$
- Radius of curvature of take-off path = $r$

### To Derive:
Expression for the reactive gyroscopic couple on the aeroplane nose.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Express the angular velocity of the propeller about its spin axis (longitudinal axis X):
$$\omega_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

**Step 2:** When the aeroplane takes off, the nose pitches up. This pitching motion is a precession about the lateral (Y) axis. The angular velocity of pitching is:
$$\omega_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{angular velocity of nose rotation}}{\text{1 second}}$$

**Step 3:** Relate $\omega_{p}$ to the linear velocity $v$ and radius $r$ of the flight path. Since the centre of gravity of the aeroplane traces a curved path of radius $r$ at velocity $v$:
$$\omega_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{v}{r}$$

**Step 4:** The angular momentum vector $\vec{H}$ of the propeller points along the spin axis (X-axis):
$$\vec{H} \;=\; I_{s}\,\vec{\omega}_{s}$$

**Step 5:** The rate of change of angular momentum is given by the cross product:
$$\vec{T}_{gyro} \;=\; \dfrac{d\vec{H}}{dt} \;=\; \vec{\omega}_{p} \times \vec{H} \;=\; \vec{\omega}_{p} \times (I_{s}\vec{\omega}_{s})$$

**Step 6:** Taking the magnitudes, and noting that $\vec{\omega}_{s}$ (along X) and $\vec{\omega}_{p}$ (along Y) are perpendicular:
$$\left\vert \vec{T}_{gyro} \right\vert \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p}\,\sin(90^{\circ}) \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $\omega_{p} \;=\; v/r$:
$$\boxed{\,T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\left(\dfrac{v}{r}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,v}{r}\,}$$

**Step 8 (Direction):** Apply the right-hand rule. If $\omega_{s}$ is along +X and $\omega_{p}$ is along +Y (nose up), then the **active couple** acts along +Z, and the **reactive couple** acts along −Z (or vice-versa depending on the sense of rotation). The reactive couple produces the *nose swing*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gyroscopic Couple During a Turning/Banking Manoeuvre

### Given:
- Mass moment of inertia of propeller = $I_{s}$
- Spin angular velocity = $\omega_{s}$
- Forward velocity = $v$
- Radius of turn = $R$
- Banking angle = $\theta$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** The angular velocity of turning (precession) of the aeroplane about a vertical axis is:
$$\omega_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{v}{R}$$

**Step 2:** The propeller spin axis (longitudinal axis of aircraft) is inclined at the banking angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The component of $\omega_{s}$ that is *vertical* (i.e., parallel to the precession axis) does **not** contribute to the gyroscopic effect, because the cross product with the parallel vector vanishes.

**Step 3:** The component of $\omega_{s}$ that is **horizontal** and perpendicular to $\omega_{p}$ contributes the gyroscopic effect. This horizontal component is:
$$\omega_{s,h} \;=\; \omega_{s}\,\sin(\theta)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the gyroscopic couple equation:
$$T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s,h}\,\omega_{p} \;=\; I_{s}\,(\omega_{s}\,\sin(\theta))\,\left(\dfrac{v}{R}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{gyro} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,v\,\sin(\theta)}{R}\,}$$

**Step 5 (Direction):** Using the right-hand rule, the active couple tends to *dip* or *raise* the nose, while the reactive couple tends to *roll* the aeroplane in the opposite direction. This is why the pilot must apply aileron correction during a banked turn in a propeller-driven aircraft.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reactive Couple in Terms of Velocity Components (Generic Aeroplane)

A complete vector form of the gyroscopic couple acting on an aeroplane is:

$$\vec{T}_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\left[\,\vec{\omega}_{s} \times \vec{\omega}_{p}\,\right]$$

Expanding for an aeroplane with velocities along all three body axes (roll $p$, pitch $q$, yaw $r$):

$$\vec{T}_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\begin{bmatrix}\,\omega_{s} - q \\ p \\ r\,\end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix}\,p \\ q \\ r\,\end{bmatrix}$$

This matrix form is used in advanced flight dynamics simulations.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

### Problem Statement:
A propeller-driven aeroplane weighing **15 kN** takes off at a velocity of **120 km/h**, the propeller rotates at **2400 rpm**, and the mass moment of inertia of the propeller is **25 kg·m²**. If the centre of gravity of the aeroplane moves along a circular path of radius **200 m**, find:
1. The gyroscopic couple on the aeroplane.
2. The angular velocity of precession.
3. The nose-dive or nose-rise tendency.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Convert all units to SI.

$$v \;=\; 120 \, \text{km/h} \;=\; \dfrac{120 \times 1000}{3600} \;=\; 33.33 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$\omega_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \times 2400}{60} \;=\; 80\pi \;\approx\; 251.33 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{v}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{33.33}{200} \;=\; 0.1667 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the gyroscopic couple magnitude.

$$T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p} \;=\; 25 \times 251.33 \times 0.1667$$

$$T_{gyro} \;\approx\; 25 \times 251.33 \times 0.1667 \;\approx\; 1047.2 \, \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{gyro} \;\approx\; 1.047 \, \text{kN·m}\,}$$

**Step 3 (Direction):** Assuming the propeller rotates clockwise when viewed from the rear (i.e., right-hand propeller) and the aeroplane is pitching nose-up during take-off, the reactive gyroscopic couple tends to **swing the nose to the left** of the pilot. The pilot must apply **right rudder** to counteract this swing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Gyroscopic Effect Pathway on an Aeroplane

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **functional cause-effect pathway** of how gyroscopic couple arises and manifests in an aeroplane. This replaces the physical 3D drawings typically required.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Torque Applied] --> B[Propeller Spinning at High Omega_s]
    B --> C{Aeroplane Manoeuvre Type}
    C -- Taking Off --> D[Nose Pitches Up - Pitching Omega_p]
    C -- Landing --> E[Nose Pitches Down - Pitching Omega_p]
    C -- Turning --> F[Aeroplane Yaws - Banking Angle Theta]
    D --> G[Spin Axis tilts relative to Precession Axis]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Angular Momentum Vector H changes direction]
    H --> I[Active Gyroscopic Couple T_active = I_s Omega_s Omega_p]
    I --> J[Reactive Gyroscopic Couple acts on Aeroplane Frame]
    J --> K{Effect on Aeroplane}
    K -- Take-off --> L[Nose Swings Left or Right]
    K -- Landing --> M[Nose Swings Opposite Direction]
    K -- Turn --> N[Dip or Raise of Nose - Aileron Trim Needed]
    L --> O[Pilot Applies Rudder Correction]
    M --> O
    N --> P[Pilot Applies Aileron Trim]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three Axes Reference Frame

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Longitudinal X axis - Spin Axis - Propeller]
    Y[Lateral Y axis - Pitch Axis - Wingtip]
    Z[Vertical Z axis - Yaw Axis - Top to Bottom]
    X -- Perpendicular --> Y
    Y -- Perpendicular --> Z
    Z -- Perpendicular --> X
    X --> XS[Omega_s - High Magnitude 200 to 300 rad per sec]
    Y --> YP[Omega_p - Low Magnitude 0.1 to 1 rad per sec]
    Z --> ZR[Omega_r - Yaw Precession during Turns]
```

## 4.3 Body-Axis Vector Geometry (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Body_Axes
        SA[Spin Axis - Longitudinal X]
        PA[Pitch Axis - Lateral Y]
        YA[Yaw Axis - Vertical Z]
    end
    SA --> SP[Vector Omega_s along +X]
    PA --> PP[Vector Omega_p along +Y for pitching]
    YA --> YP[Vector Omega_r along +Z for turning]
    SP --> CR[Cross Product Omega_s cross Omega_p]
    CR --> COUPLE[Gyroscopic Couple Vector along Z or -Z]
    COUPLE --> REACT[Reactive Couple swings the Nose]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Summary of Direction Rules (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Propeller rotates CW from Rear Right Hand Propeller] --> R2[Nose Pitches Up - Take off]
    R2 --> R3[Active Couple: Towards Top of Aeroplane]
    R3 --> R4[Reactive Couple: Nose Swings LEFT]
    R4 --> R5[Pilot applies RIGHT Rudder]
    L1[Propeller rotates CCW from Rear Left Hand Propeller] --> L2[Nose Pitches Up - Take off]
    L2 --> L3[Active Couple: Towards Bottom of Aeroplane]
    L3 --> L4[Reactive Couple: Nose Swings RIGHT]
    L4 --> L5[Pilot applies LEFT Rudder]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🎯 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define gyroscopic couple. State the formula for gyroscopic couple and explain the terms.**

**Model Answer:**
The gyroscopic couple is the reactive torque generated in a spinning body when the axis of rotation is forced to change its direction in space. In an aeroplane, the propeller acts as a gyroscope.

$$T_{gyro} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p}\,\sin(\theta)$$

Where $I_{s}$ = mass moment of inertia of the propeller about its spin axis, $\omega_{s}$ = angular velocity of spin (rad/s), $\omega_{p}$ = angular velocity of precession (rad/s), and $\theta$ = angle between the spin and precession axes. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between active and reactive gyroscopic couples in an aeroplane.**

**Model Answer:**
The **active gyroscopic couple** is the couple required to precess the spinning rotor; it acts about the precession axis and is supplied externally (by pilot input or air loads). The **reactive gyroscopic couple** is equal in magnitude but opposite in direction, exerted by the rotor on the supporting structure (the aircraft). It is the reactive couple that produces the unwanted yaw or roll of the aeroplane. **(3 marks)**

---

## 🎯 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**(14 Marks) Question A:**

A four-engine propeller aeroplane weighing **180 kN** flies at **400 km/h**. Each engine runs at **3000 rpm** and the mass moment of inertia of each propeller is **20 kg·m²**.

**(a)** During take-off, the aeroplane moves in a circular arc of radius **500 m**. Determine the magnitude and direction of the reactive gyroscopic couple on the aeroplane. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a banked turn at angle **15°**, with radius of turn **1500 m**, calculate the gyroscopic couple and explain its effect on the aircraft's nose attitude. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Given:**
- Weight $W = 180$ kN (mass = $180{,}000/9.81 \approx 18{,}349$ kg)
- Velocity $v = 400$ km/h $= 400 \times 1000/3600 = 111.11$ m/s
- Each propeller: $N = 3000$ rpm, $I_{s} = 20$ kg·m²
- Radius of take-off path $r = 500$ m
- Total number of propellers = 4 (assume all rotate in same sense)

**Step 1:** Convert spin speed to rad/s for one propeller.
$$\omega_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} \;=\; 100\pi \;\approx\; 314.16 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Angular velocity of precession.
$$\omega_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{v}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{111.11}{500} \;=\; 0.2222 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Gyroscopic couple for one propeller.
$$T_{1} \;=\; I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p} \;=\; 20 \times 314.16 \times 0.2222 \;\approx\; 1396.3 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4:** Total couple for four propellers.
$$\boxed{\,T_{total} \;=\; 4 \times 1396.3 \;\approx\; 5585.2 \, \text{N·m} \;\approx\; 5.585 \, \text{kN·m}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Direction:** Assuming right-hand propeller (clockwise from rear), nose pitching up during take-off → reactive couple swings the nose to the **left**. Pilot applies right rudder.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:**
- $v = 111.11$ m/s, $R = 1500$ m, $\theta = 15°$
- $I_{s} = 20$ kg·m², $\omega_{s} = 314.16$ rad/s (per propeller)

**Step 1:** Precession angular velocity.
$$\omega_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{v}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{111.11}{1500} \;=\; 0.0741 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply banked-turn formula.
$$T_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,v\,\sin(\theta)}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{20 \times 314.16 \times 111.11 \times \sin(15°)}{1500}$$

$$T_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{20 \times 314.16 \times 111.11 \times 0.2588}{1500}$$

$$T_{1} \;\approx\; \dfrac{180{,}743}{1500} \;\approx\; 120.5 \, \text{N·m per propeller}$$

**Step 3:** Total couple for 4 propellers.
$$\boxed{\,T_{total} \;\approx\; 4 \times 120.5 \;=\; 482.0 \, \text{N·m}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Effect on Nose Attitude:** The reactive couple during a banked turn tends to *dip* the nose on the outside of the turn (or raise it on the inside), depending on propeller sense. Pilot must apply aileron trim to maintain level banked flight.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]` — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**(14 Marks) Question B:**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the gyroscopic couple on an aeroplane during **take-off** when it is pitching upwards in a circular path. State clearly the direction of the reactive couple. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-engine aeroplane has a propeller of moment of inertia **18 kg·m²** rotating at **2000 rpm**. The aeroplane flies at **250 km/h** and executes a banked turn of radius **1200 m** at banking angle **20°**. Calculate:
1. Angular velocity of precession.
2. Magnitude of the gyroscopic couple.
3. State and justify whether the nose will pitch up or pitch down during the turn. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The derivation proceeds as follows:

1. Propeller spin angular velocity: $\omega_{s} = 2\pi N/60$ rad/s.
2. During take-off, nose pitches up at angular velocity $\omega_{p} = v/r$ about the lateral (Y) axis.
3. Spin axis (X) and precession axis (Y) are perpendicular: $\theta = 90°$.
4. Angular momentum vector $\vec{H} = I_{s}\vec{\omega}_{s}$ along X.
5. Time rate of change: $\vec{T} = \vec{\omega}_{p} \times \vec{H}$.
6. Magnitude: $T = I_{s}\omega_{s}\omega_{p} = I_{s}\omega_{s}(v/r)$.

Direction (right-hand rule): For a right-hand propeller rotating CW viewed from rear and nose pitching up, the reactive couple swings the nose to the **left**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1:** Convert units.
$$\omega_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \times 2000}{60} \;=\; 209.44 \, \text{rad/s}$$
$$v \;=\; 250 \, \text{km/h} \;=\; 69.44 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Angular velocity of precession.
$$\omega_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{v}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{69.44}{1200} \;=\; 0.0579 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude of gyroscopic couple (banked).
$$T_{gyro} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,v\,\sin(\theta)}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{18 \times 209.44 \times 69.44 \times \sin(20°)}{1200}$$

$$T_{gyro} \;=\; \dfrac{18 \times 209.44 \times 69.44 \times 0.342}{1200} \;\approx\; \dfrac{89{,}574}{1200} \;\approx\; 74.6 \, \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{gyro} \;\approx\; 74.6 \, \text{N·m}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Nose Attitude Justification:** Using right-hand rule with right-hand propeller and standard left banked turn, the reactive couple tends to **dip the nose downward** on the inside of the turn. Pilot must apply slight **back-elevator pressure** to maintain altitude.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in this Question Type:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Using rpm directly instead of converting to rad/s via $2\pi N/60$. This alone costs **2 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by number of propellers** in multi-engine aircraft. Always check whether the problem specifies single-engine or multi-engine.
> 3. **Direction description missing:** KTU examiners award up to **2 marks** explicitly for stating the *direction* of the reactive couple and the corrective rudder/aileron action. Skipping this loses easy marks.
> 4. **Using $W$ (weight) instead of mass:** The mass moment of inertia $I_s$ uses mass (kg), not weight (N). Divide $W$ by $g = 9.81$ m/s² when needed.
> 5. **Confusing $\theta$ in the banked-turn formula:** $\theta$ is the **banking angle** from horizontal, not the angle of incidence. Students often mistakenly use $\theta = 90°$ which reduces the formula to the take-off case.
> 6. **Sign convention:** Clearly state whether the nose swings left/right or up/down. Vague answers are penalised.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> 📌 **Core Concepts:**
> - Propeller / turbine = **Gyroscope**; spin axis is the longitudinal X-axis.
> - Three primary manoeuvres: **Take-off (pitch up), Landing (pitch down), Turning (bank).**
> - Reactive gyroscopic couple causes **unintended yaw or roll**; pilot counters with rudder/aileron.

> 📌 **Key Formulae to Memorise:**
> - $T_{gyro} = I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,\omega_{p}\,\sin(\theta)$ — General
> - $T_{gyro} = I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,(v/r)$ — Take-off / Landing (perpendicular axes)
> - $T_{gyro} = I_{s}\,\omega_{s}\,(v/R)\,\sin(\theta)$ — Banked turn
> - $\omega_{s} = 2\pi N/60$ — Always convert rpm to rad/s
> - $\omega_{p} = v/r$ or $v/R$ — Precession angular velocity

> 📌 **Direction Rules (Right-Hand Rule):**
> - Right-hand propeller + nose up take-off → Reactive couple → **Nose LEFT**.
> - Right-hand propeller + nose down landing → Reactive couple → **Nose RIGHT**.
> - Pilot always applies rudder **opposite** to the reactive swing.

> 📌 **Typical Numerical Values (for sanity check):**
> - Propeller $I_{s}$: 15 – 30 kg·m²
> - Propeller speed: 1500 – 3000 rpm ($\omega_{s}$ ≈ 150 – 315 rad/s)
> - Take-off $v$: 100 – 400 km/h
> - Take-off radius: 300 – 1000 m
> - Resultant couple: typically **0.5 to 10 kN·m**

> 📌 **Examiner-Friendly Tips:**
> - Always show **unit conversion steps** explicitly.
> - Always state the **direction of the reactive couple** and the **corrective action**.
> - Use **vector cross-product notation** for clarity: $\vec{T} = \vec{\omega}_{p} \times \vec{H}$.
> - Mention that the effect is **most pronounced** at high $\omega_{s}$ (fast propellers) and high $\omega_{p}$ (sharp turns / quick pitch-up).

> 📌 **Common Errors to Avoid:**
> - Mixing up **active and reactive couples**.
> - Treating $\omega_{p}$ as a constant — it depends on $v$ and $r$.
> - Ignoring the $\sin(\theta)$ factor in banked-turn problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ships - Numerical Examples - Balancing of Rotating Masses: Static and Dynamic Balancing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ships - Numerical Examples - Balancing of Rotating Masses: Static and Dynamic Balancing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — SHIPS, NUMERICAL EXAMPLES & BALANCING OF ROTATING MASSES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** Balancing of rotating masses — Static & Dynamic balancing; Gyroscopic effects — Ships (rolling, pitching, steering); Numerical problem solving.

### 1.1 Static Balancing — Formal Definition

A rotating mass system is said to be **statically balanced** when the **vector sum of the centrifugal forces (mass × radius) of all the revolving masses is equal to zero**. In simpler terms, the resultant of all unbalanced forces must vanish when the masses are considered to lie in a single transverse plane.

Mathematically, the condition for static balance is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i = 0$$

where:
- $m_i$ = mass of the $i^{th}$ rotating mass in **kg**
- $r_i$ = radius of the $i^{th}$ mass from the axis of rotation in **metres (m)**
- $m_i r_i$ is the **mass-radius product** (also called the "weight" of the unbalance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Static balance does NOT guarantee smooth running at high speed. A shaft can be statically balanced but still vibrate violently in operation — this leads us to *dynamic* balancing.

### 1.2 Dynamic Balancing — Formal Definition

A rotating mass system is said to be **dynamically balanced** when:
1. It is **statically balanced** (resultant centrifugal force = 0), **AND**
2. The **resultant couple** of all the centrifugal forces (taken about any reference plane) is also equal to zero.

The dynamic balance conditions are:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(horizontal component)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(vertical component)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \, d_i = 0 \quad \text{(couple / moment condition)}$$

where:
- $\theta_i$ = angular position of the $i^{th}$ mass measured from a reference (in **radians**)
- $d_i$ = axial distance of the $i^{th}$ mass from a reference plane (in **m**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** In a single plane (e.g., a thin disc rotor), only static balance is required. In MULTIPLE planes (multi-disc rotors like turbines, marine propellers, ship engines), both static AND dynamic balance are essential to prevent destructive vibrations.

### 1.3 Gyroscopic Effects on Ships — Formal Definition

A ship at sea is a **gyroscopic body** because of its heavy rotating mass — the **propeller shaft and turbine rotor**. When the ship undergoes a change in orientation (rolling, pitching, or steering), the spinning rotor resists this change in direction due to the **Rigid Body Rotational Inertia Principle**, generating a **reactive gyroscopic couple**.

The magnitude of the active gyroscopic couple is:

$$C = I \, \omega \, \omega_p$$

where:
- $C$ = active/reactive gyroscopic couple in **N·m**
- $I$ = mass moment of inertia of the rotor about its spin axis in **kg·m²**
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of spin of rotor in **rad/s**
- $\omega_p$ = angular velocity of precession in **rad/s**

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bicycle Wheel Intuition for Gyroscopes**
>
> Imagine spinning a heavy bicycle wheel by its axle and holding it horizontally by the axle ends. Now try to tilt the axle sideways. You will feel a strong **resisting torque** that twists the axle perpendicular to your tilt. This is the gyroscopic effect — and this is EXACTLY what happens to a ship's propeller when the ship rolls, pitches or turns. The engineer's job is to **calculate and accommodate** this couple.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Car Tyre Analogy for Balancing**
>
> When you take your car for wheel balancing, the mechanic adds small lead weights on opposite sides of the wheel rim. This is exactly "static balancing" — making the centre of mass lie on the rotation axis. If the wheel still shimmies at high speed (vibration), the mechanic balances it "dynamically" by also correcting couples across the wheel width — which is why tyre/wheel assemblies are balanced on a spinning machine.

### 1.5 Significance in Ships and Marine Engineering

In marine engineering, the balancing problem is amplified because:
1. **Marine propellers** rotate in 3 to 4 planes with massive radii (1–3 metres) — any unbalance causes hull vibration and bearing wear.
2. **Ship turbines** (geared steam turbines driving propellers) have multi-stage rotors requiring precise dynamic balancing.
3. **Rolling of a ship** (about its longitudinal axis) generates a gyroscopic couple that tries to turn the ship sideways — important for naval architects.
4. **Pitching of a ship** (about its transverse axis) generates a gyroscopic couple that tries to turn the ship about a vertical axis.
5. **Steering/turning of a ship** creates the largest gyroscopic effects — critical for stability during sharp turns.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Why and How

### 2.1.1 Why Centrifugal Forces Cause Unbalance

When a mass $m$ rotates at radius $r$ with angular velocity $\omega$, it produces an outward (centrifugal) force:

$$F_c = m \, \omega^2 \, r$$

This force rotates with the shaft. If the **resultant** of all such forces is non-zero, the bearings experience cyclic loading at the rotational frequency — producing vibration, noise, and eventual fatigue failure.

### 2.1.2 Why Dynamic Balance is More Than Static Balance

Consider two equal masses on opposite sides of a shaft, diametrically placed, but at different axial locations. The centrifugal forces **cancel** (statically balanced), but they form a **couple** that rotates with the shaft. This couple bends the shaft, causing whirl and vibration. **Dynamic balance kills this couple.**

### 2.1.3 Steps of Logical Reasoning for Static Balancing

1. Identify all rotating masses and their angular positions.
2. Resolve each mass-radius product $m_i r_i$ into two perpendicular components using $\cos\theta_i$ and $\sin\theta_i$.
3. Sum all horizontal components → must equal zero.
4. Sum all vertical components → must equal zero.
5. Add a trial mass-radius product at a chosen angular position such that both sums become zero.

### 2.1.4 Steps of Logical Reasoning for Dynamic Balancing

1. Choose a **reference plane** (typically one of the planes containing a mass).
2. Compute the algebraic sum of $m_i r_i \cos\theta_i$ and $m_i r_i \sin\theta_i$ — these give the unbalanced force components.
3. Introduce a **correction mass** in the reference plane to cancel this force.
4. Compute the algebraic sum of $m_i r_i d_i \cos\theta_i$ and $m_i r_i d_i \sin\theta_i$ — these give the unbalanced couple components about the reference plane.
5. Introduce a **second correction mass** in a parallel plane to cancel this couple.
6. Verify using vector polygon closure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These balancing techniques are used in **every** rotating machine: turbines, compressors, electric motors, jet engines, helicopter rotors, washing machines, and ship propellers. Without proper balancing, the service life reduces by 60–80%.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table — these are the only equations tested in KTU for this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal force of a rotating mass | $F_c = m \, \omega^2 \, r$ | $m$: kg, $r$: m, $\omega$: rad/s, $F_c$: N | Acts outward along radius |
| 2 | Static balance condition | $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ | $m_i r_i$: kg·m (vector) | Two perpendicular components must vanish |
| 3 | Horizontal component condition | $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ | $\theta_i$ in radians | Sign by quadrant |
| 4 | Vertical component condition | $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | $\theta_i$ in radians | Sign by quadrant |
| 5 | Couple about reference plane | $C = m_i r_i d_i$ | $d_i$: m (axial distance) | Vector couple |
| 6 | Dynamic balance force condition | $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ and $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | — | Both must hold |
| 7 | Dynamic balance couple condition | $\sum m_i r_i d_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ and $\sum m_i r_i d_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | — | Both must hold |
| 8 | Gyroscopic couple (active) | $C = I \, \omega \, \omega_p$ | $I$: kg·m², $\omega, \omega_p$: rad/s, $C$: N·m | Vector cross product |
| 9 | Gyroscopic couple for ship rolling | $C = I \, \omega \, (2\pi \, N_p)$ where $N_p$ = roll frequency in rev/s | — | Effect: ship turns |
| 10 | Gyroscopic couple for ship pitching | $C = I \, \omega \, (2\pi \, N_p)$ where $N_p$ = pitch frequency | — | Effect: ship yaws |
| 11 | Gyroscopic couple for ship steering/turning | $C = I \, \omega \, (2\pi \, N_p)$ where $N_p$ = turn rate in rev/s | — | Largest effect |
| 12 | Angular velocity conversion | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | $N$: rpm | $N$ in rev/min |
| 13 | Mass-radius product magnitude | $W_i = m_i r_i$ (kg·m) | — | Treated as force vector at $\omega = 1$ |
| 14 | Couple polygon closure | Vector sum of $m_i r_i d_i$ forms a closed polygon | — | Confirms dynamic balance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Factor:** $N \text{ (rpm)} \rightarrow \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \text{ rad/s}$ — Most marks lost in KTU papers are due to this single unit mistake.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Balancing Matters |
|---|---|
| **Ship propellers** | 4-bladed, multi-ton propellers; imbalance causes hull vibration and bearing failure |
| **Steam turbine rotors** | Multi-disc rotors running at 3000–3600 rpm require precision dynamic balance |
| **Helicopter rotors** | Even small imbalance causes destructive ground resonance |
| **Automobile wheels & tyres** | Wheel balancing for vibration-free running at 80–120 km/h |
| **Centrifugal pumps** | Impellers must be balanced to prevent shaft deflection |
| **Jet engines** | Fan and compressor stages balanced to micron precision |
| **Gyrocompass on ships** | Uses intentional gyroscopic effect for navigation |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solved Example Block — Worked out exactly as required in KTU ESE valuation. Read each step carefully.**

---

## 3.1 Example 1 — Static Balancing of Four Masses in the Same Plane

**Problem Statement:** Four masses $m_1 = 30$ kg, $m_2 = 40$ kg, $m_3 = 35$ kg, $m_4 = 50$ kg are revolving in the **same plane**. Their radii are $r_1 = 100$ mm, $r_2 = 150$ mm, $r_3 = 200$ mm, $r_4 = 80$ mm respectively, located at angular positions $\theta_1 = 0°$, $\theta_2 = 90°$, $\theta_3 = 180°$, $\theta_4 = 270°$. Determine the magnitude and angular position of the **balancing mass** if its radius is $200$ mm.

### Step 1 — Compute the Mass-Radius Products (W = mr)

$$W_1 = m_1 r_1 = 30 \times 0.100 = 3.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$W_2 = m_2 r_2 = 40 \times 0.150 = 6.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$W_3 = m_3 r_3 = 35 \times 0.200 = 7.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$W_4 = m_4 r_4 = 50 \times 0.080 = 4.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

### Step 2 — Resolve Each into Horizontal (X) and Vertical (Y) Components

| Mass | $W$ (kg·m) | $\theta$ | $\cos\theta$ | $\sin\theta$ | $W\cos\theta$ (X) | $W\sin\theta$ (Y) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3.0 | 0° | 1 | 0 | +3.0 | 0 |
| 2 | 6.0 | 90° | 0 | 1 | 0 | +6.0 |
| 3 | 7.0 | 180° | -1 | 0 | -7.0 | 0 |
| 4 | 4.0 | 270° | 0 | -1 | 0 | -4.0 |

### Step 3 — Sum All X and Y Components (Algebraic)

$$\sum W_x = 3.0 + 0 - 7.0 + 0 = -4.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\sum W_y = 0 + 6.0 + 0 - 4.0 = +2.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Magnitude of the Resultant Unbalance

$$\sum W = \sqrt{(-4.0)^2 + (2.0)^2}$$

$$\sum W = \sqrt{16 + 4} = \sqrt{20}$$

$$\sum W = 4.472 \text{ kg·m}$$

### Step 5 — Determine the Angular Position of the Resultant

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\sum W_y}{\sum W_x} = \frac{+2.0}{-4.0} = -0.5$$

Since $\sum W_x$ is **negative** and $\sum W_y$ is **positive**, the resultant lies in the **second quadrant**.

$$\alpha_{ref} = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57°$$

Measured from positive X-axis (anticlockwise):

$$\alpha = 180° - 26.57° = 153.43°$$

### Step 6 — Compute Balancing Mass

The balancing mass-radius product $W_b$ must be **equal in magnitude and opposite in direction** to the resultant:

$$W_b = m_b \, r_b = 4.472 \text{ kg·m}$$

Given $r_b = 200$ mm $= 0.200$ m:

$$m_b = \frac{W_b}{r_b} = \frac{4.472}{0.200} = 22.36 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 7 — Angular Position of Balancing Mass

The balancing mass is placed **opposite** to the resultant (i.e., $180°$ away):

$$\theta_b = 153.43° + 180° = 333.43° \equiv -26.57°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer — Example 1:** Balancing mass $m_b = 22.36$ kg located at $\theta_b = 333.43°$ (i.e., $26.57°$ below positive X-axis on the right side) at radius $r_b = 200$ mm.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Dynamic Balancing of Four Masses in Multiple Planes

**Problem Statement:** A shaft carries four masses $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ in four different planes. The data is:

| Mass | $m$ (kg) | $r$ (mm) | $\theta$ (°) | Plane location from LHS bearing (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 12 | 100 | 0 | 0 |
| B | 16 | 120 | 60 | 200 |
| C | 18 | 100 | 150 | 400 |
| D | 14 | 120 | 270 | 600 |

Find the magnitude and angular position of the **balancing masses** in planes L (at 0 mm) and M (at 800 mm).

### Step 1 — Compute Mass-Radius Products (W = mr)

$$W_A = 12 \times 0.100 = 1.20 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$W_B = 16 \times 0.120 = 1.92 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$W_C = 18 \times 0.100 = 1.80 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$W_D = 14 \times 0.120 = 1.68 \text{ kg·m}$$

### Step 2 — Compute X and Y Components

**For mass A** ($\theta = 0°$):
$$W_{Ax} = 1.20 \times \cos 0° = +1.200, \quad W_{Ay} = 1.20 \times \sin 0° = 0$$

**For mass B** ($\theta = 60°$):
$$W_{Bx} = 1.92 \times \cos 60° = 1.92 \times 0.500 = +0.960$$
$$W_{By} = 1.92 \times \sin 60° = 1.92 \times 0.866 = +1.663$$

**For mass C** ($\theta = 150°$):
$$W_{Cx} = 1.80 \times \cos 150° = 1.80 \times (-0.866) = -1.559$$
$$W_{Cy} = 1.80 \times \sin 150° = 1.80 \times 0.500 = +0.900$$

**For mass D** ($\theta = 270°$):
$$W_{Dx} = 1.68 \times \cos 270° = 0$$
$$W_{Dy} = 1.68 \times \sin 270° = -1.680$$

### Step 3 — Sum the Force Components (Static Unbalance)

$$\sum W_x = 1.200 + 0.960 - 1.559 + 0 = +0.601 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\sum W_y = 0 + 1.663 + 0.900 - 1.680 = +0.883 \text{ kg·m}$$

### Step 4 — Reference Plane Selection and Couple Computation

We choose **plane L** (at 0 mm) as the reference. The axial distances $d_i$ from plane L are:

$$d_A = 0, \quad d_B = 200, \quad d_C = 400, \quad d_D = 600 \text{ mm}$$

Compute $W_i \, d_i$ components for each mass:

**Mass A** ($d_A = 0$):
$$(W_A d_A)_x = 0, \quad (W_A d_A)_y = 0$$

**Mass B** ($d_B = 200$ mm = 0.200 m):
$$(W_B d_B)_x = 0.960 \times 0.200 = +0.192$$
$$(W_B d_B)_y = 1.663 \times 0.200 = +0.333$$

**Mass C** ($d_C = 0.400$ m):
$$(W_C d_C)_x = -1.559 \times 0.400 = -0.624$$
$$(W_C d_C)_y = +0.900 \times 0.400 = +0.360$$

**Mass D** ($d_D = 0.600$ m):
$$(W_D d_D)_x = 0 \times 0.600 = 0$$
$$(W_D d_D)_y = -1.680 \times 0.600 = -1.008$$

### Step 5 — Sum the Couple Components About Plane L

$$\sum (W d)_x = 0 + 0.192 - 0.624 + 0 = -0.432 \text{ kg·m²}$$

$$\sum (W d)_y = 0 + 0.333 + 0.360 - 1.008 = -0.315 \text{ kg·m²}$$

### Step 6 — Determine Balancing Mass in Plane L (Force Balance)

The balancing mass in plane L must cancel $\sum W_x$ and $\sum W_y$:

$$W_{bL} = \sqrt{(0.601)^2 + (0.883)^2} = \sqrt{0.361 + 0.780} = \sqrt{1.141} = 1.068 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\tan\alpha_L = \frac{0.883}{0.601} = 1.469, \quad \alpha_L = 55.74° \text{ (1st quadrant, both components +ve)}$$

Therefore balancing mass in plane L is at angle $\alpha_L + 180° = 235.74°$ from positive X-axis.

### Step 7 — Determine Balancing Mass in Plane M (Couple Balance)

Plane M is at $d_M = 800$ mm $= 0.800$ m from plane L. The couple due to the mass in plane M is $W_{bM} \times d_M$. This couple must cancel $\sum(Wd)_x$ and $\sum(Wd)_y$:

$$W_{bM} \times d_M = \sqrt{(-0.432)^2 + (-0.315)^2} = \sqrt{0.187 + 0.099} = \sqrt{0.286} = 0.535 \text{ kg·m²}$$

$$W_{bM} = \frac{0.535}{0.800} = 0.669 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\tan\alpha_M = \frac{-0.315}{-0.432} = 0.729, \quad \alpha_M = 36.07°$$

Both components negative → **third quadrant**: $\alpha_M = 180° + 36.07° = 216.07°$

Balancing mass angle in plane M (opposite to couple direction):

$$\theta_{bM} = 216.07° - 180° = 36.07°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer — Example 2:**
> - **Plane L (0 mm):** $W_{bL} = 1.068$ kg·m at $\theta_{bL} = 235.74°$
> - **Plane M (800 mm):** $W_{bM} = 0.669$ kg·m at $\theta_{bM} = 36.07°$

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Gyroscopic Couple on a Ship (Steering / Turning)

**Problem Statement:** A ship of mass moment of inertia $I = 4 \times 10^6$ kg·m² is propelled by a turbine rotor of mass moment of inertia $I_r = 2500$ kg·m², spinning at $2400$ rpm clockwise when viewed from the stern. The ship steers to the left along a curved path of mean radius $R = 400$ m at a forward speed of $36$ km/h. Calculate:
1. The active gyroscopic couple.
2. The effect (pitching/yaw/roll tendency) and its magnitude.
3. The maximum angular speed of precession of the ship during the turn.

### Step 1 — Convert All Angular Velocities to rad/s

**Spin angular velocity** of the turbine rotor:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 2400}{60} = 80\pi = 251.33 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Forward speed conversion:**
$$v = 36 \text{ km/h} = \frac{36 \times 1000}{3600} = 10 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Angular Velocity of Precession (Turning Rate)

The ship turns along a circular path of radius $R$. The turning (precession) angular velocity is:

$$\omega_p = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{10}{400} = 0.025 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Active Gyroscopic Couple

$$C = I_r \, \omega \, \omega_p = 2500 \times 251.33 \times 0.025$$

$$C = 15{,}708 \text{ N·m} = 15.71 \text{ kN·m}$$

### Step 4 — Determine the Direction of Effect

**Spinning axis** = Longitudinal (along ship's length, fore-aft)
**Precession axis** = Vertical (the ship turns about a vertical axis through its centre)
**Gyroscopic couple axis** = Transverse (port-starboard direction)

Using the **right-hand rule**:
- Spin vector $\vec{\omega}$ points from stern to bow (along +X)
- Precession vector $\vec{\omega}_p$ points upward (along +Z) for a left turn
- Active gyroscopic couple $\vec{C} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{\omega}_p$ → directed **toward port** (toward the outer side of the turn)

The **reactive couple** acts in the opposite direction (toward starboard), which tends to **pitch the ship's bow downward** (since the bow is the far end of the spin axis from the turning centre). 

Wait — for **steering/turning**, the effect is that the ship tends to **pitch**. Specifically:
- **Left turn** → bow dips (noses down) and stern rises
- **Right turn** → stern dips and bow rises

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer — Example 3:**
> - Active gyroscopic couple $C = 15.71$ kN·m
> - Effect: When the ship turns LEFT, the bow tends to NOSE DOWN and the stern to RISE.
> - Maximum precession rate $\omega_p = 0.025$ rad/s

---

## 3.4 Example 4 — Gyroscopic Effect on a Ship ROLLING

**Problem Statement:** A ship of $12000$ tonnes rolls (oscillates) about its longitudinal axis with a period of $20$ seconds. The ship has a turbine rotor of mass moment of inertia $3500$ kg·m² spinning at $1800$ rpm about the longitudinal axis. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the active gyroscopic couple. Given: $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### Step 1 — Spin Angular Velocity

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1800}{60} = 60\pi = 188.50 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 2 — Rolling (Precession) Angular Velocity

The ship rolls with period $T = 20$ s, so the angular velocity of rolling is:

$$\omega_p = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{20} = \frac{\pi}{10} = 0.3142 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 3 — Active Gyroscopic Couple

$$C = I \, \omega \, \omega_p = 3500 \times 188.50 \times 0.3142$$

$$C = 207{,}290 \text{ N·m} \approx 207.29 \text{ kN·m}$$

### Step 4 — Direction of Effect

**Spin axis** = Longitudinal (X-axis, fore-aft)
**Precession axis** = Longitudinal (also X-axis — but it's the rolling oscillation!)

Wait — for **rolling**, the precession is also about the longitudinal axis. The **active gyroscopic couple** appears about the **transverse** (port-starboard) axis. This couple tends to **yaw** (turn) the ship sideways.

Using vector cross-product:
- $\vec{\omega}$ along +X (fore-aft)
- $\vec{\omega}_p$ along +X (when rolling to starboard)
- $\vec{C}_{active} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{\omega}_p$ → direction perpendicular to X, i.e., along **transverse** axis

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer — Example 4:**
> - Active gyroscopic couple $C = 207.29$ kN·m
> - Direction: Tends to turn the ship (yaw) about a vertical axis. Specifically, if the ship rolls to starboard, the gyroscopic couple turns the ship toward the port side.

---

## 3.5 Example 5 — Gyroscopic Effect on a Ship PITCHING

**Problem Statement:** A ship pitches (oscillates) with a period of $15$ seconds. The turbine rotor has mass moment of inertia $I = 3000$ kg·m² and rotates at $1500$ rpm. Find the active gyroscopic couple and the effect on the ship's heading.

### Step 1 — Spin Angular Velocity

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 2 — Pitching (Precession) Angular Velocity

$$\omega_p = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{15} = 0.4189 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 3 — Active Gyroscopic Couple

$$C = I \, \omega \, \omega_p = 3000 \times 157.08 \times 0.4189$$

$$C = 197{,}392 \text{ N·m} \approx 197.39 \text{ kN·m}$$

### Step 4 — Direction of Effect

**Spin axis** = Longitudinal (X)
**Precession axis** = Transverse (Y) — pitching is about the port-starboard axis
**Gyroscopic couple axis** = Vertical (Z) — this **yaws** the ship

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer — Example 5:**
> - Active gyroscopic couple $C = 197.39$ kN·m
> - Effect: Tends to swing the ship about a vertical axis (yaw / turn sideways). During pitching, the ship's heading is deflected — this is undesirable for navigation.

---

## 3.6 Consolidated Direction Rules for Ship Gyroscopic Effects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners LOVE testing these direction rules. Memorise them using right-hand rule.**

| Ship Motion | Precession Axis | Active Couple Axis | Tendency | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Steering / Turning** | Vertical (Z) | Transverse (Y) | Ship pitches (nose dips / rises) | Affects steering control; dangerous at high speed |
| **Rolling** | Longitudinal (X) | Transverse (Y) | Ship yaws sideways | Affects course stability |
| **Pitching** | Transverse (Y) | Vertical (Z) | Ship turns about vertical axis | Affects heading; minor effect |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Static Balancing Procedure — Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Multi-mass rotor]) --> stepA[Step A: Identify all masses, radii and angular positions]
    stepA --> stepB[Step B: Compute mass-radius products W sub i = m sub i times r sub i]
    stepB --> stepC[Step C: Resolve each W sub i into X component and Y component using cos and sin]
    stepC --> stepD[Step D: Algebraic sum of X components gives Sum X]
    stepD --> stepE[Step E: Algebraic sum of Y components gives Sum Y]
    stepE --> stepF[Step F: Compute resultant magnitude Sum W = sqrt of Sum X squared plus Sum Y squared]
    stepF --> stepG[Step G: Compute resultant angle alpha = arctan of Sum Y over Sum X with quadrant correction]
    stepG --> stepH[Step H: Balancing mass-radius product W sub b equals negative of Sum W]
    stepH --> stepI[Step I: Place balancing mass at angle alpha + 180 degrees]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step J: Verify Sum X and Sum Y both become zero]
    stepJ --> done([Static Balance Achieved])
```

## 4.2 Dynamic Balancing Procedure — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Multi-plane rotor with N masses]) --> planeChoice[Choose Reference Plane RP]
    planeChoice --> forceCalc[Compute Force Vector Sum: Sum m sub i r sub i in X and Y directions]
    forceCalc --> forceCheck{Is Force Sum equal to zero?}
    forceCheck -- No --> addMass1[Add Correction Mass W sub L in Reference Plane RP to cancel Force]
    addMass1 --> coupleCalc
    forceCheck -- Yes --> coupleCalc[Compute Couple Vector Sum about RP: Sum m sub i r sub i d sub i in X and Y]
    coupleCalc --> coupleCheck{Is Couple Sum equal to zero?}
    coupleCheck -- No --> addMass2[Add Second Correction Mass W sub M in Plane M to cancel Couple]
    addMass2 --> verify
    coupleCheck -- Yes --> verify[Verify using Couple Polygon Closure]
    verify --> done([Dynamic Balance Achieved])
```

## 4.3 Couple Polygon Method for Dynamic Balancing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vector1[Vector 1: W sub A times d sub A at angle theta sub A] --> vector2[Vector 2: W sub B times d sub B at angle theta sub B]
    vector2 --> vector3[Vector 3: W sub C times d sub C at angle theta sub C]
    vector3 --> vector4[Vector 4: W sub D times d sub D at angle theta sub D]
    vector4 --> closing[Closing Vector: W sub b times d sub b at angle theta sub b]
    closing -.Closes polygon.-> vector1
```

## 4.4 Ship Gyroscopic Effects — Vector Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHIP[SHIP ORIENTATION]
        directionFWD[Longitudinal Axis: Fore-Aft, X]
        directionTR[Transverse Axis: Port-Starboard, Y]
        directionV[Vertical Axis: Z]
    end

    subgraph PROP[PROPELLER SPIN]
        spinVec[Spin Vector omega along Longitudinal Axis X]
    end

    subgraph MOTIONS[SHIP MOTIONS]
        roll[Rolling about Longitudinal Axis X]
        pitch[Pitching about Transverse Axis Y]
        turn[Turning about Vertical Axis Z]
    end

    subgraph COUPLES[GYROSCOPIC COUPLES]
        coupleRoll[Couple on Transverse Axis Y: Ship yaws]
        couplePitch[Couple on Vertical Axis Z: Ship yaws]
        coupleTurn[Couple on Transverse Axis Y: Ship pitches]
    end

    spinVec --> roll
    spinVec --> pitch
    spinVec --> turn
    roll --> coupleRoll
    pitch --> couplePitch
    turn --> coupleTurn
```

## 4.5 Multi-Plane Rotor Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bearingL[LHS Bearing] --> planeA[Plane A: Mass m1, theta1]
    planeA --> planeB[Plane B: Mass m2, theta2, distance d2]
```
