# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A carries 3 marks per question. Answers should be in **3–4 lines** with a **diagram** if applicable.

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between static and dynamic balancing of rotating masses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Balancing | Dynamic Balancing |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The resultant of all centrifugal forces is zero. | The resultant of centrifugal forces AND the resultant couple are both zero. |
| **Condition** | $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (vector form) | $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta = 0$, $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta = 0$, $\sum m_i r_i d_i = 0$ |
| **Plane** | Single plane rotors | Multi-plane rotors |
| **Checks** | Translates only | Translates AND rotates |
| **Use** | Thin discs, flywheels | Shafts, propellers, turbines |

**[3 marks]** — Awarded for table with conditions. Diagram of multi-plane rotor is **bonus**.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Define gyroscopic couple. Write its expression and explain its significance for marine vessels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **gyroscopic couple** is the moment produced by a spinning rotor when it is forced to change its plane of rotation (i.e., undergo precession). It is the resistance offered by the angular momentum of the rotor against any change in orientation.

$$C = I \, \omega \, \omega_p$$

where $I$ = mass moment of inertia of rotor, $\omega$ = spin angular velocity, $\omega_p$ = precessional angular velocity.

**Significance for ships:** When a ship rolls, pitches, or steers, the spinning propeller creates a gyroscopic couple that affects the ship's motion. For example, during a sharp left turn, the bow tends to dip downward and the stern to rise. Naval architects must consider this in ship design.

**[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice: answer EITHER (a) OR (b) of each sub-question — typically. For this module, we provide TWO alternative 14-mark questions.

---

### 📘 Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A. (a) [7 Marks]** A shaft carries three masses in three different planes. The data is:

| Mass | $m$ (kg) | $r$ (mm) | $\theta$ | Axial distance from LHS (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 10 | 100 | 0° | 0 |
| B | 18 | 150 | 90° | 200 |
| C | 15 | 120 | 180° | 400 |

Find the balancing masses in planes **L (at 0 mm)** and **M (at 600 mm)**.

### Solution A(a) — Step-by-Step

#### Step 1 — Compute Mass-Radius Products

$$W_A = 10 \times 0.100 = 1.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$W_B = 18 \times 0.150 = 2.7 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$W_C = 15 \times 0.120 = 1.8 \text{ kg·m}$$

#### Step 2 — Resolve into X and Y Components

$$W_{Ax} = 1.0 \cos 0° = +1.00; \quad W_{Ay} = 1.0 \sin 0° = 0$$

$$W_{Bx} = 2.7 \cos 90° = 0; \quad W_{By} = 2.7 \sin 90° = +2.70$$

$$W_{Cx} = 1.8 \cos 180° = -1.80; \quad W_{Cy} = 1.8 \sin 180° = 0$$

#### Step 3 — Sum the Force Components

$$\sum W_x = 1.00 + 0 - 1.80 = -0.80 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\sum W_y = 0 + 2.70 + 0 = +2.70 \text{ kg·m}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute Couples About Plane L

Axial distances: $d_A = 0$, $d_B = 200$ mm $= 0.200$ m, $d_C = 400$ mm $= 0.400$ m.

For mass A ($d_A = 0$): couple contribution = 0.

For mass B:
$$(W_B d_B)_x = 0 \times 0.200 = 0; \quad (W_B d_B)_y = 2.70 \times 0.200 = +0.540$$

For mass C:
$$(W_C d_C)_x = -1.80 \times 0.400 = -0.720; \quad (W_C d_C)_y = 0 \times 0.400 = 0$$

#### Step 5 — Sum the Couple Components

$$\sum (Wd)_x = 0 + 0 - 0.720 = -0.720 \text{ kg·m²}$$

$$\sum (Wd)_y = 0 + 0.540 + 0 = +0.540 \text{ kg·m²}$$

#### Step 6 — Balancing Mass in Plane L

$$W_{bL} = \sqrt{(-0.80)^2 + (2.70)^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 7.29} = \sqrt{7.93} = 2.816 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\tan\alpha_L = \frac{2.70}{-0.80} = -3.375 \Rightarrow \alpha_L = 180° - 73.49° = 106.51° \text{ (second quadrant)}$$

Balancing mass direction in plane L (opposite): $\theta_{bL} = 106.51° - 180° = -73.49° \equiv 286.51°$.

#### Step 7 — Balancing Mass in Plane M ($d_M = 600$ mm $= 0.600$ m)

$$W_{bM} \times d_M = \sqrt{(-0.720)^2 + (0.540)^2} = \sqrt{0.518 + 0.292} = \sqrt{0.810} = 0.900 \text{ kg·m²}$$

$$W_{bM} = \frac{0.900}{0.600} = 1.500 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\tan\alpha_M = \frac{0.540}{-0.720} = -0.75 \Rightarrow \alpha_M = 180° - 36.87° = 143.13° \text{ (second quadrant)}$$

Balancing mass direction in plane M: $\theta_{bM} = 143.13° - 180° = -36.87° \equiv 323.13°$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer A(a):**
> - Plane L: $W_{bL} = 2.816$ kg·m at $\theta_{bL} = 286.51°$ (or $-73.49°$)
> - Plane M: $W_{bM} = 1.500$ kg·m at $\theta_{bM} = 323.13°$ (or $-36.87°$)

---

**A. (b) [7 Marks]** A marine turbine rotor of mass moment of inertia $4000$ kg·m² spins at $1500$ rpm. The ship steers to the right on a circular path of radius $500$ m at a forward speed of $30$ km/h. Find the gyroscopic couple and its effect.

### Solution A(b) — Step-by-Step

#### Step 1 — Convert Speeds

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$v = \frac{30 \times 1000}{3600} = 8.333 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\omega_p = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{8.333}{500} = 0.01667 \text{ rad/s}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Gyroscopic Couple

$$C = I \, \omega \, \omega_p = 4000 \times 157.08 \times 0.01667$$

$$C = 10{,}470 \text{ N·m} = 10.47 \text{ kN·m}$$

#### Step 3 — Effect on Ship

Since the ship is steering to the right, using the right-hand rule:
- Spin vector $\vec{\omega}$ along longitudinal (fore-aft) axis
- Precession vector $\vec{\omega}_p$ downward (for right turn)
- Active couple tends to pitch the ship: **stern dips and bow rises**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer A(b):** Active gyroscopic couple $C = 10.47$ kN·m. Effect: When turning RIGHT, the ship's stern dips DOWNWARD and the bow RISES.

---

### 📘 Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**B. (a) [7 Marks]** A ship rolls (oscillates about its longitudinal axis) with a period of $25$ s. The ship's propeller rotor has $I = 5000$ kg·m² and spins at $2000$ rpm clockwise when viewed from the stern. Calculate the active gyroscopic couple and explain its effect on the ship's heading.

### Solution B(a) — Step-by-Step

#### Step 1 — Spin Angular Velocity

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 2000}{60} = \frac{4000\pi}{60} = 209.44 \text{ rad/s}$$

#### Step 2 — Rolling (Precession) Angular Velocity

$$\omega_p = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{25} = 0.2513 \text{ rad/s}$$

#### Step 3 — Active Gyroscopic Couple

$$C = I \, \omega \, \omega_p = 5000 \times 209.44 \times 0.2513$$

$$C = 263{,}189 \text{ N·m} = 263.19 \text{ kN·m}$$

#### Step 4 — Effect on Ship's Heading

The rolling motion precesses about the longitudinal axis. The active gyroscopic couple appears about the **transverse (port-starboard) axis**, which tries to **yaw** the ship. The ship tends to **turn about a vertical axis** during rolling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer B(a):** Active gyroscopic couple $C = 263.19$ kN·m. Effect: The ship experiences a yawing tendency (turns sideways) while rolling.

---

**B. (b) [7 Marks]** A shaft rotates at $600$ rpm and carries four masses $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ at radii $100$, $150$, $200$, $125$ mm respectively. The angular positions are $0°$, $90°$, $180°$, $270°$ and the masses are $20$, $30$, $25$, $40$ kg respectively. Find the magnitude and angular position of the balancing mass if its radius is $200$ mm.

### Solution B(b) — Step-by-Step

#### Step 1 — Compute Mass-Radius Products

$$W_A = 20 \times 0.100 = 2.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$W_B = 30 \times 0.150 = 4.5 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$W_C = 25 \times 0.200 = 5.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$W_D = 40 \times 0.125 = 5.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

#### Step 2 — Resolve into X and Y

| Mass | $W$ | $\theta$ | $W\cos\theta$ | $W\sin\theta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 2.0 | 0° | +2.0 | 0 |
| B | 4.5 | 90° | 0 | +4.5 |
| C | 5.0 | 180° | -5.0 | 0 |
| D | 5.0 | 270° | 0 | -5.0 |

#### Step 3 — Sum Components

$$\sum W_x = 2.0 + 0 - 5.0 + 0 = -3.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\sum W_y = 0 + 4.5 + 0 - 5.0 = -0.5 \text{ kg·m}$$

#### Step 4 — Resultant Magnitude

$$\sum W = \sqrt{(-3.0)^2 + (-0.5)^2} = \sqrt{9.0 + 0.25} = \sqrt{9.25} = 3.041 \text{ kg·m}$$

#### Step 5 — Resultant Angle

Both components negative → third quadrant.

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{0.5}{3.0} = 0.1667, \quad \alpha_{ref} = 9.46°$$

$$\alpha = 180° + 9.46° = 189.46°$$

#### Step 6 — Balancing Mass

$$W_b = m_b \times r_b = 3.041 \text{ kg·m}$$

Given $r_b = 200$ mm $= 0.200$ m:

$$m_b = \frac{3.041}{0.200} = 15.21 \text{ kg}$$

#### Step 7 — Angle of Balancing Mass

$$\theta_b = 189.46° - 180° = 9.46°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer B(b):** Balancing mass $m_b = 15.21$ kg at $\theta_b = 9.46°$ (measured anticlockwise from reference of mass A).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU Exams (Examiner Insights)**
>
> 1. **Wrong sign of $\sin$ or $\cos$ component** — Students often forget quadrant correction. For angles in $90°$–$180°$, $\cos$ is **negative**; for $180°$–$270°$, both $\cos$ and $\sin$ are negative. **Loses 1–2 marks per occurrence.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s** — Always convert $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$. Using $\omega = N$ directly is the **#1 cause of wrong answers.**
>
> 3. **Confusing static and dynamic balance questions** — If the question says "different planes" or gives axial distances, it is **dynamic balance**. If all masses are in **one plane**, it is static balance.
>
> 4. **Wrong direction of gyroscopic effect on ships** — During a **LEFT turn**, the bow **dips DOWN** (does NOT rise!). Memorise using right-hand rule.
>
> 5. **Not verifying the couple polygon closes** — Always do a final check by drawing the vector polygon (or mathematically verifying that the components sum to zero).
>
> 6. **Unit inconsistency** — Use kg·m for mass-radius product, kg·m² for couple. Mixing units loses marks.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to specify the radius of the balancing mass** — In static balancing, the question usually gives $r_b$ — if not, you must state an assumed radius.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This and Read 5 Times Before Exam**

### ✅ Static Balancing
- [ ] Condition: $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (vector sum)
- [ ] Implies $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ AND $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$
- [ ] Single-plane rotor only
- [ ] Centrifugal force $F_c = m\omega^2 r$
- [ ] Balancing mass placed diametrically opposite to the resultant

### ✅ Dynamic Balancing
- [ ] Static balance (force = 0) PLUS couple = 0
- [ ] Force conditions: $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$, $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$
- [ ] Couple conditions: $\sum m_i r_i d_i \cos\theta_i = 0$, $\sum m_i r_i d_i \sin\theta_i = 0$
- [ ] Requires TWO balancing masses in TWO different planes
- [ ] Couple polygon method for verification
- [ ] Used for multi-plane rotors: turbines, propellers, multi-disc shafts

### ✅ Ship Gyroscopic Effects
- [ ] Active gyroscopic couple: $C = I\omega\omega_p$
- [ ] **Rolling** → yaws the ship (turns about vertical axis)
- [ ] **Pitching** → turns the ship sideways (yaws)
- [ ] **Steering/Turning** → pitches the ship (bow dips, stern rises or vice versa)
- [ ] Direction by right-hand rule: $\vec{C}_{active} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{\omega}_p$
- [ ] Precession rate for turning: $\omega_p = v/R$
- [ ] Precession rate for rolling/pitching: $\omega_p = 2\pi/T$

### ✅ Numerical Solving Checklist
- [ ] Draw a clear diagram with reference axes
- [ ] Tabulate masses, radii, angles, axial distances
- [ ] Compute $m_i r_i$ first
- [ ] Resolve into X and Y using $\cos$ and $\sin$
- [ ] Sum algebraically (signs from quadrant)
- [ ] For dynamic: compute $m_i r_i d_i$ separately
- [ ] Compute magnitude and angle of balancing vectors
- [ ] Add $180°$ for balancing direction
- [ ] State units clearly
- [ ] State direction of gyroscopic effect explicitly

### ✅ Quick Reference Conversions
- [ ] $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ (N in rpm → ω in rad/s)
- [ ] $\omega_p = 2\pi / T$ (T in seconds)
- [ ] $\omega_p = v / R$ (turning of ship)
- [ ] $v$ (km/h) → $v \times 5/18$ (m/s)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reference Plane Method for Balancing of Masses for Different Planes - Analytical and Graphical Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Reference Plane Method for Balancing of Masses for Different Planes - Analytical and Graphical Approach # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Reference Plane Method** is a simplified analytical and graphical technique employed in the dynamic balancing of a **rotor carrying masses distributed in several different axial planes**. Instead of placing a balancing mass in each plane where a disturbing mass exists (which is mechanically complex and uneconomical), this method **transfers all the centrifugal forces generated by the rotating masses to a single arbitrarily chosen reference plane** along the axis of rotation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)** > *In the reference plane method, the equivalent masses are mathematically transferred to a chosen reference plane. The two conditions for complete dynamic balancing are:* > 1. *The **resultant centrifugal force** of all masses (including transferred masses) must be zero.* > 2. *The **resultant couple** of all forces about the reference plane must be zero.* The force due to a mass $m_i$ located at a distance $l_i$ from the reference plane is transferred by: - **Force Component:** $F_i = m_i r_i \omega^2$ (transferred to reference plane, with same direction) - **Couple Component:** $C_i = F_i \times l_i = m_i r_i \omega^2 \cdot l_i$ (about the reference plane) The reference plane is also called the **polar plane** or **datum plane** and is conventionally chosen at the **center of the rotor span** for mathematical convenience, although any plane can be selected. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are pushing a **seesaw** at different points along its length. A small force applied at the far end can produce the same tipping effect (moment) as a much larger force applied near the pivot. In the same way, a small mass rotating at a **far-away plane** can produce a significant tilting moment on the shaft, just like a heavier mass at a closer plane. The **Reference Plane Method** is the mechanical engineer's trick to "move" all the rotating masses onto a single imaginary slice (the reference plane) by: 1. **Copying the force** directly onto that plane (so the force magnitude remains the same). 2. **Adding a "twist" (couple)** equal to force × distance, so the moment effect about the shaft is preserved. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary** > We don't physically move the masses. We mathematically replace the multi-plane problem with an **equivalent single-plane force-couple system**, then balance the resultant force and resultant couple with two correction masses. ## 1.3 Why Two Correction Masses? Since the system has **two degrees of freedom for imbalance** (a force vector in 2D and a couple vector in 2D), we need exactly: - **Two correction masses** (or balancing masses) placed in **two selected planes** to fully eliminate both the resultant force and the resultant couple. These two planes are usually denoted as the **Left Correction Plane (L)** and the **Right Correction Plane (R)**, situated at distances $L$ and $R$ from the reference plane, often at the **bearing locations** of the rotor. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |-----------|--------|------------------------| | Angular velocity | $\omega$ | **rad/s** | | Centrifugal force | $F_c$ | **N** | | Mass radius product | $mr$ | **kg·m** | | Plane distance | $l$ | **mm** or **m** | | Couple per unit $mr \omega^2$ | $mr \cdot l$ | **kg·m²** | > [!TIP] > For KTU numerical problems, $\omega^2$ is often treated as a common factor and cancelled out. The standard practice is to balance the **mass-radius products** ($m_i r_i$) rather than the actual forces. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theory The Reference Plane Method unfolds in a structured sequence: ### Step 1: Identify the Disturbing Masses - Note the mass ($m_i$), radius ($r_i$), angular position ($\theta_i$), and axial location ($l_i$) of every rotating mass. - Reference all axial distances from the chosen **reference plane** (positive in one direction, negative in the other). ### Step 2: Choose Correction Planes - Pick two planes where balancing masses will be placed — typically the planes of the bearings for practical access. - Denote their distances from the reference plane as $L$ (left) and $R$ (right). ### Step 3: Compute Resultant Force at Reference Plane The **resultant force vector** in the horizontal ($x$) and vertical ($y$) directions is: $$\Sigma F_x = \omega^2 \sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$ $$\Sigma F_y = \omega^2 \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$ Since $\omega^2 \neq 0$, the condition reduces to: $$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(1)}$$ $$\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(2)}$$ ### Step 4: Compute Resultant Couple at Reference Plane The **couple** produced by each force about the reference plane equals $F_i \times l_i$: $$\sum (m_i r_i l_i) \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(3)}$$ $$\sum (m_i r_i l_i) \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(4)}$$ ### Step 5: Place Correction Masses in Two Planes Let the correction masses be $m_L$ and $m_R$ at radii $r_L$ and $r_R$ in the left and right planes at distances $L$ and $R$ from the reference plane. Let their angular positions be $\phi_L$ and $\phi_R$. Applying equations (1)-(4) including the correction masses: $$m_L r_L \cos\phi_L + m_R r_R \cos\phi_R = -\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i$$ $$m_L r_L \sin\phi_L + m_R r_R \sin\phi_R = -\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i$$ $$m_L r_L L \cos\phi_L + m_R r_R R \cos\phi_R = -\sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i$$ $$m_L r_L L \sin\phi_L + m_R r_R R \sin\phi_R = -\sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i$$ ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Equation > [!NOTE] > **Force Equilibrium (Eq. 1 & 2)** — Ensures no net centrifugal force pushes the shaft sideways. This is essentially the **static balancing condition**. > [!NOTE] > **Couple Equilibrium (Eq. 3 & 4)** — Ensures no net twisting couple acts on the shaft, preventing wobble and vibration. This is the **dynamic balancing condition**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Insight** > For a system with $N$ masses in different planes, we have **4 equations** (2 force + 2 couple) and **4 unknowns** (the two $mr$ products and their two angular positions) in the two correction planes — provided the radii $r_L$ and $r_R$ are pre-decided. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---------|-------------|------| | 1 | $F_c = m r \omega^2$ | Centrifugal force of a rotating mass | N | | 2 | $\Sigma (m r \cos\theta) = 0$ | Static balance in $x$-direction | kg·m | | 3 | $\Sigma (m r \sin\theta) = 0$ | Static balance in $y$-direction | kg·m | | 4 | $\Sigma (m r l \cos\theta) = 0$ | Couple balance in $x$-direction | kg·m² | | 5 | $\Sigma (m r l \sin\theta) = 0$ | Couple balance in $y$-direction | kg·m² | | 6 | $R_f = \sqrt{(\Sigma m r \cos\theta)^2 + (\Sigma m r \sin\theta)^2}$ | Magnitude of resultant $mr$ vector | kg·m | | 7 | $\tan\alpha = \frac{\Sigma m r \sin\theta}{\Sigma m r \cos\theta}$ | Direction of resultant $mr$ vector | rad/deg | | 8 | $R_c = \sqrt{(\Sigma m r l \cos\theta)^2 + (\Sigma m r l \sin\theta)^2}$ | Magnitude of resultant couple vector | kg·m² | | 9 | $m_L r_L = \frac{R_c \cdot R}{(L+R) \cdot r_{ref}}$ | Magnitude of correction $mr$ in left plane | kg·m | | 10 | $m_R r_R = \frac{R_c \cdot L}{(L+R) \cdot r_{ref}}$ | Magnitude of correction $mr$ in right plane | kg·m | | 11 | $\phi_L = \alpha_c + 180°$ | Angular position of $m_L$ | degrees | | 12 | $\phi_R = \alpha_c + 180°$ | Angular position of $m_R$ | degrees | > [!CAUTION] > When using the simplified formulas (9) and (10), it is assumed that both correction masses are placed at the **same radius** $r_{ref}$ and at the **same angular position** as the resultant couple vector. The position is **opposite** to the couple direction (hence the $180°$ addition). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | |----------|-------------| | **Turbomachinery** | Balancing high-speed steam/gas turbine rotors (often >3000 rpm) | | **Automotive** | Balancing crankshafts, drive shafts, and propeller shafts | | **Aerospace** | Helicopter rotor blades, jet engine compressor discs | | **Heavy Machinery** | Centrifuges, pumps, large fans, paper mill rolls | | **Power Generation** | Hydro-turbine runners, generator armatures | > [!TIP] > In practice, this method is the foundation of **ISO 1940-1 balancing grades**, where the permissible residual unbalance ($G \cdot e$ in mm/s) depends on rotor mass and operational speed. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Four Balancing Equations ### 3.1.1 Setup and Free Body Diagram Consider a rotor of length $L_{total}$ rotating at constant angular velocity $\omega$ about the $z$-axis. The reference plane is the **$xy$-plane** located at $z = 0$. A disturbing mass $m_i$ is located at: - **Axial position:** $z = l_i$ (positive to the right) - **Radial position:** $r_i$ from the axis - **Angular position:** $\theta_i$ measured from the positive $x$-axis The position vector of the center of mass in the rotating frame is: $$\vec{r}_i = r_i \cos\theta_i \hat{i} + r_i \sin\theta_i \hat{j} + l_i \hat{k}$$ The acceleration vector (in the inertial frame, with $\theta_i = \omega t$): $$\vec{a}_i = -\omega^2 r_i \cos\theta_i \hat{i} - \omega^2 r_i \sin\theta_i \hat{j}$$ The centrifugal force vector is: $$\vec{F}_i = m_i \vec{a}_i = -m_i \omega^2 (r_i \cos\theta_i \hat{i} + r_i \sin\theta_i \hat{j})$$ ### 3.1.2 Force and Couple Resolution This force acts at the axial location $z = l_i$. To transfer it to the reference plane ($z = 0$): **Step 1:** Apply the same force at the reference plane (parallel translation). **Step 2:** Add a couple equal to the moment of the original force about the reference plane. The moment of $\vec{F}_i$ about the reference plane is: $$\vec{M}_i = \vec{r}_{transfer} \times \vec{F}_i = (l_i \hat{k}) \times \vec{F}_i$$ Expanding: $$\vec{M}_i = l_i \hat{k} \times [-m_i \omega^2 (r_i \cos\theta_i \hat{i} + r_i \sin\theta_i \hat{j})]$$ Using the cross product identities ($\hat{k} \times \hat{i} = \hat{j}$ and $\hat{k} \times \hat{j} = -\hat{i}$): $$\vec{M}_i = -m_i \omega^2 l_i r_i \cos\theta_i \hat{j} + m_i \omega^2 l_i r_i \sin\theta_i \hat{i}$$ Rearranging: $$\vec{M}_i = m_i \omega^2 l_i r_i \sin\theta_i \hat{i} - m_i \omega^2 l_i r_i \cos\theta_i \hat{j}$$ ### 3.1.3 Summing All Masses and Setting to Zero For **complete dynamic balance**, the sum of all forces AND the sum of all couples transferred to the reference plane must vanish. **Force equations (x and y components):** $$\sum_i m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$ $$\sum_i m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$ **Couple equations (x and y components):** $$\sum_i m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$ $$\sum_i m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$ > [!NOTE] > Note the **sign swap** in the couple equations. The $x$-couple depends on $\sin\theta_i$ and the $y$-couple on $\cos\theta_i$, opposite to the force equations. This is a direct consequence of the cross product. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example ### Problem Statement A shaft carries four masses in four different planes. The data is given below. **Find the magnitude and position of the balancing masses** in planes **L** and **R** at radii of **100 mm** each, located at the **left and right bearing positions** at distances of **0.5 m and 0.7 m** from the reference plane. | Mass (kg) | $r$ (mm) | Plane Distance $l$ (m) | $\theta$ (degrees) | |-----------|----------|------------------------|---------------------| | $m_1 = 10$ | $100$ | $+0.2$ | $0°$ | | $m_2 = 15$ | $120$ | $-0.1$ | $60°$ | | $m_3 = 20$ | $90$ | $+0.3$ | $120°$ | | $m_4 = 12$ | $110$ | $-0.2$ | $240°$ | ### Solution — Step-by-Step #### Step 1: Calculate $m_i r_i$ for each mass $$m_1 r_1 = 10 \times 0.100 = 1.000 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$m_2 r_2 = 15 \times 0.120 = 1.800 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$m_3 r_3 = 20 \times 0.090 = 1.800 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$m_4 r_4 = 12 \times 0.110 = 1.320 \text{ kg·m}$$ #### Step 2: Compute $\Sigma m_i r_i \cos\theta_i$ (Horizontal Component) $$\begin{aligned} \Sigma m_i r_i \cos\theta_i &= (1.000)(\cos 0°) + (1.800)(\cos 60°) + (1.800)(\cos 120°) + (1.320)(\cos 240°) \\ &= (1.000)(1) + (1.800)(0.5) + (1.800)(-0.5) + (1.320)(-0.5) \\ &= 1.000 + 0.900 - 0.900 - 0.660 \\ &= 0.340 \text{ kg·m} \end{aligned}$$ #### Step 3: Compute $\Sigma m_i r_i \sin\theta_i$ (Vertical Component) $$\begin{aligned} \Sigma m_i r_i \sin\theta_i &= (1.000)(\sin 0°) + (1.800)(\sin 60°) + (1.800)(\sin 120°) + (1.320)(\sin 240°) \\ &= (1.000)(0) + (1.800)(0.866) + (1.800)(0.866) + (1.320)(-0.866) \\ &= 0 + 1.5588 + 1.5588 - 1.1431 \\ &= 1.9745 \text{ kg·m} \end{aligned}$$ #### Step 4: Compute $\Sigma m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i$ (Couple Horizontal) $$\begin{aligned} \Sigma m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i &= (1.000)(0.2)(1) + (1.800)(-0.1)(0.5) + (1.800)(0.3)(-0.5) + (1.320)(-0.2)(-0.5) \\ &= 0.2000 - 0.0900 - 0.2700 + 0.1320 \\ &= -0.0280 \text{ kg·m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ #### Step 5: Compute $\Sigma m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i$ (Couple Vertical) $$\begin{aligned} \Sigma m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i &= (1.000)(0.2)(0) + (1.800)(-0.1)(0.866) + (1.800)(0.3)(0.866) + (1.320)(-0.2)(-0.866) \\ &= 0 - 0.1559 + 0.4676 + 0.2286 \\ &= 0.5403 \text{ kg·m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ #### Step 6: Solve the Four Linear Equations Let $m_L r_L = a$ and $m_R r_R = b$ at radii $r_L = r_R = 0.100$ m in planes L and R. **From force equations:** $$a \cos\phi_L + b \cos\phi_R = -0.340 \quad \text{...(I)}$$ $$a \sin\phi_L + b \sin\phi_R = -1.9745 \quad \text{...(II)}$$ **From couple equations (with $L = 0.5$ m, $R = 0.7$ m):** $$0.5 a \cos\phi_L + 0.7 b \cos\phi_R = +0.0280 \quad \text{...(III)}$$ $$0.5 a \sin\phi_L + 0.7 b \sin\phi_R = -0.5403 \quad \text{...(IV)}$$ **Subtracting (III) from (I) × 0.5:** $$0.5 b \cos\phi_R - 0.7 b \cos\phi_R = -0.170 + 0.0280 = -0.1420$$ $$-0.2 b \cos\phi_R = -0.1420$$ $$b \cos\phi_R = 0.710 \text{ kg·m}$$ **Subtracting (IV) from (II) × 0.5:** $$-0.2 b \sin\phi_R = -0.9873 + 0.5403 = -0.4470$$ $$b \sin\phi_R = 2.235 \text{ kg·m}$$ **Magnitude of $b = m_R r_R$:** $$b = \sqrt{0.710^2 + 2.235^2} = \sqrt{0.504 + 4.995} = \sqrt{5.499} = 2.345 \text{ kg·m}$$ **Angular position of $m_R$:** $$\phi_R = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2.235}{0.710}\right) = \tan^{-1}(3.148) = 72.36°$$ **Mass of $m_R$ (since $r_R = 0.100$ m):** $$m_R = \frac{2.345}{0.100} = 23.45 \text{ kg}$$ **Substituting back to find $a$:** $$a \cos\phi_L = -0.340 - 0.710 = -1.050 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$a \sin\phi_L = -1.9745 - 2.235 = -4.2095 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$a = \sqrt{1.050^2 + 4.2095^2} = \sqrt{1.1025 + 17.7199} = \sqrt{18.8224} = 4.338 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$\phi_L = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-4.2095}{-1.050}\right) = \tan^{-1}(4.009) \text{ in third quadrant} = 180° + 76.01° = 256.01°$$ **Mass of $m_L$:** $$m_L = \frac{4.338}{0.100} = 43.38 \text{ kg}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer** > - **Left Plane (L)**: $m_L = 43.38$ kg at $\phi_L = 256.01°$ > - **Right Plane (R)**: $m_R = 23.45$ kg at $\phi_R = 72.36°$ > - Both at radius $r = 100$ mm. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Quantities for Quick Reference | Quantity | Horizontal ($x$) | Vertical ($y$) | |----------|-------------------|-----------------| | Force ($mr$ term) | $\Sigma m r \cos\theta = 0.340$ | $\Sigma m r \sin\theta = 1.9745$ | | Couple ($mrl$ term) | $\Sigma m r l \cos\theta = -0.0280$ | $\Sigma m r l \sin\theta = 0.5403$ | | Correction $m_L r_L$ | $1.050$ (opposite) | $4.2095$ (opposite) | | Correction $m_R r_R$ | $0.710$ (opposite) | $2.235$ (opposite) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Free Body Diagram — Multi-Plane Masses on a Shaft ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph shaft["ROTOR SHAFT - MULTI-PLANE LAYOUT"] direction LR planeL["PLANE L
LEFT BEARING
L = +0.5 m
correction mL
rL = 100 mm"] refPlane["REFERENCE PLANE
z = 0
All forces and
couples transferred"] m1["m1 = 10 kg
r1 = 100 mm
l1 = +0.2 m
theta1 = 0 deg"] m2["m2 = 15 kg
r2 = 120 mm
l2 = -0.1 m
theta2 = 60 deg"] m3["m3 = 20 kg
r3 = 90 mm
l3 = +0.3 m
theta3 = 120 deg"] m4["m4 = 12 kg
r4 = 110 mm
l4 = -0.2 m
theta4 = 240 deg"] planeR["PLANE R
RIGHT BEARING
R = -0.7 m
correction mR
rR = 100 mm"] end planeL --- refPlane refPlane --- m2 m2 --- m1 m1 --- m3 m3 --- m4 m4 --- planeR ``` ## 4.2 Force and Couple Transfer Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TB start["Mass m_i in plane l_i"] start --> forceCalc["Compute Centrifugal Force
F_i = m_i times r_i times omega squared"] forceCalc --> vectorComp["Resolve F_i into
F_ix = m_i r_i cos theta_i
F_iy = m_i r_i sin theta_i"] vectorComp --> transfer["Transfer to Reference Plane"] transfer --> addForce["Add Force F_i directly
at reference plane"] transfer --> addCouple["Add Couple
C_ix = F_iy times l_i
C_iy = F_ix times l_i"] addForce --> sumForce["Sum of Forces at Ref Plane
Sigma F_x = 0
Sigma F_y = 0"] addCouple --> sumCouple["Sum of Couples at Ref Plane
Sigma C_x = 0
Sigma C_y = 0"] sumForce --> balEq["4 Balancing Equations"] sumCouple --> balEq balEq --> solve["Solve for mL and mR
in two correction planes"] solve --> result["Balanced Rotor"] ``` ## 4.3 Graphical Approach — Force Polygon and Couple Polygon ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph vectorSpace["VECTOR SPACE - FORCE AND COUPLE POLYGONS"] direction TB ox["ORIGIN O"] ox --> forceVec["FORCE VECTORS
Represent m times r values
Length proportional to m times r
Angle equal to theta"] forceVec --> draw1["Step 1 Draw m1r1 at 0 deg"] forceVec --> draw2["Step 2 Draw m2r2 at 60 deg"] forceVec --> draw3["Step 3 Draw m3r3 at 120 deg"] forceVec --> draw4["Step 4 Draw m4r4 at 240 deg"] draw4 --> forcePoly["Step 5 Force Polygon
Closure vector Rf
Resultant force vector"] forcePoly --> coupleStart["Step 6 COUPLE VECTORS
Represent m times r times l values"] coupleStart --> draw1c["Draw m1r1l1 at 0 deg"] coupleStart --> draw2c["Draw m2r2l2 at 60 deg"] coupleStart --> draw3c["Draw m3r3l3 at 120 deg"] coupleStart --> draw4c["Draw m4r4l4 at 240 deg"] draw4c --> couplePoly["Step 7 Couple Polygon
Closure vector Rc
Resultant couple vector"] couplePoly --> scale["Step 8 Scale to find mLrL and mRr
Using formulas 9 and 10"] end ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Complete Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TB input["INPUT DATA
m_i, r_i, theta_i, l_i
L and R correction plane locations
r_L and r_R correction radii"] input --> refCheck{"Reference Plane
Selected"} refCheck --> |No| chooseRef["Choose reference plane
typically at center of rotor"] refCheck --> |Yes| compute chooseRef --> compute compute["COMPUTE COMPONENTS
Compute m_i times r_i cos theta_i
Compute m_i times r_i sin theta_i
Compute m_i times r_i l_i cos theta_i
Compute m_i times r_i l_i sin theta_i"] compute --> methodChoice{"Choose Method"} methodChoice --> |Analytical| analytical["ANALYTICAL PATH
Set up 4 simultaneous equations
Solve 4 unknowns
a = mLrL, b = mRr
phiL, phiR"] methodChoice --> |Graphical| graphical["GRAPHICAL PATH
Draw force polygon to find Rf
Draw couple polygon to find Rc
Compute mLrL and mRr
from Rc, L, R"] analytical --> validate graphical --> validate validate["VALIDATE
Check resultant force equals zero
Check resultant couple equals zero
Report mL, phiL, mR, phiR"] validate --> output["OUTPUT BALANCED CONFIGURATION"] ``` ## 4.5 Graphical Approach Step-by-Step Description > [!NOTE] > **Graphical Procedure (KTU Standard)** > 1. **Force Polygon:** Draw vectors of magnitudes $m_i r_i$ at angles $\theta_i$ from a common origin. The closure side (from tip of last vector to origin) represents the **Resultant Force Vector** $R_f$ with magnitude $|\Sigma m_i r_i|$ and direction $\alpha_f$. > 2. **Couple Polygon:** Similarly, draw vectors of magnitudes $m_i r_i l_i$ at angles $\theta_i$. The closure side is the **Resultant Couple Vector** $R_c$ with direction $\alpha_c$. > 3. **Compute Correction $mr$ products:** > - $m_L r_L = \frac{R_c \cdot R}{L + R}$ (placed at $\alpha_c + 180°$) > - $m_R r_R = \frac{R_c \cdot L}{L + R}$ (placed at $\alpha_c + 180°$) > 4. **Verify** that the resultant force and couple are zero (or accept the small resultant as **residual unbalance**). > [!TIP] > **Engineering Tip for KTU Exam** > The graphical method is most useful for **3 or 4 masses**. For more masses, the analytical method is preferred. Always clearly label vector magnitudes and angles on the diagram to get full marks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **What is the reference plane method of balancing? Why is it preferred over direct balancing in multiple planes?** **Model Answer:** The reference plane method is a technique in which the centrifugal forces produced by rotating masses located in different planes are transferred mathematically to a single, arbitrarily chosen reference plane. This is done by replacing each force with an equivalent **force-couple system**, where the force is applied at the reference plane and a couple equal to the moment of the original force about the reference plane is added. It is preferred because: 1. **Economical:** Only **two correction masses** are needed (instead of one in each plane), reducing cost and complexity. 2. **Practical:** Correction masses can be placed in the **bearing planes** where access is convenient. 3. **Efficient:** Reduces an $N$-plane problem to a **single-plane force-couple system** that can be solved by simple 4-equation analysis. *(3 marks)* ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **State and explain the conditions for complete dynamic balancing of a rotor with masses in different planes.** **Model Answer:** For complete dynamic balancing, the following **four conditions** must be satisfied simultaneously: 1. **Resultant force in $x$-direction = 0:** $$\Sigma m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$ 2. **Resultant force in $y$-direction = 0:** $$\Sigma m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$ 3. **Resultant couple in $x$-direction = 0:** $$\Sigma m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$ 4. **Resultant couple in $y$-direction = 0:** $$\Sigma m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$ These four conditions ensure that the **resultant centrifugal force is zero** (static balance) and the **resultant moment is zero** (dynamic balance), preventing both translation and wobble of the rotor. *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] (14 Marks) **A rotating shaft carries four masses A, B, C, and D in four different planes. The data is given below. Determine the magnitude and angular position of the balancing masses to be placed in planes L and R at radii of 80 mm each. The planes L and R are located at 400 mm and 600 mm respectively to the left of the reference plane.** | Mass | $m$ (kg) | $r$ (mm) | Plane distance from ref (mm) | $\theta$ (degrees) | |------|----------|----------|-------------------------------|---------------------| | A | 12 | 100 | +200 | 0 | | B | 18 | 90 | -150 | 45 | | C | 16 | 110 | +250 | 135 | | D | 14 | 95 | -300 | 270 | #### Part (a) — Force Balance Computation (7 Marks) **Step 1: Calculate $m_i r_i$ values** | Mass | $m_i r_i$ (kg·m) | $\cos\theta_i$ | $\sin\theta_i$ | |------|------------------|----------------|----------------| | A | $12 \times 0.100 = 1.200$ | $1.000$ | $0.000$ | | B | $18 \times 0.090 = 1.620$ | $0.7071$ | $0.7071$ | | C | $16 \times 0.110 = 1.760$ | $-0.7071$ | $0.7071$ | | D | $14 \times 0.095 = 1.330$ | $0.000$ | $-1.000$ | **Step 2: Compute $\Sigma m_i r_i \cos\theta_i$ (x-component of force)** $$\begin{aligned} \Sigma m_i r_i \cos\theta_i &= (1.200)(1.000) + (1.620)(0.7071) + (1.760)(-0.7071) + (1.330)(0.000) \\ &= 1.200 + 1.1455 - 1.2445 + 0 \\ &= 1.1010 \text{ kg·m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Compute $\Sigma m_i r_i \sin\theta_i$ (y-component of force)** $$\begin{aligned} \Sigma m_i r_i \sin\theta_i &= (1.200)(0) + (1.620)(0.7071) + (1.760)(0.7071) + (1.330)(-1) \\ &= 0 + 1.1455 + 1.2445 - 1.330 \\ &= 1.0600 \text{ kg·m} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Resultant Force Vector** $$R_f = \sqrt{1.1010^2 + 1.0600^2} = \sqrt{1.2122 + 1.1236} = \sqrt{2.3358} = 1.5283 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$\alpha_f = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.0600}{1.1010}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.9628) = 43.91°$$ #### Part (b) — Couple Balance and Correction Masses (7 Marks) **Step 1: Calculate $m_i r_i l_i$ values** | Mass | $m_i r_i l_i$ (kg·m²) | |------|------------------------| | A | $1.200 \times 0.200 = 0.2400$ | | B | $1.620 \times (-0.150) = -0.2430$ | | C | $1.760 \times 0.250 = 0.4400$ | | D | $1.330 \times (-0.300) = -0.3990$ | **Step 2: Compute $\Sigma m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i$** $$\begin{aligned} \Sigma m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i &= (0.2400)(1) + (-0.2430)(0.7071) + (0.4400)(-0.7071) + (-0.3990)(0) \\ &= 0.2400 - 0.1718 - 0.3111 + 0 \\ &= -0.2429 \text{ kg·m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Compute $\Sigma m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i$** $$\begin{aligned} \Sigma m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i &= (0.2400)(0) + (-0.2430)(0.7071) + (0.4400)(0.7071) + (-0.3990)(-1) \\ &= 0 - 0.1718 + 0.3111 + 0.3990 \\ &= 0.5383 \text{ kg·m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Resultant Couple Vector** $$R_c = \sqrt{0.2429^2 + 0.5383^2} = \sqrt{0.0590 + 0.2898} = \sqrt{0.3488} = 0.5906 \text{ kg·m}^2$$ $$\alpha_c = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.5383}{-0.2429}\right) = 180° - 65.69° = 114.31° \text{ (Quadrant II)}$$ **Step 5: Correction Masses (Using $L = 0.400$ m, $R = 0.600$ m, $r_L = r_R = 0.080$ m)** $$m_L r_L = \frac{R_c \cdot R}{L + R} = \frac{0.5906 \times 0.600}{1.000} = 0.3544 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$m_L = \frac{0.3544}{0.080} = 4.430 \text{ kg}$$ $$m_R r_R = \frac{R_c \cdot L}{L + R} = \frac{0.5906 \times 0.400}{1.000} = 0.2362 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$m_R = \frac{0.2362}{0.080} = 2.953 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 6: Angular Positions** Both correction masses are placed at $\alpha_c + 180°$: $$\phi_L = \phi_R = 114.31° + 180° = 294.31°$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** > - $m_L = 4.430$ kg at $\phi_L = 294.31°$ in plane L > - $m_R = 2.953$ kg at $\phi_R = 294.31°$ in plane R > - Both at radius $r = 80$ mm --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2023] (14 Marks) — Internal Choice **A shaft is supported in two bearings 1.2 m apart. It carries three masses in three different planes between the bearings. The data is given below. The two correction masses are to be placed in the planes of the bearings. Find their magnitude and angular position, given that the reference plane is at the midpoint.** | Mass | $m$ (kg) | $r$ (mm) | Position from left bearing (m) | $\theta$ (degrees) | |------|----------|----------|-------------------------------|---------------------| | 1 | 8 | 120 | 0.2 | 60 | | 2 | 10 | 100 | 0.5 | 150 | | 3 | 6 | 150 | 0.9 | 270 | #### Part (a) — Setup and Reference Plane Conversion (7 Marks) **Step 1: Convert axial positions to reference plane (mid-point at 0.6 m from left bearing)** - $l_1 = 0.2 - 0.6 = -0.4$ m - $l_2 = 0.5 - 0.6 = -0.1$ m - $l_3 = 0.9 - 0.6 = +0.3$ m - Left bearing: $L = 0 - 0.6 = -0.6$ m - Right bearing: $R = 1.2 - 0.6 = +0.6$ m **Step 2: Calculate $m_i r_i$ values** - $m_1 r_1 = 8 \times 0.120 = 0.960$ kg·m - $m_2 r_2 = 10 \times 0.100 = 1.000$ kg·m - $m_3 r_3 = 6 \times 0.150 = 0.900$ kg·m **Step 3: Force components** | Mass | $m r \cos\theta$ | $m r \sin\theta$ | |------|------------------|------------------| | 1 | $0.960 \times 0.5 = 0.480$ | $0.960 \times 0.866 = 0.831$ | | 2 | $1.000 \times (-0.866) = -0.866$ | $1.000 \times 0.5 = 0.500$ | | 3 | $0.900 \times 0 = 0$ | $0.900 \times (-1) = -0.900$ | **Step 4: Sum of force components** $$\Sigma m r \cos\theta = 0.480 - 0.866 + 0 = -0.386 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$\Sigma m r \sin\theta = 0.831 + 0.500 - 0.900 = 0.431 \text{ kg·m}$$ #### Part (b) — Couple Balance and Solution (7 Marks) **Step 1: Calculate $m_i r_i l_i$** - $m_1 r_1 l_1 = 0.960 \times (-0.4) = -0.384$ kg·m² - $m_2 r_2 l_2 = 1.000 \times (-0.1) = -0.100$ kg·m² - $m_3 r_3 l_3 = 0.900 \times 0.3 = 0.270$ kg·m² **Step 2: Couple components** | Mass | $m r l \cos\theta$ | $m r l \sin\theta$ | |------|---------------------|---------------------| | 1 | $-0.384 \times 0.5 = -0.192$ | $-0.384 \times 0.866 = -0.333$ | | 2 | $-0.100 \times (-0.866) = 0.0866$ | $-0.100 \times 0.5 = -0.050$ | | 3 | $0.270 \times 0 = 0$ | $0.270 \times (-1) = -0.270$ | **Step 3: Sum of couple components** $$\Sigma m r l \cos\theta = -0.192 + 0.0866 + 0 = -0.1054 \text{ kg·m}^2$$ $$\Sigma m r l \sin\theta = -0.333 - 0.050 - 0.270 = -0.653 \text{ kg·m}^2$$ **Step 4: Apply the four-equation system** Let $m_L r_L = a$ and $m_R r_R = b$. Choose $r_L = r_R = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m. The four equations are: - $a \cos\phi_L + b \cos\phi_R = +0.386$ - $a \sin\phi_L + b \sin\phi_R = -0.431$ - $-0.6 \cdot a \cos\phi_L + 0.6 \cdot b \cos\phi_R = +0.1054$ - $-0.6 \cdot a \sin\phi_L + 0.6 \cdot b \sin\phi_R = +0.653$ **Step 5: Solve for $b$ (right plane)** Multiply the third equation by $1/0.6$ and subtract from the first: $$(1 - (-1)) b \cos\phi_R = 0.386 - 0.1054/0.6 = 0.386 - 0.1757 = 0.2103$$ $$2 b \cos\phi_R = 0.2103 \Rightarrow b \cos\phi_R = 0.1052$$ Similarly for the fourth: $$2 b \sin\phi_R = -0.431 - 0.653/0.6 = -0.431 - 1.0883 = -1.5193$$ $$b \sin\phi_R = -0.7597$$ **Step 6: Magnitude and angle of $b$** $$b = \sqrt{0.1052^2 + 0.7597^2} = \sqrt{0.0111 + 0.5771} = \sqrt{0.5882} = 0.7670 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$\phi_R = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-0.7597}{0.1052}\right) = -82.11° \text{ or } 277.89°$$ $$m_R = \frac{0.7670}{0.100} = 7.670 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 7: Solve for $a$ (left plane)** $$a \cos\phi_L = 0.386 - 0.1052 = 0.2808$$ $$a \sin\phi_L = -0.431 - (-0.7597) = 0.3287$$ $$a = \sqrt{0.2808^2 + 0.3287^2} = \sqrt{0.0788 + 0.1080} = \sqrt{0.1868} = 0.4323 \text{ kg·m}$$ $$\phi_L = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.3287}{0.2808}\right) = 49.49°$$ $$m_L = \frac{0.4323}{0.100} = 4.323 \text{ kg}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** > - $m_L = 4.323$ kg at $\phi_L = 49.49°$ (left bearing plane) > - $m_R = 7.670$ kg at $\phi_R = 277.89°$ (right bearing plane) > - Both at radius $r = 100$ mm --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Forgetting that $l_i$ is **positive on one side** and **negative on the other** of the reference plane. Many students use absolute values, leading to completely wrong couple components. > > 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in the same calculation. Always convert all distances to **meters** before computing $m_i r_i l_i$ in kg·m². > > 3. **Forgetting to Include the Correction Masses:** The four-equation system must include the **two correction mass terms** (e.g., $m_L r_L \cos\phi_L$). Students often solve for the resultant force/couple but forget to add the correction terms in the equations. > > 4. **Quadrant Ambiguity in Angles:** The $\tan^{-1}$ function gives angles in the first or fourth quadrant. If both $\cos$ and $\sin$ components are negative, the angle is in the **third quadrant** (add $180°$). KTU evaluators deduct 1 mark for wrong quadrant placement. > > 5. **Decoupling Force and Couple:** Many students solve the force and couple equations **separately** as if they were independent. This is INCORRECT in the general case — they are **coupled** through the same correction masses. The simplified "decoupled" formulas (9 & 10) are only valid when both corrections are placed at the **same angle**. > > 6. **Forgetting $\omega^2$ Cancellation:** While not always penalized, writing forces explicitly with $\omega^2$ when only $m r$ is needed clutters the solution and may invite deductions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** > > ✅ **Reference Plane Method** transfers all rotating masses to ONE chosen plane using **force + couple** equivalence. > > ✅ **Four conditions** for complete dynamic balance: 2 force equations + 2 couple equations. > > ✅ Only **two correction masses** are needed (placed in two chosen planes — usually the bearing planes). > > ✅ Standard form: $\Sigma m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ and $\Sigma m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ (force) and $\Sigma m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ and $\Sigma m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ (couple). > > ✅ **Sign convention** for $l_i$: positive on one side of the reference plane, negative on the other (strictly). > > ✅ **Graphical method:** force polygon and couple polygon, with closure vectors giving the **resultant $mr$ vector** $R_f$ and **resultant couple vector** $R_c$. > > ✅ **Simplified correction mass formulas:** > - $m_L r_L = \frac{R_c \cdot R}{L + R}$ (placed at $\alpha_c + 180°$) > - $m_R r_R = \frac{R_c \cdot L}{L + R}$ (placed at $\alpha_c + 180°$) > > ✅ **Quadrant rules:** > - (+, +) → 1st quadrant > - (−, +) → 2nd quadrant > - (−, −) → 3rd quadrant > - (+, −) → 4th quadrant > > ✅ Units consistency: $m$ in **kg**, $r$ and $l$ in **m**, $mrl$ in **kg·m²**. > > ✅ **Resultant force zero** = static balance; **Resultant couple zero** = dynamic balance. Both needed for **complete** dynamic balance. > > ✅ Engineering applications: turbines, IC engine crankshafts, propeller shafts, helicopter rotors, centrifuges.

topics.title: Single and Two Degree Freedom System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single and Two Degree Freedom System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Degree of Freedom (DOF)?

The **minimum number of independent coordinates** required to completely specify the configuration (position, orientation) of a vibrating system at any instant of time is called the **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of the system.

Formally, as per KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:

> **Degree of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent displacements (or generalized coordinates) needed to uniquely define the displaced position of a mass or rigid body in a vibrating system.

It is the most fundamental descriptor of a vibrating mechanical model and dictates whether the system is treated as a **lumped (discrete) parameter** model or a **distributed** model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For this module, we deal strictly with **lumped-parameter systems** (discrete systems) where mass, stiffness, and damping are concentrated at distinct points. Continuous systems are out of scope for Module 3.

## 1.2 Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) System

A **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) system** is one that requires only **one** independent coordinate to fully describe its vibration.

### Examples of SDOF Systems
| Physical System | Independent Coordinate |
|---|---|
| Mass-spring (mass on a spring) | Vertical displacement $x$ |
| Simple pendulum (small angle) | Angular displacement $\theta$ |
| A torsional shaft with a disc | Angular twist $\theta$ |
| Water sloshing in a U-tube | Height of water column $h$ |

### Canonical SDOF Model

The generalized SDOF model consists of:
- **Mass ($m$)** — represents inertia, unit **kg**
- **Spring stiffness ($k$)** — represents restoring force, unit **N/m**
- **Damping coefficient ($c$)** — represents energy dissipation, unit **Ns/m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Imagine a **building floor with a heavy air-conditioner unit** mounted on rubber pads. When the AC motor starts, it gives one tiny "push." The floor (with AC unit) bounces up and down. You only need **one number** (the up-down position) to describe where the floor is. That's SDOF — *one coordinate, one bounce, one story*.

## 1.3 Two Degree of Freedom (TDOF) System

A **Two Degree of Freedom (TDOF) system** requires **two independent coordinates** to fully describe the configuration of the system.

### Examples of TDOF Systems
| Physical System | Independent Coordinates |
|---|---|
| Two masses connected by springs on a frictionless surface | $x_1, x_2$ (displacements of each mass) |
| Vehicle suspension (sprung + unsprung mass) | $x_1$ (body), $x_2$ (axle) |
| Coupled pendulums | $\theta_1, \theta_2$ |
| Torsional system with two discs | $\theta_1, \theta_2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Picture **two boats tied together with ropes on a lake**. When one boat is pushed, BOTH boats move — and they don't move identically. You need **two numbers** (where Boat-1 is AND where Boat-2 is) to describe the system. That's TDOF — *two coordinates, two coupled stories*. They "talk" to each other through the connecting rope (spring).

## 1.4 Why Study SDOF and TDOF?

Almost every real mechanical structure (vehicle suspension, machine tool, building under earthquake, turbine blade) can be modeled initially as an SDOF or TDOF lumped system. The behavior predicted by these models — **natural frequency, mode shapes, resonance, transmissibility** — directly drives engineering decisions on:

- **Machine design** — avoid resonance operation
- **Vibration isolation** — protect sensitive instruments
- **Dynamic absorber design** — cancel harmful vibrations
- **Structural safety** — predict failure under cyclic loads

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SDOF System — Equation of Motion

For the canonical mass–spring–damper SDOF system subjected to a time-varying force $F(t)$:

$$
m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)
$$

where:
- $m$ = mass (kg)
- $c$ = viscous damping coefficient (Ns/m)
- $k$ = spring stiffness (N/m)
- $x$ = displacement (m)
- $\dot{x}$ = velocity (m/s)
- $\ddot{x}$ = acceleration (m/s²)
- $F(t)$ = applied external force (N)

### 2.1.1 Free Undamped Vibration

When $c = 0$ and $F(t) = 0$:

$$
m\ddot{x} + kx = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0
$$

Solution:

$$
x(t) = X \cos(\omega_n t) + Y \sin(\omega_n t) = A \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)
$$

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \;\;\text{(rad/s)} \quad ; \quad f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} \;\;\text{(Hz)} \quad ; \quad T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} \;\;\text{(s)}
$$

### 2.1.2 Free Damped Vibration

With damping ($c > 0$) and $F(t) = 0$:

$$
\ddot{x} + 2\zeta \omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0
$$

where the **damping ratio** is:

$$
\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{2m\omega_n} = \frac{c}{c_c}
$$

and $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ is the **critical damping coefficient**.

The **damped natural frequency** is:

$$
\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}
$$

Three regimes:
| Damping Ratio | Behavior |
|---|---|
| $\zeta = 0$ | Undamped — pure sinusoidal oscillation |
| $0 < \zeta < 1$ | **Underdamped** — decaying oscillation |
| $\zeta = 1$ | Critically damped — fastest non-oscillatory return |
| $\zeta > 1$ | Overdamped — slow non-oscillatory return |

### 2.1.3 Forced Harmonic Vibration (Magnification Factor)

For excitation $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$, the steady-state amplitude is:

$$
X = \frac{F_0 / k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} \quad ; \quad r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n}
$$

The **Magnification Factor (MF)** or **Dynamic Amplifier**:

$$
\text{MF} = \frac{X}{X_{\text{static}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} \quad ; \quad X_{\text{static}} = \frac{F_0}{k}
$$

### 2.1.4 Transmissibility

Ratio of transmitted force $F_T$ to applied force $F_0$:

$$
\text{TR} = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **vibration isolation**, we require $r > \sqrt{2}$. Below $r = \sqrt{2}$, transmissibility is **amplified** — disastrous. Above $r = \sqrt{2}$, transmissibility is **attenuated** — desirable.

## 2.2 TDOF System — Equations of Motion

For two masses $m_1, m_2$ connected by springs $k_1, k_2, k_3$ (or a generic TDOF):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_{11} & k_{12} \\ k_{21} & k_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

In compact form: $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$.

### 2.2.1 Free Vibration — Natural Frequencies

Assume harmonic solution $x_i = A_i \sin(\omega t)$:

$$
(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{M}) \mathbf{A} = \mathbf{0}
$$

Non-trivial solution requires:

$$
\det(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{M}) = 0
$$

This is the **frequency (characteristic) equation**, giving two natural frequencies $\omega_1, \omega_2$.

### 2.2.2 Mode Shapes

Substituting each $\omega_i$ back into $(\mathbf{K} - \omega_i^2 \mathbf{M}) \mathbf{A}^{(i)} = \mathbf{0}$ gives the **mode shape vector** $\mathbf{A}^{(i)}$ (typically normalized by setting one entry to 1).

### 2.2.3 Principal / Normal Coordinates

Using the **modal matrix** $\mathbf{P} = [\mathbf{A}^{(1)} \;\; \mathbf{A}^{(2)}]$, the coordinates $\mathbf{x}$ are transformed to principal coordinates $\mathbf{q}$ via $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{q}$.

In $\mathbf{q}$, the equations decouple:

$$
\mathbf{P}^T \mathbf{M} \mathbf{P} \ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{P}^T \mathbf{K} \mathbf{P} \mathbf{q} = \mathbf{0}
$$

Each equation in $\mathbf{q}$ becomes independent — equivalent to **two independent SDOF systems**.

### 2.2.4 Forced Vibration (Matrix Form)

$$
\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{F}(t)
$$

Using modal analysis (assuming proportional damping), the steady-state response is the superposition of contributions from each mode.

### 2.2.5 Dynamic Vibration Absorber

A secondary mass-spring system tuned to the forcing frequency of the primary system. The absorber's natural frequency is set to:

$$
\omega_a = \sqrt{\frac{k_a}{m_a}}
$$

At exact tuning, the primary mass $m_1$ becomes stationary when $\omega = \omega_a$ (no damping in absorber).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — Pin this for last-minute revision.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Natural frequency (SDOF) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Natural frequency (Hz) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Time period | $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{m/k}$ | s |
| 4 | Critical damping | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ | Ns/m |
| 5 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = c / c_c = c / (2\sqrt{km})$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Damped natural freq. | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| 7 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$ | for underdamped |
| 8 | Magnification factor | $\text{MF} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ |
| 9 | Transmissibility | $\text{TR} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | force ratio |
| 10 | Isolation threshold | $r > \sqrt{2}$ | for vibration isolation |
| 11 | TDOF char. eqn. | $\det(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{M}) = 0$ | gives $\omega_1, \omega_2$ |
| 12 | Mode shape relation | $(\mathbf{K} - \omega_i^2 \mathbf{M})\mathbf{A}^{(i)} = \mathbf{0}$ | eigenvector |
| 13 | Absorber tuning | $\omega_a = \omega_{\text{forcing}}$ | dynamic absorber |
| 14 | Absorber amplitude | $X_1 = 0, X_2 = F_0/k_a$ | at exact tuning |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SDOF modeling** — building floors under foot traffic, vehicle engine on mounts, instrument isolation tables
- **TDOF modeling** — vehicle suspension (bounce + pitch), coupled pendulums in clocks, building floors with secondary equipment
- **Vibration absorbers** — Tall skyscrapers (e.g., Taipei 101's 660-ton pendulum), power transmission lines, helicopter blade damping
- **Resonance prevention** — every rotating machine (turbine, pump, motor) must operate away from $\omega_n$ of supporting structure

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: SDOF Natural Frequency from Newton's Second Law

**Setup:** A mass $m$ on a smooth horizontal surface connected to a fixed wall via a linear spring of stiffness $k$. Mass displaced by $x$ from equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Free body diagram and force balance:**
At displacement $x$, the spring exerts a restoring force $-kx$ on the mass (negative sign because the force opposes displacement).

Newton's second law:

$$
m \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -kx
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to standard form:**

$$
m \ddot{x} + kx = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m} x = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with standard SHM equation:**
The standard simple harmonic motion equation is:

$$
\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0
$$

By comparison:

$$
\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}}
$$

**Step 4 — General solution:**

$$
x(t) = C_1 \cos(\omega_n t) + C_2 \sin(\omega_n t)
$$

Using initial conditions $x(0) = x_0$, $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$:

$$
C_1 = x_0, \quad C_2 = \frac{v_0}{\omega_n}
$$

So:

$$
x(t) = x_0 \cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n} \sin(\omega_n t)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Damped Natural Frequency

**Setup:** Add viscous damper with coefficient $c$ parallel to spring.

**Step 1 — Force balance:**
Damping force is proportional to velocity, opposing motion:

$$
F_{\text{damp}} = -c \dot{x}
$$

Newton's second law:

$$
m\ddot{x} = -kx - c\dot{x}
$$

**Step 2 — Standard form:**

$$
\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$:**

$$
\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Assume solution $x(t) = e^{st}$:**
The characteristic equation:

$$
s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0
$$

Roots:

$$
s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Case $\zeta < 1$ (underdamped):**
Let $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$. Then:

$$
x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left( C_1 \cos\omega_d t + C_2 \sin\omega_d t \right)
$$

The system oscillates at $\omega_d$ inside an exponentially decaying envelope.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Steady-State Forced Vibration Response

**Setup:** System subjected to harmonic excitation $F(t) = F_0 \cos(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Governing ODE:**

$$
m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — Assume steady-state solution:**

$$
x_p(t) = X \cos(\omega t - \phi)
$$

Compute derivatives:

$$
\dot{x}_p = -\omega X \sin(\omega t - \phi), \quad \ddot{x}_p = -\omega^2 X \cos(\omega t - \phi)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the ODE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m[-\omega^2 X \cos(\omega t - \phi)] + c[-\omega X \sin(\omega t - \phi)] + k[X \cos(\omega t - \phi)] = F_0 \cos(\omega t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Group $\cos$ and $\sin$ terms. After projecting onto $\cos(\omega t - \phi)$ basis and equating coefficients, one obtains:

$$
(k - m\omega^2)X \cos\phi - c\omega X \sin\phi = F_0
$$

$$
(k - m\omega^2)X \sin\phi + c\omega X \cos\phi = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the second equation for $\phi$:**

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2} = \frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Square and add both equations, then divide by $k^2$:**

$$
\left[ \frac{X}{X_{\text{static}}} \right]^2 = \frac{1}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MF} = \frac{X}{F_0/k} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}
$$

where $r = \omega / \omega_n$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: TDOF Natural Frequencies

**Setup:** Two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$, three springs $k_1$ (left wall to $m_1$), $k_2$ (between $m_1$ and $m_2$), $k_3$ ($m_2$ to right wall).

**Step 1 — Free body of $m_1$:**
Spring 1 pulls left when $m_1$ moves right; spring 2 pulls right when $m_2$ moves further than $m_1$.

$$
m_1 \ddot{x}_1 = -k_1 x_1 + k_2(x_2 - x_1)
$$

**Step 2 — Free body of $m_2$:**

$$
m_2 \ddot{x}_2 = -k_2(x_2 - x_1) - k_3 x_2
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$
m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2) x_1 - k_2 x_2 = 0
$$

$$
m_2 \ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + (k_2 + k_3) x_2 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Matrix form:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_1+k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2+k_3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Assume $x_1 = A_1 \sin\omega t$, $x_2 = A_2 \sin\omega t$:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} k_1+k_2 - \omega^2 m_1 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2+k_3 - \omega^2 m_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} A_1 \\ A_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Non-trivial solution:**

$$
\det = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; (k_1+k_2 - \omega^2 m_1)(k_2+k_3 - \omega^2 m_2) - k_2^2 = 0
$$

This is a quadratic in $\omega^2$. Solving yields two natural frequencies $\omega_1 < \omega_2$.

**Step 7 — Mode shapes:**
For each $\omega_i$, the first row gives the ratio:

$$
\frac{A_2}{A_1}\bigg|_i = \frac{k_1+k_2 - \omega_i^2 m_1}{k_2} = \frac{k_2}{k_2+k_3 - \omega_i^2 m_2}
$$

(consistent due to symmetry of determinant condition).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — SDOF

> **Problem:** $m = 10$ kg, $k = 4000$ N/m, $c = 40$ Ns/m. Find (i) $\omega_n$, (ii) $\zeta$, (iii) $c_c$.

**Solution:**

(i) $\omega_n = \sqrt{4000/10} = \sqrt{400} = \mathbf{20 \;\text{rad/s}}$

(ii) $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{4000 \times 10} = 2\sqrt{40000} = 2(200) = \mathbf{400 \;\text{Ns/m}}$

(iii) $\zeta = c/c_c = 40/400 = \mathbf{0.1}$ (lightly damped)

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — TDOF

> **Problem:** $m_1 = m_2 = m$, $k_1 = k_3 = k$, $k_2 = 2k$. Find natural frequencies and mode shapes.

**Step 1 — Mass and stiffness matrices:**

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} m & 0 \\ 0 & m \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 3k & -2k \\ -2k & 3k \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Characteristic equation:**

$$
\det(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{M}) = (3k - \omega^2 m)^2 - (2k)^2 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Expand:**

$$
9k^2 - 6k\omega^2 m + \omega^4 m^2 - 4k^2 = 0
$$

$$
\omega^4 m^2 - 6k\omega^2 m + 5k^2 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve quadratic in $\omega^2 m / k$.** Let $\lambda = \omega^2 m / k$:

$$
\lambda^2 - 6\lambda + 5 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; (\lambda - 1)(\lambda - 5) = 0
$$

So $\lambda_1 = 1, \lambda_2 = 5$:

$$
\boxed{\omega_1 = \sqrt{k/m}, \quad \omega_2 = \sqrt{5k/m}}
$$

**Step 5 — Mode shapes:**
For $\omega_1^2 = k/m$: $(3k - k)A_1 - 2k A_2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 2k A_1 = 2k A_2 \;\Rightarrow\; A_2 = A_1$.

First mode (symmetric — both masses move in phase):

$$
\mathbf{A}^{(1)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

For $\omega_2^2 = 5k/m$: $(3k - 5k)A_1 - 2k A_2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; -2k A_1 = 2k A_2 \;\Rightarrow\; A_2 = -A_1$.

Second mode (antisymmetric — masses move out of phase):

$$
\mathbf{A}^{(2)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Degree of Freedom. A torsional system consists of a shaft of stiffness $k = 2 \times 10^5$ Nm/rad fixed to a disc of moment of inertia $I = 50$ kg·m². Calculate its natural frequency.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The minimum number of independent coordinates required to completely specify the configuration of a vibrating system at any instant is called its Degree of Freedom (DOF).

**Calculation (1 mark):**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{I}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{50}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

$$
f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{63.25}{2\pi} = 10.07 \;\text{Hz}
$$

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between undamped and damped natural frequencies. When does the system exhibit critical damping?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Undamped $\omega_n$ | Damped $\omega_d$ |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\sqrt{k/m}$ | $\omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ |
| Energy | Conservative | Dissipative |
| Amplitude | Constant | Decays exponentially |

**Critical damping (1 mark):** Occurs when $\zeta = 1$, i.e., $c = c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$. It is the **smallest damping** that prevents oscillation — the system returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time** without overshoot.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU ESE - Module 3 - Dec 2023 - Set A]`

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion of a damped single degree of freedom system subjected to a harmonic force $F_0 \sin\omega t$. Obtain the expression for **magnification factor** and **phase angle**. Plot a typical frequency response curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine of mass 200 kg is mounted on springs of total stiffness $4 \times 10^5$ N/m with a damping ratio of 0.2. The machine operates at 1500 rpm. Find (i) the magnification factor and (ii) the transmitted force. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Equation of motion (2 marks):**
Newton's second law with all forces acting on mass:

$$
m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin\omega t
$$

**Step 2 — Assume steady-state solution (1 mark):**

$$
x_p(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and equate coefficients (2 marks):**
After differentiating and substituting, we obtain:

$$
X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}, \quad \tan\phi = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Magnification factor (1 mark):** Divide numerator and denominator by $k$ and use $r = \omega/\omega_n$:

$$
\text{MF} = \frac{X}{F_0/k} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Frequency response sketch (1 mark):**
MF starts near 1 for $r \to 0$, rises to peak $1/(2\zeta)$ at $r = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$, then decays as $1/r^2$ for large $r$.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** $m = 200$ kg, $k = 4 \times 10^5$ N/m, $\zeta = 0.2$, $N = 1500$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Natural frequency (1 mark):**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 10^5}{200}} = \sqrt{2000} = 44.72 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Operating frequency (1 mark):**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Frequency ratio (1 mark):**

$$
r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{157.08}{44.72} = 3.512
$$

**Step 4 — Magnification factor (2 marks):**

$$
\text{MF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 3.512^2)^2 + (2 \times 0.2 \times 3.512)^2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 12.33)^2 + (1.405)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{128.5 + 1.974}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{130.5}} = 0.0875}
$$

**Step 5 — Transmitted force (2 marks):**

$$
\text{TR} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2 \times 0.2 \times 3.512)^2}{(1 - 3.512^2)^2 + (1.405)^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 1.974}{130.5}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.974}{130.5}} = 0.151
$$

Static force $F_0$ can be assumed 1 N (or given separately). For 1 N excitation:

$$
F_T = 0.151 \;\text{N}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - **Common mistake (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. Many students use $N$ directly, getting wrong $r$.
> - **Common mistake (–1 mark):** Using $\omega_n$ in Hz instead of rad/s. All formulas here use **rad/s**.
> - **Common mistake (–1 mark):** Confusing magnification factor with transmissibility. They are NOT the same — MF compares displacement, TR compares transmitted force.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU ESE - Module 3 - May 2024 - Set B]`

**(a)** For a two-degree-of-freedom system with masses $m_1, m_2$ connected by three springs $k_1, k_2, k_3$ (wall–$m_1$–$m_2$–wall), derive the equations of motion in matrix form. Explain the method to determine natural frequencies and mode shapes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system $m_1 = 20$ kg, $m_2 = 30$ kg, $k_1 = k_3 = 10000$ N/m, $k_2 = 5000$ N/m, determine (i) natural frequencies and (ii) mode shapes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Equations of motion (3 marks):**
Newton's law applied to each mass:

$$
m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1+k_2) x_1 - k_2 x_2 = 0
$$

$$
m_2 \ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + (k_2+k_3) x_2 = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Matrix form (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_1+k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2+k_3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

i.e., $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 3 — Method to find natural frequencies & mode shapes (2 marks):**
Assume $x_i = A_i \sin\omega t$, substitute to get eigenvalue problem $(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{M})\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{0}$. Setting the determinant to zero gives the characteristic equation; its roots are $\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2$. Substituting each back yields the mode shape vectors $\mathbf{A}^{(1)}, \mathbf{A}^{(2)}$.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Stiffness matrix (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 10000+5000 & -5000 \\ -5000 & 5000+10000 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 15000 & -5000 \\ -5000 & 15000 \end{bmatrix} \;\text{N/m}
$$

$$
\mathbf{M} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & 30 \end{bmatrix} \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Characteristic equation (2 marks):**

$$
\det(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{M}) = 0
$$

$$
(15000 - 20\omega^2)(15000 - 30\omega^2) - (5000)^2 = 0
$$

Expand:

$$
2.25 \times 10^8 - 6 \times 10^5 \omega^2 - 3 \times 10^5 \omega^2 + 600 \omega^4 - 2.5 \times 10^7 = 0
$$

$$
600 \omega^4 - 9 \times 10^5 \omega^2 + 2 \times 10^8 = 0
$$

Divide by 100:

$$
6\omega^4 - 9000\omega^2 + 2 \times 10^6 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve quadratic in $\omega^2$ (2 marks):**

$$
\omega^2 = \frac{9000 \pm \sqrt{9000^2 - 4 \times 6 \times 2 \times 10^6}}{2 \times 6}
$$

$$
= \frac{9000 \pm \sqrt{8.1 \times 10^7 - 4.8 \times 10^7}}{12} = \frac{9000 \pm \sqrt{3.3 \times 10^7}}{12}
$$

$$
\sqrt{3.3 \times 10^7} = 5744.56
$$

$$
\omega_1^2 = \frac{9000 - 5744.56}{12} = \frac{3255.44}{12} = 271.29
$$

$$
\omega_2^2 = \frac{9000 + 5744.56}{12} = \frac{14744.56}{12} = 1228.71
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_1 = 16.47 \;\text{rad/s}, \quad \omega_2 = 35.05 \;\text{rad/s}}
$$

**Step 4 — Mode shapes (2 marks):**
For $\omega_1^2 = 271.29$:

$$
(15000 - 20 \times 271.29) A_1 - 5000 A_2 = 0
$$

$$
(15000 - 5425.8) A_1 = 5000 A_2 \;\Rightarrow\; 9574.2 A_1 = 5000 A_2 \;\Rightarrow\; A_2/A_1 = 1.915
$$

First mode (in-phase): $\mathbf{A}^{(1)} = \{1, \;\; 1.915\}^T$

For $\omega_2^2 = 1228.71$:

$$
(15000 - 20 \times 1228.71) A_1 - 5000 A_2 = 0
$$

$$
(15000 - 24574.2) A_1 = 5000 A_2 \;\Rightarrow\; -9574.2 A_1 = 5000 A_2 \;\Rightarrow\; A_2/A_1 = -1.915
$$

Second mode (out-of-phase): $\mathbf{A}^{(2)} = \{1, \;\; -1.915\}^T$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - **Common mistake (–2 marks):** Sign error in stiffness matrix off-diagonals. Off-diagonal entry must be **$-k_2$**, NOT $+k_2$. This is a **very common error** and examiner will deduct full 2 marks.
> - **Common mistake (–2 marks):** Forgetting to multiply by mass when forming $\mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{M}$. Students often write $\mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{I}$, missing the mass weighting.
> - **Common mistake (–1 mark):** Not normalizing mode shapes. Either convention is fine, but must be clearly stated.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Single & Two DOF Systems**

- **DOF** = minimum number of independent coordinates needed to fully specify a vibrating system's configuration.
- **SDOF system**: standard form $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$.
- **Natural frequency**: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$; period $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$; **never use Hz units directly in formulas** — convert to rad/s.
- **Damping ratio** $\zeta = c / c_c$ where $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$. For underdamped vibration, $\zeta < 1$; for critical, $\zeta = 1$; overdamped, $\zeta > 1$.
- **Damped natural frequency**: $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$.
- **Magnification Factor** = $1 / \sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$; peaks at $r \approx 1$ for small damping (resonance).
- **Transmissibility** differs from MF — it is the ratio of **transmitted force** to applied force.
- **Vibration isolation** is effective only when $r > \sqrt{2}$.
- **TDOF system**: matrix equation $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$. Off-diagonal stiffness terms are **negative** (coupling).
- **Natural frequencies** come from $\det(\mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{M}) = 0$ — a polynomial of degree $n$ in $\omega^2$ for an $n$-DOF system.
- **Mode shapes** are eigenvectors; they tell the **shape** of vibration at each natural frequency.
- **First mode** is always lowest frequency; masses tend to move **in phase** (symmetric mode).
- **Higher modes** have masses moving **out of phase** (antisymmetric modes).
- **Dynamic vibration absorber**: a secondary tuned mass-spring used to suppress primary system vibration at a specific forcing frequency.
- **Always** distinguish **forced response** (steady-state) from **transient response** (decaying).
- **Units** — keep $m$ in kg, $k$ in N/m, $c$ in Ns/m, $\omega$ in rad/s. Inconsistent units = lost marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> In KTU 2024 ESE, problems often ask BOTH free and forced vibration of SDOF in one question. Practice writing the **complete solution** in **one uninterrupted flow** — never skip stating the governing ODE before solving. Examiners allocate **2 marks** for the equation of motion alone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Free Vibration Responses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview of Free Vibration Responses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Free Vibration** is a type of oscillatory motion of a mechanical system that occurs when the system is disturbed from its equilibrium position and then allowed to move *without the influence of any external (time-varying) force*. The only forces acting on the mass during motion are the **restoring force** (spring force) and, depending on the model, the **damping force**.

In the context of Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) systems, the general equation of motion for free vibration is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of the system (**kg**)
- $c$ = viscous damping coefficient (**Ns/m**)
- $k$ = stiffness of the spring (**N/m**)
- $x$ = displacement from equilibrium position (**m**)
- $\dot{x}$ = velocity (**m/s**)
- $\ddot{x}$ = acceleration (**m/s²**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3, the *Overview of Free Vibration Responses* establishes the foundation for analyzing **transient behavior** of SDOF systems before moving to forced vibration. Students must clearly distinguish between **undamped** and **damped** responses.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

Imagine you pluck the string of a guitar and then release it. Once released, **no one is pushing the string** — yet the string continues to oscillate back and forth until it eventually stops. Why does it stop? Because of **internal friction** (damping) in the string and the surrounding air.

- **Without damping (Ideal Case):** A perfectly frictionless mass-spring system in a vacuum would oscillate **forever** with the same amplitude — like a theoretical "perpetual swing."
- **With damping (Real Case):** The amplitude gradually **decays** with time, similar to a swing in a playground that eventually comes to rest due to air resistance and friction at the pivot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - *Free Vibration* → No external excitation force after initial disturbance.
> - *Forced Vibration* → System is driven by a continuous external force (covered in later modules).

### 1.3 Classification of Free Vibration Responses

Free vibration responses are classified into **three fundamental categories** based on the damping ratio ($\zeta$):

| Damping Condition | Damping Ratio $\zeta$ | Response Type | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| No damping | $\zeta = 0$ | Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) | Ideal pendulum in vacuum |
| Light damping | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Underdamped (oscillatory decay) | Car suspension after a bump |
| Critical damping | $\zeta = 1$ | Critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory return) | Door-closing pneumatic piston |
| Heavy damping | $\zeta > 1$ | Overdamped (slow non-oscillatory return) | Cushioned shock absorbers |

where the **damping ratio** is defined as:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

and $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ is the **critical damping coefficient**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Undamped Free Vibration

When $c = 0$, the equation of motion reduces to:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

Rearranging gives the classical harmonic oscillator equation:

$$\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ is the **natural (undamped) circular frequency** in **rad/s**.

The general solution is:

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$$

Or equivalently, in amplitude-phase form:

$$x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$$

where:
- $X$ = amplitude of vibration (**m**)
- $\phi$ = phase angle (**rad**)
- $A = X\sin\phi$, $B = X\cos\phi$ are constants determined by **initial conditions**

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Damped Free Vibration

When $c \neq 0$, the equation becomes:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Dividing throughout by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

The solution depends on the roots of the **characteristic equation**:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

which yields:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

#### Case 1: Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$)

The roots are **complex conjugates**, producing oscillatory decay:

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ is the **damped natural frequency**.

#### Case 2: Critically Damped ($\zeta = 1$)

The roots are **real and equal**, producing the fastest non-oscillatory return:

$$x(t) = (A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$$

#### Case 3: Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$)

The roots are **real and distinct**, producing a slow non-oscillatory return:

$$x(t) = A e^{s_1 t} + B e^{s_2 t}$$

with $s_1, s_2$ both negative.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | **rad/s** |
| 2 | Natural Frequency (Hz) | $f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi}$ | **Hz** |
| 3 | Critical Damping Coefficient | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | **Ns/m** |
| 4 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Damped Natural Frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | **rad/s** (valid for $\zeta < 1$) |
| 6 | Undamped Response | $x(t) = X\sin(\omega_n t + \phi)$ | Oscillates indefinitely |
| 7 | Underdamped Response | $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$ | Decaying oscillation |
| 8 | Logarithmic Decrement | $\delta = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \approx 2\pi\zeta$ | For small $\zeta$ |
| 9 | Decay Ratio | $\dfrac{x_1}{x_2} = e^{\delta}$ | Ratio of successive peaks |
| 10 | Period of Damped Oscillation | $T_d = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$ | **seconds** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The free vibration response is essential in **machine design** for determining the natural frequencies of rotating shafts, turbine blades, and structural members. Avoiding resonance (where excitation frequency ≈ $\omega_n$) is the primary reason engineers study this topic.

### 2.4 Logarithmic Decrement — Detailed Insight
