A common KTU question is to compare the decay rates. For the overdamped case:
- The "slow" root is $s_1 = -\zeta\omega_n + \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$
- As $\zeta \to 1^+$, this slow root approaches $-\omega_n$ from above
- This means **critical damping is the fastest non-oscillatory decay**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** If we plot the envelope of the response for various $\zeta > 1$, the system with $\zeta = 1$ always reaches equilibrium faster than any $\zeta > 1$. This is why critical damping is the engineering design target.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Damping Classification and Solution Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SDOF Damped Free Vibration System] --> B[Equation of Motion: m*x_ddot + c*x_dot + k*x = 0]
    B --> C[Normalize: x_ddot + 2*zeta*omega_n*x_dot + omega_n_sq*x = 0]
    C --> D[Characteristic Equation: s_sq + 2*zeta*omega_n*s + omega_n_sq = 0]
    D --> E{Compute zeta = c / 2*sqrt m*k}
    E -->|zeta is less than 1| F[UNDERDAMPED - Complex Roots - Oscillatory Decay]
    E -->|zeta equals 1| G[CRITICALLY DAMPED - Repeated Real Root - Fastest Non-Oscillatory]
    E -->|zeta is greater than 1| H[OVERDAMPED - Two Distinct Real Roots - Slow Non-Oscillatory]
    G --> G1[Roots: s1 = s2 = -omega_n]
    G1 --> G2[Solution: x of t = C1 + C2*t times e to the power of -omega_n*t]
    H --> H1[Roots: s1 and s2 are real and negative]
    H1 --> H2[Solution: x of t = C1*e to the power of s1*t + C2*e to the power of s2*t]
    G2 --> I[Apply Initial Conditions x_0 and v_0 to find C1 and C2]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Final Time Response x of t]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Mass m] --> SUM[Force Summation Junction]
    SP[Spring Stiffness k] --> SUM
    DP[Viscous Damper c] --> SUM
    SUM --> EOM[Equation of Motion Solver]
    EOM --> CHK{Damping Ratio Check}
    CHK -->|zeta equals 1| CR[Critically Damped Module - Returns Solution x of t = C1 + C2*t times e to the power of -omega_n*t]
    CHK -->|zeta greater than 1| OV[Overdamped Module - Returns Solution x of t = C1*e to the power of s1*t + C2*e to the power of s2*t]
    CR --> OUT[Displacement Response x of t]
    OV --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Schematic Representation of the Mass-Spring-Damper System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Wall Fixed Support] -->|Connects| S[Spring with Stiffness k N per m]
    W -->|Connects| D[Viscous Damper with Coefficient c N s per m]
    S -->|Attaches to| MS[Mass m kg]
    D -->|Attaches to| MS
    MS -->|Displacement x from equilibrium| AR[Arrow indicating x direction]
    AR --> ARL[Positive x is rightward, negative x is leftward]
    MS -.->|Free to vibrate| FR[Frictionless Horizontal Surface]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The mass $m$ is connected to a fixed wall by **two parallel elements**: a linear spring (which stores potential energy) and a viscous dashpot (which dissipates energy). When the mass is displaced by $x(0) = x_0$ and released, the system will return to equilibrium following the dynamics dictated by its damping ratio.

### 4.4 Decision Logic for Finding C1 and C2

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Conditions: x of 0 equals x_0, x_dot of 0 equals v_0] --> B{Case Selection}
    B -->|Overdamped| C[Set up two linear equations in C1 and C2]
    B -->|Critically Damped| D[Use x of 0 equals C1 to find C1, then differentiate to find C2]
    C --> E[Solve 2 by 2 linear system using Cramer rule or substitution]
    D --> F[C1 equals x_0, C2 equals v_0 plus omega_n times x_0]
    E --> G[Substitute back into general solution]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Final closed-form expression for x of t]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "critical damping coefficient" $c_c$. Derive its expression in terms of mass $m$ and stiffness $k$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The critical damping coefficient $c_c$ is defined as the **minimum value of damping** required to prevent any oscillation in a free vibrating system. At this value, the damping ratio $\zeta = 1$, and the system returns to equilibrium in the shortest possible time without overshooting.

**Derivation:**
The damping ratio is given by:
$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \frac{c}{c_c}$$

For the system to be critically damped, $\zeta = 1$. Therefore:
$$1 = \frac{c_c}{2\sqrt{mk}}$$

Solving for $c_c$:
$$c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2m\omega_n$$

This represents the **threshold damping** at which the response transitions from oscillatory to non-oscillatory.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between overdamped and critically damped systems with respect to: (a) damping ratio, (b) roots of characteristic equation, and (c) nature of response.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Property** | **Overdamped** | **Critically Damped** |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Damping ratio | $\zeta > 1$ | $\zeta = 1$ |
| (b) Roots | Two distinct real, negative roots | One repeated real, negative root |
| (c) Response | Slow exponential decay to equilibrium, no oscillation | Fastest non-oscillatory decay to equilibrium |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A SDOF damped system has a mass of $m = 10$ kg, a spring stiffness of $k = 4000$ N/m, and a damping coefficient of $c = 300$ N·s/m. Determine the **damping ratio** $\zeta$, the **damped natural frequency**, and identify whether the system is overdamped, critically damped, or underdamped. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same system, the mass is released from rest at an initial displacement of $x_0 = 0.05$ m. Derive the complete **overdamped/critically damped response** $x(t)$ and compute the displacement at $t = 0.5$ s. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the natural frequency:
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4000}{10}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the critical damping coefficient:
$$c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2\sqrt{10 \times 4000} = 2\sqrt{40000} = 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the damping ratio:
$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{300}{400} = 0.75$$

**Step 4:** Since $\zeta = 0.75 < 1$, the system is **underdamped**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly compute the natural frequency as $f_n$ (in Hz) when the question asks for the *damped natural frequency* or simply *natural frequency*. Always clarify whether the answer should be in rad/s or Hz. The convention in most KTU problems is to use $\omega_n$ in rad/s.

> [!NOTE]
> Since the system is underdamped, the problem of "Overdamped/Critically Damped Response" is *technically not applicable* here. However, the question's framing in KTU exams is often pedagogical — students must first identify the case correctly. The solution for part (b) should explicitly state that the underdamped case applies, OR the student may re-check the numbers. **For pedagogical completeness**, let us consider a modified version where the damping coefficient is $c = 500$ N·s/m to demonstrate the overdamped case.

**Modified Part (b) — 7 marks (Overdamped Demonstration):**

Assume $c = 500$ N·s/m (overdamped), $m = 10$ kg, $k = 4000$ N/m, $x_0 = 0.05$ m, $v_0 = 0$.

**Step 1:** New damping ratio:
$$\zeta = \frac{500}{400} = 1.25 \quad (\text{overdamped, } \zeta > 1)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the roots:
$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$
$$s_{1,2} = -(1.25)(20) \pm 20\sqrt{1.5625 - 1}$$
$$s_{1,2} = -25 \pm 20\sqrt{0.5625}$$
$$s_{1,2} = -25 \pm 20(0.75)$$
$$s_{1,2} = -25 \pm 15$$
$$s_1 = -10 \text{ s}^{-1}, \quad s_2 = -40 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Write the general solution:
$$x(t) = C_1 e^{-10t} + C_2 e^{-40t}$$

**Step 4:** Apply initial conditions $x(0) = 0.05$ and $\dot{x}(0) = 0$:

From $x(0)$:
$$C_1 + C_2 = 0.05 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

From $\dot{x}(0)$:
$$-10 C_1 - 40 C_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = -4 C_2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5:** Solve the system. Substituting (ii) into (i):
$$-4 C_2 + C_2 = 0.05 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -3 C_2 = 0.05$$
$$C_2 = -\frac{0.05}{3} = -0.01667 \text{ m}$$
$$C_1 = -4(-0.01667) = 0.06667 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Final overdamped response:
$$x(t) = 0.06667 \, e^{-10t} - 0.01667 \, e^{-40t} \quad \text{(in meters)}$$

**Step 7:** Compute displacement at $t = 0.5$ s:
$$x(0.5) = 0.06667 \, e^{-5} - 0.01667 \, e^{-20}$$
$$e^{-5} = 0.006738, \quad e^{-20} \approx 2.06 \times 10^{-9} \text{ (negligible)}$$
$$x(0.5) \approx 0.06667 \times 0.006738 \approx 4.49 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.449 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Students often forget to **square the damping ratio** when computing $\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$. A common mistake is $\sqrt{\zeta - 1}$ instead of $\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$. This leads to a completely wrong root and a wrong final answer. Always double-check the formula!

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **critically damped response** of a SDOF system released from initial displacement $x_0$ with initial velocity $v_0$. Show that the system reaches equilibrium without oscillation in the minimum possible time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle suspension system is modeled as a SDOF system with $m = 250$ kg and $k = 25000$ N/m. The suspension is designed to be **critically damped**. Determine: (i) the critical damping coefficient $c_c$, (ii) the natural frequency $\omega_n$, and (iii) the displacement response $x(t)$ if the vehicle hits a bump and is displaced by $x_0 = 0.08$ m with zero initial velocity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Start from the normalized EOM with $\zeta = 1$:
$$\ddot{x} + 2\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

**Step 2:** The characteristic equation is:
$$s^2 + 2\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (s + \omega_n)^2 = 0$$

**Step 3:** This gives a repeated root $s = -\omega_n$.

**Step 4:** The general solution (using reduction of order) is:
$$x(t) = (C_1 + C_2 t) e^{-\omega_n t}$$

**Step 5:** Apply initial conditions $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$:

From $x(0)$:
$$C_1 = x_0$$

Differentiating:
$$\dot{x}(t) = C_2 e^{-\omega_n t} - \omega_n(C_1 + C_2 t) e^{-\omega_n t}$$

At $t = 0$:
$$v_0 = C_2 - \omega_n C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = v_0 + \omega_n x_0$$

**Step 6:** Final critically damped response:
$$x(t) = \left[x_0 + (v_0 + \omega_n x_0) t\right] e^{-\omega_n t}$$

**Step 7: Proof of Minimum Time:**

To compare decay rates, consider the **magnitude of the dominant exponential term** $e^{-\alpha t}$ where $\alpha$ characterizes the decay rate.

For the overdamped case, the "slow" root is:
$$\alpha_{over} = \zeta\omega_n - \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

For the critically damped case:
$$\alpha_{crit} = \omega_n$$

We need to show that $\alpha_{crit} > \alpha_{over}$ for any $\zeta > 1$.

Define $f(\zeta) = \zeta\omega_n - \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$ for $\zeta \geq 1$. We compare with $\omega_n$:

$$\zeta\omega_n - \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} \quad \text{vs} \quad \omega_n$$

Divide by $\omega_n$:
$$\zeta - \sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} \quad \text{vs} \quad 1$$

For $\zeta > 1$: $\zeta^2 - 1 < \zeta^2$, so $\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} < \zeta$, which means $\zeta - \sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} > 0$. Moreover, since $\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} > \zeta - 1$ for $\zeta > 1$, we have:
$$\zeta - \sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} < \zeta - (\zeta - 1) = 1$$

Therefore: $\zeta - \sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1} < 1$, which gives:
$$\alpha_{over} = \omega_n(\zeta - \sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}) < \omega_n = \alpha_{crit}$$

This proves that the critically damped system decays **faster** than any overdamped system, confirming that critical damping achieves the **minimum time to equilibrium** without oscillation.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $m = 250$ kg, $k = 25000$ N/m, critically damped ($\zeta = 1$), $x_0 = 0.08$ m, $v_0 = 0$.

**(i) Critical damping coefficient:**
$$c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2\sqrt{250 \times 25000} = 2\sqrt{6,250,000} = 2 \times 2500 = 5000 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**(ii) Natural frequency:**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{25000}{250}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iii) Displacement response:**

Using the critically damped solution with $v_0 = 0$ and $x_0 = 0.08$ m:
$$C_1 = x_0 = 0.08$$
$$C_2 = v_0 + \omega_n x_0 = 0 + 10 \times 0.08 = 0.8$$

Therefore:
$$x(t) = (0.08 + 0.8 t) e^{-10t} \quad \text{(in meters)}$$

**Verification of non-oscillation:** Setting $\dot{x}(t) = 0$ to find the extremum:
$$\dot{x}(t) = 0.8 e^{-10t} - 10(0.08 + 0.8 t) e^{-10t} = 0$$
$$0.8 - 0.8 - 8t = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = 0$$

The only extremum is at $t = 0$, which is the initial condition itself. There are no further peaks or valleys, confirming **no oscillation**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Three common places students lose marks on this problem:
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write units (N·s/m for damping, rad/s for frequency, m for displacement). Marks are deducted for unitless answers.
> 2. **Skipping the verification of non-oscillation:** KTU examiners specifically look for the student to demonstrate that the system is non-oscillatory. A complete answer must include this check (e.g., by showing $\dot{x} = 0$ only at $t = 0$).
> 3. **Not applying the initial conditions correctly:** A frequent error is mixing up $C_1$ and $C_2$, or using the wrong sign in $C_2 = v_0 + \omega_n x_0$. Always re-derive this from scratch.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Overdamped & Critically Damped Response**

- **Damping Ratio Formula:** $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \dfrac{c}{c_c}$ — Memorize this.

- **Critical Damping Coefficient:** $c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2m\omega_n$ — The threshold between oscillation and no oscillation.

- **Natural Frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ — Always in rad/s unless stated otherwise.

- **Characteristic Equation:** $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$ — The "DNA" of the system.

- **Three Cases (Sorted by $\zeta$):**
  - $\zeta < 1$ → Underdamped (oscillates with decay)
  - $\zeta = 1$ → Critically Damped (fastest non-oscillatory decay)
  - $\zeta > 1$ → Overdamped (slow non-oscillatory decay)

- **Overdamped Roots:** $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$ — Both real, negative, distinct. **The minus branch is more negative** (faster decay), the **plus branch is less negative** (slower decay, dominates long-term).

- **Overdamped Solution:** $x(t) = C_1 e^{s_1 t} + C_2 e^{s_2 t}$ — Pure sum of two exponentials.

- **Critically Damped Roots:** $s_1 = s_2 = -\omega_n$ — Repeated real root.

- **Critically Damped Solution:** $x(t) = (C_1 + C_2 t) e^{-\omega_n t}$ — Has the special $t e^{-\omega_n t}$ term (not a sum of two independent exponentials).

- **Initial Conditions Always Required:** You cannot determine $C_1$ and $C_2$ without $x(0) = x_0$ and $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$. Two equations, two unknowns.

- **Engineering Significance:** Critical damping is the **design target** for most control and instrumentation systems (galvanometers, door closers, vehicle suspensions, servo motors). Overdamping is generally undesirable as it slows response.

- **Comparison Trick:** To compare decay rates, compare the **dominant (slowest-decaying) exponential** in each case. The one with the **larger magnitude negative exponent** decays faster.

- **Time to Reach Equilibrium:** As $\zeta$ increases from 1 to $\infty$, the time to reach equilibrium **increases**. Minimum time is at $\zeta = 1$.

- **Never confuse $c$ with $c_c$:** $c$ is the actual damping coefficient, $c_c$ is the critical (threshold) damping coefficient.

- **Graphs to Visualize:** In the overdamped case, the displacement decays monotonically to zero with a smooth, non-oscillatory curve. In the critically damped case, the same monotonic decay occurs, but **faster**. The curves never cross zero.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forced Vibration: Frequency Response Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forced Vibration: Frequency Response Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — FORCED VIBRATION: FREQUENCY RESPONSE CURVE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forced Vibration** is a type of vibration in which a system is subjected to an external time-dependent excitation (force or displacement) that drives the motion of the system. When this excitation is **harmonic (sinusoidal)** and persists for a sufficiently long duration, the system reaches a **Steady-State Response**, whose amplitude and phase angle depend solely on the ratio of the forcing frequency ($\omega$) to the natural frequency ($\omega_n$).

A **Frequency Response Curve (FRC)** — also termed the **Response Curve**, **Resonance Curve**, or **Magnification Factor Curve** — is a graphical plot that depicts the **Magnification Factor ($M$)** as a function of the **Frequency Ratio ($r = \omega / \omega_n$)** for varying degrees of damping in the system. It is the single most important diagnostic plot in vibration analysis, allowing engineers to:
- Identify the **critical / resonant speed** of rotating machinery.
- Quantify the **Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF)** at any operating speed.
- Evaluate the **dangerous operating zone** where forced vibration may cause structural failure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Swinging Door and a Bully** 💡
>
> Imagine a swing (a single-degree-of-freedom mass-spring system) on a playground. The swing has a natural rhythm — it loves to rock back and forth at its **own natural frequency** ($\omega_n$). Now, picture a big kid (the "forcing function") pushing the swing rhythmically.
>
> 1. If the big kid pushes **very slowly** (low $\omega$), the swing barely moves. The kid has to put in the same effort every time — there is no amplification. This is the **quasi-static region** ($r \ll 1$).
> 2. If the big kid pushes at **exactly the swing's natural rhythm** ($\omega = \omega_n$, $r = 1$), the swing goes **wildly higher and higher** with each push — energy keeps accumulating. This is the **resonance condition** ($r = 1$). In the undamped case, the amplitude is theoretically infinite.
> 3. If the big kid pushes **way too fast** (high $\omega$, $r \gg 1$), the swing never catches up with the pushes; the swing barely responds, and the motion becomes out-of-phase. The mass effectively "stays still" because the inertia prevents it from following the rapidly changing force.
>
> The **Frequency Response Curve** is the plot of "how high does the swing go?" (Magnification Factor) versus "how fast is the kid pushing?" (Frequency Ratio), drawn for different amounts of "air resistance / friction" (damping ratio $\zeta$).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants / Reference Quantities (Bolded):**
> - **$\omega_n$** — Natural (undamped) circular frequency, in **rad/s**.
> - **$\omega$** — Forced (excitation) circular frequency, in **rad/s**.
> - **$f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi)$** — Natural frequency in **Hz**.
> - **$r = \omega / \omega_n$** — Frequency ratio (dimensionless).
> - **$\zeta = c / c_c = c / (2m\omega_n)$** — Damping ratio (dimensionless), where $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ is the **critical damping coefficient**.
> - **$M$** — Magnification Factor or Dynamic Magnification Factor (DMF), dimensionless.
> - **$x_{st} = F_0 / k$** — Static deflection due to peak force $F_0$, in **m**.
> - **$F_0$** — Peak amplitude of the harmonic excitation force, in **N**.

### 1.4 Classification of Forced Vibration Responses

> [!NOTE]
> The total response of a damped forced system consists of **two superimposed components**:
>
> 1. **Transient (Homogeneous) Response** — Also called the *complementary function* or *decaying vibration*. It exists due to initial conditions and dies out as $t \to \infty$ because of damping. Frequency = $\omega_d$ (damped natural frequency).
>
> 2. **Steady-State (Particular) Response** — Also called the *forced response*. It is sustained indefinitely as long as the excitation persists, and oscillates at the **forcing frequency $\omega$** (NOT at $\omega_n$).
>
> For KTU examinations, we are almost always concerned with the **steady-state amplitude** and **phase**, because the transient vanishes after a few cycles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Differential Equation of Motion

Consider a standard single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) viscously damped system of mass $m$, spring constant $k$, and damping coefficient $c$, subjected to a harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$.

Applying Newton's Second Law of Motion:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

This is a **second-order linear non-homogeneous ordinary differential equation with constant coefficients**.

- **Why linear?** — Because the system is assumed to obey Hooke's law ($F_{spring} = kx$) and viscous damping ($F_{damper} = c\dot{x}$); there are no nonlinear terms.
- **Why non-homogeneous?** — The right-hand side $F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is non-zero; hence the equation is driven.
- **Why constant coefficients?** — The mass, spring, and damper properties do not change with time.

### 2.2 Solution Structure — Step-by-Step Logic

The general solution is the sum of two parts:

$$x(t) = x_{transient}(t) + x_{steady}(t)$$

**Step 1: Homogeneous (Transient) Solution**
Setting the RHS to zero gives the classical free-vibration equation. For an **underdamped** system ($\zeta < 1$):

$$x_{transient}(t) = X_0 \, e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \sin\left(\omega_d t + \phi_0\right)$$

where the **damped natural frequency** is:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

This term **decays exponentially** due to the $e^{-\zeta \omega_n t}$ envelope, and vanishes for $t \gg 1/(\zeta \omega_n)$.

**Step 2: Particular (Steady-State) Solution**
Since the forcing is sinusoidal at frequency $\omega$, the steady-state response is assumed harmonic at the **same frequency**:

$$x_{steady}(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

where $X$ is the steady-state amplitude and $\phi$ is the **phase angle by which the displacement lags the force**.

### 2.3 Steady-State Amplitude and Phase Derivation (Compact Form)

Substituting $x_{steady}(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$ into the equation of motion, expanding $\sin(\omega t - \phi) = \sin\omega t \cos\phi - \cos\omega t \sin\phi$, and equating coefficients of $\sin\omega t$ and $\cos\omega t$ yields:

$$X \left[(k - m\omega^2)\cos\phi + (c\omega)\sin\phi \right] = F_0$$
$$X \left[(k - m\omega^2)\sin\phi - (c\omega)\cos\phi \right] = 0$$

Squaring and adding these two equations:

$$X^2 \left[(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2 \right] = F_0^2$$

Therefore:

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

The phase angle is given by:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}\right)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 1 — Master Formula Sheet for Forced Vibration & Frequency Response Curve**

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Equation of Motion | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin\omega t$ | $m$ in kg, $k$ in N/m |
| 2 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Critical Damping | $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$ | N·s/m |
| 4 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = c/c_c = c/(2m\omega_n)$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Damped Natural Freq. | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| 6 | Frequency Ratio | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Static Deflection | $x_{st} = F_0/k$ | metres (m) |
| 8 | **Steady-State Amplitude** $X$ | $X = \dfrac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | metres (m) |
| 9 | **Magnification Factor (DMF)** $M$ | $M = X/x_{st} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Phase Angle** $\phi$ | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}\right)$ | radians or degrees |
| 11 | $M$ at $r = 0$ | $M = 1$ (quasi-static) | — |
| 12 | $M$ at $r = 1$ (Resonance) | $M_{res} = 1/(2\zeta)$ | peaks for finite damping |
| 13 | $M$ at $r \to \infty$ | $M \to 0$ | mass-controlled region |
| 14 | Peak $r$ (actual max of $M$) | $r_p = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ (for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$) | only if $\zeta < 0.707$ |
| 15 | Peak Magnification $M_{max}$ | $M_{max} = 1/(2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$ | only if $\zeta < 0.707$ |
| 16 | **Transmissibility** $TR$ | $TR = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 17 | Half-Power Bandwidth | $\Delta\omega = 2\zeta\omega_n$ | rad/s |
| 18 | Quality Factor | $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical pipes to avoid markdown breakage:** All absolute values / vertical bars in formulas use **$\vert$ or $\mid$** (LaTeX math delimiters), never a raw `|` character.

### 2.5 Three Operating Zones on the Frequency Response Curve

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Zone I — Quasi-Static (Stiffness-Controlled):** $0 \le r < 1$. The system behaves almost like a static spring; $M \approx 1$ and $\phi \approx 0$. The forcing is slow enough that inertia and damping are negligible.
> 2. **Zone II — Resonance (Transition):** $r \approx 1$. The denominator $(1-r^2)$ becomes very small; the magnification factor shoots up dramatically — limited only by the damping $\zeta$. The phase angle passes through $\phi = 90°$.
> 3. **Zone III — Mass-Controlled (Inertia-Controlled):** $r > \sqrt{2}$. The inertia term $m\omega^2$ dominates over the spring; $M$ decays toward zero as $r$ increases. The phase angle $\phi \to 180°$ (response is **out-of-phase** with the force).

### 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why Does This Matter?

> [!NOTE]
> The Frequency Response Curve is **the** fundamental tool in:
> - **Rotating Machinery Design:** Designing shafts, turbine blades, and pump impellers to operate **away from** critical speeds (where $r = 1$).
> - **Structural Engineering:** Ensuring buildings and bridges do not resonate with wind, seismic, or crowd-loading frequencies.
> - **Automotive Engineering:** Tuned mass dampers, engine-mount design, and suspension tuning rely on shifting the response curve's peak.
> - **Instrumentation:** Accelerometers and seismometers are designed to operate in the **mass-controlled region** ($r \gg 1$) so that output is proportional to acceleration ($\ddot{x}$).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Steady-State Amplitude $X$ and Magnification Factor $M$

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation is exhaustive; **every algebraic step** is explicitly shown, in alignment with the KTU 2024 Scheme expectation of full working in 14-mark problems.

**Given:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ ... (Eq. 1)

**Step 1:** Assume the steady-state solution in the form:
$$x_{ss}(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$
where $X$ = amplitude (m), $\phi$ = phase lag (rad).

**Step 2:** Compute the first and second time derivatives:

$$\dot{x}_{ss}(t) = X\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

$$\ddot{x}_{ss}(t) = -X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into Eq. 1:

$$m\left[-X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi)\right] + c\left[X\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)\right] + k\left[X \sin(\omega t - \phi)\right] = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 4:** Expand the right-hand side using the identity $\sin(\omega t - \phi) = \sin\omega t \cos\phi - \cos\omega t \sin\phi$:

$$F_0 \sin(\omega t) = F_0 \left[\sin(\omega t - \phi)\cos\phi + \cos(\omega t - \phi)\sin\phi\right]$$
$$\Rightarrow \quad F_0 \sin(\omega t) = F_0 \left[(\sin\omega t \cos\phi - \cos\omega t \sin\phi)\cos\phi + (\cos\omega t \cos\phi + \sin\omega t \sin\phi)\sin\phi\right]$$

After multiplying and simplifying (using $\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi = 1$):

$$F_0 \sin(\omega t) = F_0 \sin\omega t$$

This identity is trivially true and serves as a consistency check. Now, group the LHS by $\sin(\omega t - \phi)$ and $\cos(\omega t - \phi)$:

$$\left[(k - m\omega^2)X\right]\sin(\omega t - \phi) + \left[c\omega X\right]\cos(\omega t - \phi) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 5:** Convert RHS to the same basis:

$$F_0 \sin(\omega t) = F_0 \cos\phi \cdot \sin(\omega t - \phi) + F_0 \sin\phi \cdot \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 6:** Equate the coefficients of $\sin(\omega t - \phi)$ and $\cos(\omega t - \phi)$ on both sides:

$$(k - m\omega^2)X = F_0 \cos\phi \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}$$

$$c\omega X = F_0 \sin\phi \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}$$

**Step 7:** Divide Eq. (B) by Eq. (A) to obtain the phase angle:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}$$

$$\boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}\right)}$$

**Step 8:** Square Eq. (A) and Eq. (B) and add them (using $\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi = 1$):

$$(k - m\omega^2)^2 X^2 + (c\omega)^2 X^2 = F_0^2$$

$$X^2 \left[(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2\right] = F_0^2$$

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 9:** Introduce the **static deflection** $x_{st} = F_0/k$ and the **frequency ratio** $r = \omega/\omega_n$. Divide the numerator and denominator inside the square root by $k^2$:

$$(k - m\omega^2)^2 = k^2 \left(1 - \frac{m\omega^2}{k}\right)^2 = k^2 (1 - r^2)^2$$

$$(c\omega)^2 = k^2 \left(\frac{c\omega}{k}\right)^2$$

Now, use the identity $c\omega / k = (2\zeta\omega_n)(\omega)/\omega_n^2 = 2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n) = 2\zeta r$:

$$(c\omega)^2 = k^2 (2\zeta r)^2$$

Therefore:

$$X = \frac{F_0 / k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Step 10:** Define the **Magnification Factor (DMF)** as $M = X / x_{st} = X \cdot (k/F_0)$:

$$\boxed{M(r, \zeta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

**Step 11 (Sanity Checks):**
- At $r = 0$: $M = 1/\sqrt{1} = 1$ ✅ (purely static)
- At $r = 1$: $M = 1/(2\zeta)$ ✅ (resonance peak — finite if $\zeta \ne 0$)
- At $r \to \infty$: $M \approx 1/r^2 \to 0$ ✅ (mass-controlled)

### 3.2 Derivation of the Location of the Peak Magnification

To find the $r$ that maximizes $M(r)$, we minimize the denominator $D(r) = (1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2$.

$$\frac{dD}{dr} = 2(1 - r^2)(-2r) + 2(2\zeta r)(2\zeta) = 0$$

$$-4r(1 - r^2) + 8\zeta^2 r = 0$$

$$4r \left[ -(1 - r^2) + 2\zeta^2 \right] = 0$$

The non-trivial root is:

$$1 - r^2 = 2\zeta^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_p = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$

This root is real **only if** $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$. Beyond this, the magnification curve is monotonically decreasing and has no interior peak.

The **peak magnification factor** is obtained by substituting $r_p^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$ into $M$:

$$1 - r_p^2 = 2\zeta^2$$

$$(1 - r_p^2)^2 = 4\zeta^4$$

$$2\zeta r_p = 2\zeta \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$

$$(2\zeta r_p)^2 = 4\zeta^2(1 - 2\zeta^2)$$

$$D_{min} = 4\zeta^4 + 4\zeta^2(1 - 2\zeta^2) = 4\zeta^4 + 4\zeta^2 - 8\zeta^4 = 4\zeta^2 - 4\zeta^4$$

$$D_{min} = 4\zeta^2(1 - \zeta^2)$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\zeta^2(1 - \zeta^2)}} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Half-Power Bandwidth

The **half-power points** are the values of $r$ where $M = M_{max}/\sqrt{2}$. They are also the points where $|X/x_{st}|^2$ drops to half of its peak value, which is why they are called half-power points (power is proportional to amplitude squared).

Setting $M^2 = \frac{1}{2} M_{max}^2$:

$$\frac{1}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{4\zeta^2(1-\zeta^2)}$$

$$(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2 = 8\zeta^2(1 - \zeta^2)$$

Expanding:

$$1 - 2r^2 + r^4 + 4\zeta^2 r^2 = 8\zeta^2 - 8\zeta^4$$

$$r^4 + (4\zeta^2 - 2) r^2 + (1 - 8\zeta^2 + 8\zeta^4) = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula with $u = r^2$:

$$u = \frac{(2 - 4\zeta^2) \pm \sqrt{(2-4\zeta^2)^2 - 4(1 - 8\zeta^2 + 8\zeta^4)}}{2}$$

The discriminant simplifies to $16\zeta^2(1-\zeta^2)$, giving:

$$u = (1 - 2\zeta^2) \pm 2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

The two roots $r_1, r_2$ for the half-power frequencies satisfy $r_2^2 - r_1^2 = 4\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ (for light damping $\zeta \ll 1$, $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 1$):

$$r_2^2 - r_1^2 \approx 4\zeta$$

Recognizing that the bandwidth in $r$ is $\Delta r = r_2 - r_1$, and for small $\zeta$ we can approximate $r_2 + r_1 \approx 2$ (around $r = 1$):

$$\Delta r = \frac{r_2^2 - r_1^2}{r_2 + r_1} \approx \frac{4\zeta}{2} = 2\zeta$$

Converting back to actual frequencies: $\Delta \omega = \Delta r \cdot \omega_n$

$$\boxed{\Delta \omega = 2\zeta \omega_n}$$

And the **Quality Factor** is:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\omega_n}{\Delta \omega} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A machine of mass 50 kg is mounted on a spring of stiffness $2 \times 10^5$ N/m with a damping ratio of $\zeta = 0.2$. A harmonic force of amplitude $F_0 = 1000$ N acts on the system at $\omega = 80$ rad/s. Determine:
1. The natural frequency $\omega_n$ and the frequency ratio $r$.
2. The steady-state amplitude $X$ and the magnification factor $M$.
3. The peak magnification factor $M_{max}$ and the corresponding frequency ratio $r_p$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Natural frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{50}} = \sqrt{4000} \approx 63.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

Frequency ratio:

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{80}{63.25} \approx 1.265$$

**Part (b):** Static deflection:

$$x_{st} = \frac{F_0}{k} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 10^5} = 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

Compute the denominator terms:

$$(1 - r^2)^2 = (1 - 1.265^2)^2 = (1 - 1.600)^2 = (-0.600)^2 = 0.360$$

$$(2\zeta r)^2 = (2 \times 0.2 \times 1.265)^2 = (0.506)^2 = 0.256$$

Sum:

$$(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2 = 0.360 + 0.256 = 0.616$$

Magnification Factor:

$$M = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.616}} = \frac{1}{0.785} \approx 1.274$$

Steady-state amplitude:

$$X = M \cdot x_{st} = 1.274 \times 0.005 = 6.37 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 6.37 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (c):** Since $\zeta = 0.2 < 0.707$, a peak exists:

$$r_p = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2} = \sqrt{1 - 2(0.04)} = \sqrt{0.92} \approx 0.959$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} = \frac{1}{2(0.2)\sqrt{1 - 0.04}} = \frac{1}{0.4 \times 0.9798} = \frac{1}{0.3919} \approx 2.551$$

**Verification:** The peak occurs very close to $r = 1$ (resonance) as expected for low damping.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Define **Magnification Factor** in forced vibration. What is its significance in vibration analysis?

**Model Answer:**
The **Magnification Factor (DMF)** is the ratio of the **steady-state vibration amplitude** $X$ of a forced system to the **static deflection** $x_{st} = F_0/k$ that would result from a static application of the peak force $F_0$.

$$M = \frac{X}{x_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Significance:**
- It quantifies how severely the dynamic amplitude amplifies the static response.
- It is the basis of the **Frequency Response Curve**, which is used to identify the **resonant speed** and the **safe operating zone** of a machine.
- It enables engineers to design mounts and dampers that keep $M$ acceptably low across the expected range of excitation frequencies.

---

> **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** State the condition for **resonance** in a forced damped vibration system. How does damping affect the resonant amplitude?

**Model Answer:**
**Condition for resonance:** Resonance occurs when the frequency ratio $r = \omega / \omega_n = 1$, i.e., the excitation frequency equals the natural frequency of the system.

**Resonant Magnification Factor:** At $r = 1$:

$$M_{res} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$$

**Effect of damping:**
- If $\zeta \to 0$ (undamped): $M_{res} \to \infty$. The amplitude grows without bound and may cause catastrophic failure.
- As $\zeta$ increases, $M_{res}$ decreases rapidly. For example, $\zeta = 0.1$ gives $M_{res} = 5$, and $\zeta = 0.5$ gives $M_{res} = 1$.
- For $\zeta \ge 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$, the magnification curve has no peak — it monotonically decreases from $M = 1$ at $r = 0$, meaning the system never amplifies the static deflection.

> **Important:** Damping is **the** primary engineering control to limit resonant amplitude in machinery.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### ✅ **CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Modified]`**
> A single-degree-of-freedom damped vibrating system has the following properties: mass $m = 20$ kg, spring stiffness $k = 8000$ N/m, damping coefficient $c = 80$ N·s/m. It is subjected to a harmonic force $F(t) = 500 \sin(30t)$ N.
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the equation of motion. Determine the **damping ratio $\zeta$**, **natural frequency $\omega_n$**, **frequency ratio $r$**, and **damped natural frequency $\omega_d$**.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **steady-state amplitude $X$** and the **phase angle $\phi$** of the response. Also determine the **peak magnification factor $M_{max}$** and the **frequency ratio $r_p$** at which it occurs.

##### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)**

**Step 1:** Equation of motion:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$
$$20\ddot{x} + 80\dot{x} + 8000 x = 500 \sin(30 t) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2:** Natural frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{8000}{20}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Critical damping coefficient:

$$c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2 \times 20 \times 20 = 800 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**Step 4:** Damping ratio:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{80}{800} = 0.1$$

**Step 5:** Frequency ratio (with $\omega = 30$ rad/s):

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{30}{20} = 1.5$$

**Step 6:** Damped natural frequency:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 20 \sqrt{1 - 0.01} = 20 \times 0.995 = 19.90 \text{ rad/s}$$

##### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)**

**Step 1:** Static deflection:

$$x_{st} = \frac{F_0}{k} = \frac{500}{8000} = 0.0625 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute terms of the denominator of $M$:

$$(1 - r^2)^2 = (1 - 2.25)^2 = (-1.25)^2 = 1.5625$$

$$(2\zeta r)^2 = (2 \times 0.1 \times 1.5)^2 = (0.30)^2 = 0.09$$

**Step 3:** Magnification factor at the operating point:

$$M = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.5625 + 0.09}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.6525}} = \frac{1}{1.2855} \approx 0.7779$$

**Step 4:** Steady-state amplitude:

$$X = M \cdot x_{st} = 0.7779 \times 0.0625 \approx 0.0486 \text{ m} = 48.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Phase angle:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.30}{-1.25}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.24)$$

Since the denominator is negative and the numerator is positive, $\phi$ lies in the **second quadrant** (between 90° and 180°). Equivalently:

$$\phi = 180° - \tan^{-1}(0.24) = 180° - 13.5° = 166.5° \text{ (or } 2.906 \text{ rad)}$$

**Step 6:** Peak magnification (valid since $\zeta = 0.1 < 0.707$):

$$r_p = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.02} = \sqrt{0.98} \approx 0.9899$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} = \frac{1}{2(0.1)\sqrt{0.99}} = \frac{1}{0.2 \times 0.9950} = \frac{1}{0.199} \approx 5.025$$

> **Interpretation:** Although the actual operating point ($r = 1.5$, $M = 0.78$) is past the resonance peak and the response is small, the *maximum possible* magnification is **5.025** at $r = 0.99$ — very close to natural frequency. Care must be taken to **avoid operating near $r = 1$**.

---

#### ✅ **CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Question**

> **Q.B `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]`**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Frequency Response Curve (FRC)** for a damped single-degree-of-freedom system. Discuss the effect of damping ratio on the curve. Also explain the significance of the **bandwidth** and the **quality factor**.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A machine tool of mass 100 kg is supported on springs of total stiffness $4 \times 10^5$ N/m. The system has a damping ratio of 0.15. During a cutting operation, the machine experiences a harmonic excitation at a frequency of 50 rad/s.
> Determine:
> 1. The magnification factor $M$.
> 2. The actual amplitude of vibration $X$.
> 3. The phase lag $\phi$.
> 4. The half-power bandwidth $\Delta\omega$ and quality factor $Q$.

##### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a)**

The **Frequency Response Curve (FRC)** is a plot of the **Magnification Factor $M$** (Y-axis) versus the **Frequency Ratio $r = \omega/\omega_n$** (X-axis), drawn as a family of curves for different values of damping ratio $\zeta$. (See schematic in Section 4.2.)

**Key features of the FRC:**

1. **For $r \to 0$ (low frequency):** $M \to 1$ regardless of damping. The system behaves as a static spring; the displacement equals $x_{st}$.

2. **For $r = 1$ (resonance):** The curve peaks at $M = 1/(2\zeta)$. Higher damping flattens the peak dramatically.

3. **For $r > \sqrt{2}$:** All curves merge and decay as $M \approx 1/r^2$. Damping has little effect in this region; the response is dominated by inertia.

**Effect of damping ratio:**
- $\zeta = 0$: Resonance peak is infinite; the curve is a discontinuity.
- $\zeta = 0.1$: Sharp peak of $M = 5$.
- $\zeta = 0.2$: Peak of $M \approx 2.55$.
- $\zeta = 0.5$: Peak of $M \approx 1.15$.
- $\zeta \ge 0.707$: No peak; the curve is monotonic and $M \le 1$ everywhere.

**Bandwidth** is the range of excitation frequencies over which the magnification factor is at least $M_{max}/\sqrt{2}$. It is given by $\Delta\omega = 2\zeta\omega_n$.

**Quality Factor** $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ measures the **sharpness** of the resonance peak. A high $Q$ (low damping) implies a sharp, narrow peak and a "selective" system; a low $Q$ (high damping) implies a broad, flat peak and a "robust" system.

##### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b)**

**Step 1:** Natural frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 10^5}{100}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Frequency ratio:

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{50}{63.25} \approx 0.7905$$

**Step 3:** Denominator of $M$:

$$(1 - r^2)^2 = (1 - 0.6249)^2 = (0.3751)^2 \approx 0.1407$$

$$(2\zeta r)^2 = (2 \times 0.15 \times 0.7905)^2 = (0.2372)^2 \approx 0.0563$$

Sum: $0.1407 + 0.0563 = 0.1970$

**Step 4:** Magnification Factor:

$$M = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.1970}} = \frac{1}{0.4438} \approx 2.253$$

**Step 5:** Static deflection and actual amplitude:

$$x_{st} = \frac{F_0}{k}$$

*Note: $F_0$ is not given; for unit peak force $F_0 = 1$ N, $x_{st} = 1/k = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m. The amplitude is then:*

$$X = M \cdot x_{st} = 2.253 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \approx 5.63 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m (per N of $F_0$)}$$

**Step 6:** Phase angle:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2} = \frac{0.2372}{0.3751} = 0.6323$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.6323) \approx 32.3°$$

**Step 7:** Half-power bandwidth:

$$\Delta\omega = 2\zeta\omega_n = 2 \times 0.15 \times 63.25 \approx 18.98 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 8:** Quality factor:

$$Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.333$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The system operates at $r \approx 0.79$, which is approaching the resonance zone. To reduce $M$, the designer can either **increase the damping** (add dampers) or **shift $\omega_n$ away from $\omega$** (change the spring stiffness or mass).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Loss of Marks in KTU 14-Mark Problems**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\omega$ and $\omega_n$:** Students frequently interchange the forcing and natural frequencies. Always define $r = \omega/\omega_n$ clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting the static deflection division:** The Magnification Factor $M$ is **dimensionless**. Students often forget to divide by $x_{st} = F_0/k$ and confuse amplitude with magnification.
> 3. **Sign errors in the phase angle:** When $r > 1$, the denominator $(1 - r^2)$ is **negative**, placing $\phi$ in the second quadrant (between 90° and 180°). A common error is to compute only $\tan^{-1}$ and report a negative angle in degrees (e.g., $-32°$ instead of $148°$).
> 4. **Confusing $M_{res}$ with $M_{max}$:** $M_{res} = 1/(2\zeta)$ is the magnification **at $r = 1$**. $M_{max}$ is the **peak** of the curve, occurring at $r_p = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ (only if $\zeta < 0.707$). They are equal **only when $\zeta = 0$**.
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer:** $X$ should always be in metres (or mm); $\phi$ in radians or degrees; $M$ dimensionless; $Q$ dimensionless.
> 6. **Missing the case $\zeta \ge 0.707$:** For high damping, the curve has no interior peak. Students who blindly use $r_p = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ will get a complex number and lose marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist — Forced Vibration & Frequency Response Curve**
>
> ☐ The **equation of motion** is $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$.
>
> ☐ The total response is **transient** (dies out) + **steady-state** (persists at $\omega$).
>
> ☐ **Magnification Factor (DMF):** $M = 1/\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$, dimensionless.
>
> ☐ **Phase Angle:** $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right)$, response **lags** force by $\phi$.
>
> ☐ **Resonance condition:** $r = 1$; at resonance, $M = 1/(2\zeta)$ and $\phi = 90°$.
>
> ☐ **Peak magnification (for $\zeta < 0.707$):** $r_p = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$, $M_{max} = 1/(2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$.
>
> ☐ **Three operating zones:** Quasi-static ($r \ll 1$, $M \to 1$), Resonance ($r \approx 1$, $M$ peaks), Mass-controlled ($r \gg 1$, $M \to 0$).
>
> ☐ **Static deflection:** $x_{st} = F_0/k$ is the reference; the actual amplitude is $X = M \cdot x_{st}$.
>
> ☐ **Bandwidth:** $\Delta\omega = 2\zeta\omega_n$ — the width of the response peak at $M_{max}/\sqrt{2}$.
>
> ☐ **Quality Factor:** $Q = 1/(2\zeta) = \omega_n/\Delta\omega$ — sharpness of the peak.
>
> ☐ **Transmissibility:** $TR = \sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}/\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ — useful for vibration isolation.
>
> ☐ **Damping limits the peak** but does **not** shift the resonance frequency; it only reduces the magnitude.
>
> ☐ For $\zeta \ge 1/\sqrt{2}$, **no resonance peak** exists; $M$ is bounded by 1 everywhere.
>
> ☐ In KTU 14-mark problems, **always show full working** for the derivation of $M$ and $\phi$ to secure full marks.
>
> ☐ **Phase angle sign convention:** $\phi$ measured as the **lag** of $x$ with respect to $F$. Positive $\phi$ = response lags force.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibration Isolation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibration Isolation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Vibration Isolation** is the process of reducing the transmission of vibratory forces or motions from a vibrating source to a receiving system (or vice-versa) by introducing an elastic and dissipative element (spring-damper) between them. The effectiveness of isolation is quantitatively measured using a parameter called the **Transmissibility Ratio (TR)**, which is defined as the ratio of the force (or displacement) transmitted to the foundation to the exciting force (or input displacement).

In formal KTU syllabus terms, vibration isolation is the technique of inserting a flexible medium between a vibrating system and the structure that supports it, such that the disturbing effects of vibration are minimized or attenuated below an acceptable level. It is broadly classified into two categories:
- **Force Isolation (Active Isolation)**: Isolating a vibrating machine from its supporting foundation.
- **Motion Isolation (Passive Isolation)**: Isolating a sensitive instrument from a vibrating floor or base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Transmissibility (TR) is the SINGLE most important parameter in this topic. A transmissibility value of **TR = 1** means no isolation has occurred. For effective isolation, the design target must always be **TR < 1**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a washing machine that violently shakes when the spin cycle starts. If you place the washing machine directly on a hard floor, every vibration force is transmitted straight to the floor, and the floor shakes the whole house. Now, place a thick **rubber mat (spring)** under the machine, optionally with a soft damper pad. The rubber mat compresses and stretches, absorbing the shock, and very little shaking reaches the floor. The rubber mat acts as a **vibration isolator**.

In a different scenario, consider a precision surgical microscope in a hospital operating theatre located above a metro line. Vibrations from the metro tunnel travel up the building structure. To prevent the metro's vibrations from disturbing the surgeon's microscope, the microscope is mounted on a **soft air-spring platform**. The platform "floats" the instrument, absorbing the floor vibrations before they reach the optics. This is **motion isolation** — the inverse of the washing machine problem.

Both scenarios use the same physics — a flexible spring element that stores energy and (optionally) a damper that dissipates energy — to decouple one body from another. The designer's goal is to ensure the operating frequency of the source is **much greater than the natural frequency** of the isolator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The word *isolation* in this topic does NOT mean "no motion at all." It means the *transmitted* motion or force is a small fraction of the *input* motion or force. The isolated system still moves — but the foundation does not.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Units | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stiffness of isolator | $k$ | **N/m** | Spring constant of the elastic element |
| Damping coefficient | $c$ | **Ns/m** | Viscous damping coefficient |
| Mass of isolated body | $m$ | **kg** | Mass supported by the isolator |
| Natural (damped) frequency | $\omega_n$ | **rad/s** | Undamped natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ |
| Excitation frequency | $\omega$ | **rad/s** | Frequency of the disturbing force/motion |
| Frequency ratio | $r$ | **dimensionless** | Ratio $r = \omega/\omega_n$ |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta$ | **dimensionless** | Ratio $c/c_c$, where $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ |
| Transmissibility | TR | **dimensionless** | Ratio of transmitted amplitude to input amplitude |

The frequency ratio $r$ is the most critical dimensionless parameter — it alone dictates whether the system amplifies or attenuates vibration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Types of Vibration Isolation

### A. Force Isolation (Active Isolation)
A machine of mass $m$ generates a harmonic disturbing force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ internally. The machine is mounted on a spring-damper system of stiffness $k$ and damping $c$. The goal is to minimize the force $F_T$ transmitted to the foundation.

The equation of motion is:
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

The steady-state amplitude of transmitted force is:
$$F_T = F_0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

The Transmissibility Ratio is:
$$TR = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

### B. Motion Isolation (Passive Isolation)
A base/ground undergoes harmonic motion $y(t) = Y \sin(\omega t)$. An instrument of mass $m$ is mounted on a spring-damper isolator. The relative motion is $x - y$, so the equation of motion is:
$$m\ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0$$

The steady-state amplitude ratio of the instrument's motion to the base motion is:
$$TR = \frac{X}{Y} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The mathematical form of Transmissibility is identical** for both force isolation and motion isolation. The KTU examiner frequently tests whether students recognize this symmetry.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind the Transmissibility Curve

The behavior of the isolator is governed by **three distinct regions** of the frequency ratio $r$:

1. **Region 1 — Resonance ($0 \leq r \leq \sqrt{2}$)**: TR $> 1$, meaning the system *amplifies* vibration rather than isolating it. At $r = 1$ (true resonance), TR is theoretically infinite for undamped systems. Damping dramatically reduces this peak.
2. **Region 2 — Transition Point ($r = \sqrt{2}$)**: TR $= 1$, regardless of damping. At this frequency, the system neither amplifies nor attenuates — the input equals the output.
3. **Region 3 — Isolation ($r > \sqrt{2}$)**: TR $< 1$, the system attenuates vibration. Damping *reduces* the effectiveness of isolation in this region.

**The Why**: Physically, below the natural frequency, the spring is "stiff" compared to the inertia, and the mass moves with the input. Above the natural frequency, the inertia dominates, and the mass tends to remain stationary while the base moves — hence isolation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Undamped Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Critical Damping Coefficient | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | Ns/m |
| 3 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Frequency Ratio | $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | **TR (Undamped, $\zeta = 0$)** | $\dfrac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$ | valid for $r \ne 1$ |
| 6 | **TR (Damped)** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | general case |
| 7 | Magnification Factor (MF) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | for displacement under force |
| 8 | Condition for isolation | $r > \sqrt{2}$ | TR $< 1$ |
| 9 | Best operating $r$ (undamped) | as large as possible | but practical limit ≈ 3 to 5 |
| 10 | Best damping for isolation | as small as practical | damping hurts isolation at high $r$ |
| 11 | Resonance peak amplitude (damped) | $\dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$ | at $r = 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Magnification Factor (MF)** with **Transmissibility (TR)**. MF gives the ratio of *displacement amplitude* of mass to the *static displacement* (i.e., $X/(F_0/k)$). TR gives the ratio of *transmitted force amplitude* to *applied force amplitude*. They differ by the term $r^2$ in some formulations, but for force isolation they are identical when force is applied.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Vibration isolation is used in:
- **Automotive**: Engine mounts, suspension systems
- **Civil Engineering**: Base isolation of buildings in earthquake zones
- **Manufacturing**: Machine tool foundations, optical benches, cleanrooms
- **Aerospace**: Helicopter rotor isolation, spacecraft payload isolation
- **Electronics**: Hard-drive mounts in laptops to survive drops
- **Medical**: MRI isolation from building vibration
- **Power Engineering**: Diesel generator mounts, gas turbine pedestals

The standard design procedure in industry is: (1) Identify the disturbing frequency $\omega$. (2) Select a target TR (typically **TR $\leq 0.1$ to $0.2$**). (3) Back-calculate the required $r$ and hence the required $\omega_n$. (4) Choose $k$ and $m$ accordingly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equation of Motion for Force Isolation

**Setup:** A rigid machine of mass $m$ rests on a spring (stiffness $k$) and a viscous damper (damping coefficient $c$). The machine has an internal unbalanced mechanism that generates a harmonic disturbing force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ acting on the mass.

**Free-Body Diagram (mental model):**
- Inertial force on mass: $m\ddot{x}$ (opposing acceleration)
- Spring restoring force: $kx$ (opposing displacement)
- Damper resisting force: $c\dot{x}$ (opposing velocity)
- Applied harmonic force: $F_0 \sin(\omega t)$

Applying Newton's second law (sum of forces = mass × acceleration):
$$m\ddot{x} = -kx - c\dot{x} + F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

Rearranging to standard form:
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

This is the **classical single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) forced vibration equation** with damping.

## 3.2 Steady-State Solution and Transmitted Force

The steady-state (particular) solution is assumed as:
$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Substituting into the differential equation and matching amplitudes:

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

The **force transmitted to the foundation** passes through both the spring and the damper in parallel. The transmitted force at any instant is:
$$F_T(t) = kx + c\dot{x}$$

The amplitude of this transmitted force is:
$$F_T = \sqrt{(kX)^2 + (c\omega X)^2} = X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

Substituting $X$:
$$F_T = F_0 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

Now introduce the dimensionless substitutions:
- $r = \omega/\omega_n$
- $\omega_n^2 = k/m$
- $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km}) = c/(2m\omega_n)$
- $c = 2\zeta m\omega_n$

The numerator becomes:
$$\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2} = k\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}$$

The denominator becomes:
$$\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2} = k\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$$

The transmissibility ratio therefore simplifies to:
$$TR = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

This is the **General Transmissibility Equation** for damped vibration isolation.

## 3.3 Special Case 1: Undamped System ($\zeta = 0$)

Setting $\zeta = 0$ in the general equation:
$$TR = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2}} = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$$

**Interpretation:**
- For $r < 1$: $TR = \dfrac{1}{1 - r^2} > 1$ → Amplification
- For $r > 1$: $TR = \dfrac{1}{r^2 - 1}$ → Attenuation
- At $r = 1$: $TR \to \infty$ → Resonance catastrophe

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Writing $TR = 1/(1 - r^2)$ without absolute value bars. The absolute value is mandatory because for $r > 1$, the denominator is negative and the square root flips the sign. Use $\vert 1 - r^2 \vert$.

## 3.4 Special Case 2: Transition Frequency $r = \sqrt{2}$

Substituting $r = \sqrt{2}$ into the general equation:
$$TR = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta \sqrt{2})^2}{(1 - 2)^2 + (2\zeta \sqrt{2})^2}}$$

$$TR = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 8\zeta^2}{1 + 8\zeta^2}} = 1$$

**Conclusion:** At $r = \sqrt{2}$, **TR = 1** regardless of the damping ratio. This is the point of "no isolation, no amplification." This is why $\sqrt{2}$ is the design boundary between the amplification and isolation regions.

## 3.5 Derivation: Motion Isolation (Base Excitation)

**Setup:** A precision instrument of mass $m$ is mounted on a spring-damper system. The base (floor) moves harmonically: $y(t) = Y\sin(\omega t)$.

The instrument's absolute displacement is $x(t)$. The spring and damper experience the *relative* deformation between the mass and the base.

Newton's second law on the mass:
$$m\ddot{x} = -k(x - y) - c(\dot{x} - \dot{y})$$

Rearranging:
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = c\dot{y} + ky$$

The right-hand side is the "effective force" transmitted through the damper and spring from the base.

Substituting $y = Y\sin(\omega t)$ and assuming $x = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$:
$$X = Y \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

Therefore:
$$TR = \frac{X}{Y} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

The form is **identical** to force isolation. The transmissibility ratio is the same.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Table

| $r$ | Damping $\zeta$ | TR | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| $r = 0$ | any | $1$ | Static: input equals output |
| $0 < r < 1$ | $\zeta = 0$ | $> 1$ | Amplification, peak at $r = 1$ |
| $r = 1$ | $\zeta = 0$ | $\infty$ | Resonance (theoretical) |
| $r = 1$ | $\zeta > 0$ | $1/(2\zeta)$ | Finite peak |
| $r = \sqrt{2}$ | any | $1$ | Transition point |
| $r > \sqrt{2}$ | $\zeta = 0$ | $1/(r^2 - 1)$ | Pure isolation, decays as $1/r^2$ |
| $r \to \infty$ | any | $\to 0$ | Perfect isolation |

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A motor weighing **250 N** is mounted on a rubber pad isolator. The motor operates at **1500 RPM**. The static deflection of the isolator is **5 mm**. Determine:
(a) The transmissibility ratio (undamped).
(b) The frequency ratio.
(c) Comment on the design effectiveness.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Static deflection $\delta_{st} = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m.

Step 2 — Natural frequency from the standard relation $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$:
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.005}} = \sqrt{1962} \approx 44.29 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 3 — Operating (excitation) frequency:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 4 — Frequency ratio:
$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{157.08}{44.29} \approx 3.546$$

Step 5 — Undamped Transmissibility:
$$TR = \frac{1}{r^2 - 1} = \frac{1}{(3.546)^2 - 1} = \frac{1}{12.575 - 1} = \frac{1}{11.575} \approx 0.0864$$

**Interpretation:** Only **8.64%** of the disturbing force reaches the foundation. The isolator is **highly effective** since $r = 3.546 > \sqrt{2}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Vibration Isolation System

The following block diagram maps the energy flow, signal pathways, and physical interactions within a generic vibration isolation system, covering both force isolation and motion isolation modes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT SOURCE] --> B{SOURCE TYPE}
    B -->|Harmonic Force| C[FORCE EXCITATION F0 sin wt]
    B -->|Harmonic Motion| D[MOTION EXCITATION Y sin wt]
    C --> E[SDOF SYSTEM]
    D --> E
    E --> F[MASS M]
    F --> G[KINETIC ENERGY m x dot dot]
    F --> H[POTENTIAL ENERGY 0.5 k x squared]
    F --> I[DISSIPATION c x dot squared]
    E --> J[TRANSMISSIBILITY TR CALC]
    J --> K{FREQUENCY RATIO r}
    K -->|r less than sqrt2| L[AMPLIFICATION REGION]
    K -->|r equals sqrt2| M[TRANSITION POINT]
    K -->|r greater than sqrt2| N[ISOLATION REGION]
    L --> O[OUTPUT TO FOUNDATION]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[TRANSMITTED FORCE FT]
    O --> Q[TRANSMITTED DISPLACEMENT X]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Transmissibility Behavior Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Vibration Isolation TR Curve Analysis] --> Zeta{Damping Ratio}
    Zeta -->|zeta equals 0| Undamped[Undamped System]
    Zeta -->|zeta greater than 0| Damped[Damped System]
    Undamped --> LowR[r less than 1 Amplification]
    Undamped --> HighR[r greater than 1 Isolation]
    Damped --> Resonance[r equals 1 Finite Peak]
    Damped --> Transition[r equals sqrt2 TR equals 1]
    Damped --> HighRDamped[r greater than sqrt2 Isolation but degraded by damping]
    Resonance --> PeakAmp[Peak TR equals 1 by 2 zeta]
    Transition --> Boundary[Boundary Between Amplification and Isolation]
    HighR --> Asymptotic[TR approaches 1 by r squared for large r]
    HighRDamped --> Asymptotic
```

## 4.3 Physical System Schematic (Block Format)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Foundation[FOUNDATION BASE]
        F[Foundation Block]
    end
    subgraph Isolator[SPRING DAMPER ISOLATOR]
        S[Spring k]
        D[Damper c]
    end
    subgraph Mass[VIBRATING BODY]
        M[Mass m]
        UF[Unbalanced Force F0 sin wt]
    end
    F --- S
    F --- D
    S --- M
    D --- M
    M --- UF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The foundation, isolator (spring + damper), and mass are stacked in series. The disturbing force acts on the mass; the transmitted force passes through both the spring and the damper in parallel to reach the foundation. This is the canonical model used in every KTU 2024 Scheme exam problem on this topic.

## 4.4 Design Decision Flow for Vibration Isolators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What is the disturbing frequency omega] --> Q2[What is the target TR]
    Q2 --> Q3[Compute required frequency ratio r]
    Q3 --> Q4{Is r greater than sqrt2}
    Q4 -->|No| Q5[Increase static deflection or decrease stiffness]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q6[Compute natural frequency omega n]
    Q5 --> Q4
    Q6 --> Q7[Choose mass m and stiffness k]
    Q7 --> Q8[Verify damping does not degrade isolation]
    Q8 --> Q9[Final Design Specification]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term Transmissibility in the context of vibration isolation. State its mathematical expression for an undamped system.**

**Model Answer:**

Transmissibility is defined as the ratio of the amplitude of the force (or motion) transmitted to the foundation to the amplitude of the exciting force (or input motion).

$$TR = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{X}{Y}$$

For an undamped system ($\zeta = 0$):
$$TR = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert} \quad \text{where } r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n}$$

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the difference between Force Isolation and Motion Isolation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Force Isolation | Motion Isolation |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of excitation** | Internal harmonic force $F_0\sin\omega t$ generated by machine | External base motion $Y\sin\omega t$ imposed on foundation |
| **Quantity isolated** | Force transmitted to foundation | Motion transmitted to isolated instrument |
| **Typical application** | Mounting motors, engines, pumps | Mounting precision instruments, microscopes |
| **TR expression** | $\dfrac{F_T}{F_0}$ | $\dfrac{X}{Y}$ |
| **Math form** | Identical | Identical |

Both yield the same transmissibility formula but differ in the physical interpretation of what is being attenuated.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice Option 1]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for the transmissibility ratio of an undamped single-degree-of-freedom system subjected to base excitation. Discuss the regions of amplification and isolation.

**(b)** A delicate electronic instrument weighing **40 N** is mounted on a cushion of rubber having a stiffness of **$2 \times 10^4$ N/m**. The base on which the instrument is mounted undergoes harmonic motion at frequency **20 Hz** with amplitude of **1 mm**. Determine: (i) the frequency ratio, (ii) the absolute amplitude of motion of the instrument, and (iii) the transmissibility ratio. Assume no damping.

---

**Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Sketch (2 marks):** The system consists of a mass $m$ supported by a spring of stiffness $k$ on a base that moves harmonically as $y(t) = Y\sin(\omega t)$. The absolute displacement of mass is $x(t)$.

**Equation of motion (2 marks):** Considering relative motion $z = x - y$, the spring force is $kz$ acting on the mass. Newton's second law:
$$m\ddot{x} + k(x - y) = 0$$
$$m\ddot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{y} = m\omega^2 Y \sin(\omega t)$$

**Steady-state solution (2 marks):** Assuming $z = Z\sin(\omega t)$:
$$Z = \frac{m\omega^2 Y}{k - m\omega^2} = \frac{Y \cdot r^2}{1 - r^2}$$

The absolute displacement amplitude of mass:
$$X = Z + Y = Y\left[\frac{r^2}{1 - r^2} + 1\right] = \frac{Y}{1 - r^2}$$

Transmissibility:
$$TR = \frac{X}{Y} = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$$

**Regions (1 mark):**
- For $r < \sqrt{2}$: $TR > 1$ → Amplification
- For $r > \sqrt{2}$: $TR < 1$ → Isolation
- At $r = \sqrt{2}$: $TR = 1$ → Transition point

---

**Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

Given:
- Weight $W = 40$ N, so $m = W/g = 40/9.81 = 4.077$ kg
- Stiffness $k = 2 \times 10^4$ N/m
- Excitation frequency $f = 20$ Hz, so $\omega = 2\pi f = 40\pi \approx 125.66$ rad/s
- Base amplitude $Y = 1$ mm $= 0.001$ m

**Step 1 — Natural frequency (1 mark):**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^4}{4.077}} = \sqrt{4906.4} \approx 70.04 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Frequency ratio (1 mark):**
$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{125.66}{70.04} \approx 1.794$$

**Step 3 — Transmissibility (2 marks):**
$$TR = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert} = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - 3.219 \vert} = \frac{1}{2.219} \approx 0.4506$$

**Step 4 — Absolute amplitude of instrument (2 marks):**
$$X = TR \times Y = 0.4506 \times 0.001 = 4.506 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.4506 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):**
Since $r = 1.794 > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, the system is in the **isolation region** with TR = 0.45. About 45% of the base motion is transmitted to the instrument. Better isolation would require reducing stiffness or increasing mass to lower $\omega_n$ further.

---

### Question B **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice Option 2]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the transmissibility of a damped single-degree-of-freedom system under harmonic force excitation. Explain why damping reduces the effectiveness of isolation at high frequency ratios but is essential at resonance.

**(b)** A machine of mass **50 kg** is mounted on a spring-damper system. The machine generates a harmonic disturbing force of amplitude **500 N** at **3000 RPM**. The spring stiffness is **$8 \times 10^5$ N/m** and the damping ratio is **0.15**. Determine: (i) the natural frequency, (ii) the frequency ratio, (iii) the transmissibility ratio, and (iv) the force transmitted to the foundation.

---

**Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Equation of motion (2 marks):**
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

**Steady-state amplitude (2 marks):** Assuming $x = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$:
$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

The transmitted force is $F_T = kx + c\dot{x}$, with amplitude:
$$F_T = X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

**Transmissibility derivation (2 marks):** Dividing and substituting $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$, $r = \omega/\omega_n$:
$$TR = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Discussion (1 mark):** 
- **At low $r$ (near resonance):** Damping dramatically reduces the infinite theoretical peak at $r = 1$, preventing catastrophic amplitudes. Without damping, even small unbalances cause unbounded growth.
- **At high $r$ (isolation region, $r > \sqrt{2}$):** Damping *increases* the numerator $1 + (2\zeta r)^2$, raising TR. Hence damping degrades isolation effectiveness. An ideal isolator should have minimum damping at high $r$.

The compromise: Choose damping just large enough to limit resonance peak to safe amplitude, but as small as possible to maintain good isolation.

---

**Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

Given:
- $m = 50$ kg
- $F_0 = 500$ N
- $N = 3000$ RPM → $\omega = 2\pi \times 3000/60 = 100\pi \approx 314.16$ rad/s
- $k = 8 \times 10^5$ N/m
- $\zeta = 0.15$

**Step 1 — Natural frequency (1 mark):**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 10^5}{50}} = \sqrt{16000} \approx 126.49 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Frequency ratio (1 mark):**
$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{314.16}{126.49} \approx 2.484$$

**Step 3 — Damped Transmissibility (3 marks):**

Compute each piece:
- $r^2 = (2.484)^2 = 6.170$
- $2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.15 \times 2.484 = 0.7452$
- $(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.5553$
- $1 - r^2 = 1 - 6.170 = -5.170$
- $(1 - r^2)^2 = 26.73$
- Numerator: $1 + 0.5553 = 1.5553$
- Denominator: $26.73 + 0.5553 = 27.285$
- $TR = \sqrt{1.5553 / 27.285} = \sqrt{0.05700} \approx 0.2388$

**Step 4 — Transmitted Force (1 mark):**
$$F_T = TR \times F_0 = 0.2388 \times 500 = 119.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
Since $r = 2.484 > \sqrt{2}$, the system is in the **isolation region**. Only about **23.9%** of the disturbing force reaches the foundation. Without damping ($\zeta = 0$), TR would be $1/(r^2 - 1) = 1/5.17 = 0.1934$, slightly better — confirming that damping reduces isolation effectiveness at this frequency ratio.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting absolute value** in undamped TR formula. Marks lost on every occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing MF with TR.** Remember: MF = $1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ but TR = $\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$. They differ by the term $(2\zeta r)^2$ in the numerator.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion** for RPM to rad/s: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$, NOT $\omega = N/60$.
> 4. **Failing to comment on whether the system is in amplification or isolation region.** Always state whether $r > \sqrt{2}$ or not.
> 5. **Forgetting the static deflection formula** $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$, which gives $\omega_n$ directly from the deflection without needing to know $k$ and $m$ separately. This shortcut is highly valued by KTU examiners.
> 6. **Not mentioning practical design constraint** that increasing $r$ beyond 3–5 brings diminishing returns; large static deflections compromise stability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Summary Checklist

- [ ] **Vibration Isolation** = Decoupling a source from receiver using spring-damper.
- [ ] **Two types**: Force isolation (machine → foundation) and Motion isolation (foundation → instrument). Both share the **same transmissibility formula**.
- [ ] **Transmissibility (TR)** = Ratio of transmitted amplitude to input amplitude.
- [ ] **General formula**:
$$TR = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$
- [ ] **Undamped special case**: $TR = 1/\vert 1 - r^2 \vert$.
- [ ] **Three critical regions**:
  - $r < \sqrt{2}$ → **Amplification** (TR > 1)
  - $r = \sqrt{2}$ → **Transition** (TR = 1, regardless of damping)
  - $r > \sqrt{2}$ → **Isolation** (TR < 1)
- [ ] **Damping role**: Essential at resonance to limit peak; harmful in isolation region. Compromise is needed.
- [ ] **Design target**: Typically TR $\leq 0.1$ to $0.2$, achieved by making $r$ large (commonly $r = 3$ to $5$).
- [ ] **Static deflection shortcut**: $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ — extremely useful when $k$ and $m$ are not individually known.
- [ ] **Best operating point**: As high an $r$ as practical, with minimum damping.
- [ ] **Practical rule**: Doubling the static deflection (halving $\omega_n$) roughly doubles the isolation effectiveness (for undamped systems).
- [ ] **At very high $r$**: TR $\approx 1/r^2$ for undamped systems. The isolation decays as $1/r^2$.
- [ ] **Remember**: $r = \omega/\omega_n = f/f_n$ is **dimensionless**, always use the same unit system.
- [ ] **Conversion**: $\omega$ (rad/s) $= 2\pi f$ (Hz) $= 2\pi N/60$ (RPM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Memory Aid:** If a student remembers only one equation for this topic, it should be:
> $$\boxed{TR = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}, \quad r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n}}$$
> All numerical problems in KTU exams reduce to evaluating this expression with correctly computed $r$ and $\zeta$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmissibility - Numerical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transmissibility - Numerical Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transmissibility (TR)** is defined as the ratio of the maximum force transmitted to the foundation (supporting structure) through a vibrating system to the amplitude of the exciting (applied) harmonic force acting on the system.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$TR = \frac{F_{T,\max}}{F_0}$$

where $F_{T,\max}$ is the peak transmitted force and $F_0$ is the amplitude of the applied harmonic excitation force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Transmissibility governs the design of vibration isolators, machine foundations, automotive suspensions, and precision instrument mountings. It directly indicates whether the system **isolates** (TR < 1) or **amplifies** (TR > 1) the input disturbance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you place a delicate scientific instrument (a vibration-sensitive weighing scale) directly on the floor of a factory where a heavy punch press operates every second. The floor shakes violently and the scale readings jump erratically — this is **bad isolation** (high transmissibility, TR > 1).

Now, imagine placing the same instrument on a soft rubber pad (a spring-damper cushion). The rubber absorbs the floor's vibrations, and the scale reads smoothly — this is **good isolation** (low transmissibility, TR < 1).

The rubber pad is essentially a **vibration isolator** — its job is to make the *force reaching the instrument* much smaller than the *force in the floor*. The numerical value of transmissibility quantifies how effective that rubber pad actually is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Vibration isolation is only effective when the excitation frequency $\omega$ is **greater than** $\sqrt{2}\,\omega_n$ (for undamped systems). Below this critical frequency, the isolator actually makes things *worse* by amplifying the transmitted force.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Frequency ratio:** $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}$ (dimensionless)
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ (dimensionless)
- **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ in **rad/s**
- **Isolation efficiency:** $\eta = (1 - TR) \times 100\,\%$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Derivation Outline (Operational Logic)

The system considered is a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) viscous-damped system subjected to base excitation or a directly applied harmonic force.

**Step 1 — Equation of Motion:** For mass $m$, damping $c$, stiffness $k$, with harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$:
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Steady-State Response Amplitude:** The amplitude $X$ of the steady-state response is:
$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Transmitted Force:** The force transmitted to the base is the sum of spring force and damper force (Newton's third law on the support):
$$F_T(t) = kx + c\dot{x}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of Transmitted Force:** The peak magnitude is:
$$F_{T,\max} = X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Non-Dimensionalisation:** Dividing numerator and denominator by $k$, and substituting $r = \omega/\omega_n$ and $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$:
$$TR = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Special Case / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $TR = \dfrac{\sqrt{1+(2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Damped transmissibility (general) | Valid for all $r$ and $\zeta$ |
| 2 | $TR = \dfrac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$ | Undamped transmissibility ($\zeta = 0$) | Critical at $r = 1$ (resonance) |
| 3 | $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}$ | Frequency ratio (dimensionless) | $\omega$ = excitation freq.; $\omega_n$ = natural freq. |
| 4 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural circular frequency | Units: **rad/s** |
| 5 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency in Hz | Units: **Hz** |
| 6 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Damping ratio | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ is critical damping |
