# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Define an accelerometer. State the condition under which a seismic instrument behaves as an accelerometer.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** An accelerometer is a seismic-type vibration measuring instrument used to measure the acceleration of a vibrating body. It consists of a seismic mass $m$ supported by a spring of stiffness $k$ and a damper of coefficient $c$ inside a casing attached to the vibrating body. The relative displacement $x$ between the mass and the casing is measured and is directly proportional to the acceleration of the vibrating body.
>
> **Condition (1 mark):** The instrument behaves as an accelerometer when the frequency ratio $r = \omega / \omega_n \gg 1$, i.e., when the forcing (vibration) frequency is much larger than the natural frequency of the seismic system. Under this condition, the relative displacement $X \approx Y$ (the base displacement amplitude), so the base acceleration $\ddot{y}_{max} = Y\omega^2 \approx X\omega^2$ can be directly computed from the measured $X$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: Why is damping necessary in a seismic accelerometer? What is the optimum damping ratio and why?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Necessity of Damping (2 marks):**
> 1. Without damping ($\zeta = 0$), the magnification factor $\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{r^2}{1-r^2}$ becomes **infinite at resonance** ($r = 1$), which is practically unacceptable and can damage the instrument.
> 2. Damping flattens the response curve, allowing the accelerometer to give accurate readings over a wider band of frequencies without spurious peaks.
> 3. Damping also reduces phase distortion, improving waveform accuracy.
>
> **Optimum Damping (1 mark):** The optimum damping ratio for an accelerometer is **$\zeta \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$**. This value provides the **flattest possible amplitude response** over the widest frequency range while maintaining a well-defined output that is proportional to acceleration.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derive the expression for the magnification factor of a damped accelerometer. Show that the instrument measures the base acceleration when $r \gg 1$.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 — Equation of Motion (2 marks):**
> 
> For a seismic mass $m$ with relative displacement $x$ from a base undergoing $y = Y\sin(\omega t)$:
> 
> $$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = mY\omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$
>
> Dividing by $m$ and using $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$:
> 
> $$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = Y\omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$$
>
> **Step 2 — Steady-State Assumption (1 mark):**
> 
> Assume $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$. Substituting and eliminating the phase:
> 
> $$X = \frac{Y\omega^2}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2}}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Magnification Factor in Dimensionless Form (2 marks):**
> 
> Dividing by $Y$ and letting $r = \omega/\omega_n$:
> 
> $$\boxed{\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Asymptotic Behavior (2 marks):**
> 
> For $r \gg 1$:
> 
> $$\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{r^2}{r^2\sqrt{(1/r^2 - 1)^2 + (2\zeta/r)^2}} \to \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0}} = 1$$
>
> So $X \approx Y$. The base acceleration amplitude is $\ddot{y}_{max} = Y\omega^2$.
> Since $X \approx Y$:
> 
> $$\ddot{y}_{max} \approx X\omega^2$$
>
> The instrument therefore measures the base acceleration directly when $r \gg 1$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A seismic instrument has a natural frequency of 4 Hz and a damping ratio of 0.65. When used as an accelerometer to measure a vibration of frequency 25 Hz and amplitude 1.2 mm, determine: (i) the relative displacement amplitude, and (ii) the peak acceleration of the base.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Given Data:**
> - $f_n = 4$ Hz $\Rightarrow \omega_n = 2\pi f_n = 8\pi$ rad/s
> - $\zeta = 0.65$
> - $f = 25$ Hz $\Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi f = 50\pi$ rad/s
> - $Y = 1.2$ mm $= 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
>
> **Step 1 — Frequency Ratio:**
> 
> $$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{50\pi}{8\pi} = 6.25$$
>
> **Step 2 — Magnification Factor:**
> 
> $$\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{(6.25)^2}{\sqrt{(1 - 39.0625)^2 + (2 \times 0.65 \times 6.25)^2}}$$
> 
> $$= \frac{39.0625}{\sqrt{(-38.0625)^2 + (8.125)^2}} = \frac{39.0625}{\sqrt{1448.76 + 66.02}} = \frac{39.0625}{\sqrt{1514.78}}$$
> 
> $$= \frac{39.0625}{38.922} \approx 1.0036$$
>
> **Step 3 — Relative Displacement Amplitude:**
> 
> $$X = 1.0036 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1.204 \text{ mm}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Peak Acceleration:**
> 
> $$\ddot{y}_{max} = Y\omega^2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times (50\pi)^2$$
> 
> $$= 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 24674.01 \approx 29.61 \text{ m/s}^2$$
>
> **Verification using accelerometer relation:**
> 
> $$\ddot{y}_{max} \approx X\omega^2 = 1.204 \times 10^{-3} \times 24674.01 \approx 29.71 \text{ m/s}^2 \checkmark$$
>
> **Final Answers:**
> - **(i)** $X \approx 1.204$ mm
> - **(ii)** $\ddot{y}_{max} \approx 29.61$ m/s²

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a seismic accelerometer. Also discuss the role of each component.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Sketch Description (3 marks):**
>
> A seismic accelerometer consists of:
> - A **rigid casing** (housing) attached to the vibrating body whose acceleration is to be measured.
> - A **seismic mass** $m$ suspended inside the casing by a **light spring** of stiffness $k$.
> - A **dashpot** (damper) of coefficient $c$ connected between the mass and the casing to provide damping.
> - A **displacement transducer** (LVDT or strain gauge) to measure the relative displacement $x$ between the mass and the casing.
>
> **Working Principle (2 marks):**
>
> When the casing (base) vibrates with $y(t) = Y\sin(\omega t)$, the seismic mass tends to remain stationary due to inertia. The relative displacement $x$ between the mass and the casing is detected by the transducer. For high frequency ratios ($r \gg 1$), this relative displacement is directly proportional to the base acceleration: $x \approx y$, so $\ddot{y} \approx x\omega^2$.
>
> **Role of Each Component (2 marks):**
>
> | Component | Role |
> |---|---|
> | Seismic mass $m$ | Provides inertia — reference element |
> | Spring $k$ | Provides restoring force; sets natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ |
> | Damper $c$ | Dissipates energy; suppresses resonance; flattens response |
> | Transducer | Converts relative displacement to electrical signal |
> | Casing | Mechanical interface with vibrating body; protects internals |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the effect of damping on the response of a seismic accelerometer. Discuss why the choice of damping ratio is critical for accelerometer applications.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Effect of Damping on Response (3 marks):**
>
> The magnification factor $\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ depends strongly on $\zeta$:
>
> - **At resonance ($r = 1$):** $\frac{X}{Y} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$. For $\zeta = 0$, this is infinite — physically impractical. Higher $\zeta$ reduces the peak.
> - **At low $r$ ($r < 1$):** Damping reduces the response below the undamped value.
> - **At high $r$ ($r > 1$):** Damping causes $X/Y$ to drop below 1 and asymptotically approach 1 only slowly.
> - **Phase response:** Damping ensures smooth phase transition from 0° to 180°, avoiding abrupt 180° jumps at resonance.
>
> **Graphical Interpretation (2 marks):**
>
> For $\zeta = 0$: sharp infinite peak at $r = 1$.
> For $\zeta = 0.2$: peak of magnitude 2.5 at $r = 1$.
> For $\zeta = 0.7$: peak of magnitude 0.71 at $r = 1$, with the **flattest response** in the $r \gg 1$ region.
>
> **Why $\zeta \approx 0.7$ is Optimal (2 marks):**
>
> 1. The maximum value of $\frac{X}{Y}$ in the accelerometer regime ($r > 1$) is closest to unity when $\zeta \approx 0.7$, providing **accurate measurements**.
> 2. The response curve is **monotonically decreasing** for $r > 1$, eliminating measurement ambiguity.
> 3. Phase varies nearly linearly with frequency, enabling **faithful waveform reconstruction**.
> 4. Trade-off between peak suppression and steady-state accuracy is optimally balanced.
>
> **Conclusion:** $\zeta = 0.6$ to $0.7$ is the **KTU-recommended design value** for seismic accelerometers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the three seismic instruments:** A displacement meter, velocity meter, and accelerometer all use the same physical hardware (mass-spring-damper). They differ ONLY in the operating frequency range:
>    - **Displacement meter:** $r \ll 1$ → $X \approx Y r^2$
>    - **Velocity meter:** $r \approx 1$ → $X \approx Y r$ (with optimal damping)
>    - **Accelerometer:** $r \gg 1$ → $X \approx Y$ (output × $\omega^2$ = base acceleration)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $\omega^2$:** When asked "what does the instrument measure?", students often write "$x$ measures $y$" — WRONG. The instrument measures $x$, but the **quantity of physical interest** is the **base acceleration** $Y\omega^2 \approx X\omega^2$.
>
> 3. **Incorrect limits:** Many students write "$X/Y \to \infty$ as $r \to \infty$". The correct limit is $X/Y \to 1$.
>
> 4. **Units confusion:** $Y$ in mm must be converted to m before computing acceleration in m/s².
>
> 5. **Not specifying the operating range:** An accelerometer only works for $r > 3$ (typical design rule). Stating just the formula without the operational condition loses 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Mixing up $\omega$ and $\omega_n$:** In numerical problems, $\omega$ is the excitation frequency, $\omega_n$ is the natural frequency — **never interchange them**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Accelerometer**
>
> ✅ An accelerometer is a **seismic-type instrument** that measures base **acceleration**.
>
> ✅ Physical components: **seismic mass $m$**, **spring $k$**, **damper $c$**, **displacement transducer**, **casing**.
>
> ✅ **Governing equation:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = mY\omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$
>
> ✅ **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — must be **low** (soft spring, heavy mass).
>
> ✅ **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ — **optimum value 0.6–0.7**.
>
> ✅ **Frequency ratio:** $r = \omega/\omega_n$ — **must be $\gg 1$** for accelerometer operation.
>
> ✅ **Magnification factor:** $\frac{X}{Y} = \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$
>
> ✅ **Phase angle:** $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}\right)$
>
> ✅ **Operating condition:** $r \gg 1$ (typically $r > 3$) gives $X \approx Y$.
>
> ✅ **Measured acceleration:** $\ddot{y}_{max} \approx X \cdot \omega^2$ (where $\omega$ is the known forcing frequency).
>
> ✅ **At resonance ($r = 1$):** $X/Y = 1/(2\zeta)$ — **must be avoided**; damping is essential to keep this finite.
>
> ✅ **Three instrument comparison:**
> - Displacement meter: $r \ll 1$
> - Velocity meter: $r \approx 1$ (with $\zeta = 0.6$–$0.7$)
> - Accelerometer: $r \gg 1$
>
> ✅ **Practical design tip:** Spring stiffness must satisfy $k \leq m\omega_{min}^2/9$ to ensure operation in accelerometer regime for the lowest frequency of interest.
>
> ✅ **Applications:** Crash testing, structural health monitoring, machine vibration analysis, seismic instruments, inertial navigation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seismometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Seismometer ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):** > A **Seismometer** is a *translational vibration measuring instrument* used to record the **amplitude of displacement** of a vibrating body. It operates on the principle of a *seismic mass* suspended inside a casing by a spring–damper system. When the casing is attached to the vibrating body, the seismic mass tends to remain **stationary in absolute space** (due to inertia) while the casing moves with the body. The **relative displacement** between the seismic mass and the casing is recorded and is directly proportional to the actual displacement of the vibrating body. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sitting in a boat on a wavy sea. If you hold a pencil steady in the air (your hand does not move with the boat), the pencil appears to "draw" the motion of the boat relative to your steady hand. The **seismometer works on the same idea**: - The **boat** = the vibrating body (or ground). - **You + pencil** = the seismic mass, which tries to stay still. - The **pencil trace** = the recorded relative displacement. The mass is "lazy" — it has a very low natural frequency — so it cannot keep up with the rapid oscillations of the body, and effectively remains a fixed reference in space. The relative motion between the mass and the casing is then exactly equal (in magnitude) to the displacement of the body itself. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Highlight:** > The natural frequency $f_n$ (or $\omega_n$) of a seismometer is intentionally designed to be **MUCH LOWER** than the forcing (vibration) frequency $f$ (i.e., $\omega \gg \omega_n$). This is the *operational condition* that distinguishes a seismometer from an accelerometer (where $\omega \ll \omega_n$) and a vibrometer (where $\omega \approx \omega_n$). ### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Standard Damping Ratio Range** for a seismometer: $\zeta = 0.4$ to $0.7$. - **Typical Frequency Ratio Condition:** $\omega / \omega_n \geq 3$ (rule of thumb for accurate measurement). - **Standard Seismic Mass Range:** $0.1 \text{ kg}$ to $5 \text{ kg}$ (depending on application). - **Typical Recorded Quantity:** Displacement $x$ in **mm** or **micrometers ($\mu$m)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic) - **Step 1 — Attachment:** The seismometer casing is rigidly bolted to the vibrating body. The casing therefore moves with the body: $x_c = X \sin(\omega t)$. - **Step 2 — Inertia of the Mass:** The seismic mass $m$ suspended inside the casing tends to remain stationary due to its inertia (Newton's First Law), because its natural frequency is designed to be very low. - **Step 3 — Spring–Damper Action:** The mass is connected to the casing by a spring (stiffness $k$) and a damper (damping coefficient $c$). As the casing moves, the spring and damper exert forces on the mass. - **Step 4 — Relative Motion Recording:** A recording mechanism (stylus on a drum, or modern electronic transducer) measures the **relative displacement** $z = x_m - x_c$ between the mass and the casing. - **Step 5 — Why It Equals the Body's Displacement:** Because the mass is essentially stationary ($x_m \approx 0$ in absolute terms), $z = -x_c$, meaning the **magnitude of the recorded relative motion equals the actual displacement of the body**. ### The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why low natural frequency?** So the mass cannot respond to the high-frequency forcing; it remains a near-stationary reference. - **Why a damper?** To suppress resonance at start-up and prevent excessive oscillations during transient vibrations. Without damping, the seismometer would over-read or oscillate wildly. - **Why measure relative motion?** It is physically impossible to measure the absolute motion of a body; the only frame of reference available is the body itself, so the relative measurement is the practical alternative. ### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the symbols, units, and conditions. $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Table: Seismometer Formula Reference (KTU 2024)}\\[4pt] &\text{Natural Frequency:} \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} \quad [\text{rad/s}] \\ &\text{Damping Ratio:} \quad \zeta = \dfrac{c}{2m\omega_n} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} \quad [\text{dimensionless}] \\ &\text{Forcing Frequency:} \quad \omega = 2\pi f \quad [\text{rad/s}] \\ &\text{Frequency Ratio:} \quad r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n} \\ &\text{Amplitude Ratio (General):} \quad \dfrac{Z}{X} = \dfrac{r^{2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta r)^{2}}} \\ &\text{Phase Angle:} \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1-r^{2}}\right) \quad [\text{radians or degrees}] \\ &\text{Seismometer Condition:} \quad r \gg 1 \;\;(\text{i.e., } \omega \gg \omega_n) \\ &\text{Amplitude Ratio (Limit } r \gg 1\text{):} \quad \dfrac{Z}{X} \rightarrow 1 \\ &\text{Phase Angle (Limit } r \gg 1\text{):} \quad \phi \rightarrow 180^{\circ} \\ &\text{Error Percentage:} \quad \%E = \left(\dfrac{Z}{X} - 1\right) \times 100\% \\ \end{aligned} $$ | Symbol | Quantity | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $m$ | Seismic mass | kg | | $k$ | Spring stiffness | N/m | | $c$ | Damping coefficient | N·s/m | | $\omega_n$ | Natural (natural) circular frequency | rad/s | | $f_n$ | Natural frequency in Hz | Hz | | $\zeta$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless | | $\omega$ | Forcing circular frequency | rad/s | | $f$ | Forcing frequency in Hz | Hz | | $X$ | Actual displacement amplitude of body | m or mm | | $Z$ | Recorded relative displacement amplitude | m or mm | | $z$ | Relative displacement $x_m - x_c$ | m or mm | | $\phi$ | Phase lag of $z$ relative to $x_c$ | rad or deg | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Earthquake Engineering:** Seismometers record ground motion during seismic events, used in geophysics and structural safety audits. - **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** Modern bridge and high-rise building diagnostics employ seismometers to detect resonant modes and foundation vibrations. - **Heavy Machinery Diagnostics:** Monitoring vibrations of turbines, generators, and large IC engines during operation. - **Aerospace and Defense:** Measuring vibrations of aircraft wings, missile structures, and rocket payloads during flight. - **Mining and Blasting:** Recording ground tremors from blasting operations to monitor environmental impact. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Equation of Motion of a Seismometer (Full Derivation) **Setup:** Consider a seismic mass $m$ suspended inside a casing by a spring (stiffness $k$) and a viscous damper (coefficient $c$). The casing is bolted to a vibrating body whose displacement is given by: $$ x_c(t) = X \sin(\omega t) $$ Let the **absolute displacement** of the seismic mass be $x_m(t)$. Define the **relative displacement**: $$ z(t) = x_m(t) - x_c(t) $$ **Forces acting on the seismic mass** (in the absolute inertial frame): - Spring force (tries to restore the mass relative to casing): $F_s = -k z$ - Damping force (resists relative velocity): $F_d = -c \dot{z}$ **Newton's Second Law** (no external force, only spring and damper act on the mass): $$ m \ddot{x}_m = -k z - c \dot{z} $$ Substitute $x_m = z + x_c$ so that $\ddot{x}_m = \ddot{z} + \ddot{x}_c$: $$ m (\ddot{z} + \ddot{x}_c) = -k z - c \dot{z} $$ Rearrange: $$ m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{x}_c $$ Now, since $x_c = X \sin(\omega t)$, we have $\ddot{x}_c = -\omega^{2} X \sin(\omega t)$: $$ m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \left(-\omega^{2} X \sin(\omega t)\right) = m \omega^{2} X \sin(\omega t) $$ Divide through by $m$: $$ \ddot{z} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{z} + \omega_n^{2} z = \omega^{2} X \sin(\omega t) $$ This is the **standard second-order linear differential equation** governing the relative motion of the seismometer mass. ### 3.2 Steady-State Solution & Amplitude Ratio Assume a steady-state harmonic solution of the form: $$ z(t) = Z \sin(\omega t - \phi) $$ Compute the derivatives: $$ \dot{z} = Z \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) $$ $$ \ddot{z} = -Z \omega^{2} \sin(\omega t - \phi) $$ Substitute into the equation of motion: $$ -Z \omega^{2} \sin(\omega t - \phi) + 2\zeta\omega_n Z \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) + \omega_n^{2} Z \sin(\omega t - \phi) = \omega^{2} X \sin(\omega t) $$ Group the sine and cosine terms on the left: $$ Z \left[(\omega_n^{2} - \omega^{2})\sin(\omega t - \phi) + 2\zeta\omega_n \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)\right] = \omega^{2} X \sin(\omega t) $$ Expand $\sin(\omega t - \phi) = \sin(\omega t)\cos\phi - \cos(\omega t)\sin\phi$: $$ \begin{aligned} Z \Big[ &(\omega_n^{2} - \omega^{2})\left(\sin\omega t \cos\phi - \cos\omega t \sin\phi\right) \\ &+ 2\zeta\omega_n \omega \left(\cos\omega t \cos\phi + \sin\omega t \sin\phi\right) \Big] = \omega^{2} X \sin\omega t \end{aligned} $$ Collect $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$ terms: $$ \begin{aligned} Z \Big[ & \underbrace{\left((\omega_n^{2} - \omega^{2})\cos\phi + 2\zeta\omega_n \omega \sin\phi\right)}_{\text{Coefficient of } \sin\omega t} \sin\omega t \\ + & \underbrace{\left(-(\omega_n^{2} - \omega^{2})\sin\phi + 2\zeta\omega_n \omega \cos\phi\right)}_{\text{Coefficient of } \cos\omega t} \cos\omega t \Big] = \omega^{2} X \sin\omega t \end{aligned} $$ Equate coefficients of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$ on both sides: $$ Z \left[(\omega_n^{2} - \omega^{2})\cos\phi + 2\zeta\omega_n \omega \sin\phi\right] = \omega^{2} X \tag{1} $$ $$ Z \left[-(\omega_n^{2} - \omega^{2})\sin\phi + 2\zeta\omega_n \omega \cos\phi\right] = 0 \tag{2} $$ From equation (2), since $Z \neq 0$: $$ \tan\phi = \dfrac{2\zeta\omega_n \omega}{\omega_n^{2} - \omega^{2}} = \dfrac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^{2}} \quad \text{where } r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n} $$ > **Note:** A negative denominator (when $r > 1$) means the phase is in the second quadrant ($90^{\circ}$ to $180^{\circ}$), confirming the **inversion of motion** typical of a seismometer. Square equation (1) and (2) and add to eliminate $\phi$: $$ Z^{2} \left[(\omega_n^{2} - \omega^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta\omega_n \omega)^{2}\right] = (\omega^{2} X)^{2} $$ Solve for the amplitude ratio: $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{Z}{X} = \dfrac{r^{2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta r)^{2}}}\;} $$ ### 3.3 The Seismometer Operating Condition (Limit Analysis) For a **true seismometer**, the forcing frequency must be much greater than the natural frequency: $r = \omega/\omega_n \gg 1$. Substitute $r^{2} \gg 1$ in the denominator: $$ (1 - r^{2})^{2} \approx r^{4}, \qquad (2\zeta r)^{2} \ll r^{4} $$ Therefore: $$ \dfrac{Z}{X} \approx \dfrac{r^{2}}{\sqrt{r^{4} + (2\zeta r)^{2}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta/r)^{2}}} \approx 1 - 2\left(\dfrac{\zeta}{r}\right)^{2} $$ So: $$ \boxed{\;Z \approx X \quad \text{and} \quad \phi \approx 180^{\circ}\;} $$ This is the **fundamental seismometer identity**: the recorded relative displacement equals the actual displacement of the body, with a $180^{\circ}$ phase inversion (which is corrected in the recording mechanism). ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style) **Problem:** A seismometer has a natural frequency $f_n = 8 \text{ Hz}$ and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.4$. It is used to measure a vibration of frequency $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ with an actual amplitude $X = 2 \text{ mm}$. Find the recorded amplitude $Z$ and the phase lag $\phi$. **Solution:** Step 1: Compute the frequency ratio. $$ r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{f}{f_n} = \dfrac{50}{8} = 6.25 $$ Step 2: Substitute into the amplitude ratio formula. $$ \dfrac{Z}{X} = \dfrac{(6.25)^{2}}{\sqrt{(1 - 6.25^{2})^{2} + (2 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 6.25)^{2}}} $$ $$ = \dfrac{39.0625}{\sqrt{(1 - 39.0625)^{2} + (5.0)^{2}}} = \dfrac{39.0625}{\sqrt{(-38.0625)^{2} + 25}} $$ $$ = \dfrac{39.0625}{\sqrt{1448.75 + 25}} = \dfrac{39.0625}{\sqrt{1473.75}} = \dfrac{39.0625}{38.390} $$ $$ \dfrac{Z}{X} \approx 1.0175 $$ Step 3: Compute the recorded amplitude. $$ Z = 1.0175 \times 2 \text{ mm} \approx 2.035 \text{ mm} $$ Step 4: Compute the phase angle. $$ \tan\phi = \dfrac{2 \zeta r}{1 - r^{2}} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.4 \times 6.25}{1 - 39.0625} = \dfrac{5.0}{-38.0625} = -0.1313 $$ $$ \phi = \tan^{-1}(-0.1313) \approx -7.49^{\circ} $$ Since the denominator is negative, the actual phase is in the second quadrant: $$ \phi = 180^{\circ} - 7.49^{\circ} = 172.51^{\circ} $$ **Answer:** The seismometer records an amplitude of $Z \approx 2.035 \text{ mm}$ (≈ 1.75% error from the true $2 \text{ mm}$) with a phase lag of $\phi \approx 172.5^{\circ}$ (which is approximately $180^{\circ}$, confirming proper seismometer behavior). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Seismometer (Block Flow) The following Mermaid block diagram maps the physical components and signal flow of a seismometer from external vibration to recorded data: ```mermaid graph TD A[VIBRATING BODY
X sin wt] --> B[SEISMOMETER CASING
Rigidly Bolted to Body] B --> C[SPRING ELEMENT
Stiffness k] B --> D[DAMPER ELEMENT
Coefficient c] C --> E[SEISMIC MASS m
Natural Freq wn] D --> E E --> F[RELATIVE DISPLACEMENT z
z equals xm minus xc] F --> G[MECHANICAL AMPLIFIER
Lever and Stylus] G --> H[RECORDING DRUM
or Electronic Transducer] H --> I[OUTPUT TRACE
Amplitude vs Time] I --> J{VALIDATION CHECK
r greater than 3} J -->|PASS| K[DISPLACEMENT X
equals Recorded Z] J -->|FAIL| L[REJECT READING
Increase Forcing Freq] ``` ### 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram: Seismometer vs. Accelerometer vs. Vibrometer ```mermaid graph LR S1[FORCING FREQUENCY w] --> S2{NATURAL FREQ wn} S2 -->|w much less than wn| S3[ACCELEROMETER
Z equals X times w squared over wn squared] S2 -->|w approx equals wn| S4[VIBROMETER
Z equals X times w over wn times damping] S2 -->|w much greater than wn| S5[SEISMOMETER
Z approx equals X] S3 --> S3A[MEASURES ACCELERATION] S4 --> S4A[MEASURES VELOCITY] S5 --> S5A[MEASURES DISPLACEMENT] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The same spring-mass-damper system, when operated at three different frequency ranges, behaves as three completely different instruments. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this *unified theory* of vibration instruments. ### 4.3 Physical Schematic Description (For Board Exam Sketches) Although Mermaid cannot render a true physical schematic, the conventional KTU board-exam sketch of a seismometer consists of the following labeled elements: - **Outer Casing** (rectangular box, drawn with hatching to indicate rigid body). - **Top Mounting Bolt** on the casing (for attachment to vibrating body). - **Spring** (drawn as a coil) connecting the top of the casing to the seismic mass. - **Seismic Mass** (a shaded rectangular block hanging inside the casing). - **Damper** (drawn as a piston inside a cylinder) connected between the mass and the casing base. - **Recording Stylus** (a pointed pen) attached to the mass, writing on a rotating drum. - **Labeled Quantities:** $m$, $k$, $c$, $x_c = X\sin(\omega t)$, $z = x_m - x_c$, $x_m$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a seismometer. State the condition under which a vibration instrument acts as a seismometer.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > A **seismometer** is a vibration measuring instrument that records the **displacement amplitude** of a vibrating body by measuring the relative motion between a seismic mass and its casing. *(1 mark)* > The instrument acts as a seismometer when the **forcing frequency $\omega$ is much greater than the natural frequency $\omega_n$** of the spring-mass system, i.e., $\omega / \omega_n \gg 1$. *(1 mark)* > Under this condition, the seismic mass remains nearly stationary in absolute space due to its inertia, and the recorded relative displacement $Z$ becomes approximately equal to the actual displacement $X$ of the body (with a $180°$ phase shift). *(1 mark)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Write down the amplitude ratio expression for a seismometer. What is the limiting value of the phase angle as $\omega/\omega_n \to \infty$?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > The amplitude ratio for a seismometer is: > $$\dfrac{Z}{X} = \dfrac{(\omega/\omega_n)^{2}}{\sqrt{\left(1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^{2}\right)^{2} + \left(2\zeta \omega/\omega_n\right)^{2}}}$$ *(2 marks)* > As $\omega/\omega_n \to \infty$, the phase angle $\phi \to 180°$. *(1 mark)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Every Part B question has an **internal choice** between two questions (A or B). Solve the one you are most confident about. Each question carries sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model Question] **(a) Derive the equation of motion of a seismometer and obtain the expression for its amplitude ratio.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution (7 Marks):** - **Diagram and Setup (1 mark):** Draw a free-body diagram of a seismic mass $m$ suspended in a casing by a spring $k$ and damper $c$. Let casing displacement be $x_c = X \sin(\omega t)$ and mass absolute displacement be $x_m$. Define relative displacement $z = x_m - x_c$. - **Newton's Second Law (1 mark):** Apply Newton's law in the inertial frame: $$m\ddot{x}_m = -k z - c \dot{z}$$ - **Substitution (1 mark):** Substitute $x_m = z + x_c$ and $\ddot{x}_c = -\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t)$ to obtain: $$m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + k z = m\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t)$$ - **Steady-State Assumption (1 mark):** Assume $z = Z \sin(\omega t - \phi)$ and substitute into the equation. - **Equating Coefficients (1 mark):** Separate sine and cosine terms; obtain two equations. - **Eliminating Phase (1 mark):** Square and add both equations to get: $$\dfrac{Z}{X} = \dfrac{r^{2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta r)^{2}}}$$ - **Final Boxed Result (1 mark):** Present the final amplitude ratio with $r = \omega/\omega_n$ clearly defined. --- **(b) A seismometer has a seismic mass of $0.5 \text{ kg}$ and a spring stiffness of $2000 \text{ N/m}$. When the instrument is placed on a vibrating body operating at $50 \text{ Hz}$, the relative amplitude recorded is $1.2 \text{ mm}$. Determine:** *(7 marks)* - **(i)** The natural frequency of the seismometer. - **(ii)** The actual amplitude of vibration of the body. - **(iii)** The percentage error in measurement (assume $\zeta = 0.5$). **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **(i) Natural Frequency (2 marks):** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2000}{0.5}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{63.25}{2\pi} \approx 10.07 \text{ Hz}$$ **(ii) Actual Amplitude (3 marks):** First compute $r = f/f_n = 50 / 10.07 \approx 4.965$. $$\dfrac{Z}{X} = \dfrac{r^{2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta r)^{2}}}$$ Substitute $r = 4.965$, $\zeta = 0.5$: $$ 1 - r^2 = 1 - 24.65 = -23.65 $$ $$ (1 - r^2)^2 = 559.32 $$ $$ 2\zeta r = 2(0.5)(4.965) = 4.965 $$ $$ (2\zeta r)^2 = 24.65 $$ $$ \dfrac{Z}{X} = \dfrac{24.65}{\sqrt{559.32 + 24.65}} = \dfrac{24.65}{\sqrt{583.97}} = \dfrac{24.65}{24.17} \approx 1.0199 $$ $$ X = \dfrac{Z}{1.0199} = \dfrac{1.2}{1.0199} \approx 1.177 \text{ mm} $$ **(iii) Percentage Error (2 marks):** $$\%E = \left(\dfrac{Z - X}{X}\right) \times 100\% = \left(\dfrac{1.2 - 1.177}{1.177}\right) \times 100\% \approx 1.95\%$$ **Final Answers:** $f_n \approx 10.07 \text{ Hz}$, $X \approx 1.177 \text{ mm}$, Error $\approx 1.95\%$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a seismometer. List the necessary conditions for an instrument to act as a displacement measuring seismometer.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution (7 Marks):** - **Sketch (2 marks):** Draw and label the casing, seismic mass $m$, spring $k$, damper $c$, stylus, and recording drum. Indicate $x_c = X\sin(\omega t)$ and relative displacement $z$. - **Working Principle (3 marks):** - The casing is attached to the vibrating body and moves with it. - The seismic mass, due to its high inertia, tends to remain stationary in absolute space. - The relative motion $z = x_m - x_c$ between the mass and casing is recorded. - For high forcing frequency, the recorded $Z$ equals the body's actual displacement $X$. - **Conditions for Displacement Measurement (2 marks):** 1. The forcing frequency must be much greater than the natural frequency: $\omega \gg \omega_n$. 2. The damping ratio should be in the range $0.4 \leq \zeta \leq 0.7$ to prevent resonance and ensure quick settling. 3. The spring–mass system should be linear (no plastic deformation). 4. The natural frequency should be stable and not drift with temperature. --- **(b) A seismometer is to be designed to measure vibrations in the frequency range $20 \text{ Hz}$ to $500 \text{ Hz}$ with a maximum permissible error of $5\%$. If the damping ratio is $\zeta = 0.6$, determine the maximum allowable natural frequency of the seismometer.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Identify the critical condition (2 marks):** The maximum error occurs at the **lowest** forcing frequency in the operating range, i.e., $f_{\min} = 20 \text{ Hz}$, because $r = f/f_n$ is smallest there. **Step 2 — Set up the error inequality (2 marks):** We require $\left|\dfrac{Z}{X} - 1\right| \leq 0.05$, so: $$0.95 \leq \dfrac{Z}{X} \leq 1.05$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the amplitude ratio formula (2 marks):** $$ \dfrac{r^{2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta r)^{2}}} \geq 0.95 $$ Square both sides: $$ \dfrac{r^{4}}{(1 - r^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta r)^{2}} \geq 0.9025 $$ $$ r^{4} \geq 0.9025 \left[(1 - r^{2})^{2} + 1.44 r^{2}\right] \quad \text{(since } 2\zeta = 1.2\text{)} $$ **Step 4 — Iterative solution (1 mark):** Try $r = 3.5$: LHS: $r^4 = 150.06$. $1 - r^2 = -11.25$, $(1-r^2)^2 = 126.56$. $1.44 r^2 = 17.64$. RHS: $0.9025 \times (126.56 + 17.64) = 0.9025 \times 144.20 = 130.14$. LHS > RHS ✓. So $r = 3.5$ works. Then $f_n = f_{\min} / r = 20 / 3.5 \approx 5.71 \text{ Hz}$. **Final Answer:** Maximum allowable natural frequency $f_n \approx 5.7 \text{ Hz}$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes:** > 1. **Sign error in the forcing term:** The right-hand side of the seismometer equation is $+m\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t)$ (positive), NOT $-m\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t)$. This is because the relative motion equation has an **inverted** sign compared to the standard forced-vibration equation. Students frequently carry over the wrong sign. *(Lose up to 2 marks if missed.)* > 2. **Forgetting the $180°$ phase shift:** For a seismometer, the recorded motion is **opposite in direction** to the body motion. Always state the phase angle explicitly, not just the amplitude. > 3. **Confusing seismometer with accelerometer:** Seismometer has $\omega \gg \omega_n$; accelerometer has $\omega \ll \omega_n$. Mixing these gives a 0 for the operating condition. > 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Always convert Hz to rad/s (multiply by $2\pi$) before substituting into formulas involving $\omega_n$, $\omega$, or $\zeta$. Mixing units in the same equation is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > 5. **Missing the internal choice in Part B:** KTU ESE strictly requires attempting the question of your choice. Read both Question A and Question B before starting. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this section for last-minute KTU exam revision.** - **Seismometer Definition:** Vibration instrument that measures **displacement** of a body by recording the relative motion between a seismic mass and its casing. *(Module 3, KTU 2024)* - **Operating Condition:** $\omega \gg \omega_n$ (forcing frequency much greater than natural frequency). *(Highest-weightage concept.)* - **Mass Behavior:** The seismic mass remains essentially **stationary in absolute space** due to inertia. - **Equation of Motion:** $m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = m\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t)$ — note the **positive** forcing term. - **Amplitude Ratio:** $Z/X = r^2 / \sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ where $r = \omega/\omega_n$. - **Limit:** As $r \to \infty$, $Z/X \to 1$ and phase $\phi \to 180°$. - **Recommended Damping:** $\zeta = 0.4$ to $0.7$. - **Difference Table to Memorize:** - *Seismometer* → $\omega \gg \omega_n$ → measures **displacement**. - *Accelerometer* → $\omega \ll \omega_n$ → measures **acceleration**. - *Vibrometer* → $\omega \approx \omega_n$ → measures **velocity**. - **Phase Lag:** Always close to $180°$ for a properly operating seismometer. - **Error Formula:** $\%E = \left|\frac{Z - X}{X}\right| \times 100\%$. - **Unit Conversions:** $f(\text{Hz}) \to \omega = 2\pi f$ (rad/s); $1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$. - **Real-World Use:** Earthquake recording, structural health monitoring, machinery diagnostics, aerospace instrumentation.

topics.title: Vibration of Two-Degree of Freedoms System: Influence Coefficient Method - Numerical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibration of Two-Degree of Freedoms System: Influence Coefficient Method - Numerical Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Two-Degree-of-Freedom (2-DOF) System** is a dynamic system whose spatial configuration is completely described by **two independent generalized coordinates**. The **Influence Coefficient Method** is a systematic matrix-based procedure for formulating the equations of motion of such systems using either **flexibility influence coefficients** $a_{ij}$ (deflection at coordinate $i$ due to a unit static force at coordinate $j$) or **stiffness influence coefficients** $k_{ij}$ (force at coordinate $i$ required to produce a unit static deflection at coordinate $j$, all other coordinates being constrained to zero).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The phrase "influence coefficient method" in KTU 2024 syllabus covers both:
> 1. **Flexibility Influence Coefficient Method** (also called *Maxwell-Mohr Method*)
> 2. **Stiffness Influence Coefficient Method**
>
> Both methods are equivalent but use reciprocal mathematical formulations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **clothesline** stretched between two poles, and you hang **two shirts of different weights** at two different points. If you push the first shirt by 1 cm, you will notice that:
- The first shirt moves a certain amount (call it $a_{11}$)
- The second shirt also jiggles a bit (call it $a_{21}$) — because the line connects them!

Now, if you instead push the **second** shirt by 1 cm, you measure:
- Movement of the second shirt ($a_{22}$)
- Movement of the first shirt ($a_{12}$)

This physical intuition — that **a force at one location affects displacements everywhere** — is precisely what the influence coefficients capture. They form the elements of the **flexibility matrix**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem:** For a linear elastic system, the displacement at coordinate $i$ due to a unit load at coordinate $j$ equals the displacement at coordinate $j$ due to a unit load at coordinate $i$.
>
> Mathematically: $a_{ij} = a_{ji}$ (The flexibility matrix is **symmetric**).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics Used

- **Stiffness:** $k$ in **N/m**
- **Mass:** $m$ in **kg**
- **Flexibility:** $a$ in **m/N**
- **Natural Frequency:** $\omega_n$ in **rad/s**
- **Frequency:** $f_n$ in **Hz**, where $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi)$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Steps

The Influence Coefficient Method follows a structured **six-step logical flow**:

1. **Step 1 — Identify DOFs:** Choose two independent coordinates $x_1$ and $x_2$ (typically translational or rotational displacements of the masses).

2. **Step 2 — Build Flexibility/Stiffness Matrix:**
   - For flexibility method: Apply a **unit static force** at each DOF (one at a time) and measure deflections at all DOFs.
   - This yields $a_{ij}$ values which form the flexibility matrix $[a]$.
   - For stiffness method: Apply a **unit static displacement** at each DOF and measure the reaction forces.

3. **Step 3 — Construct Mass Matrix $[M]$:** A diagonal matrix for uncoupled lumped masses:
   $$[M] = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

4. **Step 4 — Formulate Eigenvalue Problem:**
   - **Flexibility Method:** $[a][M]\{X\} = \lambda\{X\}$, where $\lambda = 1/\omega_n^2$
   - **Stiffness Method:** $[K]\{X\} = \omega_n^2[M]\{X\}$

5. **Step 5 — Solve Frequency Equation:** Set $\det \vert [a][M] - \lambda[I] \vert = 0$ to obtain $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$, and hence natural frequencies.

6. **Step 6 — Extract Mode Shapes:** Substitute each $\lambda_i$ back to find the principal mode vectors $\{X\}_i$.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Explained

| Concept | Why It Matters | How It Is Used |
|---|---|---|
| Flexibility matrix | Captures complete static behaviour of the structure | Inverts the equation $[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$ |
| Reciprocal symmetry | Energy conservation principle | Halves the experimental work to obtain $a_{ij}$ |
| Eigenvalue problem | Decouples the system into independent SDOF oscillators | Yields natural frequencies and mode shapes |
| Orthogonality of modes | Allows modal superposition for forced response | Simplifies the forced vibration solution |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_{ij} = \dfrac{\delta_i}{F_j}$ | Flexibility influence coefficient: deflection at $i$ due to unit force at $j$ |
| 2 | $a_{ij} = a_{ji}$ | Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem (symmetry of $[a]$) |
| 3 | $[a] = [K]^{-1}$ | Relationship between flexibility and stiffness matrices |
| 4 | $\vert [a][M] - \lambda[I] \vert = 0$ | Frequency equation (flexibility method) |
| 5 | $\vert [K] - \omega_n^2[M] \vert = 0$ | Frequency equation (stiffness method) |
| 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2}$ | Eigenvalue relationship |
| 7 | $\{X\}^{(r)T}[M]\{X\}^{(s)} = 0$ for $r \neq s$ | Orthogonality condition of mode shapes |
| 8 | $\{X\}^{(r)T}[a]\{X\}^{(s)} = 0$ for $r \neq s$ | Orthogonality w.r.t. flexibility matrix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Note:** In the formula table above, the symbol "$\vert \dots \vert$" denotes the **determinant** of the enclosed matrix, **NOT** absolute value. The determinant operator is essential when writing KTU exam answers for setting up the characteristic equation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The influence coefficient method is the cornerstone of:
- **Structural dynamics** of multi-storey buildings under seismic loads
- **Rotor dynamics** of turbomachinery with multiple discs on a shaft
- **Vehicle suspension** modelling with coupled sprung/unsprung masses
- **Aerospace** analysis of satellite solar panel deployment vibrations
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 General Derivation — Flexibility Influence Coefficient Method

Consider a 2-DOF system with masses $m_1$ and $m_2$. Let $\{x\} = \{x_1 \;\; x_2\}^T$ be the displacement vector.

**Step A — Equation of Motion (D'Alembert's Principle):**

Let $\{F(t)\}$ be the external force vector acting on the masses. By Newton's law:

$$\{F(t)\} = [M]\{\ddot{x}\} + \{F_{restoring}\}$$

**Step B — Express Restoring Forces via Flexibility:**

The displacement at any instant is related to the inertial forces $[M]\{\ddot{x}\}$ through the flexibility matrix:

$$\{x\} = [a]\{F_{inertia}\} = [a][M]\{\ddot{x}\}$$

> **Explanation:** This is the *kinematic* flexibility relation. The flexibility matrix maps the *inertial force vector* (which acts like a static load) to the *resulting displacement vector*. This is the heart of the **flexibility influence coefficient method**.

**Step C — Rearrange into Eigenvalue Form:**

For free vibration, with harmonic motion $\{x\} = \{X\}e^{i\omega t}$ and $\{\ddot{x}\} = -\omega^2\{X\}e^{i\omega t}$:

$$\{X\} = -[a][M]\omega^2\{X\}$$

Rearranging:

$$[a][M]\{X\} = \lambda\{X\}, \quad \text{where } \lambda = \frac{1}{\omega_n^2}$$

**Step D — Non-Trivial Solution:**

For a non-zero solution, the determinant of the coefficient matrix must vanish:

$$\vert [a][M] - \lambda[I] \vert = 0$$

This is the **characteristic (frequency) equation** of the system.

**Step E — Expand Determinant:**

Let $[a][M] = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11}m_1 & a_{12}m_2 \\ a_{21}m_1 & a_{22}m_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix}$

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
(A - \lambda)(D - \lambda) - BC &= 0 \\
\lambda^2 - (A+D)\lambda + (AD - BC) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step F — Solve Quadratic for Natural Frequencies:**

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{(A+D) \pm \sqrt{(A+D)^2 - 4(AD-BC)}}{2}$$

$$\omega_{n1}^2 = \frac{1}{\lambda_1}, \quad \omega_{n2}^2 = \frac{1}{\lambda_2}$$

**Step G — Mode Shape Extraction:**

For each $\lambda_i$, substitute into $(A - \lambda_i)X_1 + B X_2 = 0$:

$$\left(\frac{X_1}{X_2}\right)_i = -\frac{B}{A - \lambda_i} = -\frac{a_{12}m_2}{a_{11}m_1 - \lambda_i}$$

### 3.2 Comprehensive Numerical Example

> **Problem Statement:**
> A 2-DOF spring-mass system has masses $m_1 = 10$ kg and $m_2 = 10$ kg. The flexibility influence coefficients of the supporting structure, obtained from static deflection tests, are:
>
> $$[a] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0.005 \\ 0.005 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m/N}$$
>
> Determine:
> (a) The natural frequencies of the system in rad/s and Hz.
> (b) The principal mode shapes.
> (c) Verify orthogonality of the mode shapes w.r.t. the mass matrix.

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Construct the Mass Matrix:**

$$[M] = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Product $[a][M]$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
[a][M] &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.01 & 0.005 \\ 0.005 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} (0.01)(10) & (0.005)(10) \\ (0.005)(10) & (0.01)(10) \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ s}^2
\end{aligned}$$

So $A = 0.1$, $B = 0.05$, $C = 0.05$, $D = 0.1$.

**Step 3 — Set Up the Characteristic Equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert [a][M] - \lambda[I] \vert &= 0 \\[4pt]
\begin{vmatrix} 0.1 - \lambda & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.1 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the Determinant:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.1 - \lambda)(0.1 - \lambda) - (0.05)(0.05) &= 0 \\[4pt]
(0.1 - \lambda)^2 - 0.0025 &= 0 \\[4pt]
0.01 - 0.2\lambda + \lambda^2 - 0.0025 &= 0 \\[4pt]
\lambda^2 - 0.2\lambda + 0.0075 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the Quadratic Equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{0.2 \pm \sqrt{(0.2)^2 - 4(1)(0.0075)}}{2(1)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.2 \pm \sqrt{0.04 - 0.03}}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.2 \pm \sqrt{0.01}}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.2 \pm 0.1}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = 0.05 \text{ s}^2, \quad \lambda_2 = 0.15 \text{ s}^2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Natural Frequencies:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_{n1}^2 &= \frac{1}{\lambda_1} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2 \\[4pt]
\omega_{n1} &= \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.472 \text{ rad/s} \\[8pt]
\omega_{n2}^2 &= \frac{1}{\lambda_2} = \frac{1}{0.15} = 6.667 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2 \\[4pt]
\omega_{n2} &= \sqrt{6.667} \approx 2.582 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

In Hz:

$$f_{n1} = \frac{\omega_{n1}}{2\pi} = \frac{4.472}{2\pi} \approx 0.712 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{n2} = \frac{\omega_{n2}}{2\pi} = \frac{2.582}{2\pi} \approx 0.411 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Principal Mode Shapes:**

Using the relation $(A - \lambda)X_1 + B X_2 = 0$, i.e., $0.1 X_1 + 0.05 X_2 = \lambda X_1$.

> **Mode 1** ($\lambda_1 = 0.05$):

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.1 - 0.05)X_1 + 0.05 X_2 &= 0 \\[4pt]
0.05 X_1 + 0.05 X_2 &= 0 \\[4pt]
X_1 &= -X_2
\end{aligned}$$

Taking $X_2 = 1$:

$$\boxed{\{X\}^{(1)} = \begin{Bmatrix} -1 \\ \phantom{-}1 \end{Bmatrix}}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** In Mode 1, the masses move **in opposite directions** with **equal amplitudes**. This is the **anti-symmetric (out-of-phase) mode**.

> **Mode 2** ($\lambda_2 = 0.15$):

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.1 - 0.15)X_1 + 0.05 X_2 &= 0 \\[4pt]
-0.05 X_1 + 0.05 X_2 &= 0 \\[4pt]
X_1 &= X_2
\end{aligned}$$

Taking $X_2 = 1$:

$$\boxed{\{X\}^{(2)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** In Mode 2, the masses move **in the same direction** with **equal amplitudes**. This is the **symmetric (in-phase) mode**.

**Step 8 — Verify Orthogonality (Mass Matrix):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\{X\}^{(1)T}[M]\{X\}^{(2)} &= \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 10 \\ 10 \end{Bmatrix} \\[6pt]
&= (-1)(10) + (1)(10) \\[6pt]
&= -10 + 10 = 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

Orthogonality condition **verified**. The two modes are linearly independent and form a complete basis for representing any vibration of the system.

### 3.3 Numerical Example — Stiffness Influence Coefficient Method

> **Problem Statement:**
> A 2-DOF system has masses $m_1 = m_2 = 5$ kg, and the stiffness influence coefficients of the system are given by:
>
> $$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 3000 & -1000 \\ -1000 & 2000 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}$$
>
> Find the natural frequencies and principal modes.

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Formulate Eigenvalue Equation:**

$$\vert [K] - \omega_n^2 [M] \vert = 0$$

$$[M] = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Form the Determinant:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{vmatrix} 3000 - 5\omega_n^2 & -1000 \\ -1000 & 2000 - 5\omega_n^2 \end{vmatrix} &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Expand and Simplify:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(3000 - 5\omega_n^2)(2000 - 5\omega_n^2) - (-1000)(-1000) &= 0 \\[4pt]
(3000)(2000) - (3000)(5\omega_n^2) - (5\omega_n^2)(2000) + 25\omega_n^4 - 10^6 &= 0 \\[4pt]
6 \times 10^6 - 15000\omega_n^2 - 10000\omega_n^2 + 25\omega_n^4 - 10^6 &= 0 \\[4pt]
25\omega_n^4 - 25000\omega_n^2 + 5 \times 10^6 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Dividing by 5:

$$5\omega_n^4 - 5000\omega_n^2 + 10^6 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve Quadratic in $\omega_n^2$:**

Let $u = \omega_n^2$:

$$u = \frac{5000 \pm \sqrt{(5000)^2 - 4(5)(10^6)}}{2(5)} = \frac{5000 \pm \sqrt{25 \times 10^6 - 20 \times 10^6}}{10}$$

$$u = \frac{5000 \pm \sqrt{5 \times 10^6}}{10} = \frac{5000 \pm 2236.07}{10}$$

$$u_1 = \frac{5000 - 2236.07}{10} = 276.39, \quad u_2 = \frac{5000 + 2236.07}{10} = 723.61$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{n1} \approx 16.63 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_{n2} \approx 26.90 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**Step 5 — Mode Shapes:**

For Mode 1: $(3000 - 5 \times 276.39)X_1 - 1000 X_2 = 0$
$$1618.05 X_1 = 1000 X_2 \Rightarrow \frac{X_1}{X_2} = 0.618$$

For Mode 2: $(3000 - 5 \times 723.61)X_1 - 1000 X_2 = 0$
$$-618.05 X_1 = 1000 X_2 \Rightarrow \frac{X_1}{X_2} = -1.618$$

$$\{X\}^{(1)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.618 \\ 1.000 \end{Bmatrix}, \quad \{X\}^{(2)} = \begin{Bmatrix} -1.618 \\ 1.000 \end{Bmatrix}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical System Schematic (2-DOF Spring-Mass System)

The classical two-mass, three-spring 2-DOF system is shown below in a block-level functional architecture flow:

```mermaid
graph TD
    Wall1[Wall Fixed Support]:::wall
    Spring1[Spring k1]:::spring
    Mass1[Mass m1 Position x1]:::mass
    Spring2[Spring k2 Coupling]:::spring
    Mass2[Mass m2 Position x2]:::mass
    Spring3[Spring k3]:::spring
    Wall2[Wall Fixed Support]:::wall

    Wall1 --> Spring1
    Spring1 --> Mass1
    Mass1 --> Spring2
    Spring2 --> Mass2
    Mass2 --> Spring3
    Spring3 --> Wall2

    Mass1 -.->|F1 External| Mass1
    Mass2 -.->|F2 External| Mass2

    classDef wall fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef spring fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mass fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Influence Coefficient Method — Procedural Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start Identify 2 DOF System]:::start
    Step1[Step 1 Choose Coordinates x1 and x2]:::step
    Step2[Step 2 Apply Unit Force at Each DOF Separately]:::step
    Step3[Step 3 Measure Deflections to Obtain a_ij]:::step
    Step4[Step 4 Construct Flexibility Matrix a and Mass Matrix M]:::step
    Step5[Step 5 Compute Product Matrix a times M]:::step
    Step6[Step 6 Form Eigenvalue Equation det of a M minus lambda I equals 0]:::step
    Step7[Step 7 Solve Quadratic for lambda1 and lambda2]:::step
    Step8[Step 8 Calculate Natural Frequencies omega_n1 and omega_n2]:::step
    Step9[Step 9 Substitute lambda to Find Mode Shapes]:::step
    Step10[Step 10 Verify Orthogonality Condition]:::step
    End[End Natural Frequencies and Mode Shapes Obtained]:::end

    Start --> Step1
    Step1 --> Step2
    Step2 --> Step3
    Step3 --> Step4
    Step4 --> Step5
    Step5 --> Step6
    Step6 --> Step7
    Step7 --> Step8
    Step8 --> Step9
    Step9 --> Step10
    Step10 --> End

    classDef start fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef step fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px
    classDef end fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Interaction Map: Flexibility vs Stiffness Method

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Flexibility Method
        F1[Flexibility Matrix a] --> F2[a times M times X equals lambda X]
        F2 --> F3[Lambda equals 1 over omega_n squared]
    end

    subgraph Stiffness Method
        S1[Stiffness Matrix K] --> S2[K times X equals omega_n squared times M times X]
        S2 --> S3[Direct eigenvalue solution]
    end

    F3 <-->|Inverse Relationship a equals K inverse| S1

    classDef flex fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stiff fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#663300,stroke-width:2px

    class F1,F2,F3 flex
    class S1,S2,S3 stiff
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define flexibility influence coefficient. State Maxwell's reciprocal theorem of reciprocal displacements.**

**Model Answer:**
A flexibility influence coefficient $a_{ij}$ is defined as the **static deflection at coordinate $i$ produced by a unit static force applied at coordinate $j$**, while all other coordinates are free to take their natural equilibrium positions.

**Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem:** For a linear elastic structure, the deflection at coordinate $i$ due to a unit load applied at coordinate $j$ is equal to the deflection at coordinate $j$ due to a unit load applied at coordinate $i$. Mathematically:
$$a_{ij} = a_{ji}$$
This implies the flexibility matrix $[a]$ is **symmetric**.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between stiffness influence coefficient and flexibility influence coefficient methods.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Stiffness Method | Flexibility Method |
|---|---|---|
| Matrix used | $[K]$ (Stiffness matrix) | $[a]$ (Flexibility matrix) |
| Definition of coefficient | $k_{ij}$ = force at $i$ for unit displacement at $j$ | $a_{ij}$ = displacement at $i$ for unit force at $j$ |
| Equation form | $\vert [K] - \omega^2[M] \vert = 0$ | $\vert [a][M] - \lambda[I] \vert = 0$ |
| Symmetry | $[K]$ is symmetric | $[a]$ is symmetric |
| Matrix size | Increases with DOF | More compact for large DOF systems |
| Best suited for | Systems with known stiffness | Systems with known flexibility (e.g., beams) |
| Relation | $[a] = [K]^{-1}$ (Inverse relation) | $[K] = [a]^{-1}$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion for a two-degree-of-freedom system using the **flexibility influence coefficient method**. Show that the resulting equation leads to an eigenvalue problem. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let the 2-DOF system have two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ with displacements $x_1$ and $x_2$. The flexibility matrix is $[a] = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{bmatrix}$.

By Newton's second law:

$$\begin{aligned}
m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + F_{s1} &= F_1(t) \\
m_2 \ddot{x}_2 + F_{s2} &= F_2(t)
\end{aligned}$$

For free vibration, $F_1 = F_2 = 0$. The restoring (inertial reaction) forces are related to displacements through the flexibility matrix. Inverting the flexibility relation gives forces in terms of displacements, but in the flexibility formulation we write:

$$\begin{aligned}
\{x\} &= [a]\{F_{inertia}\} \\
\{x\} &= [a][M]\{\ddot{x}\}
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming harmonic motion $\{x\} = \{X\}e^{i\omega t}$:

$$\{X\} = -\omega^2 [a][M]\{X\}$$

Rearranging:

$$[a][M]\{X\} = \lambda\{X\}, \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{\omega^2}$$

This is a standard eigenvalue problem with $[a][M]$ as the system matrix. **(7 Marks — Full derivation credited)**

---

**(b)** A 2-DOF system has masses $m_1 = 4$ kg and $m_2 = 6$ kg. The flexibility matrix obtained from static tests is:

$$[a] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0.02 \\ 0.02 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m/N}$$

Determine the natural frequencies and mode shapes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $[a][M]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
[a][M] &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0.02 \\ 0.02 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.16 & 0.12 \\ 0.08 & 0.30 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Set up characteristic equation $\vert [a][M] - \lambda[I] \vert = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.16 - \lambda)(0.30 - \lambda) - (0.12)(0.08) &= 0 \\[4pt]
0.048 - 0.16\lambda - 0.30\lambda + \lambda^2 - 0.0096 &= 0 \\[4pt]
\lambda^2 - 0.46\lambda + 0.0384 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Solve:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{0.46 \pm \sqrt{0.2116 - 0.1536}}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.46 \pm \sqrt{0.058}}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.46 \pm 0.2408}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\lambda_1 = 0.1096 \text{ s}^2, \quad \lambda_2 = 0.3504 \text{ s}^2$$

**Step 4:** Natural frequencies:

$$\omega_{n1} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{0.1096}} = 3.021 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{n2} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{0.3504}} = 1.689 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5:** Mode shapes:

For Mode 1: $(0.16 - 0.1096)X_1 + 0.12 X_2 = 0 \Rightarrow X_1/X_2 = -2.382$

For Mode 2: $(0.16 - 0.3504)X_1 + 0.12 X_2 = 0 \Rightarrow X_1/X_2 = 0.630$

$$\{X\}^{(1)} = \begin{Bmatrix} -2.382 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}, \quad \{X\}^{(2)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.630 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

---

#### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to find the natural frequencies and principal modes of a 2-DOF system using the **stiffness influence coefficient method**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the DOFs (typically $x_1$ and $x_2$).

**Step 2:** Construct the stiffness matrix $[K]$ by applying unit displacement at each coordinate and recording the reaction forces at all coordinates.

**Step 3:** Construct the mass matrix $[M]$ as a diagonal matrix of the lumped masses.

**Step 4:** Formulate the generalized eigenvalue problem:

$$[K]\{X\} = \omega_n^2 [M]\{X\}$$

**Step 5:** Solve the frequency equation $\vert [K] - \omega_n^2[M] \vert = 0$, which is a polynomial in $\omega_n^2$.

**Step 6:** For a 2-DOF system, this expands to a quadratic:

$$\omega_n^4 (m_1 m_2) - \omega_n^2 (k_{11}m_2 + k_{22}m_1 - 2k_{12}m_2) + (k_{11}k_{22} - k_{12}^2) = 0$$

**Step 7:** Solve the quadratic to obtain $\omega_{n1}^2$ and $\omega_{n2}^2$, then take square roots for $\omega_{n1}$ and $\omega_{n2}$.

**Step 8:** Substitute each eigenvalue back into $(k_{11} - \omega_n^2 m_1)X_1 + k_{12} X_2 = 0$ to find the mode shape ratios.

**Step 9:** Verify orthogonality using $\{X\}^{(1)T}[M]\{X\}^{(2)} = 0$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** A two-mass system has $m_1 = 2$ kg, $m_2 = 3$ kg, and the stiffness matrix is:

$$[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 4000 & -1500 \\ -1500 & 3000 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}$$

Determine the natural frequencies and mode shapes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Form the characteristic equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{vmatrix} 4000 - 2\omega_n^2 & -1500 \\ -1500 & 3000 - 3\omega_n^2 \end{vmatrix} &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Expand:

$$\begin{aligned}
(4000 - 2\omega_n^2)(3000 - 3\omega_n^2) - 1500^2 &= 0 \\[4pt]
12 \times 10^6 - 12000\omega_n^2 - 6000\omega_n^2 + 6\omega_n^4 - 2.25 \times 10^6 &= 0 \\[4pt]
6\omega_n^4 - 18000\omega_n^2 + 9.75 \times 10^6 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Dividing by 6:

$$\omega_n^4 - 3000\omega_n^2 + 1.625 \times 10^6 = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{3000 \pm \sqrt{9 \times 10^6 - 6.5 \times 10^6}}{2} = \frac{3000 \pm \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^6}}{2} = \frac{3000 \pm 1581.14}{2}$$

$$\omega_{n1}^2 = 709.43, \quad \omega_{n2}^2 = 2290.57$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{n1} = 26.64 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_{n2} = 47.86 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**Step 4:** Mode shapes:

For Mode 1: $(4000 - 2 \times 709.43)X_1 - 1500 X_2 = 0 \Rightarrow 2581.14 X_1 = 1500 X_2 \Rightarrow X_1/X_2 = 0.581$

For Mode 2: $(4000 - 2 \times 2290.57)X_1 - 1500 X_2 = 0 \Rightarrow -581.14 X_1 = 1500 X_2 \Rightarrow X_1/X_2 = -2.581$

$$\{X\}^{(1)} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0.581 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}, \quad \{X\}^{(2)} = \begin{Bmatrix} -2.581 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $[a][M]$ with $[M][a]$:** Matrix multiplication is **NOT commutative**. Always write $[a][M]$ in the flexibility method, NOT $[M][a]$.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\lambda$ and $\omega_n^2$:** In the flexibility method, $\lambda = 1/\omega_n^2$, NOT $\omega_n^2$. Forgetting this inversion causes the natural frequencies to be inverted (very common error).
>
> 3. **Forgetting Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem check:** Examiners often award marks for verifying $a_{12} = a_{21}$ as a consistency check. Skipping this loses easy marks.
>
> 4. **Wrong sign in mode shape equation:** The correct equation is $(a_{11}m_1 - \lambda)X_1 + (a_{12}m_2)X_2 = 0$. Students frequently miss the negative sign convention.
>
> 5. **Skipping the orthogonality verification:** Even when not asked, demonstrating orthogonality fetches **bonus marks** and shows conceptual clarity.
>
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing mm/N with kg or m/N with grams — keep units strictly consistent throughout.
>
> 7. **Not normalising mode shapes:** Examiners accept any non-trivial ratio, but normalising (e.g., setting the largest component to 1) is best practice for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two-DOF systems** require **two independent coordinates** to fully describe their motion.
- The **Flexibility Influence Coefficient Method** uses static deflection tests to build the flexibility matrix $[a]$, where $a_{ij}$ = deflection at $i$ due to unit load at $j$.
- **Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem:** $a_{ij} = a_{ji}$, so the flexibility matrix is always **symmetric**.
- The fundamental equation is: $[a][M]\{X\} = \lambda\{X\}$ with $\lambda = 1/\omega_n^2$.
- For a 2-DOF system, the characteristic equation is a **quadratic in $\lambda$**, yielding **two natural frequencies**.
- **Principal mode shapes** (or normal modes) are the eigenvectors corresponding to each natural frequency.
- The **lowest natural frequency** corresponds to the **largest $\lambda$** (in flexibility method).
- **Orthogonality** of mode shapes: $\{X\}^{(r)T}[M]\{X\}^{(s)} = 0$ for $r \neq s$.
- The **Stiffness Influence Coefficient Method** is the dual approach, solving $\vert [K] - \omega_n^2[M] \vert = 0$ directly.
- $[a]$ and $[K]$ are **matrix inverses** of each other: $[a] = [K]^{-1}$.
- Always **check units** (m/N for flexibility, N/m for stiffness, kg for mass, rad/s for $\omega$).
- **Out-of-phase mode** has the largest eigenvalue in the flexibility method (slowest oscillation).
- **In-phase mode** has the smallest eigenvalue in the flexibility method (fastest oscillation).
- The flexibility method is **computationally advantageous** for systems with many DOFs but few measurable coordinates.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibration Absorbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vibration Absorbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Vibration Absorber** is a passive auxiliary mechanical device or sub-system that is intentionally coupled to a primary vibrating system in order to suppress, attenuate, or eliminate the unwanted vibrational response of the primary system when subjected to harmonic, periodic, or random excitation. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, vibration absorbers are classified as a subset of **vibration control devices** and operate on the principle of *modal energy transfer* — they accept the kinetic and potential energy from the main system and dissipate or store it within the auxiliary mass-spring (or mass-damper-spring) subsystem.

The two most academically significant classes treated in Module 3 are:

1. **Dynamic Vibration Absorber (DVA)** — also called the **Tuned Mass Damper (TMD)** — first proposed by *Frahm* in 1909. It consists of an auxiliary mass $m_2$ connected to the primary mass $m_1$ through a stiffness element $k_2$ (and optionally a damping element $c_2$).
2. **Centrifugal Pendulum Vibration Absorber (CPVA)** — used in rotating machinery, automotive drivelines, and helicopter/turbine blades.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classic *Frahm undamped tuned absorber* makes the primary mass $m_1$ absolutely stationary **at a single excitation frequency only**. This is its biggest limitation and the very reason the *damped* extension was developed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Acrobat on the Wobbly Bridge"

Imagine two people standing on a long, slightly flexible steel bridge. The bridge tends to sway from side to side. The first person (the **primary mass**) is heavy and the bridge sways under them.

Now a second, lighter person (the **auxiliary absorber mass**) stands nearby holding a rope tied to the bridge rail. When the bridge sways to the **right**, the second person pulls the bridge to the **left** at exactly the right moment using the rope (the **spring $k_2$**). With careful timing — they have *tuned* their pull — the bridge essentially stops swaying. The light person is now moving a lot, but the heavy person (the equipment we wish to protect) is perfectly still.

**Key intuition points:**
- The absorber does **not destroy energy** in the undamped case — it *redistributes* it.
- The auxiliary mass oscillates with **larger amplitude** than the primary mass (this is by design).
- The absorber must be **tuned** (natural frequency matched) to the excitation frequency for the undamped case.
- If the excitation frequency changes, the absorber becomes ineffective unless it is **damped** (broadband response).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard terms must be memorized for KTU ESE answers:
> - **Absorber mass ratio** $\mu = \dfrac{m_2}{m_1}$ (typically 0.05 to 0.20 in practice)
> - **Tuning ratio** $f = \dfrac{\omega_{n2}}{\omega_{n1}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{k_2/m_2}}{\sqrt{k_1/m_1}}$ (for undamped Frahm absorber, $f = 1$)
> - **Damping ratio of absorber** $\zeta_2 = \dfrac{c_2}{2\sqrt{k_2 m_2}}$
> - **Excitation frequency ratio** $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{n1}}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Vibration Absorbers

**A. Based on Energy Dissipation Mechanism**
- **Undamped (Frahm) Absorber** — only a spring $k_2$ connects $m_2$ to $m_1$. Works at *one exact frequency*.
- **Damped (Hahnkamm / Den Hartog) Absorber** — spring $k_2$ in parallel with a damper $c_2$. Works over a *band* of frequencies.

**B. Based on Coupling Geometry**
- **Translational / Linear Absorber** — auxiliary mass moves linearly.
- **Centrifugal Pendulum Absorber (CPVA)** — auxiliary mass pivots on a rotating carrier.
- **Impact Absorber** — uses repeated collisions (used in machinery foundations).

**C. Active vs. Passive**
- **Passive** — fixed parameters (covers all derivations in Module 3).
- **Active / Semi-active** — parameters adjusted in real time (beyond KTU scope).

### 2.2 Governing Assumptions (Standard KTU Model)

1. The primary system is a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) mass-spring-damper: $m_1$, $k_1$, $c_1$.
2. The absorber is a SDOF sub-system: $m_2$, $k_2$, $c_2$.
3. The excitation is **harmonic**: $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$.
4. All motion is small, linear, and steady-state.
5. No coupling damping between primary mass and ground (other than $c_1$).

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" of the Frahm Absorber

**Why does it work?**
When the auxiliary system $m_2$–$k_2$ is tuned so that its natural frequency equals the excitation frequency $\omega$ (i.e., $\sqrt{k_2/m_2} = \omega$), it acts as a *resonant mechanical filter*. The force it transmits back to $m_1$ via spring $k_2$ is exactly **out of phase** and **equal in magnitude** to the applied force $F_0 \sin(\omega t)$. The two forces cancel, leaving $m_1$ un-driven.

**How is the response computed?**
By writing the equations of motion for the coupled 2-DOF system, solving for steady-state, and forcing the displacement of $m_1$ to be zero — this produces the *tuning condition*.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Primary natural frequency | $\omega_{n1} = \sqrt{k_1/m_1}$ | rad/s | Without absorber |
| 2 | Absorber natural frequency | $\omega_{n2} = \sqrt{k_2/m_2}$ | rad/s | Independent SDOF |
| 3 | Tuning condition (Frahm, undamped) | $\omega_{n2} = \omega$ | rad/s | Forces $X_1 = 0$ |
| 4 | Stiffness required for undamped absorber | $k_2 = m_2 \, \omega^2$ | N/m | Substitute tuning condition |
| 5 | Mass ratio | $\mu = m_2/m_1$ | — | Usually 0.05 – 0.20 |
| 6 | Frequency ratio | $r = \omega/\omega_{n1}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Tuning ratio | $f = \omega_{n2}/\omega_{n1}$ | — | For Frahm, $f = r$ |
| 8 | Amplitude $X_1$ (undamped, $c_1 = c_2 = 0$) | $\dfrac{X_1}{F_0/k_1} = \dfrac{1 - r^2}{(1 - r^2)(1 + \mu - r^2) - 1}$ | — | $X_1 = 0$ only when $r = 1$ |
| 9 | Amplitude $X_2$ (undamped, $r = 1$) | $X_2 = -F_0/k_2$ | m | Maximum stroke of absorber |
| 10 | Damped absorber amplitude $X_1$ (steady-state) | $\dfrac{X_1}{F_0/k_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(2\zeta_2 r f)^2 + (r^2 - f^2)^2}{[2\zeta_2 r f (r^2 - 1 + \mu r^2)]^2 + [(r^2 - f^2)(r^2 - 1 + \mu r^2) - \mu r^2 f^2]^2}}$ | — | General expression |
| 11 | Den Hartog optimal tuning ratio $f_{opt}$ | $f_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \mu}$ | — | Independent of $\zeta_2$ |
| 12 | Den Hartog optimal damping ratio $\zeta_{2,opt}$ | $\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\mu}{8(1 + \mu)^3}}$ | — | Achieves min-max amplitude |
| 13 | Max amplitude at optimal design | $\dfrac{X_1}{F_0/k_1}\bigg\vert_{max} = \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2}{\mu}}$ | — | Den Hartog result |
| 14 | CPVA natural frequency (centrifugal absorber) | $\omega_{n,CPVA} = \omega \, n$ | rad/s | $n$ = order of absorber (integer) |
| 15 | Effective length of CPVA | $L_{eff} = n \, l_{CPVA}$ | m | $l$ = actual pendulum length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Hint:** For a *14-mark derivation question*, expect students to be asked to derive the equation of motion for the *undamped Frahm absorber*, apply the steady-state harmonic assumption, and then force $X_1 = 0$ to obtain the tuning condition $k_2/m_2 = \omega^2$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** The **Tuned Mass Damper (TMD)** in skyscrapers such as Taipei 101 (a 660-tonne pendulum), Citicorp Center NYC, and the Burj Khalifa counteracts wind-induced sway.
- **Automotive Engineering:** **Centrifugal pendulum absorbers** in torque converters and dual-mass flywheels suppress engine-order torsional vibration.
- **Power Plant Turbines:** Blade-tuned absorbers reduce flutter and high-cycle fatigue.
- **Aerospace:** Helicopter blade dampers use elastomeric CPVA-type absorbers.
- **Machine Tools:** Internal absorbers in boring bars and long shafts avoid regenerative chatter.
