## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equation of Motion for the Undamped Frahm Absorber

**System Description:**
- Primary mass $m_1$ is connected to a fixed wall by spring $k_1$.
- Auxiliary mass $m_2$ is connected to $m_1$ by spring $k_2$ (no damper).
- Harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ acts on $m_1$.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram and Force Analysis**

Let $x_1(t)$ be the displacement of $m_1$ and $x_2(t)$ the displacement of $m_2$, both measured from the respective static equilibrium positions.

Forces on $m_1$:
- Spring $k_1$ pulls it back with force $-k_1 x_1$.
- Spring $k_2$ pulls it toward $m_2$ with force $+k_2(x_2 - x_1)$ (when $x_2 > x_1$, the spring is stretched and pulls $m_1$ to the right).
- External excitation $F_0 \sin(\omega t)$.

Forces on $m_2$:
- Spring $k_2$ pulls it back toward $m_1$ with force $-k_2(x_2 - x_1)$.

**Step 2 — Newton's Second Law for $m_1$**

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 = F_0 \sin(\omega t) - k_1 x_1 + k_2(x_2 - x_1)$$

Rearranging:

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2) x_1 - k_2 x_2 = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Newton's Second Law for $m_2$**

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 = -k_2(x_2 - x_1)$$

Rearranging:

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 + k_2 x_2 - k_2 x_1 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Matrix Formulation**

The two equations can be compactly written as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_0 \sin(\omega t) \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observe the off-diagonal symmetry** of both $[M]$ and $[K]$ — this is a *reciprocal* (symmetric) system, a property exploited in modal analysis.

**Step 5 — Steady-State Harmonic Assumption**

Assume:

$$x_1(t) = X_1 \sin(\omega t), \quad x_2(t) = X_2 \sin(\omega t)$$

Substituting $\ddot{x}_i = -\omega^2 X_i \sin(\omega t)$ into the two EOMs and cancelling $\sin(\omega t)$:

$$(k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2) X_1 - k_2 X_2 = F_0$$

$$-k_2 X_1 + (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) X_2 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solving for $X_1$ via Cramer's Rule**

The determinant of the impedance matrix:

$$D = (k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2)(k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) - k_2^2$$

Using Cramer's rule:

$$X_1 = \dfrac{F_0 (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2)}{D}, \quad X_2 = \dfrac{F_0 k_2}{D}$$

**Step 7 — Applying the Tuning Condition $X_1 = 0$**

For the primary mass to have *zero* steady-state amplitude, the numerator of $X_1$ must vanish:

$$k_2 - m_2 \omega^2 = 0$$

This yields the **Frahm Tuning Condition**:

$$\boxed{\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_2}{m_2}} = \omega_{n2}}$$

Equivalently:

$$k_2 = m_2 \, \omega^2$$

**Step 8 — Resulting $X_2$ at Resonance Match**

With $X_1 = 0$, the denominator $D$ simplifies:

$$D = -k_2^2$$

Therefore:

$$X_2 = \dfrac{F_0 k_2}{-k_2^2} = -\dfrac{F_0}{k_2}$$

The **negative sign** indicates $X_2$ is in anti-phase with the applied force. The *magnitude* of auxiliary mass motion is:

$$\vert X_2 \vert = \dfrac{F_0}{k_2} = \dfrac{F_0}{m_2 \omega^2}$$

**Step 9 — Engineering Interpretation**

- The primary mass is **perfectly stationary** ($X_1 = 0$).
- The auxiliary mass oscillates with finite amplitude $F_0/k_2$.
- The **stiffness $k_2$** must be sized to keep $X_2$ within physical limits (stroke constraint).
- The **mass $m_2$** is chosen first; the smaller $m_2$ is, the larger $X_2$ becomes for a given $k_2$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Damped (Den Hartog) Vibration Absorber

**System:** $m_1$–$k_1$–$c_1$ on the ground, with $m_2$–$k_2$–$c_2$ attached to $m_1$. Excitation: $F_0 \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Equations of Motion**

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + c_1 \dot{x}_1 + c_2(\dot{x}_1 - \dot{x}_2) + k_1 x_1 + k_2(x_1 - x_2) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 + c_2(\dot{x}_2 - \dot{x}_1) + k_2(x_2 - x_1) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Non-Dimensionalization**

Define:
- $r = \omega/\omega_{n1}$
- $f = \omega_{n2}/\omega_{n1}$
- $\mu = m_2/m_1$
- $\zeta_1 = c_1/(2\sqrt{k_1 m_1})$, $\zeta_2 = c_2/(2\sqrt{k_2 m_2})$

**Step 3 — Steady-State Complex Displacement**

Assume:

$$x_1(t) = X_1 e^{i\omega t}, \quad x_2(t) = X_2 e^{i\omega t}$$

Substituting and solving the algebraic system:

$$\begin{aligned}
X_1 = \dfrac{F_0 (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2 + i \, c_2 \omega)}{(k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2 + i \omega (c_1 + c_2))(k_2 - m_2 \omega^2 + i c_2 \omega) - (k_2 + i c_2 \omega)^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of $X_1$**

$$\dfrac{X_1}{F_0/k_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(2\zeta_2 r f)^2 + (r^2 - f^2)^2}{[2\zeta_2 r f (r^2 - 1 + \mu r^2)]^2 + [(r^2 - f^2)(r^2 - 1 + \mu r^2) - \mu r^2 f^2]^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Den Hartog Optimization**

To make the design robust against frequency drift, **Den Hartog (1956)** chose $f$ and $\zeta_2$ to minimize the **peak value** of the $X_1$ response. The two peaks of the magnitude curve are made equal in height (a "flat-top" response) by setting:

$$f_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \mu}$$

$$\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\mu}{8(1 + \mu)^3}}$$

Under this design, the **maximum** primary amplitude is:

$$\left(\dfrac{X_1}{F_0/k_1}\right)_{max} = \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2}{\mu}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the *most frequently tested* results in the KTU Module 3 part. Memorize the three Den Hartog formulas (11), (12), (13) from the cheat sheet.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Centrifugal Pendulum Vibration Absorber (CPVA)

**Physical Description:** A small mass $m$ is suspended by a light link of length $l$ from a carrier rotating at angular speed $\omega$. The link makes an angle $\phi$ with the carrier's radius.

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy**

The pendulum bob moves in two directions:
- Carried along with the carrier (centripetal component).
- Swinging tangentially relative to the carrier.

$$T = \dfrac{1}{2} m (\dot{r}_b)^2$$

The bob's absolute velocity in the rotating frame, ignoring radial motion, is dominated by tangential swing velocity $l\dot{\phi}$ as seen from carrier. In the inertial frame, the bob's kinetic energy is:

$$T = \dfrac{1}{2} m l^2 \dot{\phi}^2 + m l^2 \omega^2 (1 - \cos\phi) + \text{(constant centripetal terms)}$$

**Step 2 — Potential Energy (Gravitational)**

In a frame rotating with the carrier, an *effective* centrifugal potential appears:

$$V_{eff} = -m l \omega^2 \cos\phi + \text{constant}$$

**Step 3 — Lagrangian and Linearization**

$$L = T - V = \dfrac{1}{2} m l^2 \dot{\phi}^2 + m l^2 \omega^2 (1 - \cos\phi)$$

The equation of motion:

$$m l^2 \ddot{\phi} + m l^2 \omega^2 \sin\phi = 0$$

For small $\phi$:

$$\ddot{\phi} + \omega^2 \phi = 0$$

**Step 4 — Natural Frequency of CPVA**

$$\omega_{n,CPVA} = \omega$$

**Critical observation:** The natural frequency of a simple pendulum absorber **tracks the rotational speed of the carrier**. This is unique — it is "self-tuning." A *second-order* CPVA uses a mass on a hinged rod of length $l_1$ connected to a shorter link $l_2$; the effective length becomes:

$$L_{eff} = n \cdot l$$

where $n$ is the *order* of the absorber, giving:

$$\omega_{n,CPVA} = n \cdot \omega$$

This matches the $n$-th engine order excitation. The natural frequency is **linear in $\omega$**, unlike a fixed TMD, making the CPVA ideal for variable-speed machinery (vehicle drivelines, helicopter rotors).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the $n$-th engine order?** A 4-stroke engine firing every 2 revolutions produces torque pulses at $2\omega_{engine}$. Therefore $n = 2$ absorbers are common in driveline torsional vibration control.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A machine of mass $m_1 = 50$ kg is mounted on a foundation of stiffness $k_1 = 2 \times 10^5$ N/m. It experiences a harmonic force $F(t) = 1000 \sin(60t)$ N.
> **(a)** Design an undamped Frahm vibration absorber such that the machine becomes stationary.
> **(b)** Find the amplitude of the absorber mass.
> **(c)** Comment on the practical limitations.

**Solution:**

**(a) Step 1:** Identify excitation frequency:
$\omega = 60$ rad/s.

**Step 2:** Choose absorber mass. Common rule: $m_2 = 0.1 \, m_1 = 0.1 \times 50 = 5$ kg.

**Step 3:** Apply tuning condition $k_2 = m_2 \omega^2$:

$$k_2 = 5 \times (60)^2 = 5 \times 3600 = 18{,}000 \text{ N/m}$$

**(b)** The amplitude of $m_2$:

$$X_2 = \dfrac{F_0}{k_2} = \dfrac{1000}{18{,}000} = 0.0556 \text{ m} \approx 55.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 (Verification):** Check static deflection of $m_2$ under its own weight — it is small (3.6 mm) and acceptable.

**(c) Practical limitations:**
1. Works *only* at $\omega = 60$ rad/s. If machine speed changes, $X_1 \neq 0$.
2. Stroke $X_2 = 55.6$ mm is large — may require too much space.
3. The undamped version has *infinite* amplitude $X_1$ at the **two new natural frequencies** of the coupled system.
4. Solution: use a damped Den Hartog absorber with $f_{opt} = 1/(1+\mu) = 1/1.1 = 0.909$ and $\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{3 \times 0.1 / (8 \times 1.1^3)} = 0.183$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Define a vibration absorber. How does the undamped Frahm absorber achieve zero vibration of the primary system?

**Model Answer:**

A vibration absorber is a secondary mass-spring (or mass-damper-spring) system attached to a primary vibrating system to reduce its amplitude. In the **undamped Frahm absorber**, the auxiliary mass $m_2$ is connected to $m_1$ through a spring $k_2$, and the natural frequency of this auxiliary system is *tuned* to the excitation frequency $\omega$:

$$\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_2}{m_2}}$$

When this condition holds, the force transmitted by spring $k_2$ on $m_1$ is equal in magnitude but opposite in phase to the applied harmonic force $F_0 \sin(\omega t)$. The two forces cancel, leaving $m_1$ un-driven, and $X_1 = 0$ in the steady state.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> State the Den Hartog optimal tuning ratio and optimal damping ratio for a damped vibration absorber.

**Model Answer:**

For a damped absorber with mass ratio $\mu = m_2/m_1$, the **Den Hartog** optimum design (which minimizes the peak amplitude of the primary mass over a band of excitation frequencies) is given by:

$$f_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \mu}$$

$$\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\mu}{8(1 + \mu)^3}}$$

The maximum primary amplitude under this design is $\sqrt{1 + 2/\mu}$ times the static deflection $F_0/k_1$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** A machine of mass $m_1 = 80$ kg is mounted on a flexible support of stiffness $k_1 = 3.2 \times 10^5$ N/m. A harmonic force $F(t) = 1500 \sin(50t)$ N acts on it.
> **(i)** Calculate the natural frequency of the primary system.
> **(ii)** Determine the undamped vibration amplitude of the machine without any absorber.
> **(iii)** Design an undamped Frahm absorber with mass $m_2 = 8$ kg and find its stiffness.
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the equation of motion for the *damped* vibration absorber (Den Hartog type) and obtain the expression for the optimal tuning ratio $f_{opt}$ and optimal damping ratio $\zeta_{2,opt}$ in terms of the mass ratio $\mu$.
> **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Numerical Design**

**(i) Primary natural frequency:**

$$\omega_{n1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_1}{m_1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.2 \times 10^5}{80}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Amplitude without absorber:**

Frequency ratio: $r = \omega/\omega_{n1} = 50/63.25 = 0.7905$.

For an undamped SDOF, $X_1 = \dfrac{F_0/k_1}{1 - r^2}$:

$$X_1 = \dfrac{1500/320000}{1 - (0.7905)^2} = \dfrac{0.00469}{1 - 0.6249} = \dfrac{0.00469}{0.3751} = 0.01250 \text{ m} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Frahm absorber design:**

Tuning condition: $k_2 = m_2 \omega^2 = 8 \times 50^2 = 8 \times 2500 = 20{,}000$ N/m.

Mass ratio: $\mu = 8/80 = 0.1$.

Amplitude of auxiliary mass: $X_2 = F_0/k_2 = 1500/20000 = 0.075$ m $= 75$ mm.

**Verification:** With absorber in place, $X_1 = 0$ at $\omega = 50$ rad/s. The auxiliary mass moves 75 mm — practical if space allows.

**Part (b) — Derivation of Damped Absorber EOM and Optimization**

The equations of motion for the 2-DOF damped system are:

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + c_1 \dot{x}_1 + c_2(\dot{x}_1 - \dot{x}_2) + k_1 x_1 + k_2(x_1 - x_2) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 + c_2(\dot{x}_2 - \dot{x}_1) + k_2(x_2 - x_1) = 0$$

Assuming $c_1 = 0$ (negligible structural damping) and steady-state harmonic motion $x_i = X_i e^{i\omega t}$, we obtain the complex amplitude ratio:

$$\dfrac{X_1}{F_0/k_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(2\zeta_2 r f)^2 + (r^2 - f^2)^2}{[2\zeta_2 r f (r^2 - 1 + \mu r^2)]^2 + [(r^2 - f^2)(r^2 - 1 + \mu r^2) - \mu r^2 f^2]^2}}$$

**Optimization procedure (Den Hartog):**

Den Hartog (1956) set $P = $ numerator and $Q = $ denominator of this expression. He identified two frequencies $r_1$ and $r_2$ at which the response is locally maximum, and demanded *equal peak heights*:

$$P(r_1)/Q(r_1) = P(r_2)/Q(r_2)$$

He also demanded that the derivative vanish at both peaks. After algebra:

The **optimal tuning ratio** (frequency at which $\partial X_1 / \partial r = 0$ matches between the two peaks):

$$f_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \mu}$$

The **optimal damping ratio** that ensures the two peaks are equal in magnitude:

$$\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\mu}{8(1 + \mu)^3}}$$

The **maximum amplitude** of the primary mass under this optimal design:

$$\left(\dfrac{X_1}{F_0/k_1}\right)_{max} = \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2}{\mu}}$$

For $\mu = 0.1$ in our problem: $f_{opt} = 0.909$, $\zeta_{2,opt} = 0.183$, and max $X_1/F_0/k_1 = \sqrt{21} = 4.58$, i.e., the damped optimal absorber is 4.58 times more effective than the undamped primary, *and it works over a band of frequencies*.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat schematic, derive the equation of motion for an undamped vibration absorber and show that the primary mass remains stationary at the excitation frequency.
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the working principle of a *centrifugal pendulum vibration absorber (CPVA)*. Show that its natural frequency is $n \omega$ and discuss two practical applications.
> **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)** — Refer to Derivation 1 in Section 3.1 above. The schematic is shown in Section 4.1. The two equations of motion are:

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2) x_1 - k_2 x_2 = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 + k_2 x_2 - k_2 x_1 = 0$$

Substituting harmonic solutions and applying Cramer's rule gives $X_1 = F_0 (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) / D$. Setting the numerator to zero gives the **tuning condition** $\omega = \sqrt{k_2/m_2}$, which makes $X_1 = 0$.

**Part (b) — Centrifugal Pendulum Absorber**

A CPVA consists of a small mass $m$ suspended on a light rod of length $l$ from a rotating carrier. In the rotating frame of reference, the bob experiences an effective centrifugal force $m l \omega^2 \sin\phi$ (restoring) and a gravitational component. The Lagrangian yields:

$$m l^2 \ddot{\phi} + m l^2 \omega^2 \sin\phi = 0$$

Linearizing for small $\phi$:

$$\ddot{\phi} + \omega^2 \phi = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_{n,CPVA} = \omega$$

A second-order CPVA uses a hinged compound pendulum with effective length $L = n l$:

$$\omega_{n,CPVA} = n \, \omega$$

**Two practical applications:**

1. **Automotive Driveline Torsional Vibration:** In cars and trucks, the engine produces torque fluctuations at twice the crankshaft speed (4-stroke, 2nd order). A 2nd-order CPVA mounted on the flywheel or torque converter cancels this vibration across the entire RPM range, since its natural frequency *automatically tracks* the engine speed.
2. **Helicopter Rotor Blades:** Each blade carries a small bifilar CPVA. The rotor speed varies, but the absorber's frequency scales with it, suppressing in-plane vibration at the $n$-per-revolution excitation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Vibration Absorbers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $k_1$ and $k_2$ in the impedance matrix:** The diagonal term for $m_1$ is $k_1 + k_2$ (not $k_1$ alone), because $m_1$ is connected to *both* the ground via $k_1$ and $m_2$ via $k_2$. Students lose 1–2 marks by writing $(k_1 - m_1\omega^2)$ instead of $(k_1 + k_2 - m_1\omega^2)$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in $X_2$:** The sign matters! It shows the auxiliary mass moves *out of phase* with the applied force. In numerical problems, write the magnitude and direction.
>
> 3. **Applying the Frahm formula to a damped system:** The simple tuning condition $k_2 = m_2 \omega^2$ is for the *undamped* case only. With damping, you must use $f_{opt}$ and $\zeta_{2,opt}$.
>
> 4. **Mixing up the CPVA's natural frequency:** It is $n\omega$, not $\omega/\sqrt{L/g}$ as in a stationary gravity pendulum. The centrifugal acceleration replaces gravity.
>
> 5. **Stroke constraint oversight:** In a 14-mark numerical design problem, the examiner expects you to compute $X_2 = F_0/k_2$ and comment on whether the absorber mass motion is physically realizable. Skipping this comment costs 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Off-diagonal sign convention:** In the stiffness matrix, the off-diagonal is $-k_2$ (not $+k_2$). Use Newton's law carefully and double-check the sign of the restoring force from $k_2$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Vibration Absorbers (Module 3):**

- **Definition:** A vibration absorber is a *passive secondary system* attached to a primary system to suppress its vibration.
- **Frahm (1909) Undamped Absorber:** Comprises an auxiliary mass $m_2$ and spring $k_2$ only.
- **Tuning Condition:** $k_2 = m_2 \omega^2$ makes $X_1 = 0$ at the excitation frequency $\omega$.
- **Limitation:** Works at *one* frequency only; $X_1$ is unbounded at the two new natural frequencies of the coupled 2-DOF system.
- **Damped (Den Hartog) Absorber:** Adds $c_2$ parallel to $k_2$. Works over a *band* of frequencies.
- **Den Hartog Optimum:** $f_{opt} = 1/(1+\mu)$, $\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{3\mu/[8(1+\mu)^3]}$, $X_{1,max} = (F_0/k_1)\sqrt{1 + 2/\mu}$.
- **Mass Ratio Range:** $\mu = 0.05$–$0.20$ is typical; smaller $\mu$ gives *larger* peak response.
- **Centrifugal Pendulum Absorber (CPVA):** Auxiliary mass on a rotating carrier; $\omega_n = n\omega$ (self-tuning).
- **Applications:** Skyscrapers (TMD in Taipei 101), automotive drivelines (CPVA), helicopter blades, machine tools, turbine blades.
- **Two EOMs for 2-DOF System:** $m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2)x_1 - k_2 x_2 = F_0\sin\omega t$ and $m_2 \ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + k_2 x_2 = 0$ (undamped).
- **Steady-State Assumption:** $x_i = X_i \sin(\omega t)$ in algebraic impedance form, solved by Cramer's rule.
- **Stroke of Absorber Mass:** $X_2 = F_0/k_2$ at perfect Frahm tuning — must be checked for feasibility.
- **Energy Redistribution:** Absorber does not destroy energy; it transfers kinetic energy to the auxiliary mass.
- **KTU Frequently Tested:** Derive the two EOMs, find the tuning condition, and design a damped absorber using Den Hartog formulas.
- **Sign of $X_2$:** Negative — out of phase with applied force — proves cancellation principle.
- **Impedance Matrix Determinant:** Must not equal zero at the excitation frequency, else singular response.
- **Practical Design Rule:** Choose $m_2$ first (geometric constraint), then compute $k_2$ and $c_2$ from the Den Hartog formulas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transverse Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transverse Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transverse Vibration** is a type of mechanical oscillation in which the particles of a body (typically a shaft, beam, or rotating member) move in a direction **perpendicular (normal)** to the longitudinal axis of the member. When this oscillation occurs in a rotating shaft carrying a disc or rotor, it is specifically termed **Whirling** or **Lateral Vibration**, and the corresponding angular velocity at which the amplitude of vibration becomes theoretically infinite is called the **Critical Speed** of the shaft.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, transverse vibration deals with the **bending vibrations of a uniform shaft**, where the restoring force is provided by the **shaft's flexural stiffness (k)** against the **gravity/inertial load of the rotor (W = mg)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Transverse vibration of a beam/shaft with a single point mass (vibrations of beams, critical/whirling speed, Dunkerley's method, and Rayleigh's method for finding natural frequency of complex systems).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a thin metal ruler firmly against the edge of a desk and plucking the free end downwards. The ruler vibrates up and down (sideways), making a humming sound. This up-and-down motion—**perpendicular to the length of the ruler**—is exactly what transverse vibration is.

Now, take a long spinning shaft from a washing machine or a ceiling fan. If you add a slightly imbalanced weight to it (or even if the shaft is not perfectly straight), as the shaft spins, the unbalanced mass experiences centrifugal force. This force pulls the shaft sideways (transverse to the rotation axis). The shaft bends and then springs back, bends again, and so on. The spinning shaft traces out a **whirlpool-like path**, and this sideways whipping motion is the **whirling of a shaft**.

If the rotational speed coincides with the shaft's natural bending frequency, even a tiny imperfection causes the shaft to bend so violently that it can snap — this is the **critical speed**.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Key System Parameters for Transverse Vibration:**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (standard value used in KTU problems)
> - **Static deflection (δst):** Deflection produced by the weight **W** of the rotor at the bearing, measured in **metres (m)** or **mm**.
> - **Stiffness (k):** Force per unit deflection, in **N/m**.
> - **Natural circular frequency (ωn):** in **rad/s**.
> - **Natural frequency (fn):** in **Hz**.
> - **Critical speed (Nc):** in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mechanism of Transverse Vibration (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **A shaft of negligible mass** is considered to act purely as a **massless spring** of stiffness **k**.
2. **A rotor (disc) of mass m** is mounted on the shaft. The weight of this rotor is **W = mg**.
3. When stationary, the shaft deflects by a small amount **δst** (static deflection) due to the weight **W**:
   $$W = k \cdot \delta_{st}$$
4. The **restoring force** opposing any further transverse displacement **y** from equilibrium is the elastic force of the shaft: **F_restore = −k·y**.
5. By **Newton's second law**, the inertia force of the rotor (m·ÿ) must equal the restoring force (k·y). This gives the **Equation of Motion**:
   $$m\ddot{y} + ky = 0$$
6. This is the classical **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF)** undamped free vibration equation, identical in form to longitudinal/torsional vibration, but with **bending stiffness** substituting for axial/torsional stiffness.

### 2.2 Key Conditions for the KTU Board Exam

- The shaft is assumed to be **massless** (its own mass is neglected) — only the disc mass is considered.
- The disc is assumed to be a **point mass**, perfectly balanced when static, but allowed to whirl.
- The whirling path is assumed to be **circular** at the critical speed.
- Viscous damping is **ignored** for theoretical analysis.

### 2.3 Whirling vs. Vibration — The Whirling Speed Concept

When the shaft rotates, the eccentricity **e** of the rotor's centre of mass (relative to the geometric axis) generates a centrifugal force:

$$F_{centrifugal} = m \cdot \omega^2 (y + e)$$

Setting this equal to the elastic restoring force **k·y** gives the equilibrium radius of whirl **r**:

$$r = y + e = \frac{e \cdot \omega^2}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}$$

At **ω = ωn**, the denominator becomes zero → **r → ∞** → This is the **critical whirling speed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical (Whirling) Speed is mathematically equal to the Natural Frequency of Transverse Vibration**, but it is expressed in **rpm** instead of rad/s. The two are numerically distinct quantities representing the same physical phenomenon.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are high-yield and frequently tested in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) for this module.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $k = \dfrac{W}{\delta_{st}} = \dfrac{mg}{\delta_{st}}$ | Shaft stiffness from static deflection | N/m |
| 2 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | Natural circular frequency | rad/s |
| 3 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | Natural frequency | Hz |
| 4 | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}} \times 60 = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | Critical (whirling) speed | rpm |
| 5 | $r = \dfrac{e \cdot \omega^2}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}$ | Radius of whirl at angular speed ω | m |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_{n1}^2} + \dfrac{1}{\omega_{n2}^2} + \cdots$ | **Dunkerley's Equation** (lower-bound estimate for multi-load systems) | rad⁻²/s⁻² |
| 7 | $\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{g \sum W_i y_i}{\sum W_i y_i^2}$ | **Rayleigh's Method** (energy method for complex shafts) | rad²/s² |
| 8 | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | Critical speed in rpm using δst in metres | rpm |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steam & Gas Turbines:** Long rotors (L/D > 6) must operate **far from** their critical speeds during run-up and coast-down to avoid catastrophic failure.
- **Turbo-generators & Centrifuges:** Multi-disc rotors require Dunkerley's method for accurate lower-bound critical speed estimation.
- **Machine Tool Spindles:** Transverse stiffness determines surface finish quality during high-speed machining.
- **Aircraft Propellers & Helicopter Rotors:** Whirling analysis is mandatory for fatigue and aero-elastic stability.
- **Automotive Drive Shafts:** Critical speed limits maximum engine RPM to prevent dangerous whip vibrations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Natural Frequency of a Massless Shaft Carrying a Point Mass (Whirling of a Shaft)

**Given:** A massless shaft of stiffness **k** carrying a point mass **m** at its mid-span, rotating at angular speed **ω** with eccentricity **e** of the mass centre from the geometric centre of rotation.

**Step 1 — Static Equilibrium (shaft stationary):**
The weight **W = mg** of the rotor deflects the shaft by a static deflection **δst**. From the definition of stiffness:

$$W = k \cdot \delta_{st} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{W}{\delta_{st}} = \frac{mg}{\delta_{st}}$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic Equilibrium (shaft rotating at ω, whirl radius = y):**
The centre of mass of the rotor is at a distance **(y + e)** from the bearing axis. The centrifugal force acting on the mass is:

$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (y + e)$$

This force is balanced by the elastic restoring force of the shaft:

$$F_c = k \cdot y$$

**Step 3 — Equating forces:**

$$m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot (y + e) = k \cdot y$$

Expanding:

$$m \omega^2 y + m \omega^2 e = k y$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging to solve for y:**

$$k y - m \omega^2 y = m \omega^2 e$$

$$y(k - m\omega^2) = m \omega^2 e$$

$$y = \frac{m \omega^2 e}{k - m\omega^2}$$

**Step 5 — Expressing in terms of natural frequency:**
Substituting $k/m = \omega_n^2$:

$$y = \frac{\omega^2 e}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}$$

**Step 6 — Radius of whirl (geometric axis to mass centre):**

$$r = y + e = \frac{\omega^2 e}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2} + e = \frac{\omega^2 e + e(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}$$

$$\boxed{r = \frac{\omega_n^2 \cdot e}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}}$$

**Step 7 — Critical Whirling Speed condition:**
As **ω → ωn**, the denominator tends to zero and **r → ∞**. This defines the critical (whirling) speed. The value of the critical speed is therefore:

$$\omega_c = \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

$$\boxed{N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} \quad \text{(rpm)}}$$

**Logical interpretation:** When the rotational speed equals the natural bending frequency, any small eccentricity is amplified infinitely — the shaft becomes dynamically unstable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Dunkerley's Method (Lower-Bound Critical Speed)

**Given:** A shaft carrying **n** concentrated masses **W₁, W₂, …, Wn** at different locations, with corresponding static deflections **y₁, y₂, …, yn** at those points due to the combined loads.

**Step 1 — Natural frequency of a single-load system:**
For a single mass Wi producing static deflection δi on the shaft:

$$\omega_{ni}^2 = \frac{g}{\delta_i}$$

**Step 2 — Concept of "separate" vibration:**
Dunkerley assumed that each load vibrates *independently* (i.e., the deflection at any point is the sum of deflections due to each load acting alone).

**Step 3 — Total static deflection at location i due to all loads:**

$$y_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \delta_{ij}$$

where δij is the deflection at point i due to load Wj alone (with the shaft treated as a beam in bending).

**Step 4 — Self-deflection of load Wi:**
The deflection *at* the location of load Wi due to its own weight alone is **δii**. So:

$$\omega_{ni}^2 = \frac{g}{\delta_{ii}}$$

**Step 5 — Combined system natural frequency:**
For the actual multi-load system, the natural frequency ω satisfies Dunkerley's empirical but rigorously justified relation:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{\omega^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_{n1}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{n2}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{n3}^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{\omega_{nn}^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Dunkerley's equation in critical speed form:**

$$\frac{1}{N_c^2} = \frac{1}{N_{c1}^2} + \frac{1}{N_{c2}^2} + \frac{1}{N_{c3}^2} + \cdots$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dunkerley's method gives a **lower-bound** value of the critical speed. It is *always safer* in design because the actual critical speed is slightly higher. KTU examiners frequently mark this as a mandatory 2-mark sub-question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rayleigh's Method (Energy Method)

**Given:** A shaft carrying **n** concentrated weights **W₁, W₂, …, Wn** at points where the static deflections under the combined loading are **y₁, y₂, …, yn**.

**Step 1 — Assumed mode shape:**
Assume the shaft deflects in a shape **proportional to** the static deflection curve caused by the weights (i.e., the deflection shape is similar to the static deflection shape, with all points vibrating in phase).

**Step 2 — Maximum Kinetic Energy of the system:**
At maximum velocity (when the system passes through the mean position), the velocity of mass Wi is $\dot{y}_i = \omega \cdot y_i \cdot \sin(\omega t + \phi)$. Maximum velocity is **ωyi**.

$$T_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\omega y_i)^2 = \frac{\omega^2}{2g} \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i y_i^2$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Potential Energy of the system:**
At maximum displacement, the strain energy stored in the shaft equals the work done by the weights falling through their respective deflections:

$$U_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i y_i$$

**Step 4 — Energy conservation (T_max = U_max):**

$$\frac{\omega^2}{2g} \sum W_i y_i^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sum W_i y_i$$

**Step 5 — Solving for ω²:**

$$\boxed{\omega^2 = \frac{g \sum W_i y_i}{\sum W_i y_i^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Critical speed in rpm:**

$$\boxed{N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g \sum W_i y_i}{\sum W_i y_i^2}} \quad \text{(rpm)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Rayleigh's method gives an **upper-bound** estimate of the natural frequency / critical speed. Dunkerley's gives a **lower-bound**. The true value lies *between* the two — a fact that KTU examiners love to test as a 2-mark conceptual question.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Summary Table

| Method | Result Type | Best Use Case | KTU Exam Weightage |
|--------|-------------|---------------|--------------------|
| Dunkerley's | Lower-bound (safe estimate) | Multiple discrete loads, no continuous mass data | Very High (10 marks possible) |
| Rayleigh's | Upper-bound (slightly unsafe) | Continuous shaft with distributed mass approximated by n lumped weights | Very High (10 marks possible) |
| Direct (ω = √(g/δst)) | Exact (for 1-load system) | Single concentrated mass on a massless shaft | High (definition problem) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic of a Whirling Shaft (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> **Note:** Since transverse vibration involves a physical 3D bending phenomenon, the diagram below is rendered as a **block-level functional architecture** showing the energy/signal flow of the whirling mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Rotor Mass W = m times g"] --> node2["Centrifugal Force F equals m omega squared times y plus e"]
    node2 --> node3{"Shaft Stiffness k"}
    node3 -- "Resists" --> node4["Restoring Force F sub r equals k times y"]
    node4 --> node5["Equilibrium Radius of Whirl r"]
    node2 --> node6["Compare omega with omega sub n"]
    node6 -- "omega less than omega sub n" --> node7["Stable Whirl r small"]
    node6 -- "omega equals omega sub n" --> node8["CRITICAL SPEED r infinite"]
    node6 -- "omega greater than omega sub n" --> node9["Self Centering r small again"]
    node8 --> node10["Natural Frequency omega sub n equals sqrt g over delta st"]
    node10 --> node11["Critical Speed N sub c equals 60 over 2 pi times sqrt g over delta st rpm"]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing Between Dunkerley's and Rayleigh's

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Transverse Vibration Problem Given"]) --> q1{"Multiple concentrated loads on shaft?"}
    q1 -- "No single load" --> path1["Use Direct Formula omega n equals sqrt g over delta st"]
    q1 -- "Yes" --> q2{"Distributed mass of shaft significant?"}
    q2 -- "No shaft massless" --> path2["Use DUNKERLEY METHOD lower bound safe"]
    q2 -- "Yes shaft self mass matters" --> path3["Use RAYLEIGH METHOD upper bound energy method"]
    path2 --> rec["Compare with direct single load critical speed to validate"]
    path3 --> rec
    rec --> fin(["Report Critical Speed in rpm"])
```

### 4.3 System Architecture for Rayleigh's Energy Method

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input
        W1["Weight W1 at point 1"]
        W2["Weight W2 at point 2"]
        W3["Weight Wn at point n"]
    end
    subgraph StaticAnalysis["Static Deflection Analysis"]
        Y1["Deflection y1"]
        Y2["Deflection y2"]
        Y3["Deflection yn"]
    end
    subgraph EnergyBalance["Rayleigh Energy Equation"]
        KE["Max Kinetic Energy equals omega squared over 2g times sum Wi yi squared"]
        PE["Max Potential Energy equals 1 by 2 times sum Wi yi"]
        EQ["Set KE equals PE"]
    end
    subgraph Output
        OM["omega squared equals g times sum Wi yi divided by sum Wi yi squared"]
        Nc["Critical Speed Nc in rpm"]
    end
    W1 --> Y1
    W2 --> Y2
    W3 --> Y3
    Y1 --> KE
    Y2 --> KE
    Y3 --> KE
    Y1 --> PE
    Y2 --> PE
    Y3 --> PE
    KE --> EQ
    PE --> EQ
    EQ --> OM
    OM --> Nc
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define transverse vibration. How does it differ from longitudinal and torsional vibration?**

**Model Answer:**
Transverse vibration is the oscillatory motion of a body in which particles move **perpendicular** to the longitudinal axis of the member, such as the bending vibration of a shaft carrying a rotor. In contrast, **longitudinal vibration** involves particle motion *along* the axis (extension/compression), and **torsional vibration** involves angular oscillation *about* the axis. The governing equation $m\ddot{y} + ky = 0$ has the same form in all three cases, but the **stiffness k** has different physical meanings — bending stiffness (k_b) for transverse, axial stiffness (AE/L) for longitudinal, and torsional stiffness (GJ/L) for torsional vibration.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] State Dunkerley's method and write its formula. Why is it called a "lower bound" method?**

**Model Answer:**
Dunkerley's method is an empirical technique used to estimate the **natural frequency (or critical speed)** of a multi-degree-of-freedom transverse vibration system. The formula is:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_{n1}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{n2}^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{\omega_{nn}^2}$$

It is called a **lower-bound method** because the reciprocal summation always *reduces* the resultant natural frequency below the true value. This makes it **conservative and safe** for shaft design — the actual critical speed is always slightly higher than Dunkerley's prediction.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Pattern Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Students must attempt the *full* question after the internal choice.

---

#### **Question A — Full 14-Mark Problem (Attempt this OR Question B)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

A light horizontal shaft of length 1.2 m is supported in bearings and carries two rotors. The first rotor of mass 18 kg is located at the centre of the shaft, and the second rotor of mass 12 kg is located at 0.4 m from one of the bearings. The shaft diameter is 30 mm. The modulus of elasticity of shaft material is **E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²**. The bearings can be assumed to act as simple supports. Neglect the mass of the shaft.

**(a)** Determine the static deflections at the location of each rotor due to both loads acting together.

**(b)** Using **Dunkerley's method**, calculate the **lower-bound critical speed** of the shaft in **rpm**.

---

##### **Solution to Question A:**

**Given Data:**
- Shaft length L = 1.2 m
- Mass at centre, m₁ = 18 kg, located at x₁ = 0.6 m from bearing A
- Mass at 0.4 m from bearing A, m₂ = 12 kg, located at x₂ = 0.4 m (so 0.8 m from bearing B)
- Shaft diameter d = 30 mm → Moment of inertia $I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}$
- E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 2 × 10¹¹ N/m²
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Calculate Moment of Inertia of the shaft cross-section:**

$$I = \frac{\pi (0.030)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 8.1 \times 10^{-7}}{64}$$

$$I = 3.976 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Calculate EI (flexural rigidity):**

$$EI = (2 \times 10^{11}) \times (3.976 \times 10^{-8}) = 7952 \text{ N·m}^2$$

---

**Part (a): Static Deflections at Both Rotor Locations**

For a simply-supported beam of length L with a concentrated load P at distance **a** from the left support (so b = L − a from the right support), the deflection at distance **x** from the left support is:

- For $x \le a$:
  $$y(x) = \frac{Pbx}{6LEI}\left(L^2 - b^2 - x^2\right)$$
- For $x \ge a$:
  $$y(x) = \frac{Pa(L-x)}{6LEI}\left(2Lx - a^2 - x^2\right)$$

**Sub-case (i): Load W₁ = m₁g = 18 × 9.81 = 176.58 N at centre (a = b = 0.6 m):**

Deflection at centre (x = 0.6 m) due to W₁ alone:

$$y_{11} = \frac{W_1 L^3}{48 EI} = \frac{176.58 \times (1.2)^3}{48 \times 7952}$$

$$y_{11} = \frac{176.58 \times 1.728}{381696} = \frac{305.13}{381696} = 7.995 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.7995 \text{ mm}$$

Deflection at x = 0.4 m due to W₁ alone (a = 0.6 > x = 0.4, so use first formula with b = 0.6):

$$y_{21} = \frac{W_1 \cdot b \cdot x}{6 L EI}\left(L^2 - b^2 - x^2\right)$$

$$y_{21} = \frac{176.58 \times 0.6 \times 0.4}{6 \times 1.2 \times 7952}\left[(1.2)^2 - (0.6)^2 - (0.4)^2\right]$$

$$y_{21} = \frac{42.379}{57254.4}\left[1.44 - 0.36 - 0.16\right]$$

$$y_{21} = 7.402 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.92 = 6.81 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.681 \text{ mm}$$

**Sub-case (ii): Load W₂ = m₂g = 12 × 9.81 = 117.72 N at x = 0.4 m (a = 0.4, b = 0.8):**

Deflection at x = 0.4 m (point of load itself, x = a) due to W₂ alone:

$$y_{22} = \frac{W_2 a^2 b^2}{3 L EI} = \frac{117.72 \times (0.4)^2 \times (0.8)^2}{3 \times 1.2 \times 7952}$$

$$y_{22} = \frac{117.72 \times 0.16 \times 0.64}{28627.2} = \frac{12.054}{28627.2}$$

$$y_{22} = 4.211 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.4211 \text{ mm}$$

Deflection at centre (x = 0.6 m) due to W₂ (a = 0.4 < x = 0.6, use second formula with a = 0.4):

$$y_{12} = \frac{W_2 \cdot a \cdot (L-x)}{6LEI}\left[2Lx - a^2 - x^2\right]$$

$$y_{12} = \frac{117.72 \times 0.4 \times 0.6}{6 \times 1.2 \times 7952}\left[2(1.2)(0.6) - (0.4)^2 - (0.6)^2\right]$$

$$y_{12} = \frac{28.253}{57254.4}\left[1.44 - 0.16 - 0.36\right]$$

$$y_{12} = 4.934 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.92 = 4.539 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.4539 \text{ mm}$$

**Total static deflections (by superposition):**

$$\boxed{y_1 = y_{11} + y_{12} = 0.7995 + 0.4539 = 1.2534 \text{ mm (at centre)}}$$

$$\boxed{y_2 = y_{21} + y_{22} = 0.681 + 0.4211 = 1.1021 \text{ mm (at 0.4 m from bearing)}}$$

---

**Part (b): Critical Speed by Dunkerley's Method**

**Step 1 — Natural frequency of each mass acting alone:**

For each mass, treat it as a single load on a simply-supported beam:

- Self-deflection of mass 1: $y_{1,alone} = y_{11} = 0.7995 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Self-deflection of mass 2: $y_{2,alone} = y_{22} = 0.4211 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

**Step 2 — Individual natural frequencies:**

$$\omega_{n1}^2 = \frac{g}{y_{11}} = \frac{9.81}{0.7995 \times 10^{-3}} = 12270.2 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\omega_{n2}^2 = \frac{g}{y_{22}} = \frac{9.81}{0.4211 \times 10^{-3}} = 23296.8 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Dunkerley's formula:**

$$\frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_{n1}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{n2}^2}$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \frac{1}{12270.2} + \frac{1}{23296.8} = 8.150 \times 10^{-5} + 4.292 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega_n^2} = 1.2442 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\omega_n^2 = 8037 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = 89.65 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to critical speed in rpm:**

$$\boxed{N_c = \frac{60 \times \omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 89.65}{2\pi} = 856.1 \text{ rpm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in This Type of Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to combine deflections by superposition** (y₁ = y₁₁ + y₁₂) — losing 3 marks.
> 2. **Using the combined deflection y₁ directly in Dunkerley's formula** — this is WRONG; Dunkerley's formula uses *self-deflections* of each mass alone, not combined deflections.
> 3. **Mixing units** (using mm instead of m for δst when computing ωn) — leads to ωn off by a factor of √1000 = 31.6.
> 4. **Forgetting the ×60/2π conversion** when asked for Nc in rpm — loses 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Problem (Internal Choice Option)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]**

A horizontal shaft of length 1.5 m, simply supported at its ends, carries three point loads. The loads and their positions are:

- W₁ = 200 N at 0.4 m from the left support
- W₂ = 300 N at the centre (0.75 m)
- W₃ = 250 N at 1.1 m from the left support

**(a)** Determine the static deflection at each load point.

**(b)** Using **Rayleigh's method**, estimate the **upper-bound critical speed** in rpm.

*Assume EI of the shaft = 6000 N·m² and g = 9.81 m/s².*

---

##### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a): Static Deflections at Each Load Point**

Using the standard simply-supported beam deflection formula for a point load P at distance **a** from the left support (b = L − a), the deflection at the load point itself is:

$$y_{self} = \frac{P a^2 b^2}{3 L EI}$$

The deflection at any *other* point **x** (x ≠ a) due to a load P at **a** is:

- If $x < a$: $y(x) = \dfrac{Pbx}{6LEI}\left(L^2 - b^2 - x^2\right)$
- If $x > a$: $y(x) = \dfrac{Pa(L-x)}{6LEI}\left(2Lx - a^2 - x^2\right)$

**Self-deflections:**

$$y_{11} = \frac{200 \times (0.4)^2 \times (1.1)^2}{3 \times 1.5 \times 6000} = \frac{200 \times 0.16 \times 1.21}{27000} = \frac{38.72}{27000}$$

$$y_{11} = 1.434 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$y_{22} = \frac{300 \times (0.75)^2 \times (0.75)^2}{3 \times 1.5 \times 6000} = \frac{300 \times 0.3164}{27000} = 3.515 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$y_{33} = \frac{250 \times (1.1)^2 \times (0.4)^2}{3 \times 1.5 \times 6000} = \frac{250 \times 0.1936}{27000} = 1.793 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Cross-deflections (at x = 0.4 m):**

Deflection at x = 0.4 due to W₂ (at a = 0.75, x < a, b = 0.75):

$$y_{12} = \frac{300 \times 0.75 \times 0.4}{6 \times 1.5 \times 6000}\left[(1.5)^2 - (0.75)^2 - (0.4)^2\right]$$

$$y_{12} = \frac{90}{54000}\left[2.25 - 0.5625 - 0.16\right] = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5275 = 2.546 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

Deflection at x = 0.4 due to W₃ (at a = 1.1, x < a, b = 0.4):

$$y_{13} = \frac{250 \times 0.4 \times 0.4}{6 \times 1.5 \times 6000}\left[(1.5)^2 - (0.4)^2 - (0.4)^2\right]$$

$$y_{13} = \frac{40}{54000}\left[2.25 - 0.16 - 0.16\right] = 7.407 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.93 = 1.430 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Total deflection at point 1 (x = 0.4 m):**

$$\boxed{y_1 = y_{11} + y_{12} + y_{13} = 1.434 + 2.546 + 1.430 = 5.410 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}$$

By symmetry (since W₁ and W₃ are at mirrored positions, and the loading pattern W₁–W₂–W₃ is roughly symmetric):

$$\boxed{y_3 = 5.410 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m (at x = 1.1 m)}}$$

**Total deflection at point 2 (x = 0.75 m, centre):**

By symmetry of computation, summing self + cross from W₁ and W₃:

$$y_{21} = y_{23} = \frac{P a(L-x)}{6LEI}[2Lx - a^2 - x^2] = \frac{200 \times 0.4 \times 0.75}{54000}[2(1.5)(0.75) - 0.16 - 0.5625]$$

$$y_{21} = 1.111 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5275 = 1.697 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{y_2 = y_{21} + y_{22} + y_{23} = 1.697 + 3.515 + 1.697 = 6.909 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}$$

---

**Part (b): Critical Speed by Rayleigh's Method**

**Rayleigh's formula:**

$$\omega^2 = \frac{g \sum W_i y_i}{\sum W_i y_i^2}$$

**Compute $\sum W_i y_i$:**

$$\sum W_i y_i = (200)(5.410 \times 10^{-3}) + (300)(6.909 \times 10^{-3}) + (250)(5.410 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$= 1.082 + 2.0727 + 1.3525 = 4.5072 \text{ N·m}$$

**Compute $\sum W_i y_i^2$:**

$$\sum W_i y_i^2 = 200(5.410 \times 10^{-3})^2 + 300(6.909 \times 10^{-3})^2 + 250(5.410 \times 10^{-3})^2$$

$$= 200(2.927 \times 10^{-5}) + 300(4.774 \times 10^{-5}) + 250(2.927 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$= 5.854 \times 10^{-3} + 14.321 \times 10^{-3} + 7.318 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 27.493 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m}^2$$

**Compute $\omega^2$:**

$$\omega^2 = \frac{9.81 \times 4.5072}{27.493 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{44.216}{0.027493} = 1608.3 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\omega = 40.10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Critical speed in rpm:**

$$\boxed{N_c = \frac{60 \times 40.10}{2\pi} = 382.9 \text{ rpm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall — Rayleigh's Method:** Many students incorrectly use *self-deflections* (y₁₁, y₂₂, y₃₃) in Rayleigh's formula instead of the *combined static deflections* (y₁, y₂, y₃) due to all loads together. The combined deflections yᵢ account for the actual mode shape — using self-deflections gives an incorrect (and usually too-high) value, losing 4–5 marks. **Always use combined deflections yᵢ in Rayleigh's method.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist for Transverse Vibration (Module 4):**

- **Definition:** Vibration where particles move **perpendicular** to the shaft axis (bending oscillations).
- **Governing Equation:** $m\ddot{y} + ky = 0$ — same form as longitudinal/torsional SDOF vibration.
- **Stiffness Formula:** $k = W / \delta_{st} = mg / \delta_{st}$
- **Natural Frequency (rad/s):** $\omega_n = \sqrt{g / \delta_{st}}$
- **Critical Speed (rpm):** $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{g / \delta_{st}}$ — *must convert* to rpm.
- **Whirling Radius:** $r = \dfrac{e \cdot \omega_n^2}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}$ — diverges at $\omega = \omega_n$.
- **Dunkerley's Equation:** $\dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\omega_{ni}^2}$ — uses *self-deflections* of each mass; gives **lower bound**.
- **Rayleigh's Equation:** $\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{g \sum W_i y_i}{\sum W_i y_i^2}$ — uses *combined deflections*; gives **upper bound**.
- **True Critical Speed** lies between the Dunkerley (lower) and Rayleigh (upper) estimates.
- **Units Discipline:** Always convert δst to **metres** before substituting into g/δst; convert EI to **N·m²**; loads in **Newtons**.
- **Beam Deflection Formulas:** Memorise the simply-supported beam formulas for point loads — KTU gives no formula sheet.
- **g = 9.81 m/s²** unless otherwise specified.
- **Shaft Mass:** Neglected unless Rayleigh's method is being applied (and even then, often shaft mass is given as equivalent point loads).
- **Multiple Loads:** Use **superposition** (algebraic sum of deflections at each point due to each load separately).
- **Critical Speed = Whirling Speed = Natural Frequency** in *physical meaning* — only the units change (rad/s vs rpm).
- **Avoid Operating Near Critical Speed:** Real machines have a "run-through" range (typically ±20% of Nc) that must be traversed quickly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Node
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of Node

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Concept of Node

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Node** is defined as a specific point (or cross-section) on a vibrating mechanical system where the instantaneous displacement, velocity, or amplitude of vibration is **identically zero** during a particular natural mode of free vibration.

In the context of multi-degree-of-freedom (MDOF) systems, transverse vibrations of beams, torsional vibrations of shafts, and longitudinal vibrations of bars, a node represents a **stationary point** — it does **not move** while the rest of the system oscillates harmonically about that point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Node** → Point of **zero amplitude** (no motion).
> - **Antinode (or Loop)** → Point of **maximum amplitude** (largest motion).
> - The distance between two consecutive nodes is called a **loop length** or **half-wavelength**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Tied Rope" Picture

Imagine a long, flexible rope stretched horizontally and tied at both ends. If you pluck it in the middle, it vibrates in a single loop — both ends remain fixed (**nodes**) and the middle swings the most (**antinode**). If you instead hold the rope at the quarter point with one finger (creating an extra node there), the rope can vibrate in **two loops simultaneously**, even though your hand is barely moving.

> [!TIP]
> This finger point is the **node** — physically stationary, yet it dictates the entire vibrational pattern (mode shape) of the system.

In a **two-mass-spring system**, the node is the imaginary (or sometimes physical) point between the two masses whose displacement is zero for one of the two principal modes of vibration.

---

### Mathematical Statement of the Node Condition

For a system vibrating in its $i^{\text{th}}$ natural mode, the displacement field $y(x,t)$ can always be expressed as:

$$
y(x,t) = \phi_i(x) \cdot \sin(\omega_i t + \phi_0)
$$

The **node condition** requires:

$$
\phi_i(x_{\text{node}}) = 0
$$

where:
- $\phi_i(x)$ = mode shape function (spatial amplitude) for the $i^{\text{th}}$ mode.
- $x_{\text{node}}$ = spatial coordinate of the node.
- $\omega_i$ = natural circular frequency of the $i^{\text{th}}$ mode (rad/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> In practical machinery (turbine blades, gear shafts, automobile crankshafts, helicopter rotors), engineers deliberately identify node locations to mount **damping supports, bearings, or vibration absorbers** — because placing any constraint at a node transfers minimal dynamic force to the foundation.

---

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Standard gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Modal orthogonality constant (mode shape normalization factor):** $\int_0^L m(x)\,\phi_i(x)\,\phi_j(x)\,dx = 0 \;\;(i \neq j)$
- **Number of nodes** for the $i^{\text{th}}$ mode of a uniform fixed-fixed shaft = $(i - 1)$.
- **Half-wavelength loop length** $L_i = \dfrac{L}{i}$, where $L$ is the total span length.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Nodes — The Modal Perspective

When a conservative multi-DOF system undergoes **free undamped vibration**, the resulting motion is a **superposition of natural modes**. Each natural mode has:

1. A characteristic **natural frequency** $\omega_i$.
2. A characteristic **mode shape** $\phi_i(x)$.
3. A characteristic **node pattern** — fixed points where the spatial amplitude vanishes.

The complete response of the system is:

$$
\mathbf{y}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i \phi_i \cos(\omega_i t) + B_i \phi_i \sin(\omega_i t)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The coefficients $A_i$ and $B_i$ depend only on the **initial conditions**. The mode shapes $\phi_i$ and frequencies $\omega_i$ depend solely on the **mass and stiffness distribution** of the system.

For the $i^{\text{th}}$ mode, the spatial pattern $\phi_i$ has exactly $(i-1)$ internal nodes (plus the geometric boundary nodes for a fixed-fixed system).

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — How a Node Emerges

1. **Set up the equation of motion** for the MDOF system using Newton's second law or Lagrange's equations.
2. **Assume harmonic motion**: $\mathbf{y} = \boldsymbol{\phi} \, e^{j\omega t}$.
3. **Derive the eigenvalue problem**: $\left[ \mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{M} \right]\boldsymbol{\phi} = \mathbf{0}$.
4. **Solve for non-trivial solutions** by setting the determinant to zero:

$$
\det \left[ \mathbf{K} - \omega^2 \mathbf{M} \right] = 0
$$

5. **Compute eigenvalues** $\omega_i^2$ (squared natural frequencies).
6. **Back-substitute** each $\omega_i$ into the eigenvalue equation to find the corresponding **eigenvector** $\boldsymbol{\phi}_i$ — this is the **mode shape**.
7. **Locate the nodes**: Find positions where every component of $\boldsymbol{\phi}_i$ is zero. These are the **nodes of mode $i$**.

---

## 2.3 Nodes in a Two-Mass–Two-Spring System (Canonical KTU Problem)

Consider two masses $m_1, m_2$ connected by springs $k_1$ (left wall to $m_1$), $k_2$ (between $m_1$ and $m_2$), and $k_3$ ($m_2$ to right wall). The eigenvalue problem yields two natural frequencies $\omega_1$ (lowest, in-phase mode) and $\omega_2$ (highest, out-of-phase mode).

- **Mode 1 (lowest frequency):** Both masses move in the **same direction** with **no internal node** between them.
- **Mode 2 (highest frequency):** The masses move in **opposite directions**; a **single node** exists between them at a specific location.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Node Condition | $\phi_i(x_{\text{node}}) = 0$ | Spatial amplitude vanishes |
| 2 | Natural Frequency (general eigenvalue) | $\det[\mathbf{K} - \omega^2\mathbf{M}] = 0$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Mode Shape Equation | $\left[\mathbf{K} - \omega_i^2\mathbf{M}\right]\boldsymbol{\phi}_i = \mathbf{0}$ | Dimensionless eigenvector |
| 4 | Modal Orthogonality | $\boldsymbol{\phi}_i^T \mathbf{M} \boldsymbol{\phi}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$ | Mass-weighted |
| 5 | Modal Stiffness Orthogonality | $\boldsymbol{\phi}_i^T \mathbf{K} \boldsymbol{\phi}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$ | — |
| 6 | Number of internal nodes (uniform beam/shaft, mode $i$) | $n_i = i - 1$ | Plus geometric nodes at fixed ends |
| 7 | Loop length (uniform shaft, mode $i$, length $L$) | $L_i = L / i$ | metres |
| 8 | Torsional vibration natural freq. | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_t}{I}}$ where $K_t = \dfrac{GJ}{L}$ | $\omega_n$ in rad/s, $I$ = mass moment of inertia in kg·m² |
| 9 | Transverse vibration of uniform beam (fixed–fixed) | $\omega_n = (\beta_n L)^2 \sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$ | $\beta_n L = 4.730, 7.853, 10.996, \ldots$ |
| 10 | Effective mass at node | $\dfrac{1}{M_{\text{eff}}} = \dfrac{\phi_p^2}{\sum_i m_i \phi_i^2}$ | Used for absorber design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition Quick Reference:**
> - **Fixed end:** displacement = slope = 0 ⇒ Always a **node** (and a moment node for slope).
> - **Free end:** shear force = bending moment = 0 ⇒ Always an **antinode**.
> - **Simply supported end:** displacement = moment = 0 ⇒ Always a **node** (displacement only).
> - **Sliding/guided end:** displacement = shear = 0 ⇒ Node for displacement only.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Node Concept |
|---|---|
| 🏭 Turbomachinery | Locating bearing supports at shaft node to minimize force transmission |
| 🚗 Automotive | Engine crankshaft torsional vibration node placement for dampers |
| 🚁 Aerospace | Helicopter blade lag/feather modes — nodes define pylon load points |
| 🏗 Civil | Building floor mode shapes — node placement for seismic isolators |
| ⚙ Machine Tool | Avoiding cutting-tool chatter by operating near a tool-tip node |
| 🎷 Musical Instruments | Violin / guitar plates tuned by adding mass at nodes of dominant modes |

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Rule:** To **reduce vibration transmission** to a structure, mount a flexible support **at a node** of the dominant mode — the support sees minimal dynamic force.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Node Location in a Two-Mass Spring System

### Problem Setup

A system of two masses connected by three springs, as shown in our Mermaid diagram (Section 4). Let:
- $m_1, m_2$ — masses
- $k_1, k_2, k_3$ — spring stiffnesses
- $x_1, x_2$ — displacements from static equilibrium

### Step 1 — Equations of Motion (Newton's 2nd Law)

For mass $m_1$:

$$
m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2)x_1 - k_2 x_2 = 0
$$

For mass $m_2$:

$$
m_2 \ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + (k_2 + k_3)x_2 = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** The spring $k_2$ couples the two equations. Without coupling, there would be no internal node.

### Step 2 — Assume Harmonic Motion

Let $x_1(t) = X_1 \sin(\omega t)$ and $x_2(t) = X_2 \sin(\omega t)$.

Substituting into the equations of motion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[ (k_1 + k_2) - m_1\omega^2 \right] X_1 - k_2 X_2 &= 0 \\
-k_2 X_1 + \left[ (k_2 + k_3) - m_2\omega^2 \right] X_2 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Frequency Equation (Characteristic Equation)

For a non-trivial solution, the determinant of the coefficient matrix must vanish:

$$
\det
\begin{vmatrix}
(k_1 + k_2) - m_1\omega^2 & -k_2 \\
-k_2 & (k_2 + k_3) - m_2\omega^2
\end{vmatrix} = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\left[(k_1 + k_2) - m_1\omega^2\right]\left[(k_2 + k_3) - m_2\omega^2\right] - k_2^2 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** The product of the diagonal terms minus the product of the off-diagonal terms equals zero, which is the standard $2 \times 2$ determinant expansion rule.

### Step 4 — Solve for $\omega^2$

This is a quadratic in $\omega^2$ of the form $a\omega^4 - b\omega^2 + c = 0$. Expanding the determinant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\bigl[(k_1 + k_2)(k_2 + k_3) - k_2^2\bigr] \\
&- \omega^2 \bigl[m_1(k_2 + k_3) + m_2(k_1 + k_2)\bigr] \\
&+ m_1 m_2 \omega^4 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - \bigl[m_1(k_2 + k_3) + m_2(k_1 + k_2)\bigr]\omega^2 + \bigl[(k_1 + k_2)(k_2 + k_3) - k_2^2\bigr] = 0
$$

The two roots $\omega_1^2$ and $\omega_2^2$ are the **squared natural frequencies**.

### Step 5 — Mode Shape Ratio (Amplitude Ratio)

From the first equation:

$$
\frac{X_1}{X_2} = \frac{k_2}{(k_1 + k_2) - m_1\omega^2} = \frac{(k_2 + k_3) - m_2\omega^2}{k_2}
$$

### Step 6 — Node Identification

A node exists **between $m_1$ and $m_2$** when the two masses move in **opposite directions** with the **correct amplitude ratio** so that the intermediate spring $k_2$ has zero net extension change at that cross-section.

The **node location** as a fraction of the distance between the two masses can be shown by linear interpolation:

$$
\frac{a}{L} = \frac{\vert X_1 \vert}{\vert X_1 \vert + \vert X_2 \vert}
$$

where:
- $a$ = distance of node from $m_1$.
- $L$ = total distance between the two masses.
- $X_1, X_2$ are the components of the eigenvector for the chosen mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works:** Because the mode shape is linear between two lumped masses (linear interpolation assumption), the zero-crossing of the mode shape gives the node position.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (For KTU Reference)

> **Given:** $m_1 = m_2 = m$, $k_1 = k_3 = k$, $k_2 = 2k$.
>
> **Find:** Natural frequencies and node location of the second mode.

### Step 1 — Symmetry Simplification

With symmetric ends ($k_1 = k_3$), the system has two distinct symmetric/antisymmetric modes. By symmetry:

$$
\omega_1 = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}, \quad \omega_2 = \sqrt{\frac{5k}{m}}
$$

### Step 2 — Mode Shapes (Verification)

For **Mode 1** ($\omega_1^2 = k/m$):

$$
\frac{X_1}{X_2} = \frac{2k}{(k + 2k) - m(k/m)} = \frac{2k}{2k} = +1
$$

$\Rightarrow$ **Both masses move in phase** — **no internal node**.

For **Mode 2** ($\omega_2^2 = 5k/m$):

$$
\frac{X_1}{X_2} = \frac{2k}{(k + 2k) - m(5k/m)} = \frac{2k}{-2k} = -1
$$

$\Rightarrow$ **Masses move in opposite phase**, with **equal amplitudes** — node lies exactly at the **midpoint** between them.

$$
\frac{a}{L} = \frac{\vert X_1 \vert}{\vert X_1 \vert + \vert X_2 \vert} = \frac{1}{1 + 1} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

So the node lies at $a = L/2$, exactly between $m_1$ and $m_2$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Aspect | Mode 1 (Lowest) | Mode 2 (Highest) |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | $\omega_1$ | $\omega_2$ |
| Phase relation | In-phase | Out-of-phase |
| Internal nodes | **Zero** | **One** |
| Node location | N/A | Between masses (depends on $X_1/X_2$ ratio) |
| Energy character | Rigid-like motion | Internal deformation dominant |
| Typical ratio $\vert X_1/X_2 \vert$ | Less than 1 (mass- and stiffness-dependent) | Greater than 1 |

---

# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Diagram — Two-Mass System with Nodes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WallA[Wall A]:::boundary ---|k1| m1((Mass m1)):::mass
    m1 ---|k2| node[NODE Point]:::node
    node ---|k2 cont.| m2((Mass m2)):::mass
    m2 ---|k3| WallB[Wall B]:::boundary
    mode1[MODE 1 In phase]:::mode1 -.- m1
    mode1 -.- m2
    mode2[MODE 2 Out of phase NODE at center]:::mode2 -.- node

    classDef boundary fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mass fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef mode1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef mode2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagram is a **functional architecture flow** mapping the mechanical interactions rather than a literal physical sketch. In a real engineering drawing, the two masses would be drawn as blocks and the springs as helical coils.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Mode Shape Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Mode1[MODE 1 In-Phase Lowest Frequency]
        direction LR
        A1m1((m1)) -.->|0 amplitude| B1m1((m2))
        A1label[Both masses move together]
    end
    subgraph Mode2[MODE 2 Out-of-Phase Highest Frequency]
        direction LR
        A2m1((m1)) -.->|positive| B2center[NODE]
        B2center -.->|negative| C2m2((m2))
        A2label[Single node between masses]
    end
    Mode1 ~~~ Mode2
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — How a Node Manifests in Multi-DOF Vibration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Free vibration initiated]) --> EOM[Equations of motion written]
    EOM --> Harmonic[Assume harmonic solution]
    Harmonic --> Eigen[Form eigenvalue problem K - omega squared M]
    Eigen --> DetZero[Set determinant equal to zero]
    DetZero --> Freqs[Compute natural frequencies omega 1 and omega 2]
    Freqs --> Vectors[Back substitute to obtain mode shape vectors phi 1 and phi 2]
    Vectors --> NodeCheck{Is phi i component zero at any location?}
    NodeCheck -- Yes --> NodeFound[NODE located at that position]
    NodeCheck -- No --> NoNode[No internal node in this mode]
    NodeFound --> Mount[Damping supports placed at node]
    NoNode --> Mount2[Damping placed at antinode]
    Mount --> End([Vibration isolated])
    Mount2 --> End
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading guide:** Each block is a stage in the analytical workflow from equation formulation to physical implementation.

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Visual: Node vs. Antinode (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1((Fixed)):::fix ~~~ N1[NODE amplitude zero]:::node
    N1 ~~~ A1[ANTINODE amplitude max]:::anti
    A1 ~~~ N2[NODE amplitude zero]:::node
    N2 ~~~ F2((Fixed)):::fix

    classDef fix fill:#cccccc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef anti fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
