# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Define the term "node" as applied to vibrating systems. How does it differ from an "antinode"?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **node** is a point on a vibrating system where the amplitude of vibration is **identically zero** for a given natural mode. It is a stationary point while the rest of the system oscillates about it.

> An **antinode** (or loop/antiloop) is a point where the amplitude of vibration is **maximum** for that same mode.

> For a uniform fixed-fixed beam vibrating in its $i^{\text{th}}$ mode, the number of internal nodes is $(i - 1)$ and these alternate with antinodes along the length of the beam.

> **[3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for antinode contrast, 1 mark for example]**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Mention two practical applications of identifying nodes in vibrating machinery.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Support placement:** Dynamic vibration absorbers and bearings are positioned at **nodes** to minimize the transmission of vibratory force to the foundation.
> 2. **Damping location:** Constrained-layer damping patches are applied at **antinodes** (where strain energy is maximum) for efficient energy dissipation.
> 3. **(Optional third)** Measurement of modal frequencies via accelerometers placed at antinodes gives the highest signal-to-noise ratio.

> **[3 marks: 1.5 marks each for two applications]**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students confuse the **node** (spatial amplitude zero) with the **time of zero crossing** of displacement at a *moving* point. The node is a **fixed spatial point**, not a temporal event.
> - In multi-DOF problems, students often forget to **normalize** or **scale** the mode shape when locating the node. Always present the **ratio** of mode shape components clearly.
> - Avoid writing the determinant expansion without showing the **intermediate substitution** steps. KTU examiners penalize skipping algebraic transitions.
> - Do not confuse "node" with "neutral axis" in bending (a different concept entirely).

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**A system consists of two masses $m_1 = 10$ kg and $m_2 = 20$ kg connected by three springs $k_1 = 2000$ N/m (left wall to $m_1$), $k_2 = 3000$ N/m (between masses), and $k_3 = 1000$ N/m ($m_2$ to right wall).**

**(a) Derive the equations of motion and determine the two natural frequencies of the system. (7 Marks)**

**(b) For each natural mode, sketch the mode shape and identify the location of any internal node(s). (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-A:

##### Part (a) — Equations of Motion and Natural Frequencies (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Equations of Motion**

Using Newton's second law for each mass (measuring displacement from static equilibrium):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2)x_1 - k_2 x_2 &= 0 \\
m_2 \ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + (k_2 + k_3)x_2 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
10\,\ddot{x}_1 + 5000\,x_1 - 3000\,x_2 &= 0 \\
20\,\ddot{x}_2 - 3000\,x_1 + 4000\,x_2 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Harmonic Assumption**

Let $x_1 = X_1 \sin(\omega t)$ and $x_2 = X_2 \sin(\omega t)$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(5000 - 10\omega^2)X_1 - 3000 X_2 &= 0 \quad \text{...(I)} \\
-3000 X_1 + (4000 - 20\omega^2)X_2 &= 0 \quad \text{...(II)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Frequency Equation**

Setting the determinant to zero:

$$
\det
\begin{vmatrix}
5000 - 10\omega^2 & -3000 \\
-3000 & 4000 - 20\omega^2
\end{vmatrix} = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
(5000 - 10\omega^2)(4000 - 20\omega^2) - 9{,}000{,}000 = 0
$$

Let $\lambda = \omega^2$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(5000 - 10\lambda)(4000 - 20\lambda) - 9 \times 10^6 = 0 \\
&20{,}000{,}000 - 100{,}000\lambda - 40{,}000\lambda + 200\lambda^2 - 9{,}000{,}000 = 0 \\
&200\lambda^2 - 140{,}000\lambda + 11{,}000{,}000 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing by 200:

$$
\lambda^2 - 700\lambda + 55{,}000 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Quadratic**

Using the quadratic formula:

$$
\lambda = \frac{700 \pm \sqrt{700^2 - 4 \times 55{,}000}}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{700 \pm \sqrt{490{,}000 - 220{,}000}}{2} = \frac{700 \pm \sqrt{270{,}000}}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{700 \pm 519.6}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\lambda_1 = \frac{700 - 519.6}{2} = 90.2, \qquad \lambda_2 = \frac{700 + 519.6}{2} = 609.8
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\omega_1 = \sqrt{90.2} \approx 9.50 \text{ rad/s}, \qquad \omega_2 = \sqrt{609.8} \approx 24.69 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

> **[Marks allocation: 1 mark EOM, 1 mark harmonic substitution, 2 marks determinant, 2 marks solving quadratic, 1 mark numerical result]**

---

##### Part (b) — Mode Shapes and Node Identification (7 Marks)

**Step 5 — Mode Shape Ratios**

From equation (I):

$$
\frac{X_1}{X_2} = \frac{3000}{5000 - 10\omega^2}
$$

**Mode 1** ($\omega_1^2 = 90.2$):

$$
\frac{X_1}{X_2} = \frac{3000}{5000 - 902} = \frac{3000}{4098} \approx +0.732
$$

$\Rightarrow$ Both masses move in the **same direction**; **ratio is positive** → **No internal node**.

**Mode 2** ($\omega_2^2 = 609.8$):

$$
\frac{X_1}{X_2} = \frac{3000}{5000 - 6098} = \frac{3000}{-1098} \approx -2.732
$$

$\Rightarrow$ Masses move in **opposite directions**; **ratio is negative** → **One internal node exists**.

**Step 6 — Node Location (Mode 2)**

Using linear interpolation between the two masses, the node distance from $m_1$ is:

$$
\frac{a}{L} = \frac{\vert X_1 \vert}{\vert X_1 \vert + \vert X_2 \vert} = \frac{2.732}{2.732 + 1} = \frac{2.732}{3.732} \approx 0.732
$$

So the node is located at approximately **73.2% of the distance from $m_1$ towards $m_2$** (closer to $m_1$, as expected since $m_1$ has the smaller amplitude in this antisymmetric mode).

**Step 7 — Mode Shape Sketch Description**

- **Mode 1:** Both masses displaced to the right (say); amplitudes 0.732 and 1.0 (in same direction). Shape is a slanted line with **no zero-crossing** between the masses.
- **Mode 2:** $m_1$ displaced +2.732 units (say to the right); $m_2$ displaced −1.0 unit (to the left). The **zero crossing** is the node.

> **[Marks allocation: 2 marks mode 1 ratio, 2 marks mode 2 ratio, 2 marks node location formula and calculation, 1 mark sketch description]**

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]`

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the concept of a node in a vibrating cantilever beam fixed at one end and free at the other. State how many nodes appear in the first three modes. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A uniform shaft of length $L = 2$ m is fixed at both ends and undergoes torsional vibration. The first natural frequency is $f_1 = 25$ Hz. Determine the position(s) of the internal node(s) for the second and third modes of vibration. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-B:

##### Part (a) — Nodes in a Cantilever Beam (7 Marks)

**Explanation:**

A **cantilever beam** is rigidly clamped at one end ($x = 0$) and completely free at the other ($x = L$).

- The **fixed end** is always a **node** for displacement (because the wall enforces zero motion) and for slope (because the wall enforces zero rotation).
- The **free end** is always an **antinode** (because there is no constraint on displacement or rotation there).

For free transverse vibration, the natural frequencies are determined from the transcendental equation:

$$
\cos(\beta L) \cosh(\beta L) = -1
$$

The first three roots give:

| Mode $i$ | $\beta_i L$ | Nodes (counting only internal zero-displacement points) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.875 | 0 internal nodes |
| 2 | 4.694 | 1 internal node |
| 3 | 7.855 | 2 internal nodes |

> [!NOTE]
> **General rule:** A cantilever has $(i - 1)$ internal nodes plus the fixed end as a geometric node.

> **[Marks allocation: 2 marks diagram, 2 marks concept explanation, 2 marks count of nodes per mode, 1 mark formula mention]**

---

##### Part (b) — Torsional Vibration Node Locations (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Natural Frequencies of a Uniform Fixed-Fixed Shaft**

For torsional vibration of a uniform shaft:

$$
\omega_n = n\pi \sqrt{\frac{GJ}{\rho J_p L^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{L} \quad \text{(commonly written as } \omega_n = n\omega_1\text{)}
$$

In terms of frequency:

$$
f_n = n \cdot f_1
$$

Given $f_1 = 25$ Hz:

$$
f_2 = 50 \text{ Hz}, \qquad f_3 = 75 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Mode Shape Function**

For a uniform fixed-fixed shaft, the mode shape is sinusoidal:

$$
\phi_n(x) = \sin\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Node Condition**

A node requires $\phi_n(x) = 0$:

$$
\sin\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{n\pi x}{L} = k\pi \quad \text{for integer } k
$$

Excluding the boundary nodes ($x = 0$ and $x = L$):

$$
x_k = \frac{kL}{n}, \qquad k = 1, 2, \ldots, (n - 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply to Modes 2 and 3**

For **Mode 2** ($n = 2$):

$$
x_1 = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0 \text{ m}
$$

$\Rightarrow$ **One internal node at the midspan** ($x = 1$ m).

For **Mode 3** ($n = 3$):

$$
x_1 = \frac{L}{3} \approx 0.667 \text{ m}, \qquad x_2 = \frac{2L}{3} \approx 1.333 \text{ m}
$$

$\Rightarrow$ **Two internal nodes** at one-third and two-thirds of the shaft length.

> **[Marks allocation: 2 marks mode shape formula, 1 mark node condition, 2 marks solving for $x$, 2 marks numerical answers]**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme — Concept of Node**

- ✅ **Node Definition:** A point of **zero spatial amplitude** in a particular natural mode of vibration — *fixed in space*, not in time.
- ✅ **Antinode:** Point of **maximum amplitude** — always located midway between two consecutive nodes.
- ✅ **Fixed end = Node; Free end = Antinode** (boundary condition quick rule).
- ✅ **Number of internal nodes for mode $i$** in a uniform fixed-fixed beam/shaft = $(i - 1)$.
- ✅ **Mode shape condition:** $\phi_i(x_{\text{node}}) = 0$.
- ✅ **Node location** (2-DOF spring-mass): $a/L = \vert X_1 \vert / (\vert X_1 \vert + \vert X_2 \vert)$.
- ✅ **Modal orthogonality** of distinct modes is the foundation that guarantees distinct node patterns for each mode.
- ✅ **Engineering use:** Place **supports and absorbers at nodes** to **minimize force transmission** to the foundation.
- ✅ **In-phase mode = no internal node**; **out-of-phase mode = at least one internal node**.
- ✅ **Shaft mode shape** (fixed–fixed): $\phi_n(x) = \sin(n\pi x / L)$; **torsional vibration nodes** follow this sinusoidal pattern.
- ✅ Always show the **determinant expansion**, the **harmonic substitution**, and the **mode-shape ratio** explicitly in KTU answers — never skip steps.
- ✅ Convert angular frequency $\omega$ (rad/s) to cyclic frequency $f$ (Hz) using $f = \omega / (2\pi)$.
- ✅ Memorize the **standard transcendental roots** for cantilever beam: $\beta_1 L = 1.875$, $\beta_2 L = 4.694$, $\beta_3 L = 7.855$.
- ✅ Beware: **node ≠ neutral axis ≠ zero-crossing at a single instant** — these are commonly confused concepts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Multi-Degree-of-Freedom (MDOF) vibrating systems, a **Mode of Vibration** is defined as a *characteristic natural pattern* in which a system vibrates freely at one of its discrete natural frequencies. Mathematically, it corresponds to an **eigenvector** of the generalized eigenvalue problem:

$$[K]\{X\} = \omega^2[M]\{X\}$$

where every coordinate of the system oscillates **sinusoidally** with the **same natural frequency** $\omega_n$ and passes through its equilibrium position simultaneously, maintaining a fixed **amplitude ratio** (the mode shape).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard)**
> - **Mode Shape ($\{X\}$):** The geometric configuration (deflected shape) of the system when vibrating freely in a particular natural mode. It is a *spatial* property — independent of time.
> - **Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$ or $f_n$):** The frequency at which the system *prefers* to oscillate. It is a *temporal* property. Units: **rad/s** (for $\omega$) or **Hz** (for $f$).
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The minimum number of independent coordinates required to completely define the configuration of a vibrating system. An n-DOF system has exactly **n modes** of vibration and **n natural frequencies**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wine Glass Choir"

Imagine you have **three identical wine glasses** on a table, coupled loosely by small rubber bands (representing a 3-DOF spring-mass system). If you tap the first glass very gently, it will oscillate back and forth, and through the rubber bands, drag the others into motion. After some transient beating, the system settles into a pattern where:
- All three glasses sway back and forth
- All of them reach their maximum displacement at the **same instant**
- All of them return to the centre at the **same instant**

The *pattern* (Glass 1 moves with amplitude A, Glass 2 with amplitude B, Glass 3 with amplitude C) is the **mode shape**, and the *speed* of swaying is the **natural frequency**. The system has 3 such "songs" it can sing — these are the 3 **modes**. Any general motion of the system is simply a *superposition* (sum) of these three basic songs.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Damping Convention:** Pure mode analysis assumes **undamped** systems. In damped systems, *complex modes* arise, but KTU Module 4 focuses on the **undamped eigenvalue problem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Degrees of Freedom (n):** For a discrete lumped-parameter system consisting of *n* masses moving in a single direction, there are exactly *n* modes of vibration. For a continuous system (beam, shaft), the number of modes is *theoretically infinite*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Modal Analysis

To find the modes of an n-DOF system, follow this **4-step logical flow**:

1. **Step 1 — Set Up the Equations of Motion (EOM):**
   Using Newton's second law or Lagrange's equations, derive the matrix form:
   $$[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$$
   where $[M]$ is the **mass matrix** (always symmetric, positive definite) and $[K]$ is the **stiffness matrix** (always symmetric, positive semi-definite for a stable system).

2. **Step 2 — Assume a Harmonic Solution:**
   Since the system is undamped, assume each mass executes Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM):
   $$\{x(t)\} = \{X\}\cos(\omega t - \phi)$$
   Substituting and differentiating twice ($\ddot{x} = -\omega^2 X \cos(\omega t - \phi)$) yields the **Generalized Eigenvalue Problem**:
   $$[K]\{X\} = \omega^2[M]\{X\}$$

3. **Step 3 — Apply the Non-Triviality Condition:**
   A harmonic solution is physically meaningful only if $\{X\} \neq \{0\}$. This requires the determinant of the coefficient matrix to vanish:
   $$\det\bigl([K] - \omega^2[M]\bigr) = 0$$
   Expanding the determinant gives a **polynomial of degree n in $\omega^2$**, called the **frequency equation** or **characteristic equation**. Its *n positive real roots* are $\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \ldots, \omega_n^2$ — the **squared natural frequencies**.

4. **Step 4 — Extract the Mode Shapes:**
   Substitute each $\omega_i^2$ back into the eigenvalue problem and solve for $\{X_i\}$. Because the determinant is zero, only the **ratios** $X_1 : X_2 : \ldots : X_n$ are determined. One element is normalized (commonly set to 1) to obtain the **mode shape** $\{X_i\}$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** |
|--------------|-------------|-----------|----------------------|
| General EOM (undamped) | $[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$ | N, kg, m | Newton's law in matrix form |
| Eigenvalue problem | $([K] - \omega^2[M])\{X\} = \{0\}$ | — | Steady-state harmonic balance |
| Characteristic equation | $\det([K] - \omega^2[M]) = 0$ | — | Yields n values of $\omega_n^2$ |
| Natural frequency (single mode) | $\omega_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i}$ | rad/s | Angular natural frequency |
| Natural frequency in Hz | $f_i = \omega_i / (2\pi)$ | Hz | Cyclic natural frequency |
| Mass orthogonality | $\{X_i\}^T[M]\{X_j\} = 0$ for $i \neq j$ | kg | Modes are M-orthogonal |
| Stiffness orthogonality | $\{X_i\}^T[K]\{X_j\} = 0$ for $i \neq j$ | N/m | Modes are K-orthogonal |
| Rayleigh's quotient | $\omega^2 = \dfrac{\{X\}^T[K]\{X\}}{\{X\}^T[M]\{X\}}$ | (rad/s)² | Useful for approximate modes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Convention:** All natural frequencies must be **positive real** for a stable, undamped, conservative system. Negative or complex values indicate **instability** — a common 3-mark question in KTU.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of Mode Analysis

- **Rotor Dynamics:** Whirling of shafts is essentially the analysis of the *first flexural mode* of a continuous shaft on multiple bearings.
- **Structural Engineering:** Mode shapes determine *where to place dampers* (anti-node) and *where not to place sensors* (nodes of modes you want to measure).
- **Automotive NVH:** Car body panels are designed to keep their mode frequencies away from engine firing frequencies to avoid resonance.
- **Aerospace:** Flutter analysis requires the lowest 5–10 modes of an aircraft wing to predict the aeroelastic instability boundary.
- **Earthquake Engineering:** Mode superposition method reduces a 100,000-DOF building model into 20–30 dominant modes for time-history analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation — Two-Degree-of-Freedom Spring-Mass System

**System Description:**
Consider two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ connected in series by three springs (left wall — $k_1$ — $m_1$ — $k_2$ — $m_2$ — $k_3$ — right wall). The masses are constrained to move only in the horizontal direction. Let $x_1$ and $x_2$ be the displacements of $m_1$ and $m_2$ from their static equilibrium positions.

**Step 1: Free-Body Diagrams and EOMs**

For mass $m_1$, the springs $k_1$ and $k_2$ act on it. The spring $k_1$ pulls it back with force $k_1 x_1$, and spring $k_2$ pulls it with force $k_2(x_1 - x_2)$.

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 = -k_1 x_1 - k_2(x_1 - x_2)$$
$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2)x_1 - k_2 x_2 = 0$$

For mass $m_2$, springs $k_2$ and $k_3$ act on it. The spring $k_2$ pulls it with force $k_2(x_2 - x_1)$, and spring $k_3$ pulls it back with force $k_3 x_2$.

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 = -k_2(x_2 - x_1) - k_3 x_2$$
$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + (k_2 + k_3)x_2 = 0$$

**Step 2: Matrix Formulation**

These two coupled linear ODEs are rewritten in matrix form $[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 + k_3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Assume Harmonic Solution**

Assume $x_1(t) = X_1 \cos(\omega t)$ and $x_2(t) = X_2 \cos(\omega t)$. Then $\ddot{x}_1 = -\omega^2 X_1 \cos(\omega t)$ and $\ddot{x}_2 = -\omega^2 X_2 \cos(\omega t)$. Substituting:

$$\begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 - m_1\omega^2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 + k_3 - m_2\omega^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} X_1 \\ X_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Non-Triviality Condition (Characteristic Equation)**

For a non-zero solution, the determinant of the coefficient matrix must be zero:

$$(k_1 + k_2 - m_1\omega^2)(k_2 + k_3 - m_2\omega^2) - k_2^2 = 0$$

Expanding this product step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
&(k_1 + k_2)(k_2 + k_3) - (k_1 + k_2)m_2\omega^2 - (k_2 + k_3)m_1\omega^2 + m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - k_2^2 = 0 \\
&k_1 k_2 + k_1 k_3 + k_2^2 + k_2 k_3 - k_2^2 - m_2(k_1 + k_2)\omega^2 - m_1(k_2 + k_3)\omega^2 + m_1 m_2 \omega^4 = 0 \\
&k_1 k_2 + k_1 k_3 + k_2 k_3 - [m_2 k_1 + m_2 k_2 + m_1 k_2 + m_1 k_3]\omega^2 + m_1 m_2 \omega^4 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Grouping by powers of $\omega^2$:

$$m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - [m_1(k_2 + k_3) + m_2(k_1 + k_2)]\omega^2 + (k_1 k_2 + k_1 k_3 + k_2 k_3) = 0$$

This is a **quadratic equation in $\omega^2$**. Let $\lambda = \omega^2$. Then:

$$m_1 m_2 \lambda^2 - [m_1(k_2 + k_3) + m_2(k_1 + k_2)]\lambda + (k_1 k_2 + k_1 k_3 + k_2 k_3) = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula $\lambda = \dfrac{-B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - 4AC}}{2A}$ with $A = m_1 m_2$, $B = -[m_1(k_2 + k_3) + m_2(k_1 + k_2)]$, $C = (k_1 k_2 + k_1 k_3 + k_2 k_3)$:

$$\omega^2 = \frac{[m_1(k_2 + k_3) + m_2(k_1 + k_2)] \pm \sqrt{[m_1(k_2 + k_3) + m_2(k_1 + k_2)]^2 - 4 m_1 m_2 (k_1 k_2 + k_1 k_3 + k_2 k_3)}}{2 m_1 m_2}$$

The two roots $\omega_1^2$ and $\omega_2^2$ are the **two natural frequencies**, with $\omega_1 < \omega_2$ (i.e., $\omega_1$ is the *fundamental* and $\omega_2$ is the *second* mode).

**Step 5: Mode Shape Extraction**

To find the mode shape corresponding to $\omega_1$, substitute $\omega = \omega_1$ back into the first row of the matrix equation:

$$(k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega_1^2) X_1 - k_2 X_2 = 0$$

Solving for the ratio:

$$\frac{X_1}{X_2} = \frac{k_2}{k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega_1^2} = r_1$$

The **first mode shape** is $\{X_1\}^T = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 & r_1 \end{Bmatrix}$ (with $X_1$ normalized to 1). For the **second mode**, substitute $\omega_2$:

$$\frac{X_1}{X_2} = \frac{k_2}{k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega_2^2} = r_2$$

The **second mode shape** is $\{X_2\}^T = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 & r_2 \end{Bmatrix}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** In the **first (fundamental) mode**, both masses move *in the same direction* (ratio is positive). In the **second mode**, they move *in opposite directions* (ratio is negative), and there exists a **node** — a point between the masses that does not move.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example

**Given:** $m_1 = m_2 = m = 1$ kg, $k_1 = k_2 = k_3 = k = 1000$ N/m. Find the natural frequencies and mode shapes.

**Step 1: Set up the matrices.**

$$[M] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [K] = \begin{bmatrix} 2000 & -1000 \\ -1000 & 2000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Characteristic equation.**

$$\det\begin{bmatrix} 2000 - \omega^2 & -1000 \\ -1000 & 2000 - \omega^2 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

$$(2000 - \omega^2)^2 - 1000^2 = 0$$
$$(2000 - \omega^2 - 1000)(2000 - \omega^2 + 1000) = 0$$
$$(1000 - \omega^2)(3000 - \omega^2) = 0$$

**Step 3: Natural frequencies.**

$$\omega_1^2 = 1000 \implies \omega_1 = 31.62 \text{ rad/s}, \quad f_1 = 5.033 \text{ Hz}$$
$$\omega_2^2 = 3000 \implies \omega_2 = 54.77 \text{ rad/s}, \quad f_2 = 8.717 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4: First mode shape (using $\omega_1^2 = 1000$).**

$$(2000 - 1000)X_1 - 1000 X_2 = 0 \implies 1000 X_1 = 1000 X_2 \implies X_1 = X_2$$
$$\{X_1\}^T = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{Bmatrix} \quad \text{(both masses move together — in-phase)}$$

**Step 5: Second mode shape (using $\omega_2^2 = 3000$).**

$$(2000 - 3000)X_1 - 1000 X_2 = 0 \implies -1000 X_1 = 1000 X_2 \implies X_1 = -X_2$$
$$\{X_2\}^T = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 & -1 \end{Bmatrix} \quad \text{(masses move in opposite directions — out-of-phase)}$$

### 3.3 Orthogonality of Mode Shapes (Verification)

Using the numerical example above, verify the **M-orthogonality** condition $\{X_1\}^T[M]\{X_2\} = 0$:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{Bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix} = (1)(1) + (1)(-1) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Verify the **K-orthogonality** condition $\{X_1\}^T[K]\{X_2\} = 0$:

$$\begin{Bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{Bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2000 & -1000 \\ -1000 & 2000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix} = (1)(1000) + (1)(-3000) = -2000$$

This is **not zero** — wait, recomputing:

$$[K]\{X_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 2000 & -1000 \\ -1000 & 2000 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 2000 + 1000 \\ -1000 - 2000 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 3000 \\ -3000 \end{Bmatrix}$$

$$\{X_1\}^T[K]\{X_2\} = (1)(3000) + (1)(-3000) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The orthogonality property is now confirmed. This is a key *shortcut* used in KTU problems to avoid computing stiffness-matrix inverses.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Modal Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define MDOF System] --> B[Identify n independent coordinates]
    B --> C[Apply Newton / Lagrange to derive EOMs]
    C --> D[Form Mass Matrix M and Stiffness Matrix K]
    D --> E[Write Matrix EOM: M x_ddot + K x = 0]
    E --> F[Assume Harmonic Solution x = X cos omega t]
    F --> G[Reduce to Eigenvalue Problem: K X = omega squared M X]
    G --> H[Set det of K minus omega squared M = 0]
    H --> I[Expand Determinant to get Characteristic Polynomial of degree n]
    I --> J[Solve Polynomial for n values of omega squared]
    J --> K{For each omega_i}
    K --> L[Substitute omega_i back into K minus omega_i squared M times X_i = 0]
    L --> M[Solve for Amplitude Ratios]
    M --> N[Normalize X_i to get Mode Shape]
    N --> O{More omega values?}
    O --> K
    O --> P[Collect all omega_i and X_i as Mode Set]
    P --> Q[Verify Orthogonality Conditions]
    Q --> R[End: Modes Fully Characterized]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Two-DOF Spring-Mass System Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    wallL[Fixed Wall Left]:::wall -->|k1| m1Node((m1))
    m1Node -->|k2| m2Node((m2))
    m2Node -->|k3| wallR[Fixed Wall Right]:::wall
    m1Node -.->|x1 direction| m1Node
    m2Node -.->|x2 direction| m2Node
    classDef wall fill:#333,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Interpretation of Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FirstMode["First Mode: In-Phase"]
        direction LR
        A1((m1: +A)):::mass -- same direction --> B1((m2: +A)):::mass
    end
    subgraph SecondMode["Second Mode: Out-of-Phase"]
        direction LR
        A2((m1: +A)):::mass -- opposite direction --> B2((m2: -A)):::mass
    end
    subgraph NodePoint["Node Location in Second Mode"]
        N[Stationary Point between masses]:::node
    end
    classDef mass fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram traces the algorithmic workflow. The second shows the *physical topology* of a 2-DOF system. The third contrasts the *qualitative behaviour* of the two modes — in-phase vs. out-of-phase oscillation. The red node in the second mode is the **stationary point**, which is the most important engineering feature for sensor placement in MDOF systems.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 4]**
Define the terms (i) **mode of vibration**, and (ii) **mode shape** of a multi-degree-of-freedom vibrating system.

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Mode of vibration:** A mode of vibration is a *characteristic pattern of free vibration* in which all the coordinates of an MDOF system execute simple harmonic motion *with the same frequency* and *in phase* (or with a fixed phase difference of 0 or $\pi$). An n-DOF system has exactly n such independent modes, each occurring at one of its n natural frequencies.

(ii) **Mode shape:** The mode shape is the *geometric configuration* of the system (the set of relative displacement amplitudes of the masses) when it vibrates freely in a particular natural mode. It is described by the eigenvector $\{X_i\}$ corresponding to the natural frequency $\omega_i$. The mode shape is a *spatial* property and is independent of time, although the *amplitudes* of the masses within the mode shape oscillate sinusoidally in time.

**Marks Distribution:** (1 mark) precise definition; (1 mark) relationship to natural frequency; (1 mark) distinguishing temporal vs. spatial nature.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 4]**
State and explain the **orthogonality principle** of mode shapes in an undamped vibrating system.

**Model Answer:**

The orthogonality principle states that the mode shapes of an undamped MDOF system are mutually orthogonal with respect to both the mass matrix $[M]$ and the stiffness matrix $[K]$. Mathematically, for two distinct modes $i$ and $j$ ($i \neq j$):

$$\{X_i\}^T[M]\{X_j\} = 0 \quad \text{(Mass orthogonality)}$$
$$\{X_i\}^T[K]\{X_j\} = 0 \quad \text{(Stiffness orthogonality)}$$

**Physical significance:** Orthogonality implies that the *modal coordinates* are *decoupled* — each mode can be analyzed as an independent Single-Degree-of-Freedom (SDOF) system. This dramatically simplifies the response calculation for an n-DOF system, reducing n coupled ODEs into n *uncoupled* ODEs, each governed by its own natural frequency $\omega_i$.

**Marks Distribution:** (1 mark) statement; (1 mark) both equations; (1 mark) physical significance (decoupling).

---

### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Derive the general matrix equation of motion for a 2-DOF undamped spring-mass system. From this, develop the **frequency equation** and explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the system shown (two masses $m_1 = 4$ kg, $m_2 = 2$ kg, with springs $k_1 = 2000$ N/m, $k_2 = 1000$ N/m, $k_3 = 3000$ N/m in series, with $m_1$ on the left), determine the **two natural frequencies** and the **two mode shapes**. Verify the orthogonality of the mode shapes using the mass matrix. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Free-body analysis (2 marks).**
Apply Newton's second law to each mass in the series configuration:

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2)x_1 - k_2 x_2 = 0$$
$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + (k_2 + k_3)x_2 = 0$$

**Step 2: Matrix formulation (1 mark).**

$$\begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 + k_3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

i.e., $[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$.

**Step 3: Harmonic solution and eigenvalue problem (2 marks).**
Assume $x_i(t) = X_i \cos \omega t$. Substitute to get $([K] - \omega^2[M])\{X\} = \{0\}$.

**Step 4: Frequency equation (2 marks).**
Setting the determinant to zero:
$$\det\begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_2 - m_1\omega^2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 + k_3 - m_2\omega^2 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$
$$(k_1 + k_2 - m_1\omega^2)(k_2 + k_3 - m_2\omega^2) - k_2^2 = 0$$
This is the **frequency equation** (or characteristic equation). Its two positive real roots are $\omega_1^2$ and $\omega_2^2$, the **squared natural frequencies**. The physical significance is that *only at these specific frequencies* can the system vibrate freely in a sustained harmonic manner; otherwise, the response is transient.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Substitute values (1 mark).**
$m_1 = 4$ kg, $m_2 = 2$ kg, $k_1 = 2000$ N/m, $k_2 = 1000$ N/m, $k_3 = 3000$ N/m.

$$[M] = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [K] = \begin{bmatrix} 3000 & -1000 \\ -1000 & 4000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Characteristic equation (1 mark).**

$$\det\begin{bmatrix} 3000 - 4\omega^2 & -1000 \\ -1000 & 4000 - 2\omega^2 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$
$$(3000 - 4\omega^2)(4000 - 2\omega^2) - 10^6 = 0$$
$$12 \times 10^6 - 6000\omega^2 - 16000\omega^2 + 8\omega^4 - 10^6 = 0$$
$$8\omega^4 - 22000\omega^2 + 11 \times 10^6 = 0$$

Dividing by 8: $\omega^4 - 2750\omega^2 + 1.375 \times 10^6 = 0$.

**Step 3: Solve the quadratic in $\omega^2$ (1 mark).**

$$\omega^2 = \frac{2750 \pm \sqrt{2750^2 - 4(1.375 \times 10^6)}}{2} = \frac{2750 \pm \sqrt{7.5625 \times 10^6 - 5.5 \times 10^6}}{2}$$
$$\omega^2 = \frac{2750 \pm \sqrt{2.0625 \times 10^6}}{2} = \frac{2750 \pm 1436.14}{2}$$
$$\omega_1^2 = 657.93, \quad \omega_2^2 = 2093.07$$
$$\omega_1 = 25.65 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_2 = 45.75 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Mode shape 1 (using $\omega_1^2 = 657.93$) (1 mark).**

From the first row: $(3000 - 4 \times 657.93)X_1 - 1000 X_2 = 0$
$$(3000 - 2631.7)X_1 = 1000 X_2 \implies 368.3 X_1 = 1000 X_2 \implies \frac{X_1}{X_2} = 2.715$$
$$\{X_1\}^T = \begin{Bmatrix} 2.715 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} \quad \text{(both masses move in the same direction)}$$

**Step 5: Mode shape 2 (using $\omega_2^2 = 2093.07$) (1 mark).**

From the first row: $(3000 - 4 \times 2093.07)X_1 - 1000 X_2 = 0$
$$(3000 - 8372.3)X_1 = 1000 X_2 \implies -5372.3 X_1 = 1000 X_2 \implies \frac{X_1}{X_2} = -0.1862$$
$$\{X_2\}^T = \begin{Bmatrix} -0.1862 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} \quad \text{(masses move in opposite directions)}$$

**Step 6: Verify orthogonality (2 marks).**

$$\{X_1\}^T[M]\{X_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.715 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} -0.1862 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$
$$= \begin{bmatrix} 2.715 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} -0.7448 \\ 2 \end{Bmatrix} = 2.715 \times (-0.7448) + 1 \times 2 = -2.022 + 2 \approx 0 \quad \checkmark$$

(Minor numerical error due to rounding is acceptable and acceptable per KTU valuation.)

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 4]**

> **(a)** For a 2-DOF undamped spring-mass system, derive the expressions for the **natural frequencies** and **mode shapes** in terms of the system parameters $m_1, m_2, k_1, k_2, k_3$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A torsional system has two rotors with moments of inertia $I_1 = 10$ kg·m² and $I_2 = 5$ kg·m², connected by a shaft of torsional stiffness $k_t = 1 \times 10^5$ N·m/rad between them, with $I_1$ fixed to a wall via a shaft of stiffness $k_{t1} = 0.5 \times 10^5$ N·m/rad. Compute the natural frequencies and mode shapes. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Reuse the 2-DOF derivation from Question A(a) up to the characteristic equation:

$$m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - [m_1(k_2 + k_3) + m_2(k_1 + k_2)]\omega^2 + (k_1 k_2 + k_1 k_3 + k_2 k_3) = 0$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the quadratic formula:

$$\omega^2 = \frac{[m_1(k_2+k_3) + m_2(k_1+k_2)] \pm \sqrt{[m_1(k_2+k_3) + m_2(k_1+k_2)]^2 - 4 m_1 m_2 (k_1 k_2 + k_1 k_3 + k_2 k_3)}}{2 m_1 m_2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The two roots give $\omega_1 < \omega_2$. For each $\omega_i$, the mode shape ratio is:

$$r_i = \frac{X_{1i}}{X_{2i}} = \frac{k_2}{k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega_i^2}$$

The two mode shapes are $\{X_1\} = \{1, r_1\}^T$ and $\{X_2\} = \{1, r_2\}^T$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Orthogonality conditions hold:

$$\{X_i\}^T[M]\{X_j\} = 0, \quad \{X_i\}^T[K]\{X_j\} = 0 \quad \text{for } i \neq j$$

These orthogonality properties are direct consequences of the symmetry and positive-definiteness of $[M]$ and $[K]$.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Set up the torsional analog (1 mark).**
The torsional system is the rotational analog of the translational system. Replace $m \to I$, $k \to k_t$, and $x \to \theta$. Here, since $I_2$ has no support to ground (free end), $k_3 = 0$. The stiffness matrix becomes:

$$[I] = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [K_t] = \begin{bmatrix} k_{t1} + k_t & -k_t \\ -k_t & k_t \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \times 10^5 & -10^5 \\ -10^5 & 10^5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Characteristic equation (1 mark).**

$$\det\begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \times 10^5 - 10\omega^2 & -10^5 \\ -10^5 & 10^5 - 5\omega^2 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$
$$(1.5 \times 10^5 - 10\omega^2)(10^5 - 5\omega^2) - 10^{10} = 0$$
$$1.5 \times 10^{10} - 7.5 \times 10^5 \omega^2 - 10^6 \omega^2 + 50\omega^4 - 10^{10} = 0$$
$$50\omega^4 - 1.75 \times 10^6 \omega^2 + 0.5 \times 10^{10} = 0$$

Dividing by 50: $\omega^4 - 3.5 \times 10^4 \omega^2 + 10^8 = 0$.

**Step 3: Solve for $\omega$ (1 mark).**

$$\omega^2 = \frac{3.5 \times 10^4 \pm \sqrt{1.225 \times 10^9 - 4 \times 10^8}}{2} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^4 \pm \sqrt{0.225 \times 10^9}}{2}$$
$$\omega^2 = \frac{3.5 \times 10^4 \pm 15000}{2} = 25000 \text{ or } 10000$$
$$\omega_1 = 100 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_2 = 158.11 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Mode shape 1 (1 mark).**
For $\omega_1^2 = 10000$: $(1.5 \times 10^5 - 10^5)\Theta_1 = 10^5 \Theta_2 \implies 0.5 \Theta_1 = \Theta_2$.
$\{\Theta_1\}^T = \{2, 1\}^T$.

**Step 5: Mode shape 2 (1 mark).**
For $\omega_2^2 = 25000$: $(1.5 \times 10^5 - 2.5 \times 10^5)\Theta_1 = 10^5 \Theta_2 \implies -\Theta_1 = \Theta_2$.
$\{\Theta_2\}^T = \{-1, 1\}^T$.

**Step 6: Physical interpretation (2 marks).**
- Mode 1 (100 rad/s): Both rotors twist **in the same direction**. The shaft between them is *barely twisted*.
- Mode 2 (158.11 rad/s): The rotors twist in **opposite directions**. A **node** exists in the connecting shaft — useful for placement of anti-vibration mounts.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (KTU Board Pattern)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Normalization:** The eigenvector from $([K] - \omega^2[M])\{X\} = 0$ is only defined *up to a multiplicative constant*. The KTU board expects you to **normalize** it (usually by setting the first element to 1 or making the largest element 1). Failing to do so loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong Sign in the Off-Diagonal:** The stiffness matrix off-diagonal is **always negative** for a direct-coupling system: $K_{12} = K_{21} = -k_2$. Writing $+k_2$ here is the #1 KTU error in this topic and leads to **all subsequent computations being wrong**.
> 3. **Reporting Natural Frequency in Hz Without Unit Conversion:** $\omega$ is in rad/s; $f$ is in Hz. If the question asks for Hz, divide $\omega$ by $2\pi$. Mark loss of 1 for unit error.
> 4. **Confusing Mode Shape with Mode:** A *mode* includes both the natural frequency AND the mode shape. The mode shape *alone* is not a mode.
> 5. **Computing Only the Frequency, Not the Mode Shape:** A 7-mark question always has 3 marks for the natural frequency and 4 marks for the mode shape. Skipping the mode shape costs 4 marks.
> 6. **Determinant Sign Error:** The frequency equation is $\det([K] - \omega^2[M]) = 0$. Some students write $\det(\omega^2[M] - [K]) = 0$. This is mathematically equivalent but the polynomial coefficients will have **flipped signs** in their $\omega^2$ terms — a common source of negative results in KTU papers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Mode = Natural Frequency + Mode Shape** — these two always come as a pair; never report one without the other.
- [x] An **n-DOF system has exactly n modes** of vibration.
- [x] **First mode** is *in-phase* (all masses move in the same direction); **higher modes** have nodes and 180° phase shifts.
- [x] The **mass matrix** $[M]$ is always **diagonal** for lumped-parameter systems without inertial coupling.
- [x] The **stiffness matrix** $[K]$ is **symmetric** ($K_{ij} = K_{ji}$) and has **negative off-diagonal** terms for direct-coupling systems.
- [x] The eigenvalue problem is $\bigl([K] - \omega^2[M]\bigr)\{X\} = \{0\}$ — memorize this exact form.
- [x] The characteristic equation is obtained by setting the **determinant of $[K] - \omega^2[M]$ to zero**.
- [x] **Orthogonality** of mode shapes holds for *undamped* systems with *symmetric* $[M]$ and $[K]$ — a direct consequence of these two matrices being symmetric and positive-definite.
- [x] Orthogonality allows you to **decouple** the n coupled ODEs into n independent SDOF equations — the foundation of **modal superposition** method.
- [x] **Mode shapes are not unique** — they are defined up to a multiplicative scalar. Always normalize.
- [x] The **node** in a mode shape is the location that *does not move* during that mode — critical for sensor/actuator placement.
- [x] For a 2-DOF system, the characteristic equation is a **quadratic in $\omega^2$** — apply the quadratic formula correctly with the right sign conventions.
- [x] **Rayleigh's quotient** gives a quick estimate of the fundamental natural frequency: $\omega^2 = \dfrac{\{X\}^T[K]\{X\}}{\{X\}^T[M]\{X\}}$.
- [x] For continuous systems (shafts, beams), the number of modes is **theoretically infinite**, and mode shapes are described by *trigonometric* or *Bessel* functions — beyond the scope of KTU Module 4, but the concept generalizes.
- [x] In real engineering practice, **damping** shifts and broadens the natural frequencies and complicates mode shapes into *complex modes* — a topic for advanced study.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mode Shape
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mode Shape

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MODE SHAPE (NORMAL MODE / EIGENVECTOR):**
> A **mode shape** (also called **normal mode** or **principal mode of vibration**) is the specific *spatial deformation pattern* assumed by a vibrating system when it oscillates at one of its **natural frequencies** ($\omega_n$). Mathematically, it is the **eigenvector** $\{\phi\}$ (or $\phi(x)$) corresponding to the **eigenvalue** ($\omega_n^2$) obtained from the characteristic equation of an undamped vibrating system.

In matrix form, for an *n* degree-of-freedom (n-DOF) system governed by:

$$[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$$

each natural frequency $\omega_i$ is paired with a unique mode shape $\{\phi\}_i$ satisfying:

$$\left([K] - \omega_i^2 [M]\right)\{\phi\}_i = \{0\}$$

For a **continuous system** (e.g., shaft, beam), the mode shape is the spatial function $\phi_n(x)$ multiplying the time harmonic, obtained from the spatial differential equation after separation of variables.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition: "The Musical Note Analogy"**
> Imagine striking a *guitar string* on different positions:
> - When you pluck it at the middle → the string vibrates in **one big arc** (1st mode shape)
> - When you touch the midpoint lightly and pluck the quarter → it vibrates in **two halves** (2nd mode shape)
> - When you touch at 1/3 and 2/3 and pluck between → **three humps** (3rd mode shape)
>
> Each "shape" of vibration happens at a *specific pitch* (frequency). That fixed-shape-at-fixed-pitch is the **mode shape**!

Every real vibration of a structure is a *superposition* of its mode shapes — like a chord being a mix of individual notes.

## 1.3 Salient Features (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Natural frequency $\omega_n$** and **mode shape $\phi_n$** always come as a **pair** (eigenpair).
> - A system with **n DOF** has exactly **n mode shapes** (counting rigid-body modes for free systems).
> - Mode shapes are **defined up to a scalar multiple** (normalization is a choice).
> - They are **mutually orthogonal** with respect to mass and stiffness matrices.
> - **Number of nodes (zero crossings) increases** with mode order: 1st mode has 0 internal nodes, 2nd has 1, 3rd has 2, etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Origin of Mode Shapes — The Eigenvalue Problem

For an undamped free vibration of an *n*-DOF system:

$$[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$$

Assume a **simple harmonic solution**:

$$\{x(t)\} = \{\phi\} \, e^{i\omega t}$$

Substituting yields the **generalized eigenvalue problem**:

$$\left([K] - \omega^2 [M]\right)\{\phi\} = \{0\}$$

| Quantity | Mathematical Name | Physical Meaning |
|----------|------------------|------------------|
| $\omega_i$ | Eigenvalue (square root) | $i^{th}$ natural (angular) frequency (rad/s) |
| $\omega_i^2$ | Eigenvalue | Characteristic root |
| $\{\phi\}_i$ | Eigenvector | $i^{th}$ mode shape — relative displacement pattern |
| $\det([K] - \omega^2[M]) = 0$ | Characteristic equation | Polynomial whose roots give $\omega_i^2$ |

For a **non-trivial** solution, the determinant must vanish. Solving yields *n* values of $\omega_i^2$. Substituting each $\omega_i$ back into the eigenvalue equation gives the corresponding $\{\phi\}_i$.

## 2.2 Properties of Mode Shapes (Board-Favourite Points)

1. **Orthogonality with respect to Mass matrix**:
$$\{\phi\}_i^T [M] \{\phi\}_j = 0 \quad \text{for } i \neq j$$

2. **Orthogonality with respect to Stiffness matrix**:
$$\{\phi\}_i^T [K] \{\phi\}_j = 0 \quad \text{for } i \neq j$$

3. **Normalisation choices**:
   - **Mass-normalised**: $\{\phi\}_i^T [M] \{\phi\}_i = 1$
   - **Unity-maximum**: Largest component = 1
   - **Stiffness-normalised**: $\{\phi\}_i^T [K] \{\phi\}_i = 1$

4. **Nodes (zero-crossings)**: For an *n*-DOF system, the $i^{th}$ mode has $(i-1)$ internal nodes.

---

## 2.3 Mode Shapes of Continuous Systems — Transverse Vibration of Beams

The Euler-Bernoulli beam equation:

$$EI \frac{\partial^4 y}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

Separation of variables: $y(x,t) = Y(x)\,T(t)$

Spatial ODE:

$$\frac{d^4 Y}{dx^4} - \beta^4 Y = 0, \quad \beta^4 = \frac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI}$$

General solution:

$$Y(x) = A \sin(\beta x) + B \cos(\beta x) + C \sinh(\beta x) + D \cosh(\beta x)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MODE SHAPES**

| # | System / Context | Equation / Condition | Result |
|---|------------------|---------------------|--------|
| 1 | n-DOF free vibration | $\det([K] - \omega^2[M]) = 0$ | Characteristic equation |
| 2 | Eigenvector definition | $([K] - \omega_i^2[M])\{\phi\}_i = 0$ | Mode shape definition |
| 3 | Mass orthogonality | $\{\phi\}_i^T[M]\{\phi\}_j = 0$ | $i \neq j$ |
| 4 | Stiffness orthogonality | $\{\phi\}_i^T[K]\{\phi\}_j = 0$ | $i \neq j$ |
| 5 | Modal stiffness | $\omega_i^2 = \dfrac{\{\phi\}_i^T[K]\{\phi\}_i}{\{\phi\}_i^T[M]\{\phi\}_i}$ | Rayleigh quotient |
| 6 | Cantilever beam (fixed-free) | $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$ | Frequency equation |
| 7 | Simply-supported beam | $\sin(\beta L) = 0 \Rightarrow \beta_n L = n\pi$ | Exact closed form |
| 8 | Fixed-fixed beam | $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = 1$ | Frequency equation |
| 9 | Free-free beam | $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = 1$ | Same as fixed-fixed |
| 10 | Natural freq. (cantilever) | $\omega_n = (\beta_n L)^2 \sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$ | $n=1,2,3,\dots$ |

**Frequency parameters $\beta_n L$ for cantilever beam:**

| Mode ($n$) | $\beta_n L$ | Shape Description |
|-----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 1.875 | One big arc (no internal node) |
| 2 | 4.694 | S-shape (1 internal node) |
| 3 | 7.855 | W-shape (2 internal nodes) |
| 4 | 10.996 | Three humps (3 internal nodes) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Engineering**: Earthquake-resistant design uses the *first few mode shapes* (which carry most vibration energy).
- **Rotating Machinery**: Mode shapes of shafts determine critical speeds and torsional vibration patterns.
- **Automotive**: NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) engineers visualise body panel mode shapes to eliminate buzz and squeak.
- **Aerospace**: Flutter analysis — mode shapes of wings determine coupling with aerodynamic forces.
- **Turbomachinery**: Blade mode shapes guide frequency mistuning for vibration reduction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mode Shape Extraction for a 2-DOF System

**Problem Setup:** Two masses $m_1$, $m_2$ connected to a wall via springs $k_1$, $k_2$, coupled by spring $k_c$.

Equations of motion:

$$\begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_c & -k_c \\ -k_c & k_2 + k_c \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 1:** Assume harmonic motion $\{x\} = \{\phi\} e^{i\omega t}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
& \text{Substitute into EOM:} \quad \left([K] - \omega^2[M]\right)\{\phi\} = \{0\} \\[6pt]
& \Rightarrow \begin{bmatrix} k_1 + k_c - \omega^2 m_1 & -k_c \\ -k_c & k_2 + k_c - \omega^2 m_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \phi_1 \\ \phi_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** For non-trivial solution, set determinant to zero.

$$\det\left([K] - \omega^2[M]\right) = (k_1 + k_c - \omega^2 m_1)(k_2 + k_c - \omega^2 m_2) - k_c^2 = 0$$

**Step 3:** Expand to get the quadratic in $\omega^2$.

$$\begin{aligned}
& (k_1 + k_c)(k_2 + k_c) - (k_1 + k_c)\omega^2 m_2 - (k_2 + k_c)\omega^2 m_1 + \omega^4 m_1 m_2 - k_c^2 = 0 \\[4pt]
& \Rightarrow m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - \left[m_2(k_1 + k_c) + m_1(k_2 + k_c)\right]\omega^2 + (k_1 + k_c)(k_2 + k_c) - k_c^2 = 0 \\[4pt]
& \Rightarrow m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - \left[m_2(k_1 + k_c) + m_1(k_2 + k_c)\right]\omega^2 + (k_1 k_2 + k_1 k_c + k_2 k_c) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Solving this quadratic gives $\omega_1^2$ and $\omega_2^2$.

**Step 4:** For each $\omega_i$, obtain mode shape by back-substitution. From the first row:

$$(k_1 + k_c - \omega_i^2 m_1)\phi_1^{(i)} - k_c \phi_2^{(i)} = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{\phi_2^{(i)}}{\phi_1^{(i)}} = \frac{k_1 + k_c - \omega_i^2 m_1}{k_c} = r_i$$

Choosing $\phi_1^{(i)} = 1$:

$$\{\phi\}_i = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ r_i \end{Bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cantilever Beam Mode Shape

**Governing ODE:** $Y^{(4)}(x) - \beta^4 Y(x) = 0$

**General solution:**

$$Y(x) = A \sin(\beta x) + B \cos(\beta x) + C \sinh(\beta x) + D \cosh(\beta x)$$

**Boundary conditions for cantilever (fixed at $x=0$, free at $x=L$):**

| Location | Condition | Physical Meaning |
|----------|-----------|------------------|
| $x=0$ | $Y(0) = 0$ | No deflection at wall |
| $x=0$ | $Y'(0) = 0$ | No slope at wall |
| $x=L$ | $Y''(L) = 0$ | No bending moment at free end |
| $x=L$ | $Y'''(L) = 0$ | No shear force at free end |

**Apply $Y(0) = 0$:** $\quad B + D = 0 \Rightarrow D = -B$

**Apply $Y'(0) = 0$:** $\quad A\beta + C\beta = 0 \Rightarrow C = -A$

Simplified form:

$$Y(x) = A\bigl[\sin(\beta x) - \sinh(\beta x)\bigr] + B\bigl[\cos(\beta x) - \cosh(\beta x)\bigr]$$

**Apply $Y''(L) = 0$ and $Y'''(L) = 0$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y''(x) &= -A\beta^2\bigl[\sin(\beta x) + \sinh(\beta x)\bigr] - B\beta^2\bigl[\cos(\beta x) + \cosh(\beta x)\bigr] \\[4pt]
Y'''(x) &= -A\beta^3\bigl[\cos(\beta x) + \cosh(\beta x)\bigr] + B\beta^3\bigl[\sin(\beta x) - \sinh(\beta x)\bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

At $x = L$:

$$\begin{aligned}
-A\bigl[\sin(\beta L) + \sinh(\beta L)\bigr] - B\bigl[\cos(\beta L) + \cosh(\beta L)\bigr] &= 0 \\[4pt]
-A\bigl[\cos(\beta L) + \cosh(\beta L)\bigr] + B\bigl[\sin(\beta L) - \sinh(\beta L)\bigr] &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

For non-trivial $(A, B)$:

$$\bigl[\cos(\beta L) + \cosh(\beta L)\bigr]^2 + \bigl[\sin^2(\beta L) - \sinh^2(\beta L)\bigr] = 0$$

Using $\sin^2\theta - \sinh^2\theta = -(\cos^2\theta + \cosh^2\theta - 1)$... eventually simplifies to the **famous transcendental equation**:

$$\boxed{\cos(\beta_n L) \cosh(\beta_n L) = -1}$$

**Roots:** $\beta_1 L = 1.8751$, $\beta_2 L = 4.6941$, $\beta_3 L = 7.8548$, $\beta_4 L = 10.9955$, …

**Ratio $B/A$ (the modal coefficient):**

$$\sigma_n = \frac{B_n}{A_n} = \frac{\sin(\beta_n L) + \sinh(\beta_n L)}{\cos(\beta_n L) + \cosh(\beta_n L)}$$

**Final mode shape expression:**

$$\boxed{\phi_n(x) = \cosh(\beta_n x) - \cos(\beta_n x) - \sigma_n\bigl[\sinh(\beta_n x) - \sin(\beta_n x)\bigr]}$$

This function gives the **relative deflection** of the beam at position $x$ when vibrating in its $n^{th}$ mode. The amplitude is arbitrary (scale choice).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical Problem)

**Problem:** A uniform cantilever steel beam of length $L = 1$ m has the following properties: $E = 2\times 10^{11}$ N/m², $I = 5\times 10^{-9}$ m⁴, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $A = 2\times 10^{-4}$ m². Find the **first natural frequency** and plot/sketch the **first mode shape**.

**Step 1 — Frequency parameter:** $\beta_1 L = 1.8751$, so $\beta_1 = 1.8751$ rad/m.

**Step 2 — Compute $\omega_1$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_1 &= \beta_1^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}} \\[4pt]
\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}} &= \sqrt{\frac{(2\times 10^{11})(5\times 10^{-9})}{(7850)(2\times 10^{-4})}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{1000}{1.57}} = \sqrt{636.94} = 25.24 \;\; \text{m}^2/\text{s} \\[4pt]
\omega_1 &= (1.8751)^2 \times 25.24 = 3.516 \times 25.24 = 88.74 \;\; \text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency in Hz:**

$$f_1 = \frac{\omega_1}{2\pi} = \frac{88.74}{6.2832} = 14.12 \;\; \text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient $\sigma_1$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{\sin(1.8751) + \sinh(1.8751)}{\cos(1.8751) + \cosh(1.8751)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.9547 + 3.1831}{-0.2956 + 3.4891} = \frac{4.1378}{3.1935} = 1.2956
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — First mode shape at sample points** (with $\beta_1 = 1.8751$ m$^{-1}$):

| $x$ (m) | $\cosh(\beta x) - \cos(\beta x)$ | $\sinh(\beta x) - \sin(\beta x)$ | $\phi_1(x) = \text{col3} - 1.2956 \cdot \text{col4}$ |
|---------|---------------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| 0.0     | 0.0000                          | 0.0000                           | 0.0000 |
| 0.25    | 0.0271                          | 0.1105                           | $-0.1160$ |
| 0.50    | 0.2225                          | 0.5028                           | $-0.4289$ |
| 0.75    | 0.7436                          | 1.3315                           | $-0.9814$ |
| 1.00    | 1.7842                          | 2.2284                           | $-1.1034$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The values are **negative** because $\cosh-\cos$ grows slower than $\sigma(\sinh-\sin)$ in the early region for the 1st mode of a cantilever. The shape monotonically curves downward to its tip value, with **no internal node** — confirming 1st mode behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Flow — Mode Shape Extraction Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System Definition: n-DOF model"] --> B["Form Mass Matrix M and Stiffness Matrix K"]
    B --> C["Form Determinantal Equation: det K minus omega squared M equals 0"]
    C --> D{"Solve Characteristic Equation"}
    D --> E["Natural Frequency omega 1"]
    D --> F["Natural Frequency omega 2"]
    D --> G["Natural Frequency omega n"]
    E --> H["Back Substitute into K minus omega squared M phi equals 0"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Obtain Mode Shape phi 1"]
    H --> J["Obtain Mode Shape phi 2"]
    H --> K["Obtain Mode Shape phi n"]
    I --> L["Apply Normalisation Convention"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M["Final Modal Matrix Phi and Frequency Vector Omega"]
```

## 4.2 Mode Shape Visualisation — Cantilever Beam (Functional Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Mode1["Mode 1: Single Arc, 0 internal nodes"]
        A1["Fixed Wall"] --> B1["Beam: curves like a hockey stick"]
    end

    subgraph Mode2["Mode 2: S-shape, 1 internal node"]
        A2["Fixed Wall"] --> B2["Beam bends upward then downward"]
    end

    subgraph Mode3["Mode 3: W-shape, 2 internal nodes"]
        A3["Fixed Wall"] --> B3["Beam oscillates in three lobes"]
    end

    subgraph Mode4["Mode 4: Three humps, 3 internal nodes"]
        A4["Fixed Wall"] --> B4["Beam shows four alternating lobes"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Orthogonality Principle — Schematic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Two Distinct Modes: phi i and phi j"] --> Q["Compute phi i transpose M phi j"]
    P --> R["Compute phi i transpose K phi j"]
    Q --> S{"Result equals zero?"}
    R --> T{"Result equals zero?"}
    S -- Yes --> U["Modes are M Orthogonal"]
    T -- Yes --> V["Modes are K Orthogonal"]
    U --> W["Use for Modal Superposition"]
    V --> W
```

## 4.4 Engineering Decision Tree — Where Mode Shape Analysis is Used

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Vibration Problem"] --> Q1{"System Type?"}
    Q1 -- Discrete masses and springs --> P1["Use n-DOF Modal Analysis"]
    Q1 -- Continuous shaft or beam --> P2["Use Continuous System with Boundary Conditions"]
    P1 --> P1a["Compute Natural Frequencies"]
    P2 --> P2a["Solve Transcendental Equation for beta L"]
    P1a --> P1b["Extract Mode Shapes phi i"]
    P2a --> P2b["Build Mode Shape Function phi n of x"]
    P1b --> App1["Modal Superposition for Forced Response"]
    P2b --> App2["Critical Speed and Resonance Avoidance"]
    App1 --> Out["Predict Vibration Amplitude vs Frequency"]
    App2 --> Out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> Define *mode shape*. What is the physical significance of mode shapes in vibration analysis?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **mode shape** is the spatial pattern of deformation assumed by a vibrating system when it oscillates at one of its natural frequencies. For an *n*-DOF system, it is the eigenvector $\{\phi\}_i$ corresponding to the natural frequency $\omega_i$, obtained from $([K] - \omega_i^2[M])\{\phi\}_i = 0$.

**Physical significance:** Mode shapes reveal how different parts of the structure move *relative* to each other during free vibration. They indicate the locations of **nodes** (points of zero motion) and **antinodes** (points of maximum motion), helping engineers identify resonance-prone regions, design vibration isolators, and perform modal superposition for forced-response analysis.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mathematical statement, 1 mark for physical significance.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> State and explain the **orthogonality property** of mode shapes.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The mode shapes of an undamped vibrating system are **orthogonal with respect to both mass and stiffness matrices**. Mathematically, for $i \neq j$:

$$\{\phi\}_i^T [M]\{\phi\}_j = 0, \qquad \{\phi\}_i^T [K]\{\phi\}_j = 0$$

**Explanation:** Orthogonality arises because the eigenvalue problem yields linearly independent eigenvectors. This property allows **decoupling** of the coupled equations of motion into *n* independent single-DOF equations — one for each mode — which is the foundation of **modal superposition** analysis. *(3 marks: 1.5 for stating both equations, 1.5 for explaining decoupling.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*

**(a)** A two-degree-of-freedom system has the following matrices:

$$[M] = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{kg}, \qquad [K] = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \;\; \text{kN/m}$$

Determine the natural frequencies and the corresponding mode shapes. Normalise the mode shapes so that the first entry is unity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show that the mode shapes obtained in part (a) are orthogonal with respect to both mass and stiffness matrices. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Step 1:** Form the eigenvalue equation:

$$[K] - \omega^2[M] = \begin{bmatrix} 6000 - 2\omega^2 & -2000 \\ -2000 & 4000 - \omega^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Equate determinant to zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
& (6000 - 2\omega^2)(4000 - \omega^2) - (-2000)^2 = 0 \\[4pt]
& 24{,}000{,}000 - 6000\omega^2 - 8000\omega^2 + 2\omega^4 - 4{,}000{,}000 = 0 \\[4pt]
& 2\omega^4 - 14000\omega^2 + 20{,}000{,}000 = 0 \\[4pt]
& \omega^4 - 7000\omega^2 + 10{,}000{,}000 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Solve the quadratic in $\omega^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega^2 &= \frac{7000 \pm \sqrt{49{,}000{,}000 - 40{,}000{,}000}}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7000 \pm \sqrt{9{,}000{,}000}}{2} = \frac{7000 \pm 3000}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_1^2 = 2000 \;\; (\text{rad/s})^2, \quad \omega_2^2 = 5000 \;\; (\text{rad/s})^2}$$

$$\omega_1 = 44.72 \;\text{rad/s}, \qquad \omega_2 = 70.71 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4:** Find first mode shape (set $\phi_1^{(1)} = 1$). From the first row of the eigenvalue equation:

$$(6000 - 2\omega_1^2)(1) - 2000\,\phi_2^{(1)} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \phi_2^{(1)} = \frac{6000 - 2(2000)}{2000} = \frac{2000}{2000} = 1$$

$$\{\phi\}_1 = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Find second mode shape (set $\phi_1^{(2)} = 1$):

$$(6000 - 2\omega_2^2)(1) - 2000\,\phi_2^{(2)} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \phi_2^{(2)} = \frac{6000 - 2(5000)}{2000} = \frac{-4000}{2000} = -2$$

$$\{\phi\}_2 = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Mass orthogonality check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\{\phi\}_1^T[M]\{\phi\}_2 &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{Bmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 2 \\ -2 \end{Bmatrix} = 2 - 2 = 0 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Stiffness orthogonality check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\{\phi\}_1^T[K]\{\phi\}_2 &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{Bmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 6 - (-4) \\ -2 + (-8) \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 10 \\ -10 \end{Bmatrix} = 10 - 10 = 0 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

Both orthogonality conditions are satisfied. **(7 marks full credit.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]*

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion for transverse vibration of a uniform cantilever beam. State clearly the boundary conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a cantilever beam, derive the **frequency equation** $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$ and find the first three values of $\beta_n L$. Sketch the first three mode shapes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Derivation:**

Consider an Euler-Bernoulli beam of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, mass per unit length $\rho A$. Let $y(x,t)$ be the transverse deflection. Applying Newton's second law to an element $dx$:

$$-V + \left(V + \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}dx\right) - \rho A \, dx \, \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = \rho A \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2}$$

Using $V = \dfrac{\partial M}{\partial x}$ and $M = EI \dfrac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}$:

$$EI \frac{\partial^4 y}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

**Boundary conditions for cantilever (fixed at $x=0$, free at $x=L$):**

| Location | Condition | Reason |
|----------|-----------|--------|
| $x=0$ | $y(0,t) = 0$ | No deflection at fixed end |
| $x=0$ | $\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$ | No slope at fixed end |
| $x=L$ | $\dfrac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$ | Zero bending moment at free end |
| $x=L$ | $\dfrac{\partial^3 y}{\partial x^3}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$ | Zero shear force at free end |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

Assume solution $y(x,t) = Y(x)\cos(\omega t)$. Substituting into the beam equation gives:

$$\frac{d^4 Y}{dx^4} - \beta^4 Y = 0, \qquad \beta^4 = \frac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI}$$

General solution:

$$Y(x) = A\sin(\beta x) + B\cos(\beta x) + C\sinh(\beta x) + D\cosh(\beta x)$$

Apply boundary conditions as derived in Section 3.2. After algebraic manipulation, the non-trivial solution requires:

$$\boxed{\cos(\beta_n L)\cosh(\beta_n L) = -1}$$

**First three roots** (from numerical/transcendental solution):

$$\beta_1 L = 1.8751, \quad \beta_2 L = 4.6941, \quad \beta_3 L = 7.8548$$

**Corresponding natural frequencies:**

$$\omega_n = (\beta_n L)^2 \sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$$

**Mode shape sketches (described):**

- **Mode 1**: Smooth monotonic arc, max amplitude at tip, no internal node.
- **Mode 2**: S-curve with one node at $\approx 0.7834L$; tip and base move in opposite directions.
- **Mode 3**: Three lobes with two internal nodes at $\approx 0.5044L$ and $0.8318L$.

$$\phi_n(x) = \cosh(\beta_n x) - \cos(\beta_n x) - \sigma_n\bigl[\sinh(\beta_n x) - \sin(\beta_n x)\bigr]$$

where $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sin(\beta_n L) + \sinh(\beta_n L)}{\cos(\beta_n L) + \cosh(\beta_n L)}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS:**
> 1. **Forgetting that mode shapes are not unique** — students often "solve" and present only one vector; the magnitude is *arbitrary* (only the *ratio* matters). Normalisation convention must be stated.
> 2. **Confusing $\omega$ with $\omega^2$** — the characteristic equation is in $\omega^2$; don't forget to take the square root at the end.
> 3. **Sign errors in orthogonality check** — compute matrix product *carefully*; one transposed-vs-non-transposed slip loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Missing the negative sign in cantilever transcendental equation** — students often write $\cos\beta L \cosh\beta L = +1$ (this is for fixed-fixed beam). The cantilever requires the **$-1$** on the RHS.
> 5. **Not stating boundary conditions explicitly** — always list all four BCs with *physical reasoning*, not just mathematical statements.
> 6. **Mixing up $EI$ and $E$** in frequency calculations — $EI$ is flexural rigidity; don't drop the moment of inertia.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MODE SHAPES**
>
> ✅ Mode shape = **eigenvector** of the dynamical system corresponding to a **natural frequency**.
> ✅ Extracted from: $([K] - \omega_i^2[M])\{\phi\}_i = 0$ after solving $\det([K] - \omega_i^2[M]) = 0$.
> ✅ An *n*-DOF system has *n* mode shapes.
> ✅ Mode shapes are **defined up to a scalar multiple** — normalise before using.
> ✅ **Orthogonality**: $\{\phi\}_i^T[M]\{\phi\}_j = \{\phi\}_i^T[K]\{\phi\}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$.
> ✅ The $i^{th}$ mode has $(i-1)$ internal nodes (zero-crossings).
> ✅ Continuous beam mode shape function: $\phi_n(x) = \cosh(\beta_n x) - \cos(\beta_n x) - \sigma_n[\sinh(\beta_n x) - \sin(\beta_n x)]$.
> ✅ Cantilever frequency equation: $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$, roots $1.875, 4.694, 7.855, \ldots$
> ✅ Simply-supported: $\beta_n L = n\pi$ — exact, no transcendental equation.
> ✅ Fixed-fixed: $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = 1$ — same form as free-free beam.
> ✅ Mode shapes enable **modal superposition** to solve forced vibration problems.
> ✅ In mode 1, all masses move *in phase*; in higher modes, masses move *out of phase*.
> ✅ Rayleigh quotient gives best estimate of natural frequency: $\omega^2 = \dfrac{\phi^T K \phi}{\phi^T M \phi}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical or Whirling Speed of Shaft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Critical or Whirling Speed of Shaft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Speed (Whirling Speed)** is the angular velocity of a rotating shaft at which the **transverse vibrations** of the shaft become **resonant** with the **forced vibration** caused by the centrifugal force of the unbalanced rotor mass. At this speed, the **natural frequency of lateral vibration** of the shaft equals the **rotational frequency** of the shaft, producing **theoretically infinite amplitude** (in an undamped system) and catastrophic failure.

Mathematically, if $N_c$ is the critical speed in rpm, then:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{s}{m}} \;\;\text{rpm} \;=\; 9.55\sqrt{\frac{s}{m}} \;\text{rpm}$$

where $s$ is the lateral stiffness of the shaft (N/m) and $m$ is the equivalent rotor mass (kg).

> [!NOTE]
> **Whirling** = the phenomenon where the shaft bows/bends such that its **centerline traces a circular path** around the bearing axis. The shaft literally "whirls" like a jump-rope.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are **twirling a skipping rope** above your head. When you swing it slowly, the rope maintains its shape. But as you increase the swing speed, there is **one particular speed** at which the rope suddenly starts to wobble violently into a large looping shape — this is resonance.

A rotating shaft behaves identically:
- The **rotor mass** is the "load" sitting on the shaft.
- The **shaft** acts as a flexible spring (deflects under load).
- The **bearings** act like fixed walls.
- If the rotor has a slight **imbalance** (heavier on one side, called *eccentricity*), it generates a centrifugal force that whips the shaft.

At a specific rotational speed (the **critical speed**), this centrifugal force synchronizes with the shaft's natural vibration frequency — and **catastrophic whip** occurs. Engineers must therefore ensure that **operating speeds stay well clear** of $N_c$ (typically 20% margin above or below).

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor mass | $m$ | kg |
| Lateral stiffness of shaft | $s$ | N/m |
| Eccentricity (CG offset) | $e$ | m |
| Angular velocity | $\omega$ | rad/s |
| Critical speed | $N_c$ | rpm |
| Deflection (whirl radius) | $y$ | m |
| Modulus of elasticity (steel) | $E$ | **2.1 × 10¹¹ N/m²** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Board-favoured):** Operating speed should lie either **< 0.75 Nc** or **> 1.25 Nc** to avoid the dangerous resonance band.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Whirling Phenomenon

When a shaft carries a rotor (disc, pulley, gear) at its mid-span, the following chain of events occurs:

- **Step 1 — Eccentricity origin:** The center of gravity (CG) of the rotor never coincides *exactly* with the geometric center of rotation. This offset is the **eccentricity, $e$**.
- **Step 2 — Centrifugal excitation:** As the shaft rotates at angular velocity $\omega$, the offset CG experiences a centrifugal force $F_c = m\omega^2 r$, where $r$ is the radius from the bearing axis to the CG.
- **Step 3 — Shaft spring-back:** The shaft deflects by an amount $y$ under this force, opposing it with elastic restoring force $F_s = s y$.
- **Step 4 — Steady-state equilibrium:** When restoring force = centrifugal force, a stable whirl orbit is established at radius $(y+e)$.
- **Step 5 — Resonance trigger:** At one specific $\omega = \omega_c$, the deflection $y$ becomes mathematically unbounded (in the absence of damping). This is the **critical (whirling) speed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical speed of a shaft = natural frequency of transverse (lateral) vibration of the loaded shaft.** They are numerically identical.

---

## 2.2 Assumptions in the Standard Whirling Theory (KTU Board Favourite)

1. The shaft is **massless**; all mass is concentrated at the rotor (lumped parameter).
2. The shaft is **horizontal**, simply supported (pinned-pinned) at the bearings.
3. Rotor is **rigid** (does not deform).
4. No **damping** (idealised) — Dunkerley's method is later used to *include* self-weight effects.
5. Whirling is **uniform** — shaft traces a perfect circle (forward whirl; backward whirl is rare and is a higher-order effect).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **exam-ready kit** for Module 4 Critical Speed questions. Memorise the symbols, not just the equations.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y = \dfrac{m\,\omega^2 e}{s - m\omega^2}$ | Steady-state whirl amplitude for a single rotor on massless shaft |
| 2 | $y_{max} = \dfrac{e\,(N/N_c)^2}{1-(N/N_c)^2}$ | Amplitude expressed as a ratio of operating to critical speed |
| 3 | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{s}{m}}$ | **Critical angular velocity** (rad/s) — shaft natural frequency |
| 4 | $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{\dfrac{s}{m}}$ | Critical speed in **rpm** |
| 5 | $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | Critical speed expressed via **static deflection** $\delta_{st}=mg/s$ |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_1^2} + \dfrac{1}{\omega_2^2} + \dots + \dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2}$ | **Dunkerley's equation** for multi-rotor shafts |
| 7 | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{s}{\,m + M_{shaft}/3\,}}$ | Critical speed with **Rayleigh correction** for distributed shaft mass $M_{shaft}$ |
| 8 | $s = \dfrac{48 E I}{L^3}$ | Lateral stiffness of a **simply supported** shaft (UDL equivalent) |
| 9 | $s = \dfrac{192 E I}{L^3}$ | Lateral stiffness of a **fixed-fixed** shaft |
| 10 | $s = \dfrac{3 E I L}{a^2 b^2}$ | Lateral stiffness when load $W$ is placed at distance $a$ from one support, $b$ from other |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important — use `\vert` for absolute value in math** (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) when writing inside any table cell, to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Relevance of Critical Speed |
|---|---|
| **Steam & gas turbine rotors** | Long slender rotors spin at 3000–30000 rpm — must avoid $N_c$ by design margin |
| **Automotive drive shafts** | Cardan shafts have two critical speeds (bending + torsional) |
| **Centrifuge baskets** | Always run *through* critical speed during start-up, must cross it rapidly |
| **Aircraft propeller shafts** | Whirling can cause flutter — strict 20% safety margin |
| **Textile spindles** | High speed (~15000 rpm) — operate *below* critical speed in sub-resonant mode |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Critical Speed of a Single Rotor on a Massless Shaft

### Setup
A shaft of negligible mass, supported on bearings, carries a rigid rotor of mass $m$ at its mid-span. The shaft has lateral stiffness $s$ (force per unit deflection). The CG of the rotor is offset from the geometric axis of rotation by $e$.

### Free-Body & Force Diagram (Conceptual)
- Bearing axis = $O$
- Whirl axis = $O'$ displaced by $y$ (shaft deflection)
- CG of rotor = $G$, offset $e$ from geometric centre $C$
- Radius from $O$ to $G$ = $(y + e)$
- Shaft rotates at angular velocity $\omega$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Centrifugal force on the rotor:**

$$F_c = m\,\omega^2\,(y + e)$$

This is the force pulling the rotor outward in the whirl plane.

**Step 2 — Elastic restoring force from the deflected shaft:**

$$F_s = s\,y$$

The shaft acts like a linear spring of stiffness $s$.

**Step 3 — Dynamic equilibrium** (steady whirl, no acceleration in radial direction):

$$s\,y = m\,\omega^2\,(y + e)$$

**Step 4 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$s\,y = m\,\omega^2 y + m\,\omega^2 e$$

**Step 5 — Collect terms in $y$ on the left:**

$$s\,y - m\,\omega^2 y = m\,\omega^2 e$$

$$y\,(s - m\,\omega^2) = m\,\omega^2 e$$

**Step 6 — Solve for deflection $y$:**

$$y = \frac{m\,\omega^2 e}{s - m\,\omega^2}$$

This is the **famous whirl amplitude formula** (Formula #1 in the cheat sheet).

**Step 7 — Identify the condition for unbounded amplitude (resonance):**

For the deflection $y \to \infty$ (in an ideal undamped system), the denominator must vanish:

$$s - m\,\omega^2 = 0$$

$$\omega^2 = \frac{s}{m}$$

**Step 8 — Critical angular velocity:**

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{s}{m}} \;\;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 9 — Convert to rpm (multiply by $60/(2\pi) = 9.549$):**

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{s}{m}} = 9.55\,\sqrt{\frac{s}{m}} \;\;\text{rpm}$$

**Step 10 — Alternative form using static deflection $\delta_{st} = mg/s$:**

Substituting $s = mg/\delta_{st}$:

$$N_c = 9.55\,\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} \;\;\text{rpm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical insight:** Equation $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ shows that a shaft with **larger static deflection** (softer shaft, longer span) has a **lower critical speed** — this is why long slender rotors are so dangerous.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Dunkerley's Method (Multiple Rotors)

When a shaft carries **several rotors** $m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n$ at different positions, each contributes its own deflection. The exact natural frequency involves solving a determinant of order $n$. **Dunkerley's approximation** provides a simple lower-bound estimate by **superposing the squares of the natural frequencies**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define individual critical speeds:** Treat each rotor *alone* (others removed) and compute its critical speed:

$$\omega_1 = \sqrt{\frac{s_1}{m_1}}, \quad \omega_2 = \sqrt{\frac{s_2}{m_2}}, \quad \dots \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{s_n}{m_n}}$$

where $s_i$ is the stiffness of the shaft *as seen at the location of mass $m_i$* with all other masses removed.

**Step 2 — Total deflection at any point** is the sum of deflections caused by each mass acting independently (superposition principle valid for linear elastic systems):

$$\delta_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i$$

**Step 3 — Define the effective critical speed** $\omega_c$ such that the total deflection equals the deflection that *one* equivalent mass $m_{eq}$ would cause at the combined critical speed:

$$\delta_{total} = \frac{m_{eq}\,g}{\,s_{eq}\,} = \frac{g}{\,\omega_c^{\,2}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute individual contributions** $\delta_i = g/\omega_i^{\,2}$:

$$\frac{g}{\,\omega_c^{\,2}\,} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{g}{\,\omega_i^{\,2}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $g$ and invert both sides:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{\omega_c^{\,2}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\omega_i^{\,2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\omega_1^{\,2}} + \frac{1}{\omega_2^{\,2}} + \dots + \frac{1}{\omega_n^{\,2}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Dunkerley's result always gives a LOWER critical speed than the true value** — i.e., it is conservative (safe for design).

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Rayleigh Correction for Distributed Shaft Mass

A real shaft has its own mass $M_{shaft}$, not just the rotor mass. Rayleigh proposed adding approximately **one-third** of the shaft's mass to the rotor mass because a uniformly loaded simply-supported beam deflects with a sinusoidal shape — the average mass position is roughly $1/3$ of the maximum deflection.

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{s}{\,m + \dfrac{M_{shaft}}{3}\,}}$$

**Justification:** A uniform beam's kinetic energy during vibration equals that of a mass $M_{shaft}/3$ moving at the **maximum deflection velocity**. Equating with the strain energy gives the above correction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Formula to Use?

| Shaft Configuration | Recommended Formula | Common KTU Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Single rotor, massless shaft | $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{s/m}$ | Forgetting to convert rad/s → rpm |
| Single rotor, with shaft mass | $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{s/(m + M_{shaft}/3)}$ | Forgetting the $1/3$ factor |
| Multiple rotors | Dunkerley's equation | Adding frequencies instead of $1/\omega^2$ |
| Critical speed via static deflection | $N_c = 9.55\sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ | Mixing up $\delta_{st}$ in mm vs m |
| Simply supported vs fixed-fixed | $s_{simple}=48EI/L^3$ vs $s_{fixed}=192EI/L^3$ | Using wrong boundary condition |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Whirling Mechanism Flow

The physical phenomenon cannot be drawn in a static figure easily, so below is the **functional architecture flow** of the whirling process — exactly what a KTU board examiner expects in a 7-mark answer sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotor mass m with CG offset e] --> B[Shaft rotates at angular velocity w]
    B --> C[Centrifugal force Fc = m w square times y + e]
    C --> D{Shaft deflects by y}
```
