```mermaid
    D --> E[Elastic restoring force Fs = s y]
    E --> F{Steady State Equilib}
    F --> G[Fs = Fc]
    G --> H[Deflection y = m w square e over s minus m w square]
    H --> I{w equals w c ?}
    I -- Yes --> J[RESONANCE Whirling amplitude infinity]
    I -- No --> K[Stable whirl at finite radius]
    J --> L[CRITICAL SPEED Nc = 9.55 sqrt s over m rpm]
    K --> M[Operating region below 0.75 Nc or above 1.25 Nc SAFE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow arrows top-to-bottom. The decision block `I{w equals w c ?}` is the heart of critical-speed theory — when YES, you have resonance and the shaft destroys itself; when NO, you have a stable operating orbit.

---

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Multi-Rotor Critical Speed via Dunkerley

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Rotor 1 mass m1] --> S1[Stiffness seen s1 at location 1]
    M2[Rotor 2 mass m2] --> S2[Stiffness seen s2 at location 2]
    M3[Rotor 3 mass m3] --> S3[Stiffness seen s3 at location 3]
    S1 --> D[Dunkerley Superposition]
    S2 --> D
    S3 --> D
    D --> F[1 over wc square equals 1 over w1 square plus 1 over w2 square plus 1 over w3 square]
    F --> OUT[Combined Critical Speed wc]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram — Operating Speed Safety Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operate Below 0.75 Nc] --> B[Sub Resonant Safe Zone]
    C[Operate Between 0.75 Nc and 1.25 Nc] --> D[DANGER RESONANCE BAND Avoid]
    E[Operate Above 1.25 Nc] --> F[Super Resonant Safe Zone]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** A diagram like this, drawn in your exam script, earns easy **1–2 marks** even if your derivation has a small numerical error. Always include a labelled block diagram for critical-speed problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]

> Define **critical speed** of a shaft. State **Dunkerley's equation** and explain its significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Critical (whirling) speed is the angular velocity of the rotating shaft at which the **frequency of transverse vibration of the shaft coincides with the frequency of forced vibration due to centrifugal force of the unbalanced rotor**, leading to resonance and theoretically infinite amplitude in an undamped system.

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{s}{m}} \;\;\text{rpm}$$

**Dunkerley's equation (1 mark):** For a shaft with multiple rotors,

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^{\,2}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\omega_i^{\,2}}$$

**Significance:** It provides a quick, conservative (lower-bound) estimate of the combined critical speed without solving a large determinant, useful in design-stage calculations.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

> Distinguish between **forward whirl** and **backward whirl** in shaft dynamics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Forward Whirl | Backward Whirl |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Shaft bow rotates **same** direction as shaft spin | Shaft bow rotates **opposite** to shaft spin |
| Frequency | Equal to spin frequency $\omega$ | Different (typically higher) |
| Cause | Mass imbalance, gravity sag | Anisotropic stiffness (e.g., cracked shaft, journal bearings) |
| Common in | Simple rotors | Cracked rotors, gyroscopic systems |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 4 Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

A uniform steel shaft of length **L = 1.2 m**, diameter **d = 50 mm**, carries a single rotor of mass **m = 25 kg** at its mid-span. The shaft is simply supported, and the modulus of elasticity for steel is **E = 2.1 × 10¹¹ N/m²**. The rotor has an eccentricity of **e = 0.2 mm** and operates at **N = 1500 rpm**.

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **critical speed** of the shaft. If the operating speed is half the critical speed, find the **whirl amplitude** $y$.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Using **Dunkerley's method**, find the new critical speed if a **second rotor of mass 15 kg** is mounted at **0.3 m from one bearing**. (Hint: compute individual critical speeds treating each rotor alone.)

---

### Model Solution — Question Choice A

#### Part (a) — Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Moment of inertia of circular shaft:**

$$I = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi (0.050)^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{64}$$

$$I = 3.068 \times 10^{-7} \;\text{m}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Lateral stiffness of simply supported shaft with central load:**

$$s = \frac{48\,E\,I}{L^{3}}$$

$$s = \frac{48 \times (2.1 \times 10^{11}) \times (3.068 \times 10^{-7})}{(1.2)^{3}}$$

$$s = \frac{48 \times 2.1 \times 10^{11} \times 3.068 \times 10^{-7}}{1.728}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$48 \times 2.1 \times 10^{11} = 1.008 \times 10^{13}$$

$$1.008 \times 10^{13} \times 3.068 \times 10^{-7} = 3.0925 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $L^{3} = 1.728$:**

$$s = \frac{3.0925 \times 10^{6}}{1.728} = 1.790 \times 10^{6} \;\text{N/m}$$

**Step 5 — Critical speed:**

$$N_c = 9.55 \sqrt{\frac{s}{m}} = 9.55 \sqrt{\frac{1.790 \times 10^{6}}{25}}$$

$$\frac{s}{m} = \frac{1.790 \times 10^{6}}{25} = 7.160 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\sqrt{7.160 \times 10^{4}} = 267.6 \;\text{rad/s}$$

$$N_c = 9.55 \times 267.6 = 2556 \;\text{rpm}$$

**Step 6 — Operating speed = 0.5 × Nc, so $N/N_c = 0.5$:**

$$y = \frac{e\,(N/N_c)^{2}}{1 - (N/N_c)^{2}} = \frac{0.0002 \times 0.25}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.75}$$

$$\boxed{\,y = 6.67 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m} = 0.0667 \;\text{mm}\,}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute individual stiffnesses seen by each rotor.**

The shaft has length $L = 1.2$ m. Rotor 1 is at mid-span ($a = b = 0.6$ m). Rotor 2 is at $a = 0.3$ m from one bearing, so $b = 1.2 - 0.3 = 0.9$ m.

**Stiffness at location 1 (Rotor 1, treating Rotor 2 as absent):**

For simply supported beam with point load at distance $a$ from one end and $b$ from other:

$$s_1 = \frac{3\,E\,I\,L}{a^{2}\,b^{2}} \quad \text{with}\; a = 0.6, b = 0.6$$

$$s_1 = \frac{3 \times (2.1 \times 10^{11}) \times (3.068 \times 10^{-7}) \times 1.2}{(0.6)^{2}(0.6)^{2}}$$

$$s_1 = \frac{3 \times 2.1 \times 10^{11} \times 3.068 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.2}{0.1296}$$

**Numerator:** $3 \times 2.1 \times 10^{11} \times 3.068 \times 10^{-7} = 1.933 \times 10^{5}$
$\times 1.2 = 2.319 \times 10^{5}$

$$s_1 = \frac{2.319 \times 10^{5}}{0.1296} = 1.789 \times 10^{6} \;\text{N/m}$$

(Same as Part (a) as expected.)

**Stiffness at location 2 (Rotor 2, treating Rotor 1 as absent):**

$$s_2 = \frac{3\,E\,I\,L}{a^{2}\,b^{2}} \quad \text{with}\; a = 0.3, b = 0.9$$

$$a^{2}\,b^{2} = (0.3)^{2}(0.9)^{2} = 0.09 \times 0.81 = 0.0729$$

$$s_2 = \frac{2.319 \times 10^{5}}{0.0729} = 3.181 \times 10^{6} \;\text{N/m}$$

(Stiffer location → larger $s_2$, as expected near a support.)

**Step 2 — Individual critical speeds:**

$$\omega_1 = \sqrt{\frac{s_1}{m_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.789 \times 10^{6}}{25}} = \sqrt{71560} = 267.5 \;\text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_2 = \sqrt{\frac{s_2}{m_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.181 \times 10^{6}}{15}} = \sqrt{212067} = 460.5 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Dunkerley's equation:**

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^{\,2}} = \frac{1}{\omega_1^{\,2}} + \frac{1}{\omega_2^{\,2}}$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^{\,2}} = \frac{1}{71560} + \frac{1}{212067}$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^{\,2}} = 1.397 \times 10^{-5} + 4.716 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^{\,2}} = 1.869 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\omega_c^{\,2} = 53506$$

$$\omega_c = 231.3 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to rpm:**

$$\boxed{\,N_c = 9.55 \times 231.3 = 2209 \;\text{rpm}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compare: single rotor critical speed was 2556 rpm. Adding the second rotor **reduced** $N_c$ to 2209 rpm — Dunkerley's conservative estimate confirms that adding mass always lowers the safe operating limit.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Derive an expression for the **critical speed** of a shaft carrying a single concentrated mass, clearly stating all assumptions. Show that **critical speed = natural frequency of transverse vibration**.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A shaft of length 0.8 m, simply supported, has a rotor at mid-span that causes a **static deflection of 2 mm**. Calculate the critical speed in rpm. If a second rotor is added such that the new static deflection becomes 3 mm, find the percentage **reduction** in critical speed.

---

### Model Solution — Question Choice B

#### Part (a) — Solution [7 marks]

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Shaft is **massless**, with all inertia concentrated in rotor.
2. Rotor is **rigid disc** with CG offset by eccentricity $e$.
3. Shaft is **horizontal**, simply supported (linear spring behaviour).
4. **No damping** in the system.
5. Whirling motion is **circular** and uniform.

**Derivation (5 marks):** *(Proceed exactly as in Section 3.1 above — Steps 1 through 10 of the standard derivation)*

**Final conclusion:** $\omega_c = \sqrt{s/m}$, which is precisely the natural frequency of the equivalent spring-mass transverse vibration system. Hence **critical speed = natural frequency of transverse vibration.**

---

#### Part (b) — Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Use the static deflection form:**

$$N_c = 9.55\,\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

**Step 2 — Original critical speed with $\delta_{st,1} = 2 \text{ mm} = 0.002$ m:**

$$N_{c,1} = 9.55\,\sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.002}} = 9.55\,\sqrt{4905}$$

$$\sqrt{4905} = 70.04$$

$$N_{c,1} = 9.55 \times 70.04 = 668.9 \;\text{rpm}$$

**Step 3 — New critical speed with $\delta_{st,2} = 3 \text{ mm} = 0.003$ m:**

$$N_{c,2} = 9.55\,\sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.003}} = 9.55\,\sqrt{3270}$$

$$\sqrt{3270} = 57.18$$

$$N_{c,2} = 9.55 \times 57.18 = 546.0 \;\text{rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage reduction:**

$$\%\;\text{reduction} = \frac{N_{c,1} - N_{c,2}}{N_{c,1}} \times 100 = \frac{668.9 - 546.0}{668.9} \times 100$$

$$\%\;\text{reduction} = \frac{122.9}{668.9} \times 100 = 18.4\%$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{Critical speed reduced by } 18.4\%\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert mm → m before plugging $\delta_{st}$ into the formula. **Always convert to SI (metres)**.
> 2. **Wrong stiffness formula:** Using $s = 48EI/L^3$ for a fixed-fixed beam when the problem states simply supported — this gives a 4× error in stiffness and an erroneous critical speed.
> 3. **Adding frequencies in Dunkerley:** Students often write $\omega_c = \omega_1 + \omega_2 + \dots$. The **correct** operation is to add reciprocals of *squares*: $\sum 1/\omega_i^{\,2}$.
> 4. **Forgetting the $1/3$ factor** in Rayleigh's shaft mass correction.
> 5. **Not labelling diagrams** — a free-body diagram of the whirling rotor earns **at least 1 mark** even if numbers go wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to remember at a glance before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ **Critical speed** = speed at which transverse vibration resonates with forced vibration from unbalanced rotor.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $N_c = 9.55\,\sqrt{s/m}$ rpm $= 9.55\,\sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ rpm.
- ✅ **Whirl amplitude:** $y = m\omega^2 e / (s - m\omega^2)$.
- ✅ **Resonance condition:** $s - m\omega^2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_c = \sqrt{s/m}$.
- ✅ **Dunkerley (multi-rotor):** $1/\omega_c^{\,2} = \sum 1/\omega_i^{\,2}$ (always gives **lower** bound).
- ✅ **Rayleigh correction:** add $M_{shaft}/3$ to rotor mass to account for distributed shaft mass.
- ✅ **Stiffness tables:**
   - Simply supported, central load: $s = 48EI/L^3$.
   - Fixed-fixed, central load: $s = 192EI/L^3$.
   - Point load at $a$, $b$ on simply supported: $s = 3EIL/(a^2 b^2)$.
- ✅ **Safety rule:** Operate **below 0.75 Nc** OR **above 1.25 Nc** — never inside the resonance band.
- ✅ **Conversion:** $\omega\,(\text{rad/s}) \to N\,(\text{rpm}) = \omega \times 60/(2\pi)$.
- ✅ **Higher static deflection → lower critical speed** (soft shafts are dangerous).
- ✅ **Longer shaft → lower critical speed** (slender rotors need careful design).
- ✅ **Stiffer shaft (larger $EI$) → higher critical speed.**
- ✅ **Eccentricity $e$** amplifies whirl amplitude but does **NOT** change the critical speed value.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsional Vibration of Shaft: Torsionally Equivalent Shaft - Two Rotor and Three Rotor Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Torsional Vibration of Shaft: Torsionally Equivalent Shaft - Two Rotor and Three Rotor Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Torsionally Equivalent Shaft (Rayleigh's Equivalent Shaft):**
> A *torsionally equivalent shaft* is a fictitious **uniform shaft** of a single reference diameter (usually the diameter of the section on which a rotor is mounted) whose **angle of twist** under an applied torque is **identical** to that of the actual **stepped (multi-diameter) shaft** carrying the same torque.

In a multi-rotor system (two or three rotors mounted on a stepped shaft with sections of differing diameters), direct vibration analysis involves solving coupled differential equations. The equivalent-shaft technique reduces the stepped shaft to a uniform one, enabling the use of standard single/multi-degree-of-freedom torsional vibration formulas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic is part of *Module 4 – Torsional Vibration* and is a **frequently asked 14-mark problem** in End Semester Examinations (ESE). Students must be able to (a) compute the equivalent length, (b) write the frequency equation, and (c) determine the natural frequencies for both two- and three-rotor systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **flexible diving board** made by gluing together a thin piece of wood (near the tip) and a thick piece (near the hinge). It is hard to predict its bending vibration directly. However, if you replace the entire board with a *single uniform strip* whose tip deflection under a given load matches the original, the vibration problem reduces to a simple pendulum-on-a-spring problem.

Similarly:
- A **stepped shaft** = diving board with two/three thicknesses glued together.
- A **torsionally equivalent shaft** = single uniform strip that deflects (twists) the **same way** under the same torque.
- The **rotors** (flywheels/gears) are like **concentrated masses** on a spring.
- Once equivalent, the shaft behaves as a simple torsional spring of stiffness $k = \dfrac{GJ_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}}$, and standard vibration formulas apply.

### 1.3 Physical Quantities & Units

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Length of shaft section | $l_i$ | m |
| Diameter of shaft section | $d_i$ | m |
| Shear modulus of shaft material | $G$ | N/m² (Pa) |
| Polar moment of inertia of shaft | $J$ | m⁴ |
| Mass moment of inertia of rotor | $I$ | kg·m² |
| Torsional stiffness | $k$ | N·m/rad |
| Natural angular frequency | $\omega_n$ | rad/s |
| Natural frequency | $f_n$ | Hz (cycles/s) |

**Standard material property (Mild Steel):** $G \approx 0.83 \times 10^{11}$ **N/m²**; for **Cast Iron**, $G \approx 0.45 \times 10^{11}$ **N/m²**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why an Equivalent Shaft? — The Underlying Logic

For a uniform circular shaft of length $l$ and diameter $d$ subjected to torque $T$, the **angle of twist** is:

$$\theta = \frac{T \, l}{G \, J}, \quad \text{where} \quad J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

The **torsional stiffness** is:

$$k = \frac{T}{\theta} = \frac{G \, J}{l} = \frac{G \, \pi \, d^4}{32 \, l}$$

For a *stepped* shaft, each section has a different $J$. To replace the stepped shaft by a single equivalent uniform shaft of reference diameter $d_{\text{ref}}$, we require that the **total angle of twist** of the stepped shaft equals that of the equivalent shaft under the same torque.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Equivalence

1. **Identify the reference diameter** $d_{\text{ref}}$ (typically the diameter of the section carrying the largest rotor, or the section of constant diameter in a multi-section shaft).
2. **For each section $i$** of the actual shaft having length $l_i$ and diameter $d_i$, compute the **equivalent length** $l_{\text{eq},i}$ such that:
   $$\frac{T \, l_i}{G \, J_i} = \frac{T \, l_{\text{eq},i}}{G \, J_{\text{ref}}}$$
3. This yields the **equivalence formula**:
   $$l_{\text{eq},i} = l_i \left( \frac{d_i}{d_{\text{ref}}} \right)^{4}$$
4. **Total equivalent length**:
   $$L_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} l_i \left( \frac{d_i}{d_{\text{ref}}} \right)^{4}$$
5. The polar moment of inertia of the equivalent shaft is $J_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{\pi d_{\text{ref}}^{4}}{32}$, and its torsional stiffness is $k_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fourth power** of the diameter ratio is critical. A small change in $d$ produces a **large** change in $J$ and hence in $l_{\text{eq}}$. This is why the diameter of the thinnest section dominates the equivalent length.

### 2.3 Two-Rotor System (Stepped Shaft with Rotors at the Ends)

After conversion to a uniform equivalent shaft, the system is a uniform shaft of length $L_{\text{eq}}$ and stiffness $k_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}}$, carrying two rotors of moments of inertia $I_A$ and $I_B$ at the two ends.

- **Free vibration analysis** (treating both rotors free to oscillate) gives the natural angular frequency:

$$\boxed{\;\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{k_{\text{eq}}}{I_{\text{eq}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{G \, J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} \cdot \frac{I_A + I_B}{I_A \, I_B}}\;}$$

- **Natural frequency in Hz**:

$$\boxed{\;f_{n} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{G \, J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} \cdot \frac{I_A + I_B}{I_A \, I_B}}\;}$$

**Derivation cue:** $I_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{I_A I_B}{I_A + I_B}$ (parallel combination of inertias at the two free ends).

### 2.4 Three-Rotor System (Stepped Shaft with Three Rotors)

For three rotors of inertias $I_1, I_2, I_3$ on a stepped (now equivalent uniform) shaft, the system has **three degrees of freedom** in general. However, the *rigid-body* mode ($\omega = 0$) corresponds to all rotors rotating in unison. The two **non-trivial** natural frequencies are found from the **frequency equation** obtained by setting the determinant of the dynamic-stiffness matrix to zero.

The torsional stiffness between consecutive rotors (in terms of the equivalent shaft) is:

$$k_1 = \frac{G \, J_{\text{eq}}}{l_{12}^{\text{eq}}}, \quad k_2 = \frac{G \, J_{\text{eq}}}{l_{23}^{\text{eq}}}$$

where $l_{12}^{\text{eq}}$ and $l_{23}^{\text{eq}}$ are the equivalent lengths of the shaft sections between rotors 1–2 and 2–3 respectively.

The **frequency equation** is:

$$\begin{vmatrix} k_1 - I_1 \omega^2 & -k_1 & 0 \\ -k_1 & (k_1 + k_2) - I_2 \omega^2 & -k_2 \\ 0 & -k_2 & k_2 - I_3 \omega^2 \end{vmatrix} = 0$$

Expanding (and factoring out the $\omega^2 = 0$ root corresponding to the rigid-body mode):

$$\omega^2 \left[ (I_1 I_2 I_3) \omega^4 - (I_1 I_2 k_2 + I_2 I_3 k_1 + I_1 I_3 (k_1 + k_2)) \omega^2 + (I_1 k_1 k_2 + I_2 k_1 k_2 + I_3 k_1 k_2) \right] = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a quick KTU exam shortcut, the **fixing method** is often used: assume the **middle rotor is stationary** (node of the lowest mode), reducing the system to two independent two-rotor sub-systems. The lower natural frequency is then obtained by equating frequencies of both sub-systems.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> Use `\vert` / `\mid` for absolute values to avoid breaking table syntax.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $l_{\text{eq},i} = l_i (d_i \mid d_{\text{ref}})^{-4}$ | Equivalent length of a section | m |
| 2 | $L_{\text{eq}} = \sum l_i (d_i / d_{\text{ref}})^{4}$ | Total equivalent length of stepped shaft | m |
| 3 | $J = \pi d^{4} / 32$ | Polar moment of inertia of circular shaft | m⁴ |
| 4 | $k = G J / l$ | Torsional stiffness of a uniform shaft segment | N·m/rad |
| 5 | $k_{\text{eq}} = G J_{\text{ref}} / L_{\text{eq}}$ | Stiffness of the torsionally equivalent shaft | N·m/rad |
| 6 | $I_{\text{eq}} = (I_A I_B) / (I_A + I_B)$ | Reduced inertia for two-end-rotor system | kg·m² |
| 7 | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{k_{\text{eq}} (I_A + I_B) / (I_A I_B)}$ | Natural angular frequency, two-rotor system | rad/s |
| 8 | $f_{n} = \omega_{n} / (2\pi)$ | Natural frequency in Hz | Hz |
| 9 | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{I_{\text{eq}} / k_{\text{eq}}}$ | Time period of torsional vibration | s |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Torsionally equivalent shaft analysis is used in:
- **Automotive driveline design** — engine, gearbox, propeller shaft, and differential inertias with stepped shaft sections.
- **Turbogenerator sets** — multiple discs on a stepped shaft between HP, IP, LP turbines, and the generator rotor.
- **Marine propulsion** — long propeller shafts with multiple bearings and thrust blocks modelled as rotors.
- **Machine tool spindles** — analysing chatter caused by torsional resonance in stepped spindles carrying tooling and workpiece as effective rotors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Length of a Stepped Shaft Section

**Statement:** A shaft section of length $l_2$ and diameter $d_2$ carrying torque $T$ has angle of twist $\theta_2$. Show that this section can be replaced by an equivalent section of length $l_{\text{eq}}$ and diameter $d_1$ such that the twist remains the same.

**Proof:**

Angle of twist of the actual section:

$$\theta_{2} = \frac{T \, l_{2}}{G \, J_{2}} = \frac{32 \, T \, l_{2}}{G \, \pi \, d_{2}^{4}}$$

Angle of twist of the equivalent section (diameter $d_1$):

$$\theta_{\text{eq}} = \frac{T \, l_{\text{eq}}}{G \, J_{1}} = \frac{32 \, T \, l_{\text{eq}}}{G \, \pi \, d_{1}^{4}}$$

Setting $\theta_2 = \theta_{\text{eq}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{32 \, T \, l_{2}}{G \, \pi \, d_{2}^{4}} &= \frac{32 \, T \, l_{\text{eq}}}{G \, \pi \, d_{1}^{4}} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad l_{\text{eq}} &= l_{2} \cdot \left( \frac{d_{2}}{d_{1}} \right)^{4}
\end{aligned}$$

$\blacksquare$

This is the **fundamental relation** for equivalent length conversion. Extending to $n$ sections:

$$L_{\text{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} l_i \left( \frac{d_i}{d_{\text{ref}}} \right)^{4}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Natural Frequency of a Two-Rotor Torsional System

**Setup:** Uniform shaft of length $L_{\text{eq}}$, polar moment of inertia $J_{\text{eq}}$, shear modulus $G$, with two rotors $I_A$ and $I_B$ at the two free ends. The shaft is torsionally equivalent to the original stepped shaft.

**Step 1 — Free-body of the system:**

Let $\theta_A$ and $\theta_B$ be the small angular displacements of the two rotors from the equilibrium position. Because the shaft is massless and elastic, the torque in the shaft is uniform, equal to $T_s$.

**Step 2 — Restoring torque on each rotor:**

$$T_s = k_{\text{eq}} (\theta_A - \theta_B) = \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} (\theta_A - \theta_B)$$

**Step 3 — Equations of motion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_A \ddot{\theta}_A + \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}}(\theta_A - \theta_B) &= 0 \\[4pt]
I_B \ddot{\theta}_B - \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}}(\theta_A - \theta_B) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Assume simple harmonic motion** $\theta_i = \Theta_i \sin(\omega t)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
- I_A \omega^2 \Theta_A + \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}}(\Theta_A - \Theta_B) &= 0 \\[4pt]
- I_B \omega^2 \Theta_B - \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}}(\Theta_A - \Theta_B) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Express in matrix form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} - I_A \omega^2 & -\dfrac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} \\[8pt]
-\dfrac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} & \dfrac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} - I_B \omega^2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \Theta_A \\ \Theta_B \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Set the determinant to zero:**

$$\begin{aligned}
&\left( \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} - I_A \omega^2 \right)\left( \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} - I_B \omega^2 \right) - \left( \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} \right)^2 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Let $k = G J_{\text{eq}} / L_{\text{eq}}$. Expanding:

$$\begin{aligned}
(k - I_A \omega^2)(k - I_B \omega^2) - k^2 &= 0 \\[4pt]
k^2 - k I_B \omega^2 - k I_A \omega^2 + I_A I_B \omega^4 - k^2 &= 0 \\[4pt]
- k \omega^2 (I_A + I_B) + I_A I_B \omega^4 &= 0 \\[4pt]
\omega^2 \left[ I_A I_B \omega^2 - k(I_A + I_B) \right] &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Non-trivial solution** ($\omega \neq 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
I_A I_B \omega^2 &= k(I_A + I_B) \\[4pt]
\omega^2 &= \frac{k(I_A + I_B)}{I_A I_B} = \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} \cdot \frac{I_A + I_B}{I_A I_B} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \omega_n &= \sqrt{ \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} \cdot \frac{I_A + I_B}{I_A I_B} }
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Equivalent inertia** $I_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{I_A I_B}{I_A + I_B}$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_{\text{eq}}}{I_{\text{eq}}}}$$

$\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency Equation for a Three-Rotor System

**Setup:** Three rotors of inertias $I_1, I_2, I_3$ on a uniform equivalent shaft of length $L_{\text{eq}}$, polar moment $J_{\text{eq}}$, with shaft sections between them having stiffnesses $k_1$ and $k_2$:

$$k_1 = \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{l_{12}^{\text{eq}}}, \quad k_2 = \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{l_{23}^{\text{eq}}}$$

**Step 1 — Equations of motion** (using small angular displacements $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$):

$$\begin{aligned}
I_1 \ddot{\theta}_1 + k_1 (\theta_1 - \theta_2) &= 0 \\
I_2 \ddot{\theta}_2 + k_2 (\theta_2 - \theta_3) - k_1 (\theta_1 - \theta_2) &= 0 \\
I_3 \ddot{\theta}_3 + k_2 (\theta_3 - \theta_2) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Assume** $\theta_i = \Theta_i \sin(\omega t)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(k_1 - I_1 \omega^2) \Theta_1 - k_1 \Theta_2 &= 0 \\
-k_1 \Theta_1 + (k_1 + k_2 - I_2 \omega^2) \Theta_2 - k_2 \Theta_3 &= 0 \\
-k_2 \Theta_2 + (k_2 - I_3 \omega^2) \Theta_3 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Matrix form and determinant:**

$$\begin{vmatrix}
k_1 - I_1 \omega^2 & -k_1 & 0 \\
-k_1 & k_1 + k_2 - I_2 \omega^2 & -k_2 \\
0 & -k_2 & k_2 - I_3 \omega^2
\end{vmatrix} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Expanding the determinant** (using first-row expansion):

$$\begin{aligned}
&(k_1 - I_1 \omega^2) \begin{vmatrix} k_1 + k_2 - I_2 \omega^2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 - I_3 \omega^2 \end{vmatrix} \\[4pt]
&\quad - (-k_1) \begin{vmatrix} -k_1 & -k_2 \\ 0 & k_2 - I_3 \omega^2 \end{vmatrix} + 0 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Computing the $2 \times 2$ determinants:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= (k_1 + k_2 - I_2 \omega^2)(k_2 - I_3 \omega^2) - k_2^2 \\
&= (k_1 k_2 - k_1 I_3 \omega^2 - I_2 \omega^2 k_2 + I_2 I_3 \omega^4 + k_2^2 - I_3 \omega^2 k_2) - k_2^2 \\
&= k_1 k_2 - (k_1 I_3 + k_2 I_2 + k_2 I_3) \omega^2 + I_2 I_3 \omega^4
\end{aligned}$$

$$D_2 = (-k_1)(k_2 - I_3 \omega^2) - 0 = -k_1 (k_2 - I_3 \omega^2)$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
&(k_1 - I_1 \omega^2) D_1 + k_1 D_2 = 0 \\
&(k_1 - I_1 \omega^2)\left[ k_1 k_2 - (k_1 I_3 + k_2 I_2 + k_2 I_3) \omega^2 + I_2 I_3 \omega^4 \right] \\
&\quad + k_1 \cdot \left[ -k_1 k_2 + k_1 I_3 \omega^2 \right] = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Rearranging into a polynomial in** $\omega^2$:

After expanding and collecting like terms (tedious but straightforward), the **cubic** in $\omega^2$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\omega^2 \Bigl[ I_1 I_2 I_3 \, \omega^4 \\
&\quad - (I_1 I_2 k_2 + I_2 I_3 k_1 + I_1 I_3 (k_1 + k_2)) \omega^2 \\
&\quad + (I_1 + I_2 + I_3) k_1 k_2 \Bigr] = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Solutions:**

- $\omega^2 = 0$ → **rigid body mode** (all rotors rotate in unison — no relative twist).
- The remaining quadratic gives the **two non-trivial natural frequencies** $\omega_{n,1}$ and $\omega_{n,2}$.

$\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Two-Rotor Stepped Shaft

> A steel shaft of $G = 0.83 \times 10^{11}$ N/m² carries two rotors at its ends. Section 1 (under rotor A) has $d_1 = 60$ mm, $l_1 = 0.6$ m. Section 2 (under rotor B) has $d_2 = 40$ mm, $l_2 = 0.4$ m. $I_A = 0.5$ kg·m², $I_B = 0.3$ kg·m². Find the natural frequency.

**Step 1 — Equivalent length** (taking $d_1 = 60$ mm as reference):

$$L_{\text{eq}} = l_1 + l_2 \left( \frac{d_2}{d_1} \right)^{4} = 0.6 + 0.4 \times \left( \frac{40}{60} \right)^{4} = 0.6 + 0.4 \times (0.6667)^{4}$$

$$(0.6667)^{4} = 0.1975, \quad \Rightarrow L_{\text{eq}} = 0.6 + 0.4 \times 0.1975 = 0.6 + 0.079 = 0.679 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Polar moment of inertia** of the equivalent shaft:

$$J_{\text{eq}} = \frac{\pi d_1^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.060)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent stiffness:**

$$k_{\text{eq}} = \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{0.83 \times 10^{11} \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}}{0.679} = \frac{1.056 \times 10^{5}}{0.679} = 1.555 \times 10^{5} \text{ N·m/rad}$$

**Step 4 — Natural angular frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{ \frac{k_{\text{eq}} (I_A + I_B)}{I_A I_B} } = \sqrt{ \frac{1.555 \times 10^{5} \times 0.8}{0.5 \times 0.3} } = \sqrt{ \frac{1.244 \times 10^{5}}{0.15} } = \sqrt{8.293 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$\omega_n \approx 910.7 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Natural frequency in Hz:**

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{910.7}{6.2832} \approx 144.94 \text{ Hz}$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Three-Rotor System (Conceptual)

For a three-rotor system with $I_1 = 1.0$, $I_2 = 0.5$, $I_3 = 0.8$ kg·m², $k_1 = 8 \times 10^{5}$, $k_2 = 6 \times 10^{5}$ N·m/rad, substituting into the frequency equation:

$$(1.0)(0.5)(0.8) \omega^4 - \left[ (1.0)(0.5)(6) + (0.5)(0.8)(8) + (1.0)(0.8)(14) \right] \omega^2 \times 10^{5} + (1.0+0.5+0.8)(8)(6) \times 10^{10} = 0$$

Simplifying (with $\omega^2 = x$, $x$ in rad²/s²):

$$0.4 \, x^2 - 19.7 \times 10^{5} \, x + 110.4 \times 10^{10} = 0$$

Solving this quadratic yields the two natural frequencies. *(Numerical solution omitted in this section — included in the numerical problem bank in Section 5.)*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Equivalent-Shaft Analysis

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the logical flow of analysing a torsional vibration problem using the equivalent-shaft method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Multi-Rotor Torsional System] --> B[Identify All Shaft Sections and Their Diameters]
    B --> C[Select Reference Diameter dRef]
    C --> D[Compute Equivalent Length of Each Section]
    D --> D1["l eq i = l i × (d i / dRef) 4"]
    D --> E[Sum Equivalent Lengths to Obtain L eq]
    E --> F[Compute Equivalent Polar Moment J eq]
    F --> G{Number of Rotors?}
    G -- Two Rotors --> H[Compute k eq = GJ eq / L eq]
    H --> I["ωn = sqrt( k eq × I A + I B / I A × I B )"]
    I --> J[Convert to Hz and Report]
    G -- Three Rotors --> K[Compute k 1 and k 2 Between Rotor Pairs]
    K --> L[Form 3 × 3 Dynamic Stiffness Matrix]
    L --> M[Set Determinant Equal to Zero]
    M --> N[Solve Cubic in ω2]
    N --> O[Discard ω = 0 Rigid Body Mode]
    O --> P[Report ω n1 and ω n2 in Hz]
    J --> Q[End]
    P --> Q
```

### 4.2 Physical Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ActualSteppedShaft["Actual Stepped Shaft"]
        RA1((Rotor A)) -- D1, L1 --> RB1((Rotor B))
    end
    subgraph EquivalentShaft["Torsionally Equivalent Uniform Shaft"]
        RA2((Rotor A)) -- dRef, Leq --> RB2((Rotor B))
    end
    ActualSteppedShaft -- Equivalent transformation --> EquivalentShaft
    ActualSteppedShaft -- Property 1 --> P1[Same Angle of Twist Under T]
    ActualSteppedShaft -- Property 2 --> P2[Same Torsional Stiffness k]
    ActualSteppedShaft -- Property 3 --> P3[Same Natural Frequency]
```

### 4.3 Three-Rotor Mode Shape Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Mode1["Mode 1: Lowest Frequency"]
        R1A((Rotor 1)) -- θ1 --> N1((Node - Rotor 2 fixed)) -- θ3 --> R3A((Rotor 3))
    end
    subgraph Mode2["Mode 2: Highest Frequency"]
        R1B((Rotor 1)) -- θ1 --> AN((Anti-node Rotor 2)) -- θ3 --> R3B((Rotor 3))
    end
    Mode1 -- Rotor 2 at rest --> Note1[All three rotors oscillate in Mode 2 with rotor 2 as anti-node]
    Note1 --> Mode2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:**
> - Solid arrows in the flowcharts represent computational steps.
> - The block diagram shows the *equivalence transformation* between the actual stepped shaft and the fictitious uniform shaft.
> - The three-rotor mode shape schematic illustrates that rotor 2 is a **node** in the lowest mode (when it can be assumed stationary) and an **anti-node** in the higher mode.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define a *torsionally equivalent shaft*. Why is it used in the analysis of multi-rotor torsional systems?

**Model Answer:**
A torsionally equivalent shaft is a fictitious uniform shaft, of a single reference diameter, whose angle of twist under an applied torque is identical to that of the actual stepped (multi-diameter) shaft. It is used because a stepped shaft with multiple rotors leads to coupled differential equations that are tedious to solve. By replacing the stepped shaft with a uniform equivalent one, the system can be analysed using simple two- or three-rotor vibration formulas, and the natural frequencies are obtained directly.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample]**
State the formula for the equivalent length of a stepped shaft section of length $l_i$ and diameter $d_i$ when the reference diameter is $d_{\text{ref}}$. Why is the diameter ratio raised to the power 4?

**Model Answer:**

$$l_{\text{eq},i} = l_i \left( \frac{d_i}{d_{\text{ref}}} \right)^{4}$$

The power 4 arises because the polar moment of inertia $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ is proportional to the **fourth power** of the diameter. Equating the angle of twist of two shaft sections under the same torque:

$$\frac{l_i}{J_i} = \frac{l_{\text{eq},i}}{J_{\text{ref}}} \Rightarrow l_{\text{eq},i} = l_i \cdot \frac{J_i}{J_{\text{ref}}} = l_i \left( \frac{d_i}{d_{\text{ref}}} \right)^4$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, ESE Model Paper]**
> *(Module 4: Torsional Vibration — Torsionally Equivalent Shaft — Two Rotor System)*

A steel shaft ($G = 0.83 \times 10^{11}$ N/m²) carries two rotors A and B at its two ends. The shaft is stepped: section 1 (under rotor A) has diameter $d_1 = 75$ mm and length $l_1 = 0.5$ m; section 2 (under rotor B) has diameter $d_2 = 50$ mm and length $l_2 = 0.7$ m. The mass moments of inertia are $I_A = 0.8$ kg·m² and $I_B = 0.4$ kg·m².

**(a) (7 Marks)** Determine the equivalent length of the stepped shaft and the equivalent polar moment of inertia. Compute the torsional stiffness of the equivalent shaft.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Calculate the natural frequency of torsional vibration in Hz and the time period of vibration.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Reference diameter: $d_{\text{ref}} = d_1 = 75$ mm = $0.075$ m.

Equivalent length of section 2 (in terms of $d_1$):

$$l_{2,\text{eq}} = l_2 \left( \frac{d_2}{d_1} \right)^4 = 0.7 \times \left( \frac{50}{75} \right)^4 = 0.7 \times (0.6667)^4$$

$$(0.6667)^4 = (4/6)^4 = 256/1296 = 0.19753$$

$$l_{2,\text{eq}} = 0.7 \times 0.19753 = 0.1383 \text{ m}$$

Total equivalent length:

$$L_{\text{eq}} = l_1 + l_{2,\text{eq}} = 0.5 + 0.1383 = 0.6383 \text{ m}$$

Equivalent polar moment of inertia:

$$J_{\text{eq}} = \frac{\pi d_1^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.075)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 3.164 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 3.107 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

Torsional stiffness of the equivalent shaft:

$$k_{\text{eq}} = \frac{G J_{\text{eq}}}{L_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{0.83 \times 10^{11} \times 3.107 \times 10^{-6}}{0.6383} = \frac{2.579 \times 10^{5}}{0.6383} = 4.040 \times 10^{5} \text{ N·m/rad}$$

**Part (b):**

Natural angular frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{ k_{\text{eq}} \cdot \frac{I_A + I_B}{I_A I_B} } = \sqrt{ 4.040 \times 10^5 \times \frac{0.8 + 0.4}{0.8 \times 0.4} }$$

$$= \sqrt{ 4.040 \times 10^5 \times \frac{1.2}{0.32} } = \sqrt{ 4.040 \times 10^5 \times 3.75 } = \sqrt{ 1.515 \times 10^6 } = 1230.85 \text{ rad/s}$$

Natural frequency in Hz:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1230.85}{6.2832} \approx 195.9 \text{ Hz}$$

Time period:

$$T = \frac{1}{f_n} = \frac{1}{195.9} \approx 5.104 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5.104 \text{ ms}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, ESE Actual Paper]**
> *(Module 4: Torsional Vibration — Torsionally Equivalent Shaft — Three Rotor System)*

A uniform steel shaft of $G = 0.83 \times 10^{11}$ N/m² and diameter $d = 60$ mm carries three rotors. Rotor 1 (left end) has $I_1 = 1.2$ kg·m²; rotor 2 (middle) has $I_2 = 0.6$ kg·m² placed at $0.6$ m from rotor 1; rotor 3 (right end) has $I_3 = 0.9$ kg·m² placed at $0.5$ m from rotor 2.

**(a) (7 Marks)** Compute the torsional stiffnesses $k_1$ and $k_2$ of the two shaft sections and formulate the frequency equation for the three-rotor system.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Determine the two non-trivial natural frequencies of the system.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Polar moment of inertia of the uniform shaft:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.060)^4}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

Torsional stiffness of section 1 (length $l_1 = 0.6$ m):

$$k_1 = \frac{G J}{l_1} = \frac{0.83 \times 10^{11} \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}}{0.6} = \frac{1.056 \times 10^5}{0.6} = 1.760 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m/rad}$$

Torsional stiffness of section 2 (length $l_2 = 0.5$ m):

$$k_2 = \frac{G J}{l_2} = \frac{1.056 \times 10^5}{0.5} = 2.112 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m/rad}$$

The frequency equation (after setting the determinant of the dynamic-stiffness matrix to zero and factoring out the $\omega = 0$ root) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
&I_1 I_2 I_3 \, \omega^4 - \bigl[ I_1 I_2 k_2 + I_2 I_3 k_1 + I_1 I_3 (k_1 + k_2) \bigr] \omega^2 + (I_1 + I_2 + I_3) k_1 k_2 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting the values:

- $I_1 I_2 I_3 = 1.2 \times 0.6 \times 0.9 = 0.648$ kg³·m⁶
- $I_1 I_2 k_2 = 1.2 \times 0.6 \times 2.112 \times 10^5 = 1.521 \times 10^5$
- $I_2 I_3 k_1 = 0.6 \times 0.9 \times 1.760 \times 10^5 = 0.950 \times 10^5$
- $I_1 I_3 (k_1 + k_2) = 1.2 \times 0.9 \times (1.760 + 2.112) \times 10^5 = 1.08 \times 3.872 \times 10^5 = 4.182 \times 10^5$
- $(I_1 + I_2 + I_3) k_1 k_2 = 2.7 \times 1.760 \times 2.112 \times 10^{10} = 10.035 \times 10^{10} = 1.0035 \times 10^{11}$

Frequency equation (with $\omega^2 = x$):

$$0.648 \, x^2 - 6.653 \times 10^5 \, x + 1.0035 \times 10^{11} = 0$$

**Part (b):**

Dividing by 0.648:

$$x^2 - 1.0269 \times 10^6 \, x + 1.5486 \times 10^{11} = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$x = \frac{1.0269 \times 10^6 \pm \sqrt{ (1.0269 \times 10^6)^2 - 4 \times 1.5486 \times 10^{11} }}{2}$$

Discriminant:

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= (1.0269)^2 \times 10^{12} - 6.1944 \times 10^{11} \\
&= 1.0545 \times 10^{12} - 0.6194 \times 10^{12} \\
&= 4.351 \times 10^{11} \\
\sqrt{D} &= 6.596 \times 10^5
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$x_1 = \frac{1.0269 \times 10^6 - 6.596 \times 10^5}{2} = \frac{3.673 \times 10^5}{2} = 1.837 \times 10^5$$

$$x_2 = \frac{1.0269 \times 10^6 + 6.596 \times 10^5}{2} = \frac{1.6865 \times 10^6}{2} = 8.432 \times 10^5$$

Natural angular frequencies:

$$\omega_{n,1} = \sqrt{1.837 \times 10^5} = 428.6 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_{n,2} = \sqrt{8.432 \times 10^5} = 918.3 \text{ rad/s}$$

Natural frequencies in Hz:

$$f_{n,1} = \frac{428.6}{2\pi} \approx 68.21 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_{n,2} = \frac{918.3}{2\pi} \approx 146.1 \text{ Hz}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting the fourth power:** Writing $l_{\text{eq},i} = l_i (d_i / d_{\text{ref}})$ instead of $l_i (d_i / d_{\text{ref}})^4$ — this is the **#1 error**. Always remember: $J \propto d^4$.
> 2. **Wrong reference diameter:** Choosing $d_{\text{ref}}$ arbitrarily. The reference is usually the diameter of the section carrying the **largest rotor** or the constant-diameter portion.
> 3. **Including the rigid-body mode:** Reporting $\omega = 0$ as one of the natural frequencies in a three-rotor problem — it corresponds to all rotors rotating in unison and is *not* a vibration mode.
> 4. **Unit errors:** Mixing mm and m for diameters and lengths. Convert all to **SI units (m)** before substitution.
> 5. **Forgetting to divide by $2\pi$:** Reporting $\omega_n$ (in rad/s) as the answer when the question asks for $f_n$ (in Hz).
> 6. **Sign errors in the frequency equation:** When writing the 3 × 3 matrix, off-diagonal terms should be **negative** ($-\omega^2 I$ coupling), not positive.
> 7. **Skipping the determinant expansion:** Students often quote the frequency equation without derivation. KTU examiners award **methodology marks** for showing the determinant expansion step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- [x] **Torsionally equivalent shaft** = uniform shaft (reference diameter) with the same angle of twist as the actual stepped shaft under the same torque.
- [x] **Equivalent length formula:** $L_{\text{eq}} = \sum l_i (d_i / d_{\text{ref}})^4$ — the **fourth power is mandatory**.
- [x] **Reference diameter $d_{\text{ref}}$** is usually the diameter of the section carrying the largest rotor, or any constant-diameter section.
- [x] **Polar moment of inertia:** $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ (m⁴).
- [x] **Torsional stiffness of a uniform shaft segment:** $k = G J / l$.
- [x] **Two-rotor natural angular frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{ (G J_{\text{eq}} / L_{\text{eq}}) \cdot (I_A + I_B) / (I_A I_B) }$.
- [x] **Equivalent inertia of two end rotors:** $I_{\text{eq}} = I_A I_B / (I_A + I_B)$.
- [x] **Two-rotor natural frequency in Hz:** $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi)$.
- [x] **Three-rotor system** has three degrees of freedom → three roots of the frequency equation: one **rigid-body mode** ($\omega = 0$) and **two non-trivial natural frequencies**.
- [x] **Frequency equation** (three rotor): $I_1 I_2 I_3 \omega^4 - [I_1 I_2 k_2 + I_2 I_3 k_1 + I_1 I_3 (k_1 + k_2)] \omega^2 + (I_1 + I_2 + I_3) k_1 k_2 = 0$.
- [x] **Mode 1 (lowest):** Middle rotor behaves approximately as a **node** (stationary); Mode 2 (highest): middle rotor behaves as an **anti-node** (largest amplitude).
- [x] **Units check:** $G$ in N/m², $J$ in m⁴, $l$ in m, $I$ in kg·m², $k$ in N·m/rad, $\omega_n$ in rad/s, $f_n$ in Hz.
- [x] **For mild steel, use $G \approx 0.83 \times 10^{11}$ N/m²** unless specified otherwise.
- [x] **Quick sanity check:** If both end rotors are equal ($I_A = I_B = I$), then $I_{\text{eq}} = I/2$ and $\omega_n = \sqrt{2k/I}$.
- [x] **When $d_i < d_{\text{ref}}$:** equivalent length is *less* than the actual length (the thin section contributes less to total twist).
- [x] **When $d_i > d_{\text{ref}}$:** equivalent length is *more* than the actual length (the thick section contributes more to total twist).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free Torsional Vibration of Geared Systems - Numerical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET864 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Free Torsional Vibration of Geared Systems - Numerical Examples # Free Torsional Vibration of Geared Systems – Numerical Examples ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expected Terminology):** > *Free torsional vibration of a geared system* refers to the angular oscillatory motion of a multi-rotor shaft system (interconnected through gear trains) that occurs when the system is momentarily disturbed from its equilibrium position and then allowed to vibrate without any external time-varying excitation torque. In a geared system, two (or more) rotors mounted on different parallel shafts are kinematically coupled through gear teeth. Because the gear pair enforces a strict angular-displacement ratio, the **kinetic energy** and **strain energy** of one rotor can be *referred* (transferred) onto the other shaft, thereby collapsing the multi-degree-of-freedom (M-DOF) geared network into an **equivalent single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF)** torsional system. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Pendulum-and-Spring Analogy Imagine two pendulums of different masses hanging from two rigid ceilings, but connected by a *spring* stretched horizontally between their bobs: | Real-World Picture | Engineering Equivalent | |---|---| | Heavy pendulum bob (large mass) | Large rotor moment of inertia $I_1$ | | Light pendulum bob (small mass) | Smaller rotor $I_2$ on parallel shaft | | Stiff horizontal spring connecting them | Torsional shaft (stiffness $K$) between gears | | Pendulums swinging together | Coupled angular oscillation of gear shafts | | Gear teeth meshing | Imposed kinematic ratio $n = \omega_1/\omega_2$ | > [!NOTE] > The **gear pair acts like a kinematic transformer** — it neither stores nor dissipates energy; it merely *scaling* angular displacements, velocities, and inertias between the two shafts. This is precisely why we can mathematically "transfer" all inertia to one side and obtain a single equivalent torsional system. ### Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted for KTU Board Exams) - **Gear ratio** $n = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{\theta_1}{\theta_2}$ *(number of teeth ratio = inverse of speed ratio)* - **Equivalent moment of inertia** (referred to shaft 1): $\;I_{eq} = I_1 + \dfrac{I_2}{n^2}$ - **Equivalent torsional stiffness**: $\;K_{eq} = K$ *(shaft stiffness remains unchanged in series configuration)* - **Natural circular frequency**: $\;\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{eq}}{I_{eq}}}\;\; \text{rad/s}$ - **Natural frequency in Hz**: $\;f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi}\;\; \text{Hz}$ - **Time period**: $\;T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n}\;\; \text{seconds}$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Setup of a Two-Rotor Geared Torsional System The canonical system studied in KTU Module 4 consists of: 1. **Shaft 1** carrying a rotor of moment of inertia $I_1$ and a pinion of $N_1$ teeth. 2. **Shaft 2** carrying a gear of $N_2$ teeth meshing with the pinion, plus a rotor of moment of inertia $I_2$. 3. A **torsional shaft** of stiffness $K$ joining shaft 1 to a fixed frame (or coupling the two shafts through a flexible coupling). ### 2.2 Governing Logic — Step-by-Step Energy Transfer The procedure for converting a geared system into an SDOF equivalent involves **three logical steps**: **Step 1 — Kinematic Constraint:** Gear meshing is rigid and slip-free. Therefore, at every instant: $$ \theta_2 = \frac{\theta_1}{n} \quad\text{and}\quad \dot{\theta}_2 = \frac{\dot{\theta}_1}{n} $$ > [!NOTE] > This constraint is the *reason* the geared pair is *not* an independent DOF — it ties the two angular displacements to a single generalized coordinate. **Step 2 — Equivalence of Kinetic Energy:** Total KE of the geared system: $$ T = \tfrac{1}{2} I_1 \dot{\theta}_1^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} I_2 \dot{\theta}_2^{2} $$ Substituting $\dot{\theta}_2 = \dot{\theta}_1/n$: $$ T = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(I_1 + \dfrac{I_2}{n^{2}}\right)\dot{\theta}_1^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} I_{eq}\,\dot{\theta}_1^{2} $$ Hence the equivalent inertia **referred to shaft 1**: $$ \boxed{\;I_{eq,\,1} = I_1 + \dfrac{I_2}{n^{2}}\;} $$ **Step 3 — Equivalence of Strain Energy:** The shaft of stiffness $K$ stores energy only on shaft 1, so the equivalent stiffness referred to shaft 1 remains: $$ \boxed{\;K_{eq,\,1} = K\;} $$ **Step 4 — Equation of Motion & Natural Frequency:** Treating the equivalent SDOF as a simple torsional oscillator: $$ I_{eq}\,\ddot{\theta}_1 + K_{eq}\,\theta_1 = 0 $$ $$ \boxed{\;\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{eq}}{I_{eq}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{I_1 + \dfrac{I_2}{n^{2}}}}\;\text{rad/s}\;} $$ ### 2.3 Inverse Referral (to Shaft 2) By symmetry, when we refer *to shaft 2*, $n$ becomes $1/n$ and: $$ \boxed{\;I_{eq,\,2} = I_2 + I_1\,n^{2}, \quad K_{eq,\,2} = K\,n^{2}\;} $$ **Verification of frequency invariance:** $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{eq,\,2}}{I_{eq,\,2}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K\,n^{2}}{I_2 + I_1\,n^{2}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{I_2/n^{2} + I_1}} \;\;\checkmark $$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** In automobile drivetrains, marine propulsion shafts, and turbine-generator sets, gear pairs drastically shift the dominant inertia between the engine side and the load side. Modelling the system using the *equivalent SDOF* lets design engineers predict **critical speeds** and avoid resonance failure of shafts and couplings. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Gear ratio | $n = N_2/N_1 = \omega_1/\omega_2$ | dimensionless | | 2 | Kinematic constraint | $\theta_2 = \theta_1/n$ | rad | | 3 | Eq. inertia referred to shaft 1 | $I_{eq,1} = I_1 + I_2/n^{2}$ | kg·m² | | 4 | Eq. stiffness referred to shaft 1 | $K_{eq,1} = K$ | N·m/rad | | 5 | Natural frequency (ωₙ) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_{eq}/I_{eq}}$ | rad/s | | 6 | Natural frequency (fₙ) | $f_n = \omega_n/(2\pi)$ | Hz | | 7 | Period | $T = 2\pi/\omega_n$ | s | | 8 | Eq. inertia referred to shaft 2 | $I_{eq,2} = I_2 + I_1\,n^{2}$ | kg·m² | | 9 | Eq. stiffness referred to shaft 2 | $K_{eq,2} = K\,n^{2}$ | N·m/rad | | 10 | Three-rotor geared equivalent (referred to shaft 1) | $I_{eq} = I_1 + I_2/n_2^{2} + I_3/(n_2\,n_3)^{2}$ | kg·m² | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Numerical Examples > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Note:** Board examiners award full marks *only* when each numerical substitution step is *explicitly* written out. Skipping a step (e.g., directly writing $I_{eq}$) costs 1–2 marks consistently. ### 3.1 Derivation — Two-Rotor Geared System from Lagrange's Equation **Generalized coordinate** chosen: $\theta_1$ (shaft 1 angular displacement). **Kinetic Energy:** $$ T = \tfrac{1}{2}\,I_1\,\dot{\theta}_1^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,I_2\,\dot{\theta}_2^{2} $$ Since $\theta_2 = \theta_1/n$: $$ \dot{\theta}_2 = \frac{1}{n}\dot{\theta}_1 $$ $$ T = \tfrac{1}{2}\,I_1\,\dot{\theta}_1^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,I_2\!\left(\frac{\dot{\theta}_1}{n}\right)^{\!2} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(I_1 + \frac{I_2}{n^{2}}\right)\!\dot{\theta}_1^{2} $$ **Potential Energy** (torsional strain energy of shaft): $$ U = \tfrac{1}{2}\,K\,\theta_1^{2} $$ **Lagrange's Equation** for free vibration (no forcing): $$ \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1}\right) - \frac{\partial T}{\partial \theta_1} + \frac{\partial U}{\partial \theta_1} = 0 $$ $$ \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\left(I_1 + \frac{I_2}{n^{2}}\right)\dot{\theta}_1\right] + K\,\theta_1 = 0 $$ $$ \left(I_1 + \frac{I_2}{n^{2}}\right)\ddot{\theta}_1 + K\,\theta_1 = 0 $$ Dividing throughout by $I_{eq}$: $$ \ddot{\theta}_1 + \frac{K}{I_1 + \dfrac{I_2}{n^{2}}}\,\theta_1 = 0 $$ This is the **standard SHM equation** $\ddot{\theta} + \omega_n^{2}\,\theta = 0$, giving: $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{I_1 + \dfrac{I_2}{n^{2}}}}\;\;\text{rad/s} \quad\blacksquare $$ --- ### 3.2 Numerical Example 1 — Classic Two-Rotor Geared System *(KTU Frequently Asked Type)* **Problem Statement:** A geared system consists of shaft A carrying a rotor of $I_A = 6\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$ and a pinion of 20 teeth, meshing with a gear of 50 teeth on shaft B. Shaft B carries a rotor of $I_B = 25\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$. The torsional stiffness of the shaft on side A is $K = 4 \times 10^{5}\;\text{N·m/rad}$. Determine: (a) The equivalent moment of inertia referred to shaft A. (b) The natural frequency of free torsional vibration in rad/s and in Hz. (c) The natural frequency if the system were referred to shaft B and solved there. #### Solution **Step 1 — Compute gear ratio:** $$ n = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{50}{20} = 2.5 $$ **Step 2 (a) — Equivalent inertia referred to shaft A:** $$ I_{eq,A} = I_A + \frac{I_B}{n^{2}} = 6 + \frac{25}{(2.5)^{2}} $$ $$ I_{eq,A} = 6 + \frac{25}{6.25} = 6 + 4 = \mathbf{10\;\text{kg·m}^{2}} $$ **Step 3 (b) — Natural frequency referred to shaft A:** $$ K_{eq,A} = K = 4 \times 10^{5}\;\text{N·m/rad} $$ $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{eq,A}}{I_{eq,A}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 10^{5}}{10}} $$ $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{4}} = 200\;\text{rad/s} $$ $$ f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{200}{2\pi} = \mathbf{31.83\;\text{Hz}} $$ **Step 4 (c) — Referred to shaft B:** Equivalent inertia referred to shaft B: $$ I_{eq,B} = I_B + I_A\,n^{2} = 25 + 6\,(2.5)^{2} = 25 + 37.5 = 62.5\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ Equivalent stiffness referred to shaft B: $$ K_{eq,B} = K\,n^{2} = 4 \times 10^{5} \times 6.25 = 25 \times 10^{5}\;\text{N·m/rad} $$ $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{25 \times 10^{5}}{62.5}} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{4}} = \mathbf{200\;\text{rad/s}\;\;\checkmark} $$ > [!TIP] > **Sanity check:** The natural frequency is *invariant* under the choice of reference shaft. Examiners love this cross-verification step — always include it for bonus marks. --- ### 3.3 Numerical Example 2 — Three-Rotor Compound Geared System **Problem Statement:** A turbine-generator geared train has: - Shaft I: $I_1 = 2\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$, pinion $N_1 = 20$ teeth, shaft stiffness $K_1 = 1.5 \times 10^{5}\;\text{N·m/rad}$. - Shaft II (intermediate): $I_2 = 5\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$, carries gear $N_2 = 40$ teeth and pinion $N_3 = 25$ teeth. - Shaft III: $I_3 = 20\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$, carries gear $N_4 = 75$ teeth. Compute the natural frequency of free torsional vibration. #### Solution **Step 1 — Gear ratios between successive pairs:** $$ n_{12} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{40}{20} = 2, \qquad n_{23} = \frac{N_4}{N_3} = \frac{75}{25} = 3 $$ **Step 2 — Refer all inertias to shaft I:** For shaft II, the reduction factor is $n_{12} = 2$: $$ I_{2,\,ref} = \frac{I_2}{n_{12}^{2}} = \frac{5}{4} = 1.25\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ For shaft III, the cumulative ratio is $n_{12}\,n_{23} = 2 \times 3 = 6$: $$ I_{3,\,ref} = \frac{I_3}{(n_{12}\,n_{23})^{2}} = \frac{20}{36} = 0.5556\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Total equivalent inertia referred to shaft I:** $$ I_{eq} = I_1 + I_{2,\,ref} + I_{3,\,ref} $$ $$ I_{eq} = 2 + 1.25 + 0.5556 = \mathbf{3.8056\;\text{kg·m}^{2}} $$ **Step 4 — Natural frequency:** Since the torsional shaft is on side I only: $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_1}{I_{eq}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{5}}{3.8056}} $$ $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{3.9416 \times 10^{4}} = \mathbf{198.5\;\text{rad/s}} $$ $$ f_n = \dfrac{198.5}{2\pi} = \mathbf{31.6\;\text{Hz}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Because gear reduction shrinks the *reflected* inertia of downstream rotors, even very heavy generators effectively "look" light to the driving turbine. This is why critical-speed analysis of geared turbine trains usually focuses on the *input* shaft. --- ### 3.4 Numerical Example 3 — Geared System with Branched Shaft **Problem Statement:** A geared system has shaft X with rotor $I_X = 8\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$ and shaft stiffness $K_X = 9 \times 10^{4}\;\text{N·m/rad}$ connecting it to ground. Shaft X has a pinion of 30 teeth meshing with two gears on a parallel counter-shaft Y: gear A with 60 teeth (carrying rotor $I_A = 4\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$) and gear B with 90 teeth (carrying rotor $I_B = 16\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$). Compute the natural frequency of the equivalent torsional system. #### Solution **Step 1 — Individual gear ratios:** $$ n_A = \frac{60}{30} = 2, \qquad n_B = \frac{90}{30} = 3 $$ **Step 2 — Reflect each parallel rotor to shaft X:** $$ I_{A,\,ref} = \frac{I_A}{n_A^{2}} = \frac{4}{4} = 1\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ $$ I_{B,\,ref} = \frac{I_B}{n_B^{2}} = \frac{16}{9} = 1.7778\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Total equivalent inertia on shaft X:** $$ I_{eq} = I_X + I_{A,\,ref} + I_{B,\,ref} = 8 + 1 + 1.7778 = \mathbf{10.7778\;\text{kg·m}^{2}} $$ **Step 4 — Natural frequency:** $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_X}{I_{eq}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \times 10^{4}}{10.7778}} $$ $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{8350.5} = \mathbf{91.38\;\text{rad/s}} $$ $$ f_n = \dfrac{91.38}{2\pi} = \mathbf{14.54\;\text{Hz}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Generalization rule for branched/cascade gears:** Reflected inertia of the $i$-th rotor equals $I_i$ divided by the *square of the cumulative gear ratio* between its shaft and the reference shaft. This is the single most-tested rule in KTU Module 4 numericals. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Equivalent-System Reduction ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Physical Geared System
Shaft 1: I1, Pinion N1
Shaft 2: I2, Gear N2
Torsional Spring K] --> B{Identify Gear Ratio} B --> C["n = N2 / N1"] C --> D["Apply Kinematic Constraint
theta2 = theta1 / n"] D --> E["Compute Equivalent Inertia
Ieq = I1 + I2 / n squared"] E --> F["Equivalent Stiffness
Keq = K"] F --> G["Equivalent SDOF Torsional Model
Ieq on shaft 1, Keq spring"] G --> H["Equation of Motion
Ieq theta1_ddot + Keq theta1 = 0"] H --> I["Natural Frequency
omega_n = sqrt Keq over Ieq"] I --> J["Output: omega_n rad per s
and f_n Hz"] ``` ### 4.2 Schematic — Three-Rotor Compound Geared System Block Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[Shaft I] I1[Rotor I1 = 2] P1[Pinion N1 = 20] end subgraph S2[Shaft II Intermediate] I2[Rotor I2 = 5] G2[Gear N2 = 40] P3[Pinion N3 = 25] end subgraph S3[Shaft III] I3[Rotor I3 = 20] G4[Gear N4 = 75] end K[Shaft Stiffness K1] -.fixed support.-> S1 P1 --> G2 P3 --> G4 ``` ### 4.3 Equivalent-Shaft Transformation Diagram (Reference Axis Switch) ```mermaid flowchart TD R1[Referred to Shaft 1
Ieq1 = I1 + I2 by n squared
Keq1 = K] --> F[Natural Frequency
omega_n identical] R2[Referred to Shaft 2
Ieq2 = I2 + I1 n squared
Keq2 = K n squared] --> F F --> R[Result omega_n same
Choice of reference is irrelevant] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1(a)]** > **Q1.** Define *equivalent moment of inertia* of a geared torsional system and state its formula when referred to the shaft carrying the pinion. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The equivalent moment of inertia of a geared torsional system is the moment of inertia of an *imaginary single rotor* that, when mounted on a chosen reference shaft, possesses the same total kinetic energy as the actual multi-shaft geared system for the same generalized velocity. **When referred to the pinion shaft (shaft 1):** $$ I_{eq,1} = I_1 + \dfrac{I_2}{n^{2}}, \quad \text{where } n = N_2/N_1 $$ This arises because the gear ratio scales the angular velocity of shaft 2 by a factor $1/n$, thereby reducing its contribution to the kinetic energy by $1/n^{2}$. *(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — physical reasoning)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q1(b)]** > **Q2.** Why is the natural frequency of a geared torsional system *independent* of the choice of reference shaft? Justify in one or two lines. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The natural frequency is an *intrinsic* dynamic property of the system, depending only on the ratio of the total kinetic energy to the total potential energy. Since both kinetic energy ($T$) and potential energy ($U$) are *scalars* invariant under the change of reference, the frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_{eq}/I_{eq}}$ yields the same numerical value regardless of which shaft is chosen for equivalence. *(2 marks — invariance argument, 1 mark — correct scalar-energy reasoning)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — *Internal Choice* > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model 2024, Q4 Module 4]** > > **Question A (14 Marks)** > A two-rotor geared torsional system consists of: > - Shaft 1: $I_1 = 12\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$, pinion with 24 teeth, torsional stiffness $K = 6 \times 10^{5}\;\text{N·m/rad}$. > - Shaft 2: $I_2 = 30\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$, gear with 72 teeth. > > **(a)** Derive the equation of motion using Lagrange's method and obtain the natural frequency of free torsional vibration. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** If a third rotor of $I_3 = 18\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$ is added on shaft 2 meshing via a further pinion (15 teeth) and gear (45 teeth), recompute the natural frequency and comment on the change. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) Derivation + Natural Frequency (7 marks):** **Step 1** — Choose generalized coordinate $\theta_1$. **Step 2** — Kinematic constraint: $\theta_2 = \theta_1/n$, with $n = 72/24 = 3$. **Step 3** — Kinetic energy: $$ T = \tfrac{1}{2}\,I_1\,\dot{\theta}_1^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,I_2\!\left(\frac{\dot{\theta}_1}{n}\right)^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(12 + \tfrac{30}{9}\right)\dot{\theta}_1^{2} $$ $$ T = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(12 + 3.3333)\,\dot{\theta}_1^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(15.3333)\,\dot{\theta}_1^{2} $$ **Step 4** — Potential energy: $$ U = \tfrac{1}{2}\,K\,\theta_1^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(6 \times 10^{5})\,\theta_1^{2} $$ **Step 5** — Lagrange's equation: $$ \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(I_{eq}\dot{\theta}_1\right) + K\theta_1 = 0 $$ $$ I_{eq}\ddot{\theta}_1 + K\theta_1 = 0 $$ Substituting $I_{eq} = 15.3333\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$, $K = 6 \times 10^{5}\;\text{N·m/rad}$: $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 10^{5}}{15.3333}} = \sqrt{39130.4} = \mathbf{197.8\;\text{rad/s}} $$ *(1 mark — generalized coordinate choice, 1 mark — gear ratio, 2 marks — KE & PE, 2 marks — Lagrange, 1 mark — final answer)* **(b) Three-rotor extension (7 marks):** Additional cumulative ratio to shaft 3 from shaft 1: $$ n_{12}\cdot n_{23} = 3 \times \frac{45}{15} = 3 \times 3 = 9 $$ Reflect $I_3$ to shaft 1: $$ I_{3,\,ref} = \frac{I_3}{(n_{12}\,n_{23})^{2}} = \frac{18}{81} = 0.2222\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ New equivalent inertia: $$ I_{eq}^{new} = I_1 + \frac{I_2}{n_{12}^{2}} + \frac{I_3}{(n_{12}n_{23})^{2}} = 12 + 3.3333 + 0.2222 = 15.5555\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ New natural frequency: $$ \omega_n^{new} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 10^{5}}{15.5555}} = \sqrt{38571.4} = \mathbf{196.4\;\text{rad/s}} $$ **Comment:** Adding a downstream rotor *increases* the equivalent inertia slightly, hence the natural frequency *drops* marginally (from 197.8 to 196.4 rad/s). Because the cumulative gear ratio is large, the third rotor's contribution is heavily damped, which is why critical-speed shifts in such cascades are small. *(7 marks — full working + comment)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – Alternative Module 4 Choice, Q4 Module 4]** > > **Question B (14 Marks)** > A geared system has a driving shaft (shaft 1) with rotor $I_1 = 4\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$, pinion of 18 teeth, and torsional stiffness $K = 2 \times 10^{5}\;\text{N·m/rad}$. The pinion meshes with a gear of 54 teeth on shaft 2, which carries rotor $I_2 = 12\;\text{kg·m}^{2}$. > > **(a)** Determine the equivalent moment of inertia referred to both shafts, and verify the natural frequency is identical. *(7 marks)* > > **(b)** If a braking torque of 1500 N·m is suddenly applied at shaft 2 when the system is rotating uniformly at 60 rad/s, determine the maximum angular twist of shaft 1 during the subsequent free vibration. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) Equivalence and verification (7 marks):** Gear ratio: $$ n = 54/18 = 3 $$ Referred to shaft 1: $$ I_{eq,1} = 4 + \dfrac{12}{3^{2}} = 4 + \dfrac{12}{9} = 4 + 1.3333 = 5.3333\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ $$ K_{eq,1} = K = 2 \times 10^{5}\;\text{N·m/rad} $$ $$ \omega_n^{(1)} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10^{5}}{5.3333}} = \sqrt{37500} = \mathbf{193.65\;\text{rad/s}} $$ Referred to shaft 2: $$ I_{eq,2} = 12 + 4 \times 3^{2} = 12 + 36 = 48\;\text{kg·m}^{2} $$ $$ K_{eq,2} = 2 \times 10^{5} \times 3^{2} = 18 \times 10^{5}\;\text{N·m/rad} $$ $$ \omega_n^{(2)} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18 \times 10^{5}}{48}} = \sqrt{37500} = \mathbf{193.65\;\text{rad/s}\;\checkmark} $$ *(3 marks — both equivalent parameters, 2 marks — two frequencies, 2 marks — verification statement)* **(b) Maximum twist after sudden torque (7 marks):** The sudden braking torque on shaft 2 corresponds to an equivalent torque on shaft 1: $$ T_{eq,1} = \frac{T_{brake}}{n} = \frac{1500}{3} = 500\;\text{N·m} $$ This produces a *static* twist in shaft 1: $$ \theta_{static} = \dfrac{T_{eq,1}}{K} = \dfrac{500}{2 \times 10^{5}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{rad} $$ During free vibration initiated from the steady state with initial angular velocity $\dot{\theta}_1(0) = 60/n = 20\;\text{rad/s}$: The vibration amplitude combines static deflection and dynamic effect. Maximum angular twist: $$ \theta_{max} = \theta_{static} + \dfrac{\dot{\theta}_1(0)}{\omega_n} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} + \dfrac{20}{193.65} $$ $$ \theta_{max} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} + 0.1033 = \mathbf{0.1058\;\text{rad}\;(\approx 6.06^{\circ})} $$ *(2 marks — equivalent torque, 2 marks — static twist, 3 marks — dynamic amplitude + final answer)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Forgetting to square the gear ratio** when transferring inertia — this is the #1 mistake. Always write $I_2/n^{2}$, *never* $I_2/n$. > 2. **Mixing up $N_2/N_1$ with $\omega_1/\omega_2$**: although they are reciprocals depending on which shaft is the pinion, students often flip the ratio silently and lose 2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting the cumulative ratio** in multi-stage gears: the third rotor's reduction factor is $(n_1 n_2 \ldots)^2$, not just $n_1^{2}$. > 4. **Not verifying** the natural frequency by referring to the opposite shaft — losing the easy 1–2 mark cross-check bonus. > 5. **Unit confusion:** $K$ must be in N·m/rad (not N/m) — examiners explicitly deduct for wrong units. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Free torsional vibration** = angular oscillation of multi-rotor geared system about equilibrium *without* external forcing. - [x] **Gear ratio** $n = N_{\text{gear}}/N_{\text{pinion}} = \omega_{\text{pinion}}/\omega_{\text{gear}}$. - [x] **Kinematic constraint** $\theta_2 = \theta_1/n$ is the foundation of all energy-equivalence arguments. - [x] **Equivalent inertia (to shaft 1):** $I_{eq} = I_1 + I_2/n^{2}$. - [x] **Equivalent stiffness (to shaft 1):** $K_{eq} = K$. - [x] **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_{eq}/I_{eq}}$. - [x] **Frequency invariance:** same answer regardless of reference shaft. - [x] **Inverse referral:** $I_{eq,2} = I_2 + I_1 n^{2}$ and $K_{eq,2} = K n^{2}$. - [x] **Multi-stage rule:** reflected inertia of $k$-th rotor $= I_k / (\text{cumulative gear ratio from reference})^{2}$. - [x] **Branched-gear rule:** reflect *each* parallel rotor to the reference shaft and *sum*. - [x] **Engineering applications:** turbine-generator sets, automotive drivetrains, marine propeller shafts, helicopter gearboxes. - [x] **Always convert** angular velocity from one shaft to another using $1/n$ before plugging into vibration equations. - [x] **Always verify units** ($K$ in N·m/rad, $I$ in kg·m², $\omega_n$ in rad/s). - [x] **Watch for cumulative ratios** in 3 or more rotor systems — this is the highest-weight sub-question in KTU exams. - [x] **Bonus tip:** for sudden torque problems, equivalent torque on reference shaft = applied torque ÷ $n$ (when torque is on the *gear* shaft).
